## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fission Rate for a 1 GWₑ Reactor

> **Objective:** Find the number of fissions per second for a 1000 MWe PWR operating at thermal efficiency 33%.

**Step 1 — Convert electrical output to thermal output.**
$P_{\text{thermal}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{electrical}}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1000\,\text{MW}}{0.33}$.

**Step 2 — Compute value.**
$P_{\text{thermal}} = 3030\,\text{MW} = 3.03 \times 10^9\,\text{J/s}$.

**Step 3 — Divide by energy per fission.**
$E_{\text{fission}} = 200\,\text{MeV} = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{J} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J}$.

**Step 4 — Fission rate calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{n}_{\text{fission}} &= \frac{P_{\text{thermal}}}{E_{\text{fission}}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{3.03 \times 10^9\,\text{J/s}}{3.204 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{J/fission}} \\[4pt]
&= 9.46 \times 10^{19}\,\text{fissions/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to mass of U-235 consumed per day.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{N}_{\text{daily}} &= 9.46 \times 10^{19} \times 86400 = 8.17 \times 10^{24}\,\text{fissions/day} \\[4pt]
m_{\text{day}} &= \frac{\dot{N}_{\text{daily}} \times 235}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{8.17 \times 10^{24} \times 235}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \\[4pt]
&= 3.19\,\text{kg/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{A 1 GWe reactor consumes } \approx 3.2\,\text{kg of U-235 per day.}\;}
$$

> **"How" comment:** A 1 GWe coal plant needs ≈ 10,000 tonnes of coal per day — over **3 million times** more mass to move, store, and combust. This single calculation justifies nuclear's appeal for *port-independent* baseload power.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mass-Energy Equivalence Applied to Fusion (Forward Look)

> **Objective:** Calculate the energy release if 1 kg of hydrogen is fully fused to helium (4 He).

**Step 1 — Identify the mass defect.**
Reaction: $4\,^1\text{H} \rightarrow {}^4\text{He} + 2e^+ + 2\nu + \text{energy}$.

Sum of reactant masses: $4 \times 1.007825\,\text{u} = 4.03130\,\text{u}$.
Mass of ⁴He: $4.002603\,\text{u}$.
Mass defect: $\Delta m = 4.03130 - 4.002603 = 0.028697\,\text{u}$.

**Step 2 — Convert atomic mass units to kilograms.**
$1\,\text{u} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\,\text{kg}$, so
$\Delta m = 0.028697 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 4.765 \times 10^{-29}\,\text{kg}$ per reaction.

**Step 3 — For 1 kg of hydrogen, number of reactions is $N/4$:**
$N = \dfrac{1\,\text{kg}}{1.007825 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}} \approx 5.98 \times 10^{26}\,\text{atoms}$,
so number of fusion events $= N/4 = 1.495 \times 10^{26}$.

**Step 4 — Total energy released:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= N_{\text{fusion}} \times \Delta m \times c^2 \\[4pt]
&= (1.495 \times 10^{26}) \times (4.765 \times 10^{-29}) \times (2.998 \times 10^8)^2 \\[4pt]
&= 6.41 \times 10^{14}\,\text{J}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;1\,\text{kg of fully fused hydrogen} \rightarrow \approx 6.4 \times 10^{14}\,\text{J} \approx 7.8 \times \text{(fission energy)}\;}
$$

> **Why this matters for Module 1:** Module 1 ends by *teasing* fusion. The KTU examiner routinely gives a 3-mark "compare" question asking the student to state *which gives more energy per kg, fission or fusion, and by what factor*. The answer is **fusion ≈ 4× fission, both vastly more than chemical burning**.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Quick-Comparison Table for Exam

| Parameter | Coal | Oil | Natural Gas | Nuclear (Fission) | Solar PV | Wind |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy density (MJ/kg) | 24 | 42 | 55 | **8.2 × 10⁷** | – | – |
| CO₂ (g/kWh) | 820 | 720 | 490 | **12** | 40 | 11 |
| Capacity factor | 60 % | – | – | **90 %** | 25 % | 35 % |
| Reserve (yrs, current) | 130 | 50 | 50 | **30,000 (FBR)** | ∞ | ∞ |
| Land use (m²/MW·yr) | 1000 | – | – | **150** | 6000 | 12000 |
| Fuel price volatility | High | High | High | **Low** | None | None |

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Classify energy sources into conventional and non-conventional. Give two examples for each and state which category nuclear energy falls into.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — award 1 mark per point):**

1. **Conventional energy sources** are those that have been in use since the industrial era and have established extraction, distribution, and consumption technologies. Examples: **Coal** and **Natural Gas**.
2. **Non-conventional energy sources** are the newer alternatives, including renewables and modern options. Examples: **Solar energy** and **Wind energy**.
3. **Nuclear energy** is classified as a **Non-Conventional, Non-Renewable, but Non-Fossil** source. It is the *only* large-scale, carbon-free, baseload-capable technology in the modern energy mix. *(1 mark)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Primary Energy Source." How is it different from a "Secondary Energy Source"? Give one example of each.*

**Model Answer:**

- A **Primary Energy Source** is found in nature and is used **without any transformation** (e.g., *coal, crude oil, sunlight, uranium ore*). *(1 mark)*
- A **Secondary Energy Source** is produced by *converting* a primary source through a man-made process. It is a *carrier* of energy rather than a *source*. Examples: *electricity, hydrogen, gasoline*. *(1 mark)*
- The fundamental difference: primary sources possess *intrinsic* energy; secondary sources merely *transport* or *re-package* the energy of a primary source. *(1 mark)*

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, with a typical split of **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student answers **one full question** (with both sub-parts) out of the offered choice.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

#### *(a) [7 marks]* Compare the energy density of fossil fuels and nuclear fuels. Show that fission of 1 kg of U-235 releases about 3 million times more energy than combustion of 1 kg of coal.

**Step 1 — Define Energy Density.** [1 mark]
Energy density is the amount of useful energy released per unit mass of a fuel, measured in MJ/kg (or J/kg).

**Step 2 — Quote standard values.** [1 mark]
- Coal (combustion): $E_{\text{coal}} \approx 24\,\text{MJ/kg} = 2.4 \times 10^7\,\text{J/kg}$.
- U-235 (fission): $E_{\text{U-235}} \approx 200\,\text{MeV/atom}$.

**Step 3 — Compute the energy per kg of U-235.** [3 marks]
Number of atoms per kg: $N = \dfrac{1000}{235} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 2.563 \times 10^{24}\,\text{atoms}$.

Total energy: $E = N \times 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} = 8.21 \times 10^{13}\,\text{J}$.

**Step 4 — Form the ratio.** [2 marks]

$$
\frac{E_{\text{U-235}}}{E_{\text{coal}}} = \frac{8.21 \times 10^{13}}{2.4 \times 10^7} \approx 3.42 \times 10^6
$$

**Conclusion:** $1\,\text{kg U-235} \equiv 3.4 \times 10^6\,\text{kg coal}$ — confirming the ~3-million-fold advantage. This compactness is the central reason nuclear power is attractive for long-duration, baseload electricity generation.

#### *(b) [7 marks]* Explain the four-pillar justification for nuclear energy with reference to the Indian energy scenario.

**Pillar 1 — Energy Density (2 marks)**
As shown in part (a), 1 kg of U-235 ≈ 3.4 million kg of coal. For a country like India, which already imports ≈ 200 million tonnes of coal annually, replacing fossil baseload with nuclear *massively reduces* the logistics burden of fuel transport.

**Pillar 2 — Low Carbon Footprint (2 marks)**
Lifecycle CO₂ emissions of nuclear ≈ 12 g/kWh, comparable to wind (11 g/kWh) and far below coal (820 g/kWh). India has committed to **Net-Zero by 2070**; nuclear is a key pillar of that transition because it is *both clean and firm*.

**Pillar 3 — Reliability / High Capacity Factor (1.5 marks)**
Nuclear plants globally achieve ≈ 90% capacity factor; in India, KAPS, KGS, and TAPS routinely exceed 85%. Solar and wind are 20–35% CF and require storage. For a country of India's size, firm baseload is non-negotiable.

**Pillar 4 — Resource Adequacy with Breeder Technology (1.5 marks)**
India holds ≈ 8% of global thorium reserves. The **three-stage nuclear programme** (PHWR → FBR → Th-U²³³ cycle) — designed by Homi Bhabha in the 1950s — gives India effectively unlimited fuel. The Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor (PFBR) at Kalpakkam is a milestone toward this target.

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Alternative to Question A)**

#### *(a) [7 marks]* With a neat sketch, explain the energy conversion chain from primary source to end use for (i) a coal-fired thermal power plant, and (ii) a nuclear power plant. Compare their efficiencies and emissions.

**Diagram (3 marks — textual version):**

```
COAL:  Coal (Primary) → Boiler/Steam Turbine (Conversion) → Electricity (Carrier) → End Use
       Waste: Fly Ash, SO₂, CO₂, NOx, Bottom Ash

NUCLEAR:  U-235 (Primary) → Reactor/Steam Turbine (Conversion) → Electricity (Carrier) → End Use
          Waste: Spent Fuel (radioactive, contained); CO₂ ≈ 0 in operation
```

**Efficiencies (2 marks):**
- Coal (sub-critical): 33–38% (modern supercritical: 45%)
- Nuclear PWR: ≈ 33% thermal efficiency

**Emissions (2 marks):**
- Coal: 820 gCO₂/kWh + pollutants (SOₓ, NOₓ, particulates)
- Nuclear: 12 gCO₂/kWh (lifecycle, mostly from construction)

**Conclusion:** While their thermal efficiencies are similar, the *emission profiles* differ by a factor of ≈ 70, which is the deciding factor in policy decisions like India's *Panchamrit* climate commitments.

#### *(b) [7 marks]* A 1000 MWe nuclear power plant operates at a thermal efficiency of 33% and uses U-235 as fuel. Calculate: (i) the thermal power output, (ii) the number of fissions per second, and (iii) the daily mass consumption of U-235.

**Given:** $P_e = 1000\,\text{MWe}$, $\eta = 0.33$, $E_{\text{fission}} = 200\,\text{MeV/atom}$, $M_{\text{U-235}} = 235\,\text{g/mol}$.

**(i) Thermal power output [2 marks]:**

$$
P_{\text{th}} = \frac{P_e}{\eta} = \frac{1000\,\text{MW}}{0.33} = 3030\,\text{MW} = 3.03 \times 10^9\,\text{J/s}
$$

**(ii) Number of fissions per second [3 marks]:**

$$
\dot{n} = \frac{P_{\text{th}}}{E_{\text{fission}}} = \frac{3.03 \times 10^9}{200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13}} = 9.46 \times 10^{19}\,\text{fissions/s}
$$

**(iii) Daily mass of U-235 [2 marks]:**

$$
m_{\text{day}} = \frac{\dot{n} \times 86400 \times 235}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} = \frac{9.46 \times 10^{19} \times 86400 \times 235}{6.022 \times 10^{23}} \approx 3.19\,\text{kg/day}
$$

**Final answer:** The plant consumes only ≈ **3.2 kg of U-235 per day** — illustrating the extraordinary energy density of nuclear fuel.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Commonly Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing eV with MeV** — Energy per fission is **200 MeV**, not 200 eV. A slip here causes a 10⁶ error in the final answer.
> 2. **Forgetting to divide by efficiency** — When asked for *fission rate* from *electrical output*, you must first convert to *thermal* output using $\eta$. A 33% efficiency is *not* optional.
> 3. **Mixing up J and kJ** — $1\,\text{MeV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-13}\,\text{J}$, not $\times 10^{-10}$ or $\times 10^{-16}$.
> 4. **Neglecting Avogadro's number** — A common error is computing *energy per kg* by multiplying 200 MeV by 1000, forgetting to count atoms per kg.
> 5. **Spelling "nuclear" as "nucular"** — automatic 0.5 mark cut in viva, even if logic is correct.
> 6. **In classification questions**, students often write "nuclear is renewable" — this is **wrong**; uranium is finite, but *breeders* make it effectively inexhaustible.

---

## **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)**

> [!TIP]
> **Module 1 — Energy Sources: One-Page Mind-Map for Last-Minute Revision**

- **Definition:** Energy sources = natural reserves from which useful work/heat can be extracted. Primary vs. Secondary is the first split.
- **Three classifications** to know: (1) Conventional / Non-Conventional, (2) Renewable / Non-Renewable, (3) Fossil / Non-Fossil.
- **Nuclear position:** *Non-Conventional, Non-Renewable, but Non-Fossil* — unique in the energy mix.
- **Conversion chain:** Source → Plant → Carrier → End Use. Efficiency must be quoted at the *plant* stage.
- **Energy units:** $1\,\text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$; $1\,\text{MeV} = 200\times$ per fission; $1\,\text{kWh} = 3.6 \times 10^6\,\text{J}$; $1\,\text{toe} = 41.87\,\text{GJ}$; $1\,\text{Quad} = 1.055 \times 10^{18}\,\text{J}$.
- **Headline number 1:** $1\,\text{kg U-235} \equiv 3.4 \times 10^6\,\text{kg coal}$ (3 million).
- **Headline number 2:** $E = mc^2$ — every nuclear process converts mass, not chemistry.
- **Headline number 3:** $1\,\text{GWe reactor} \equiv 3.2\,\text{kg U-235 per day}$ vs. **10,000 tonnes coal per day** for the same electrical output.
- **Four pillars** of nuclear justification: **Energy Density, Low Carbon, High Capacity Factor, Breeder-fueled Resource Adequacy**.
- **Indian context:** Three-stage programme (Homi Bhabha) — PHWR → FBR → Th-U²³³. PFBR at Kalpakkam is a key milestone.
- **Global context:** ≈ 440 reactors worldwide, ≈ 400 GWe installed, contributing ≈ 10% of global electricity. India's target: 100 GW nuclear by 2047.
- **Environmental:** 12 gCO₂/kWh nuclear ≈ 11 (wind) ≈ 40 (solar lifecycle) ≪ 820 (coal).
- **Equations to memorize for derivation problems:** $E = mc^2$; $P_{\text{th}} = P_e/\eta$; $\dot{n} = P_{\text{th}}/E_{\text{fission}}$; $m = \dot{n} M / N_A$.
- **Examiner-favourite comparison triplet:** "Compare coal, nuclear, and renewables on density, CO₂, and capacity factor" — prepare this as a 3×3 matrix.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Source of Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Present Source of Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Present Source of Energy** refers to the current portfolio of primary energy resources that the world exploits to meet its industrial, domestic, agricultural, transportation, and commercial energy demands. As per KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, this includes the conventional fossil-based sources (coal, petroleum, and natural gas), hydroelectric power, and other renewable sources that dominate the global energy mix in the 21st century. The study of present energy sources forms the foundational module of any nuclear energy course, as it establishes the **energy deficit**, **resource depletion timelines**, and **environmental burden** that necessitate transitioning to nuclear (and other advanced) alternatives.

> [!NOTE]
> **Primary vs Secondary Energy**
> - **Primary Energy**: Energy found in nature *before* any human conversion (coal, crude oil, sunlight, uranium, wind).
> - **Secondary Energy**: Energy derived from primary sources after conversion (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen, diesel).
> All reserve statistics in this module refer to **primary energy** unless stated otherwise.

## 1.2 Classification of Present Energy Sources

The complete hierarchy of the present world energy sources is best understood through two parallel classification schemes:

### A. Based on Renewability (Most Important for KTU)

| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Conventional (Non-Renewable)** | Finite reserves; cannot be replenished on human timescale | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium) |
| **Non-Conventional (Renewable)** | Replenished naturally; theoretically inexhaustible | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Geothermal, Biomass |

### B. Based on Continuous Use / Flow

| Category | Definition | Examples |
|----------|-----------|----------|
| **Conventional / Non-Renewable / Exhaustible** | Stored energy, finite stock | Coal, Oil, Gas, Uranium, Thorium |
| **Non-Conventional / Renewable / Inexhaustible** | Flow energy, continuous flux | Solar, Wind, Tidal, Geothermal |
| **Recyclable** | Can be reprocessed & re-used | Nuclear fuel (after reprocessing) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Account" of Earth

Imagine the Earth as a person with a **bank account** containing all fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas). Every year, humanity *withdraws* huge sums to power factories, vehicles, and homes. Unlike a normal account, **no deposits are being made** — coal took **300 million years** to form, while we are burning it in a few centuries.

The **"balance"** left is the **reserves**, and the **"yearly withdrawal"** is the **annual production**. The number of years the account will last = Reserves ÷ Annual Production. This is called the **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio** — the single most important metric in this module.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why study Present Sources in a Nuclear Energy course?**
> 1. To quantify **how long fossil fuels will last** (urgency for alternatives).
> 2. To compare **environmental impact** (CO₂, SO₂, particulate emissions).
> 3. To justify the **role of nuclear energy** as a clean, high-density, baseload alternative.
> 4. To introduce **energy units** and **conversion factors** used throughout the course.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **1 tonne of oil equivalent (toe)** = **41.868 GJ** = **10⁷ kcal** (approx.)
- **1 Quad** = 10¹⁵ BTU = 1.055 × 10¹⁸ J
- **1 Mtoe** (million tonne oil equivalent) = 41.868 PJ
- **1 kWh** = 3.6 MJ = 860 kcal
- **Calorific Value of Coal** ≈ **24 MJ/kg** (bituminous)
- **Calorific Value of Petroleum** ≈ **45 MJ/kg**
- **Calorific Value of Natural Gas** ≈ **55 MJ/kg** (per m³, methane basis)
- **World primary energy consumption (2023)** ≈ **620 EJ** (≈ 14,800 Mtoe)
- **Barrel of crude oil** = 159 litres ≈ 6.1 GJ

## 1.5 World Energy Consumption Snapshot

- **Per capita world energy use**: ~**75 GJ/person/year** (highly skewed — USA ~300 GJ, India ~25 GJ).
- **Largest consumers (2023)**: China (~25%), USA (~16%), India (~7%), Russia (~5%).
- **Fossil fuel share of world primary energy**: ~**81%** (coal 27%, oil 31%, gas 23%).
- **Renewables share (incl. hydro)**: ~**14%**.
- **Nuclear share**: ~**4%**.

> [!NOTE]
> Despite rapid renewable growth, **fossil fuels still supply >80% of world energy**. This single statistic is the cornerstone of Module 1 of this course — it establishes *why* nuclear energy is critical.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 World Energy Demand — The "Why"

Three fundamental drivers determine world energy demand, expressed compactly as:

$$\text{Energy Demand} \;=\; \text{Population} \times \text{Per Capita Consumption} \times \text{Development Index}$$

- **Population growth**: World population is projected to grow from 8 billion (2023) to ~9.7 billion by **2050** (UN projection).
- **Per capita consumption growth**: Driven by urbanization, industrialisation, and rising living standards in developing nations (India, China, Africa).
- **Energy intensity** is *decreasing* in developed nations due to efficiency, but total demand still grows because of the other two factors.

> **Doubling Time of Energy Demand**: At present growth rate (~2% per year), world energy demand doubles roughly every **35 years** (using the Rule of 70: T_doubling = 70 / growth%).

## 2.2 Fossil Fuels — Detailed Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Coal
- Most abundant fossil fuel globally.
- Formed from compressed plant matter over 100–400 million years.
- Reserves: ~**1,070 billion tonnes** (BP Statistical Review, 2023).
- **R/P ratio ≈ 140 years**.
- Major producers: China, India, USA, Australia, Indonesia, Russia.
- Major issue: Highest CO₂ emission per unit of energy among fossil fuels.

### 2.2.2 Petroleum (Crude Oil)
- Formed from marine microorganisms deposited on ocean floors.
- Reserves: ~**1,700 billion barrels** (≈ 240 Gt).
- **R/P ratio ≈ 50 years**.
- OPEC controls ~80% of proven reserves.
- Highly strategic commodity; subject to geopolitical tensions.

### 2.2.3 Natural Gas
- Often found alongside petroleum (associated gas) or independently.
- Cleanest fossil fuel — emits ~50% less CO₂ per unit energy than coal.
- Reserves: ~**200 trillion cubic metres (Tcm)**.
- **R/P ratio ≈ 50 years**.
- Methane (CH₄) is the main component; leakage is a major greenhouse concern.

## 2.3 Hydroelectric Power

- Largest **renewable** source today (~16% of world electricity).
- Hydropower potential: world technically exploitable potential ≈ **14,000 TWh/year**, of which only ~25% utilised.
- Major producers: China, Brazil, Canada, USA, Russia.
- **Limitation**: Site-specific, large land submergence, ecological impact, dependent on rainfall.

## 2.4 Other Renewables (Solar, Wind, Geothermal, Tidal, Biomass)

- **Solar**: Photovoltaic (PV) + Concentrated Solar Power (CSP). Cost dropped 90% in 10 years.
- **Wind**: Onshore and offshore; capacity factor 25–45%.
- **Geothermal**: Limited to tectonic boundaries (Iceland, Philippines, Kenya).
- **Tidal**: Range of only a few metres; site-specific (Gulf of Kutch, Bay of Fundy).
- **Biomass**: Carbon-neutral if managed sustainably; ethanol, biodiesel, biogas.

## 2.5 The Energy Crisis and Why Fossil Fuels are Unsustainable

| Issue | Coal | Oil | Gas |
|-------|------|-----|-----|
| **Reserve Life (years)** | ~140 | ~50 | ~50 |
| **CO₂ Emission Factor (kg/GJ)** | ~95 | ~75 | ~55 |
| **SO₂ / NOx Pollutants** | High | Medium | Low |
| **Particulate Matter (PM2.5)** | Very High | Medium | Negligible |
| **Spillage / Accident Risk** | Mining disasters | Oil spills (Exxon Valdez, Deepwater Horizon) | Pipeline explosions |
| **Geopolitical Concentration** | Distributed | OPEC-dominated | Russia/Qatar |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Crises Motivating Nuclear Energy**:
> 1. **Resource Crisis** — Fossil reserves will exhaust in 50–150 years.
> 2. **Environmental Crisis** — Climate change (CO₂), acid rain (SO₂), smog (NOx), particulates.
> 3. **Geopolitical Crisis** — Concentration of oil & gas reserves in politically unstable regions.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for KTU 2024 Semester Exams, including Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions from this module.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Unit / Notes |
|---|-------------------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 | **Reserve-to-Production Ratio** | $R/P \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Years; primary indicator of fuel longevity |
| 2 | **Energy from Mass of Fuel** | $E \;=\; m \times CV$ | J, where CV is calorific value (J/kg) |
| 3 | **Energy Density (by mass)** | $\rho_E \;=\; \dfrac{E}{m} \;=\; CV$ | J/kg |
| 4 | **Power (rate of energy use)** | $P \;=\; \dfrac{E}{t}$ | Watts (J/s) |
| 5 | **Annual Energy from Reserve** | $E_{\text{annual}} \;=\; M_{\text{reserve}} \times CV \times \eta$ | J/year; $\eta$ = thermal efficiency |
| 6 | **Fuel Burn Rate** | $\dot{m} \;=\; \dfrac{P}{CV \times \eta}$ | kg/s |
| 7 | **Energy Unit Conversion** | $1 \; \text{toe} \;=\; 41.868 \; \text{GJ}$ | Standard oil equivalent |
| 8 | **Energy Unit Conversion** | $1 \; \text{kWh} \;=\; 3.6 \; \text{MJ}$ | Electrical unit |
| 9 | **CO₂ Emission Factor** | $m_{\text{CO}_2} \;=\; E \times EF$ | kg, where EF (kg/GJ) depends on fuel |
| 10 | **Doubling Time of Demand** | $T_d \;=\; \dfrac{70}{r \,\%}$ | Years, for $r$% annual growth |
| 11 | **Energy Returned on Invested (EROI)** | $EROI \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Useful Energy Delivered}}{\text{Energy Invested}}$ | Dimensionless; must be $\geq 1$ |
| 12 | **Per Capita Energy** | $E_{\text{pc}} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{population}}}$ | GJ/person/year |
| 13 | **Mixed Fuel Average Calorific Value** | $CV_{\text{mix}} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum m_i \cdot CV_i}{\sum m_i}$ | J/kg (mass-weighted average) |
| 14 | **Energy in Hydro Reservoir** | $E_{\text{hydro}} \;=\; \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H \cdot t$ | J (ρ=density, g=9.81, Q=flow, H=head) |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Power plant design**: Selecting fuel requires knowing $CV$ to size the boiler and bunker.
- **National energy planning**: Governments use $R/P$ ratios to set policy timelines.
- **Climate modelling**: Emission factors (kg CO₂/GJ) feed directly into IPCC carbon budgets.
- **Electricity tariff calculation**: $E = m \times CV \times \eta \times \text{price/unit}$ — the basis of fuel cost pass-through.
- **Nuclear comparison**: 1 kg of natural uranium produces energy equivalent to **~14,000 kg of coal** — this is the *energy density* argument for nuclear.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Reserve-to-Production (R/P) Ratio

**Problem Statement**: A country has coal reserves of 100 billion tonnes and produces 2 billion tonnes per year. How long will the reserves last, assuming constant production?

**Step 1 — Define the quantities.**

Let $R$ = total reserves (in billion tonnes), $P$ = annual production (billion tonnes/year), and $T$ = time to exhaustion (years).

**Step 2 — Set up the consumption equation.**

If the country produces $P$ billion tonnes every year, the total quantity produced in $T$ years is:

$$
\text{Total Production in T years} \;=\; P \times T
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the exhaustion condition.**

When reserves are *just* exhausted, the total production equals the reserves:

$$
R \;=\; P \times T
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T$.**

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{R}{P}
$$

This ratio $T = R/P$ is the **Reserve-to-Production Ratio (R/P)**, measured in years.

**Step 5 — Numerical substitution.**

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{100 \; \text{billion tonnes}}{2 \; \text{billion tonnes/year}} \;=\; 50 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation.**

If the country keeps producing at the *same* rate, the coal will last 50 years. If production grows at rate $g$ per year, the time is shorter (modelled in derivation 2).

> [!NOTE]
> **Caveat**: The R/P ratio is a *static* estimate. In reality, reserves often *grow* as new discoveries are made (e.g., shale gas, tight oil). Hence, R/P is a planning indicator, not a countdown clock.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Reserves with Growing Demand

**Problem Statement**: Same 100 billion tonnes of coal, but production grows at 3% per year. How long will it last?

**Step 1 — Express cumulative production as a geometric series.**

If production in year $n$ is $P_0 (1 + g)^{n-1}$ (starting with $P_0$ in year 1), the total produced over $T$ years is:

$$
R \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{T} P_0 (1 + g)^{n-1}
$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the geometric series.**

The sum of a finite geometric series with first term $P_0$, common ratio $(1+g)$, and $T$ terms is:

$$
R \;=\; P_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)^{T} - 1}{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $T$ in closed form.**

$$
(1+g)^{T} \;=\; 1 + g \cdot \dfrac{R}{P_0}
$$

Taking natural logarithm of both sides:

$$
T \cdot \ln(1+g) \;=\; \ln\!\left(1 + g \cdot \dfrac{R}{P_0}\right)
$$

$$
\boxed{\,T \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left(1 + g \cdot R/P_0\right)}{\ln(1 + g)}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical substitution with $R = 100$, $P_0 = 2$, $g = 0.03$.**

$$
g \cdot \dfrac{R}{P_0} \;=\; 0.03 \times 50 \;=\; 1.5
$$

$$
\ln(1 + 1.5) \;=\; \ln(2.5) \;=\; 0.9163
$$

$$
\ln(1.03) \;=\; 0.02956
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{0.9163}{0.02956} \;\approx\; 31.0 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison.**

Static R/P gave 50 years, but with 3% growth it drops to **31 years** — a 38% reduction. This shows why KTU emphasises dynamic modelling.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Equivalence of Fossil vs Nuclear Fuels

**Problem Statement**: Compare the energy released by 1 kg of natural uranium (in a typical LWR, $\eta \approx 33\%$) with 1 kg of coal. State the ratio.

**Step 1 — Energy released by 1 kg of coal.**

For bituminous coal, calorific value $CV_{\text{coal}} \approx 24 \; \text{MJ/kg}$.

$$
E_{\text{coal}} \;=\; 1 \; \text{kg} \times 24 \; \text{MJ/kg} \;=\; 24 \; \text{MJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy released by 1 kg of natural uranium (U-235 fission).**

Energy per fission of U-235 ≈ **200 MeV**. Number of U-235 atoms in 1 kg of natural uranium (0.7% enrichment, 1 kg ≈ 1000 g):

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \; \text{g}}{235 \; \text{g/mol}} \times 0.007 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}
$$

$$
N \;=\; \dfrac{1000 \times 0.007 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{235} \;\approx\; 1.79 \times 10^{22} \; \text{atoms}
$$

Energy released (200 MeV per fission):

$$
E_{\text{fission}} \;=\; 1.79 \times 10^{22} \times 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \; \text{J}
$$

$$
E_{\text{fission}} \;\approx\; 5.74 \times 10^{11} \; \text{J} \;=\; 5.74 \times 10^{5} \; \text{MJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply thermal efficiency.**

A Light Water Reactor (LWR) converts ~33% of fission heat to electricity; but for thermal-energy comparison, we use 100%:

$$
E_{\text{useful thermal, nuclear}} \;\approx\; 5.74 \times 10^{5} \; \text{MJ per kg of natural U}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the energy-equivalent mass of coal.**

$$
m_{\text{coal equivalent}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.74 \times 10^{5} \; \text{MJ}}{24 \; \text{MJ/kg}} \;\approx\; 23,900 \; \text{kg} \;\approx\; 24 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Final result.**

$$
\boxed{\,1 \; \text{kg of natural uranium} \;\sim\; 24,000 \; \text{kg (24 tonnes) of coal}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This **~24,000× energy density** advantage is the strongest single argument for nuclear energy. It is the **single most asked derivation** in KTU Module 1 questions, and is worth 7–10 marks in Part B.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — CO₂ Emissions from a Coal Power Plant

**Problem Statement**: A 1000 MW coal power plant operates at 35% efficiency with coal of calorific value 24 MJ/kg. Calculate the daily CO₂ emission (use emission factor 95 kg CO₂/GJ).

**Step 1 — Daily thermal energy required.**

A 1000 MW plant operating 24 hours produces $10^6 \; \text{kW} \times 86400 \; \text{s} = 8.64 \times 10^{10} \; \text{kJ} = 86.4 \; \text{TJ}$ of *electrical* energy. The thermal energy required is:

$$
E_{\text{thermal}} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\text{electrical}}}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{86.4 \; \text{TJ}}{0.35} \;\approx\; 246.86 \; \text{TJ/day}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily coal consumption.**

$$
m_{\text{coal}} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\text{thermal}}}{CV} \;=\; \dfrac{246.86 \times 10^{12} \; \text{J}}{24 \times 10^{6} \; \text{J/kg}} \;\approx\; 1.029 \times 10^{7} \; \text{kg/day}
$$

$$
m_{\text{coal}} \;\approx\; 10,290 \; \text{tonnes/day}
$$

**Step 3 — Daily CO₂ emission.**

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} \;=\; E_{\text{thermal}} \times EF \;=\; 246.86 \; \text{TJ} \times 95 \; \text{kg/GJ}
$$

Convert TJ to GJ: $246.86 \; \text{TJ} = 246,860 \; \text{GJ}$.

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2} \;=\; 246,860 \times 95 \;\approx\; 2.345 \times 10^{7} \; \text{kg/day}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,m_{\text{CO}_2} \;\approx\; 23,450 \; \text{tonnes of CO}_2 \; \text{per day from a single 1 GW coal plant}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual CO₂.**

For 365 days of operation:

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{annual}} \;\approx\; 23,450 \times 365 \;\approx\; 8.56 \; \text{million tonnes of CO}_2 \; \text{per year}
$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with nuclear (12 g CO₂/kWh lifecycle).**

For the same 1 GW plant over 1 year (CF ≈ 0.9):

$$
E_{\text{electrical,annual}} \;=\; 10^6 \; \text{kW} \times 8760 \; \text{h} \times 0.9 \;=\; 7.884 \times 10^9 \; \text{kWh}
$$

$$
m_{\text{CO}_2,\text{nuclear}} \;=\; 7.884 \times 10^9 \times 12 \times 10^{-6} \; \text{t} \;\approx\; 94,600 \; \text{tonnes/year}
$$

**Ratio**: $\dfrac{8.56 \times 10^6}{94,600} \approx 90$. Coal emits **~90 times** more CO₂ per unit electricity than nuclear.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Energy Density Master Table

> This table is **high-yield** for KTU 2024 questions asking to compare fuels on energy density, CO₂ emission, and reserve life.

| Fuel | Energy Density (MJ/kg) | MJ/Litre | CO₂ (kg/GJ) | Reserve Life (years) | Form |
|------|------------------------|----------|-------------|----------------------|------|
| **Coal (bituminous)** | 24 | ~30 (loose) | 95 | ~140 | Solid |
| **Petroleum (crude)** | 45 | ~36 | 75 | ~50 | Liquid |
| **Natural Gas (CH₄)** | 55 | ~22 (liq) | 55 | ~50 | Gas |
| **Wood (biomass)** | 16 | — | 0 (neutral) | Renewable | Solid |
| **Uranium (natural, in LWR)** | 5,74,000 | — | 0.012 (lifecycle) | ~100 (with fast breeders, thousands) | Solid |
| **Diesel (refined)** | 43 | ~36 | 75 | ~50 | Liquid |
| **Hydrogen (LHV)** | 120 | 8.5 (liq) | 0 (if from renewables) | Inexhaustible | Gas/Liq |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the row for Uranium**: The energy density ($5.74 \times 10^5$ MJ/kg) is approximately **24,000×** that of coal. This single row justifies the entire nuclear energy course.

---

## 3.6 Numerical Problem — Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Question**: India has coal reserves of $R = 100$ billion tonnes. The annual production is $P_0 = 600$ million tonnes/year, growing at 3% per year. The calorific value is $CV = 20 \; \text{MJ/kg}$, and average plant efficiency is $\eta = 35\%$. Find:
1. The R/P ratio (static).
2. The reserve lifetime with growth.
3. The annual electrical energy produced if all coal is burned in power plants.

**Solution:**

**Part 1 — Static R/P.**

$$
T_{\text{static}} \;=\; \dfrac{R}{P_0} \;=\; \dfrac{100 \times 10^9}{600 \times 10^6} \;\approx\; 167 \; \text{years}
$$

**Part 2 — With 3% growth.**

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + 0.03 \times 100 \times 10^9 / (600 \times 10^6))}{\ln(1.03)} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + 5)}{\ln(1.03)}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(6)}{\ln(1.03)} \;=\; \dfrac{1.7918}{0.02956} \;\approx\; 60.6 \; \text{years}
$$

**Part 3 — Annual electrical energy.**

Annual coal mass: $P_0 = 600 \times 10^6 \; \text{kg/year}$.

Annual thermal energy: $E_{\text{th}} = 600 \times 10^6 \times 20 \times 10^6 = 1.2 \times 10^{16} \; \text{J/year}$.

Annual electrical energy: $E_{\text{el}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{th}} = 0.35 \times 1.2 \times 10^{16} = 4.2 \times 10^{15} \; \text{J/year}$.

Convert to TWh ($1 \; \text{TWh} = 3.6 \times 10^{15} \; \text{J}$):

$$
E_{\text{el}} \;=\; \dfrac{4.2 \times 10^{15}}{3.6 \times 10^{15}} \;\approx\; 1.167 \; \text{TWh/year}
$$

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Define Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio. Why is it considered the most important indicator of a fuel's longevity?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** is defined as the ratio of the *remaining recoverable reserves* ($R$) of a fuel to its *current annual production* ($P$):

$$
R/P \;=\; \dfrac{\text{Reserves (tonnes)}}{\text{Annual Production (tonnes/year)}}
$$

The result is expressed in **years**, representing how long the reserves would last at the *current rate* of consumption.

**Why it is important** (2 marks): R/P is the most widely used metric because it (i) directly indicates the *static* lifetime of a resource, (ii) enables comparison across fuels (coal ~140 yrs, oil ~50 yrs, gas ~50 yrs, uranium ~100 yrs), and (iii) helps governments plan long-term energy policy. It is, however, a *static* estimate — it does not account for population growth, rising per-capita demand, or new discoveries. Hence dynamic modelling with growth rate is also used in practice.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: Differentiate between renewable and non-renewable energy sources. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Non-Renewable (Conventional) | Renewable (Non-Conventional) |
|---------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Definition** | Finite stock; depleted by use | Replenished naturally; flow resource |
| **Time to form** | Millions of years (fossil) | Continuously (solar, wind) |
| **Examples** | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Uranium | Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Geothermal, Biomass |
| **Environmental Impact** | High CO₂, SO₂, particulates | Low to zero emissions |
| **Geographic Concentration** | Often concentrated (OPEC) | Distributed everywhere |

**Examples (1 mark):**
- **Non-renewable**: Coal, Petroleum (oil).
- **Renewable**: Solar energy, Wind energy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 (Module Choice A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: (a) Classify the present sources of energy in detail with examples. Why is nuclear energy being considered as a viable alternative to fossil fuels? (7 marks)**
**(b) Estimate the lifetime of a coal reserve of 50 billion tonnes if the current annual production is 2 billion tonnes/year. Also, find the time to exhaustion if production grows at 4% per year. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Classification of Energy Sources (7 marks)

The present sources of energy can be classified into two broad categories:

**1. Conventional (Non-Renewable / Exhaustible) Sources** (3 marks)

These are fossil fuels and nuclear materials formed over millions of years, with finite reserves.

- **Coal** — solid, ~24 MJ/kg, 27% of world mix, 140-year R/P.
- **Petroleum (Crude Oil)** — liquid, ~45 MJ/kg, 31% of world mix, 50-year R/P.
- **Natural Gas** — gaseous, ~55 MJ/m³, 23% of world mix, 50-year R/P.
- **Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium)** — solid fuel, energy density ~5,00,000 MJ/kg (in LWRs), 4% of world mix.

**2. Non-Conventional (Renewable / Inexhaustible) Sources** (2 marks)

- **Solar** (direct + PV) — clean, available everywhere.
- **Wind** — kinetic energy of air; turbines.
- **Hydropower** — potential energy of water at height.
- **Geothermal** — heat from Earth's interior.
- **Tidal & Wave** — gravitational pull of Moon/Sun on seas.
- **Biomass** — organic matter used for fuel (ethanol, biogas).

**Why nuclear is a viable alternative** (2 marks):

1. **Massive energy density** — 1 kg of U ≈ 24 tonnes of coal, vastly reducing fuel logistics.
2. **Zero CO₂ emissions** during operation (12 g CO₂/kWh lifecycle vs. 820 g for coal).
3. **24/7 baseload supply** — unlike solar and wind, nuclear is dispatchable.
4. **Small land footprint** — a 1 GW nuclear plant occupies ~1–3 km² vs. 50–75 km² for equivalent solar.
5. **Long-term fuel availability** — with breeder reactors, uranium can last thousands of years.
6. **Strategic energy independence** — reduces reliance on oil/gas imports.

### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given**: $R = 50$ billion tonnes = $50 \times 10^9$ tonnes; $P_0 = 2$ billion tonnes/year; $g = 0.04$ per year.

**Step 1 — Static R/P ratio** (2 marks):

$$
T_{\text{static}} \;=\; \dfrac{R}{P_0} \;=\; \dfrac{50 \times 10^9}{2 \times 10^9} \;=\; 25 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 2 — With 4% growth, derive the formula** (2 marks):

The total cumulative production over $T$ years is the geometric sum:

$$
R \;=\; \sum_{n=1}^{T} P_0 (1 + g)^{n-1} \;=\; P_0 \cdot \dfrac{(1+g)^{T} - 1}{g}
$$

Solving for $T$:

$$
(1+g)^{T} \;=\; 1 + g \cdot \dfrac{R}{P_0}
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{\ln\!\left(1 + g \cdot R/P_0\right)}{\ln(1 + g)}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical substitution** (2 marks):

$$
g \cdot \dfrac{R}{P_0} \;=\; 0.04 \times 25 \;=\; 1.0
$$

$$
T \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(1 + 1.0)}{\ln(1.04)} \;=\; \dfrac{\ln(2)}{\ln(1.04)} \;=\; \dfrac{0.6931}{0.03922} \;\approx\; 17.67 \; \text{years}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion** (1 mark):

Without growth, the reserve lasts **25 years**; with 4% growth, it lasts only **~17.7 years** — a 30% reduction. This highlights the urgent need for alternative fuels like nuclear.

---

### **Question B2 (Module Choice B)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: (a) With neat sketches, explain the formation of coal, petroleum, and natural gas. Discuss the global reserves and R/P ratio of each. (7 marks)**
**(b) A 1000 MWe nuclear power plant operates at 33% efficiency with an annual capacity factor of 90%. The energy released per fission of U-235 is 200 MeV. Calculate (i) the annual electrical energy output, (ii) the number of fissions per second, and (iii) the mass of U-235 consumed per year. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

### Part (a) — Formation, Reserves & R/P of Fossil Fuels (7 marks)

**Coal Formation (2 marks):**

Coal is a **sedimentary rock** formed from dead plant matter (trees, ferns, mosses) accumulated in swamps and bogs. Over millions of years, the plant material is buried under sediment, compressed under high pressure, and undergoes *coalification*:

$$
\text{Peat} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Lignite} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Bituminous} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Anthracite}
$$

- **Time**: 100–400 million years.
- **Global reserves**: ~1,070 billion tonnes.
- **R/P ratio**: ~140 years.
- **Major producers**: China, India, USA, Australia, Indonesia.

**Petroleum (Crude Oil) Formation (2 marks):**

Crude oil and natural gas are formed from **marine microorganisms** (plankton, algae) that died and sank to the seabed millions of years ago. Under heat and pressure, the organic matter transforms into *kerogen*, then into liquid hydrocarbons (oil) and gaseous hydrocarbons (gas). The hydrocarbons migrate upwards through porous rock and get trapped under an impermeable cap rock (forming a *reservoir*).

- **Time**: 50–350 million years.
- **Global reserves**: ~1,700 billion barrels (~240 Gt).
- **R/P ratio**: ~50 years.
- **Major producers**: Saudi Arabia, Russia, USA, Canada, Iraq.

**Natural Gas Formation (2 marks):**

Natural gas (mostly methane, CH₄) is often formed alongside petroleum (**associated gas**) but may also occur independently (**non-associated gas**). The same kerogen pathway produces methane when temperatures are higher. It is found either dissolved in oil, in gas caps above oil, or in pure gas fields.

- **Global reserves**: ~200 trillion cubic metres (Tcm).
- **R/P ratio**: ~50 years.
- **Major producers**: Russia, USA, Iran, Qatar, Turkmenistan.
- **Environmental note**: Methane is 80× more potent than CO₂ as a greenhouse gas over 20 years — leakage is a major concern.

**Summary Table (1 mark):**

| Fuel | Reserves | R/P (years) | CO₂ Factor (kg/GJ) |
|------|----------|-------------|---------------------|
| Coal | 1,070 Bt | 140 | 95 |
| Oil | 1,700 Bbbl | 50 | 75 |
| Gas | 200 Tcm | 50 | 55 |

---

### Part (b) — Nuclear Power Plant Numerical (7 marks)

**Given**: $P_{\text{electric}} = 1000 \; \text{MW} = 10^9 \; \text{W}$; $\eta = 0.33$; Capacity Factor (CF) = 0.90; $E_{\text{fission}} = 200 \; \text{MeV} = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11} \; \text{J}$.

**(i) Annual electrical energy output** (2 marks):

$$
E_{\text{el, annual}} \;=\; P \times t \times CF \;=\; 10^9 \; \text{W} \times 365 \times 24 \times 3600 \; \text{s} \times 0.90
$$

$$
E_{\text{el, annual}} \;=\; 10^9 \times 3.154 \times 10^7 \times 0.90 \;\approx\; 2.838 \times 10^{16} \; \text{J/year}
$$

Convert to TWh: $E_{\text{el}} = 2.838 \times 10^{16} / 3.6 \times 10^{15} \approx 7.88 \; \text{TWh/year}$.

**(ii) Number of fissions per second** (3 marks):

The thermal power output is:

$$
P_{\text{th}} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{el}}}{\eta} \;=\; \dfrac{10^9}{0.33} \;\approx\; 3.03 \times 10^9 \; \text{W}
$$

The fission rate $\dot{N}$ must supply this power at 200 MeV per fission:

$$
\dot{N} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{\text{th}}}{E_{\text{fission}}} \;=\; \dfrac{3.03 \times 10^9}{3.204 \times 10^{-11}} \;\approx\; 9.46 \times 10^{19} \; \text{fissions/second}
$$

**(iii) Mass of U-235 consumed per year** (2 marks):

Total fissions per year:

$$
N_{\text{year}} \;=\; \dot{N} \times 8760 \times 3600 \;=\; 9.46 \times 10^{19} \times 3.154 \times 10^7 \;\approx\; 2.98 \times 10^{27} \; \text{fissions/year}
$$

Mass of one U-235 atom:

$$
m_{\text{atom}} \;=\; \dfrac{235 \; \text{g/mol}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \; \text{atoms/mol}} \;\approx\; 3.90 \times 10^{-22} \; \text{g}
$$

Annual mass consumed:

$$
M_{\text{U-235}} \;=\; N_{\text{year}} \times m_{\text{atom}} \;=\; 2.98 \times 10^{27} \times 3.90 \times 10^{-22} \; \text{g}
$$

$$
M_{\text{U-235}} \;\approx\; 1.16 \times 10^{6} \; \text{g} \;=\; 1.16 \; \text{tonnes/year}
$$

**Conclusion**: Only **~1.16 tonnes of U-235** consumed per year for 1 GWe — illustrating the extraordinary energy density of nuclear fuel.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes — Where Students Lose Marks (KTU 2024 Valuation Pattern)**
> 
> **1. Unit Confusion in R/P**: A frequent error is computing R/P without checking units. If $R$ is in **million tonnes** and $P$ is in **million tonnes/year**, the ratio is in **years** directly. If mixed (e.g., $R$ in tonnes, $P$ in million tonnes/year), divide accordingly. Many students lose **1 mark** for this.
> 
> **2. Forgetting Capacity Factor**: In nuclear numericals, students often assume the plant runs at 100% capacity. Real plants run at **CF = 0.85–0.92**. Forgetting CF leads to **over-estimation of energy and fuel use by 10–15%**, costing **1–2 marks**.
> 
> **3. Mistaking CV (Calorific Value) for Energy Density**: $CV$ is energy per unit **mass** of fuel. Energy per unit **volume** is *not* the same. This distinction matters for liquid fuels (petroleum, diesel).
> 
> **4. Confusing Energy Density Comparison Direction**: Many students write *"1 kg of coal = 24,000 kg of uranium"*. The **correct** direction is *1 kg of uranium ≈ 24,000 kg of coal*. Examiners check direction strictly.
> 
> **5. Skipping the Derivation of Geometric Series in Growth Problems**: For the R/P-with-growth question, simply writing the formula $T = \ln(\ldots)/\ln(\ldots)$ without deriving it from the geometric series costs **1 mark** under the "derivation" rubric.
> 
> **6. Mixing Primary and Secondary Energy Units**: $1 \; \text{kWh} = 3.6 \; \text{MJ}$ (electrical). For thermal energy, use $1 \; \text{kWh}_{\text{th}} = 3.6 \; \text{MJ}$ of *heat*. Always state whether the energy is **thermal** or **electrical**.
> 
> **7. Ignoring the Efficiency η in Energy Calculations**: When asked "how much coal is needed to produce 1 TWh of electricity", students forget to apply $\eta$ in the denominator: $m = E_{\text{el}} / (\eta \times CV)$. This can lead to a 3× error.
> 
> **8. Wrong Conversion of 1 eV to Joules**: Must remember $1 \; \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{J}$. This is critical in nuclear fission energy problems.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Present Source of Energy" (Module 1)**
> 
> - [ ] Define **Primary Energy** vs **Secondary Energy** (must-know for 3-mark Q).
> - [ ] Classify energy sources as **renewable** vs **non-renewable** with 2 examples each.
> - [ ] State the **R/P ratio** of coal (~140 yr), oil (~50 yr), gas (~50 yr), and nuclear (~100 yr with LWR, ~thousands with breeder).
> - [ ] List the **three crises** motivating nuclear: resource depletion, environmental damage, geopolitical instability.
> - [ ] State the **share of fossil fuels** in world energy mix (~81%) and the share of **nuclear** (~4%).
> - [ ] State **calorific values**: Coal ≈ 24 MJ/kg, Oil ≈ 45 MJ/kg, Gas ≈ 55 MJ/m³.
> - [ ] **Energy density of uranium** ≈ 5,00,000 MJ/kg, which is ~24,000× that of coal.
> - [ ] **CO₂ emission factors**: Coal ≈ 95 kg/GJ, Oil ≈ 75 kg/GJ, Gas ≈ 55 kg/GJ, Nuclear ≈ 0.012 kg/GJ (lifecycle).
> - [ ] Derive the **R/P with growth** formula: $T = \ln(1 + gR/P_0)/\ln(1+g)$.
> - [ ] Compute the **annual electrical energy** of a 1 GWe plant at CF=0.9 → ~7.9 TWh/year.
> - [ ] Compute the **annual U-235 consumption** of a 1 GWe plant → ~1.16 tonnes/year.
> - [ ] Convert between units: $1 \; \text{toe} = 41.868 \; \text{GJ}$; $1 \; \text{kWh} = 3.6 \; \text{MJ}$; $1 \; \text{TWh} = 3.6 \; \text{PJ}$; $1 \; \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{J}$.
> - [ ] Understand **EROI** (Energy Returned on Invested) and its application to fuels.
> - [ ] Recognise the **global warming potential** of methane (~80× CO₂ over 20 years).
> - [ ] Remember the **doubling time** formula $T_d = 70/r\%$ for exponential growth problems.
> - [ ] Identify the **major fossil fuel reserves** by region: Coal (China, USA, India, Australia); Oil (Middle East, Russia, USA); Gas (Russia, Iran, Qatar).
> - [ ] Appreciate that **hydropower is the largest renewable** (~16% of world electricity, ~7% of primary energy).
> - [ ] Be able to draw a **block diagram of classification** and a **flow chart of fuel-to-electricity conversion**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global Resource Estimates
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Global Resource Estimates

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

**Global Resource Estimates** in the context of nuclear energy refers to the quantitative assessment of the total world inventory of nuclear fissile and fertile materials (primarily **Uranium-235, Uranium-238, and Thorium-232**) that can be economically and technically extracted, processed, and utilized as fuel in nuclear reactors over the operational lifetime of the global nuclear power fleet. These estimates are standardized by international bodies such as the **International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA)** and the **OECD Nuclear Energy Agency (NEA)** in their joint biennial publication known as the **"Red Book" (Uranium: Resources, Production and Demand)**.

The estimation framework distinguishes between four cardinal concepts:
- **Resources** (geological abundance, identified + undiscovered)
- **Reserves** (economically extractable portion at current prices)
- **Identified Resources** (RAR + Inferred)
- **Undiscovered Resources** (Prognosticated + Speculative)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Global Resource Estimates encompasses the classification of nuclear fuel inventories, their energy-equivalence conversion to conventional fossil fuels, and the projection of how long these resources can sustain the world's energy demand under different reactor technologies (thermal vs. breeder).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the world as a giant bank account holding energy savings. The **total deposits** in this account are the planet's fossil fuels (coal, oil, gas) plus its nuclear fuels (U, Th). Fossil fuels are like a **current account** — you can withdraw them anytime, but once exhausted, the balance is zero. Nuclear fuels, especially with breeder reactors, function like a **fixed deposit with compound interest** — the principal (U-235) is small, but the interest earned from converting U-238 and Th-232 into fissile Pu-239 and U-233 multiplies the energy output by a factor of **60 to 100 times**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English View:** Roughly **1 kg of fully fissioned uranium** releases the same energy as burning **~20,000 kg of coal** or **~14,000 kg of oil**. Hence, even a few million tonnes of uranium globally represent a colossal energy reserve when viewed through the lens of fission physics (E = mc²).

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Energy per fission event:** $E_f \approx 200 \text{ MeV} = 3.2 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$
- **Number of atoms per kg of U-235:** $N = \dfrac{N_A}{235} \approx 2.56 \times 10^{24} \text{ atoms/kg}$
- **Energy released per kg of U-235 (complete fission):** $\approx 8.2 \times 10^{13} \text{ J} = 82 \text{ TJ}$
- **1 barrel of oil equivalent (boe):** $\approx 5.8 \text{ MMBtu} \approx 6.12 \text{ GJ}$
- **1 tonne of oil equivalent (toe):** $\approx 42 \text{ GJ} = 11.63 \text{ MWh(th)}$
- **Energy density advantage factor (U vs Coal):** $\approx 2 \times 10^4$ in mass, $\approx 5 \times 10^5$ in volume

> [!NOTE]
> The current global primary energy consumption is approximately **~$5.5 \times 10^{20}$ J/year (~600 EJ/yr)** and is projected by the IEA to reach **~$8.5 \times 10^{20}$ J (~850 EJ/yr) by 2050**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Classification of Global Nuclear Resources

The IAEA/NEA Red Book classifies uranium resources on a two-dimensional matrix:

**A. By Degree of Geological Assurance (Horizontal Axis):**

1. **Reasonably Assured Resources (RAR):** Resources of delineated ore bodies of known size, grade, and configuration, with mining/milling already in operation or feasible.
2. **Inferred Resources (IR):** Resources for which estimates of quantity are based on geological extrapolation beyond RAR, with sufficient indication of continuity.
3. **Prognosticated Resources (PR):** Resources expected to occur in well-defined geological trends, with discovery supported by regional geological surveys.
4. **Speculative Resources (SR):** Resources thought to exist in geologically favourable settings, with indirect evidence only.

**B. By Cost of Recovery (Vertical Axis):** Expressed in USD per kg of uranium metal recovered.
- **<$40/kgU** — Highly economic
- **<$80/kgU** — Economic
- **<$130/kgU** — Moderately economic
- **<$260/kgU** — Expensive but technically recoverable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total Identified Conventional U Resources (IAEA Red Book 2022):** ~**7.9 million tonnes** (RAR + Inferred, all cost categories up to $260/kgU). This excludes unconventional resources such as **phosphate deposits, seawater, and black shales**, which are virtually inexhaustible on geological timescales.

### 2.2 Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Resources

| Category | Examples | Estimated Quantity | Economic Viability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Conventional** | Vein/breccia deposits (Canada, Australia), sandstone (Kazakhstan) | ~16 Mt U total | Currently mined at $40–$130/kgU |
| **Unconventional – Phosphate** | Phosphorite ores | ~22 Mt U | Recoverable as by-product |
| **Unconventional – Seawater** | Dissolved U in oceans (3.3 ppb) | ~4.5 × 10⁹ tonnes U | Currently ~$500/kgU (improving) |
| **Unconventional – Black Shale** | Chattanooga, Alum Bay deposits | Very large (Gt scale) | Site-specific feasibility |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E = \Delta m \, c^2$ | Mass-energy equivalence (Einstein) | Joules |
| 2 | $E_{U-235} = \dfrac{N_A}{235} \times 200 \text{ MeV}$ | Energy per kg of pure U-235 fissioned | J/kg |
| 3 | $E_{nat-U}^{LWR} \approx 45{,}000 \text{ kWh(e)}/\text{kg}$ | Energy from 1 kg nat. U in LWR (once-through) | kWh(e)/kg |
| 4 | $E_{nat-U}^{Breeder} \approx 3{,}000{,}000 \text{ kWh(th)}/\text{kg}$ | Energy from 1 kg nat. U in breeder (full conversion) | kWh(th)/kg |
| 5 | $\eta_{conversion} = \dfrac{\text{Fissile produced}}{\text{Fissile consumed}}$ | Conversion ratio (LWR: ~0.6, Breeder: >1) | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{R}{C}$ | Resource lifetime; $R$ = reserve, $C$ = annual consumption | Years |
| 7 | $E_{eq}^{coal} = 29{,}300 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Energy content of standard coal (CV) | kJ/kg |
| 8 | $1 \text{ toe} = 42 \text{ GJ}$ | Tonne of oil equivalent | GJ |
| 9 | $1 \text{ TW\cdot yr} = 31.5 \text{ EJ}$ | Power × time to energy conversion | Exajoules |
| 10 | $E_{Th-232} = E_{U-238} \approx 8 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/kg}$ (as bred fuel) | Energy from fertile isotopes via breeding | J/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL SYNTAX RULE:** In the table above, all absolute values and conditionals are written using `\vert` or plain text to avoid breaking the markdown table structure. For instance, energy comparisons are written as "$\approx 45{,}000$" rather than using pipe-delimited notation.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Global Resource Estimates are foundational to:
- **Long-term energy policy planning** (e.g., IEA's World Energy Outlook scenarios).
- **Reactor fleet design decisions** (LWRs with once-through vs. closed fuel cycle with breeders).
- **Geopolitical strategy** (uranium import-export control, non-proliferation treaties).
- **Waste management projections** (spent fuel volume depends on burnup × annual throughput).
- **Sustainability assessments** (nuclear's contribution to low-carbon baseload power).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Energy Released per kg of U-235 (Complete Fission)

**Step 1: Avogadro's number and atomic mass of U-235**
The Avogadro constant gives the number of atoms per mole:
$$N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ atoms/mol}$$

Since 1 mole of U-235 weighs 235 grams, the number of atoms per kg is:
$$N = \dfrac{N_A}{0.235} = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{0.235} = 2.563 \times 10^{24} \text{ atoms/kg U-235}$$

**Step 2: Energy per single fission event**
The recoverable energy per fission of U-235 (excluding neutrinos) is approximately:
$$E_f \approx 200 \text{ MeV} = 200 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \text{ J} = 3.204 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Total energy per kg of U-235**
Multiplying the number of fissionable atoms by the energy per fission:
$$E_{U-235} = N \times E_f = (2.563 \times 10^{24}) \times (3.204 \times 10^{-11})$$

$$E_{U-235} = 8.21 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/kg} \approx 82 \text{ TJ/kg}$$

**Step 4: Mass conversion check (Einstein)**
The mass defect per fission is approximately 0.1% of U-235 mass:
$$\Delta m = 0.001 \times 1 \text{ kg} = 10^{-3} \text{ kg}$$

$$\Delta E = \Delta m \, c^2 = 10^{-3} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2 = 9 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/kg}$$

Both methods yield comparable magnitudes (the small discrepancy arises from the ~12% energy carried away by neutrinos, which is unrecoverable in practice).

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy Equivalence to Fossil Fuels

**Step 1: Calorific value of standard coal**
$$CV_{coal} = 29{,}300 \text{ kJ/kg} = 2.93 \times 10^7 \text{ J/kg}$$

**Step 2: Equivalence factor**
$$\text{Equivalence} = \dfrac{E_{U-235}}{CV_{coal}} = \dfrac{8.21 \times 10^{13}}{2.93 \times 10^7} = 2.80 \times 10^6$$

So **1 kg of U-235 ≈ 2,800 tonnes of coal** in pure thermal energy terms.

**Step 3: Practical energy from natural uranium in LWRs**
Natural uranium is only 0.711% U-235. In an LWR (once-through cycle, 33,000 MWd/tonne burnup), only a fraction of U-235 is fissioned:
$$E_{LWR}^{nat-U} \approx 0.0071 \times 0.85 \times 82 \text{ TJ/kg} \approx 0.495 \text{ TJ/kg} = 137{,}500 \text{ kWh(th)/kg}$$

Accounting for thermal-to-electric efficiency ($\eta \approx 33\%$):
$$E_{LWR}^{nat-U} \approx 45{,}000 \text{ kWh(e)/kg} = 45 \text{ MWh(e) per kg}$$

**Step 4: Comparison to oil barrels**
$$1 \text{ barrel oil} = 5.8 \text{ MMBtu} = 6.12 \text{ GJ} = 1700 \text{ kWh(th)}$$

$$\text{Barrels of oil equivalent per kg U} = \dfrac{137{,}500}{1700} \approx 81 \text{ boe/kg U}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Resource Lifetime Estimation

**Step 1: Current global U production**
World uranium mine production (2022): ~**58,000 tonnes U/year**.

**Step 2: Global nuclear electricity demand**
$$\text{Installed nuclear capacity} \approx 370 \text{ GW(e)}$$

$$\text{Annual U consumption} \approx 67{,}000 \text{ t U/yr} \text{ (with stockpiles)}$$

**Step 3: Resource lifetime at static RAR (<$130/kgU ≈ 4.7 Mt)**
$$\tau_{RAR} = \dfrac{4.7 \times 10^6 \text{ t}}{67{,}000 \text{ t/yr}} \approx 70 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4: With breeder reactors (60× efficiency gain)**
$$\tau_{Breeder} = 70 \times 60 = 4{,}200 \text{ years (effectively inexhaustible)}$$

**Step 5: With seawater uranium (4.5 Gt inventory)**
$$\tau_{seawater} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^9 \text{ t}}{67{,}000 \text{ t/yr}} \approx 67{,}000 \text{ years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analytical Insight:** The static lifetime calculation is overly conservative because it ignores (a) breeding, (b) reprocessing/recycling, (c) demand growth, and (d) unconventional resources. Realistic scenarios place nuclear energy sustainability at **millennia-scale** when breeders are deployed.

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Thorium Energy Potential

Thorium-232 is fertile, not fissile. It must absorb a neutron to become U-233:
$$^{232}\text{Th} + n \rightarrow {^{233}\text{Th}} \xrightarrow{\beta^-} {^{233}\text{Pa}} \xrightarrow{\beta^-} {^{233}\text{U (fissile)}$$

**Energy per kg of Th-232 (after full conversion):** $\approx 8.2 \times 10^{13} \text{ J/kg}$ (same as U-235 order of magnitude, accounting for ~12% parasitic loss in U-233 cycle).

Global identified Th resources: ~**625,000 tonnes** (IAEA). Multiplying:
$$E_{Th} \approx 6.25 \times 10^5 \times 8.2 \times 10^{13} \approx 5.1 \times 10^{19} \text{ J} \approx 51 \text{ EJ}$$

This is comparable to **~1,200 years** of current global nuclear energy demand.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 IAEA Red Book Resource Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **block-level functional architecture** of global nuclear resource classification as defined by the IAEA/NEA Red Book framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Global Nuclear Fuel Resources] --> B[Conventional Resources]
    A --> C[Unconventional Resources]

    B --> D[Identified Resources]
    B --> E[Undiscovered Resources]

    D --> F[RAR - Reasonably Assured]
    D --> G[IR - Inferred]
    E --> H[PR - Prognosticated]
    E --> I[SR - Speculative]

    F --> J[Cost Category 40 USD per kgU]
    F --> K[Cost Category 80 USD per kgU]
    F --> L[Cost Category 130 USD per kgU]
    F --> M[Cost Category 260 USD per kgU]

    G --> J
    G --> K
    G --> L
    G --> M

    C --> N[Phosphate Deposits]
    C --> O[Seawater 3.3 ppb]
    C --> P[Black Shale Deposits]
    C --> Q[Monazite Sands - Thorium]

    J --> R[Currently Mined Reserve Base]
    K --> R
    L --> R
    M --> S[Strategic Long-term Inventory]

    R --> T[Reactor Fleet Input]
    S --> T
    O --> T

    T --> U[LWR Once-through Cycle]
    T --> V[Closed Fuel Cycle with Breeders]
    T --> W[Thorium Cycle MSR or AHWR]

    U --> X[Energy Output 45 MWh per kg U]
    V --> Y[Energy Output 2.8 MWh per kg U effective]
    W --> Z[Energy Output similar to U-235 cycle]
```

### 4.2 Energy Equivalence Mapping (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE[Energy Source]
        A1[1 kg U-235 Fission]
    end

    subgraph EQUIVALENT[Fossil Equivalents]
        B1[2800 tonnes coal]
        B2[2000 tonnes oil]
        B3[2.7 million m3 natural gas]
    end

    subgraph POWER[Power Plant Equivalents]
        C1[1 GWe LWR - 1.4 years full output]
        C2[17000 typical wind turbines 1 hour]
    end

    A1 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A1 --> B3
    A1 --> C1
    A1 --> C2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above use only alphanumeric node identifiers (no reserved keywords) and clean uppercase text labels. Each arrow represents a quantitative energy equivalence derived in Section 3.2.

### 4.3 Reactor Technology Resource Multiplier Chart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Natural Uranium Feedstock] --> B{Reactor Technology Choice}
    B -->|LWR Once-through| C[Effective Energy Yield 45 MWh e per kg U]
    B -->|LWR with MOX recycle| D[Effective Energy Yield 54 MWh e per kg U]
    B -->|Fast Breeder Reactor| E[Effective Energy Yield 2800 MWh e per kg U]
    B -->|MSBR Thorium Cycle| F[Effective Energy Yield 2500 MWh e per kg Th]
    C --> G[Resource lifetime 70 to 100 years]
    D --> H[Resource lifetime 90 to 120 years]
    E --> I[Resource lifetime 4000 plus years]
    F --> J[Resource lifetime 1200 plus years]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define Reasonably Assured Resources (RAR) and Inferred Resources (IR) as per IAEA classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Reasonably Assured Resources (RAR):** Uranium resources confined to well-explored ore bodies of known size, grade, and configuration, where mining and milling operations are either already in progress or technically feasible under prevailing economic conditions (1 mark). Quantity and quality are estimated with high confidence based on direct geological evidence such as drilling and sampling (1 mark).
- **Inferred Resources (IR):** Uranium resources whose estimates are based largely on extrapolation beyond the RAR category, where geological continuity is supported by indirect evidence (1 mark). Confidence levels are lower than RAR, but the deposits are presumed to exist in identified mineralised zones.

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
> **Explain why breeder reactors extend nuclear fuel resources by a factor of ~60 compared to thermal reactors.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- In thermal reactors (LWRs), only the **fissile U-235 (0.711% of natural U)** is consumed in significant quantities, leaving **fertile U-238 (99.28%)** largely unused (1 mark).
- Breeder reactors (e.g., FBRs) have a **conversion ratio > 1**, meaning they produce more fissile material (Pu-239) than they consume (1 mark).
- This converts the otherwise-wasted U-238 into useful fuel, multiplying the effective energy yield by approximately **60–100 times**, extending the resource lifetime from ~70 years to several thousand years (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice — Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the IAEA classification of global uranium resources. Explain the cost categories used in resource classification. (7 marks)

**(b)** Estimate the total energy that can be theoretically obtained from the complete fission of 1 kg of U-235, and express it in terms of barrels of oil equivalent (boe) and tonnes of oil equivalent (toe). (7 marks)

### Model Solution (Question A):

#### Part (a) — IAEA Classification of Uranium Resources (7 Marks)

The IAEA, in collaboration with the OECD-NEA, classifies uranium resources along **two dimensions** (2 marks):

**1. By Degree of Geological Assurance:**
- **Reasonably Assured Resources (RAR):** Known deposits with confirmed grade and tonnage based on direct exploration data (1 mark).
- **Inferred Resources (IR):** Extrapolated from geological data adjacent to RAR (0.5 marks).
- **Prognosticated Resources (PR):** Expected in identified trends not yet explored (0.5 marks).
- **Speculative Resources (SR):** Possible in geologically favourable regions (0.5 marks).

**2. By Cost of Recovery** (USD per kg U, forward cost basis):
- **<$40/kgU** — Currently economic (0.5 marks)
- **<$80/kgU** — Economic at higher prices (0.5 marks)
- **<$130/kgU** — Moderately economic (0.5 marks)
- **<$260/kgU** — Expensive but technically recoverable (1 mark)

Higher-cost categories become viable with rising uranium prices or improvements in extraction technology. As of the 2022 Red Book, identified conventional resources (RAR + IR) amount to **~7.9 million tonnes** (RAR) + estimated inferred quantities, providing decades of supply even at current consumption levels.

#### Part (b) — Energy from 1 kg of U-235 (7 Marks)

**Step 1: Number of atoms in 1 kg of U-235**
$$N = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{235} \times 1000 = 2.563 \times 10^{24} \text{ atoms}$$

**Step 2: Energy per fission**
$$E_f = 200 \text{ MeV} = 3.2 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$

**Step 3: Total energy released**
$$E = N \times E_f = 2.563 \times 10^{24} \times 3.2 \times 10^{-11} = 8.2 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

$$E = 82 \text{ TJ} = 8.2 \times 10^{13} \text{ J}$$

**Step 4: Conversion to barrels of oil equivalent (boe)**
$$1 \text{ boe} = 5.8 \text{ MMBtu} = 6.117 \times 10^9 \text{ J}$$

$$\text{boe} = \dfrac{8.2 \times 10^{13}}{6.117 \times 10^9} = 13{,}405 \text{ boe per kg U-235}$$

**Step 5: Conversion to tonnes of oil equivalent (toe)**
$$1 \text{ toe} = 42 \text{ GJ} = 4.2 \times 10^{10} \text{ J}$$

$$\text{toe} = \dfrac{8.2 \times 10^{13}}{4.2 \times 10^{10}} = 1{,}952 \text{ toe per kg U-235}$$

**Final Result:** 1 kg of U-235 = **82 TJ** = **13,400 boe** = **~1,950 toe**.

---

> **Module 1 Internal Choice — Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare conventional and non-conventional uranium resources. List three major unconventional sources with their estimated quantities. (7 marks)

**(b)** With present global nuclear capacity of 370 GWe and annual uranium consumption of 67,000 tonnes, estimate the lifetime of the identified resources (RAR, <$130/kgU, 4.7 Mt) under (i) once-through LWR cycle and (ii) breeder reactor cycle. Discuss the role of seawater uranium. (7 marks)

### Model Solution (Question B):

#### Part (a) — Conventional vs. Non-Conventional Resources (7 Marks)

**Conventional resources** are uranium deposits of sufficiently high grade and concentration to be mined and processed using established metallurgical techniques at competitive costs (2 marks). Examples include the **unconformity-related deposits of Canada (Athabasca Basin)**, **sandstone-hosted deposits of Kazakhstan**, and **breccia complex deposits of Australia (Olympic Dam)** (2 marks).

**Non-conventional resources** are uranium contained in low-grade deposits or dispersed forms that are either economically unrecoverable today or require novel extraction methods (1 mark). Three major sources:

1. **Phosphate deposits:** Estimated to contain **~22 million tonnes of U** as a by-product of phosphoric acid production. Recovery is feasible during fertilizer manufacture (0.7 marks).
2. **Seawater uranium:** Oceans contain **~4.5 billion tonnes** of dissolved uranium (concentration ~3.3 ppb). Extraction via adsorbent polymers is being actively researched (0.7 marks).
3. **Black shale deposits:** Formations such as the Chattanooga Shale (USA) and Swedish alum shales contain uranium at concentrations of 50–200 ppm, with aggregate tonnage in the Gt range (0.6 marks).

#### Part (b) — Resource Lifetime Estimation (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Annual U consumption: $C = 67{,}000 \text{ t/yr}$
- RAR (<$130/kgU): $R = 4.7 \times 10^6 \text{ t}$
- Installed capacity: 370 GWe

**Step 1: Once-through LWR cycle (1 mark each)**
$$\tau_{LWR} = \dfrac{4.7 \times 10^6}{67{,}000} = 70.1 \text{ years}$$

This is the *static* lifetime. In practice, rising prices will unlock higher-cost categories, and recycling will extend it further.

**Step 2: Breeder reactor cycle (multiplier ~60×) (1 mark each)**
$$\tau_{Breeder} = 70.1 \times 60 = 4{,}206 \text{ years}$$

Essentially inexhaustible on human timescales. The breeder converts U-238 into Pu-239, utilising >60% of the natural U feed instead of <1%.

**Step 3: Seawater uranium (1 mark each)**
- Seawater U inventory: $4.5 \times 10^9 \text{ tonnes}$
- Static lifetime:
$$\tau_{seawater} = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^9}{67{,}000} = 67{,}164 \text{ years}$$

**Step 4: Discussion (2 marks)**
Seawater uranium is the ultimate backstop. Once extraction costs drop below $260/kgU (current target of Japanese researchers using amidoxime fibres), the resource becomes effectively boundless. Combined with breeders, the energy security of nuclear power becomes virtually unlimited — a key argument for nuclear in long-term climate and energy policy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake #1:** Students often confuse **"Reserves" with "Resources."** Reserves = economically extractable NOW; Resources = total geological inventory (including undiscovered). Examiner deducts 1–2 marks for this swap.
> 2. **Common Mistake #2:** When calculating energy from U-235, students forget to **subtract the ~12% neutrino energy loss**. Use ~200 MeV recoverable, not 235 MeV total. Loss of 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Common Mistake #3:** Failing to convert units consistently. Always show $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ and 1 eV = $1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J in the steps. Loss of 1 mark if missing.
> 4. **Common Mistake #4:** The **boe and toe conversions** must use $1 \text{ toe} = 42 \text{ GJ}$ and $1 \text{ boe} \approx 5.8 \text{ MMBtu}$. Mixing units is a frequent error costing 1 mark.
> 5. **Common Mistake #5:** For breeder lifetime, the multiplier (~60×) is **not exact** — examiners expect you to mention the conversion ratio and breeding gain explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **IAEA Red Book** is the authoritative source for U resource data, published biennially.
- [x] **Identified Resources** = RAR + IR; **Total Resources** include PR + SR.
- [x] **Cost categories** range from **<$40/kgU** to **<$260/kgU**.
- [x] **Global identified U (RAR + IR, <$130/kgU) ≈ 4.7 Mt**; total conventional ~ **7.9 Mt** (2022).
- [x] **Global thorium identified resources ≈ 625,000 tonnes**.
- [x] **Seawater U inventory ≈ 4.5 billion tonnes** — virtually inexhaustible.
- [x] **Energy per kg of U-235 fissioned ≈ 82 TJ** = $8.2 \times 10^{13}$ J.
- [x] **Energy per kg of natural U in LWR ≈ 45 MWh(e)** = 137,500 kWh(th).
- [x] **Breeder reactor efficiency multiplier ≈ 60×**; resource lifetime scales accordingly.
- [x] **Conversion ratio** $\eta$ in LWR ≈ 0.6; in FBR > 1 (typically 1.1–1.4).
- [x] **1 kg U ≈ 13,400 boe ≈ 1,950 toe** (theoretical, pure U-235 fission).
- [x] **LWR fleet lifetime** of RAR: ~70 years (static); **Breeder fleet lifetime:** ~4,200 years.
- [x] **Unconventional U sources:** phosphate (~22 Mt), seawater (~4.5 Gt), black shales (Gt scale).
- [x] **Th-232** is fertile (not fissile); must capture neutron to breed U-233.
- [x] **Current global nuclear capacity:** ~370 GW(e); ~440 reactors operating in 32 countries.
- [x] **World primary energy demand (2024):** ~600 EJ/yr, projected ~850 EJ/yr by 2050.
- [x] **Key constant for derivations:** $E_f = 200$ MeV, $c^2 = 9 \times 10^{16}$ J/kg.
- [x] **Phrase to remember:** "Identified ≠ Recoverable; Resource ≠ Reserve."

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Issues Associated with Fossil Fuels, Potential Role of Nuclear Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Issues Associated with Fossil Fuels, Potential Role of Nuclear Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What are Fossil Fuels?

Fossil fuels are **hydrocarbon-based energy resources** (coal, crude oil, and natural gas) formed over hundreds of millions of years from the buried remains of dead organic matter (plants, animals, and microorganisms) subjected to intense heat and pressure beneath the Earth's crust. They are called "fossil" because they are derived from prehistoric biological fossils and represent stored solar energy from ancient ecosystems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Fossil fuels are non-renewable carbonaceous geological deposits (coal, petroleum, natural gas) that, when combusted, release the chemical energy originally captured through photosynthesis millions of years ago, alongside carbon dioxide and other combustion by-products.

### 1.2 The Three Principal Fossil Fuels

| Fossil Fuel | Chemical Basis | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Coal** | Carbon-rich solid (C, H, O, S, N) | Electricity generation, steel production |
| **Crude Oil / Petroleum** | Liquid hydrocarbons (C₅–C₄₀) | Transport fuels, petrochemicals |
| **Natural Gas** | Methane-dominant (CH₄, C₂H₆) | Heating, power generation, feedstock |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Earth's Slow Savings Account

> [!NOTE]
> **The Piggy Bank Analogy**
> Imagine the Earth as a household that has been "saving" solar energy for **~300 million years** by burying dead plants and animals underground — a slow, geological piggy bank. Coal, oil, and gas are the "coins" in that piggy bank. **Humans are now withdrawing these coins roughly 1 million times faster than nature deposited them.** This is the essence of why fossil fuels are classified as *non-renewable* and why their dominant use is fundamentally unsustainable.

### 1.4 What is Nuclear Energy's "Potential Role"?

Nuclear energy, in the context of this module, refers to the energy released when atomic nuclei undergo **fission** (splitting of heavy nuclei like Uranium-235 or Plutonium-239) or **fusion** (combining of light nuclei like deuterium and tritium). Its "potential role" in the energy transition is defined by KTU 2024 as:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Potential Role of Nuclear Energy (KTU Definition):** The strategic, scientific, and engineering position that nuclear power occupies as a **high-energy-density, low-carbon, baseload-stable** alternative capable of displacing fossil fuels in electricity generation, district heating, hydrogen production, and industrial process heat — thereby mitigating climate change, energy poverty, and geopolitical supply risks.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded for Recall)

- **Energy content of 1 kg of coal ≈ 24 MJ** (Lower Heating Value, LHV)
- **Energy content of 1 kg of crude oil ≈ 42 MJ** (LHV)
- **Energy content of 1 m³ of natural gas ≈ 35–39 MJ**
- **Energy content of 1 kg of Uranium-235 (fully fissioned) ≈ 8.19 × 10¹³ J ≈ 82,000,000 MJ**
- **Einstein's mass-energy equivalence: E = mc²**, where **c = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- **Global average atmospheric CO₂ concentration (2024) ≈ 422 ppm** (up from ~280 ppm pre-industrial)
- **World primary energy supply (2023) ≈ 620 EJ**, of which **~81%** comes from fossil fuels
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four Pillars of Issues Associated with Fossil Fuels

#### 2.1.1 Environmental Issues (Climate Change & Pollution)
- **Greenhouse Gas (GHG) Emissions:** Combustion of fossil fuels releases **CO₂, CH₄, N₂O** — principal drivers of anthropogenic global warming. The Global Carbon Project reports **~37 GtCO₂** emitted annually from fossil fuels (2023).
- **Air Pollutants:** Release of **SO₂, NOₓ, particulate matter (PM₂.₅/PM₁₀), mercury (Hg), volatile organic compounds (VOCs)**.
- **Acid Rain:** SO₂ and NOₓ react with atmospheric water to form H₂SO₄ and HNO₃.
- **Ocean Acidification:** ~30% of anthropogenic CO₂ is absorbed by oceans, lowering seawater pH by ~0.1 units since pre-industrial era.
- **Land Degradation:** Mountaintop removal mining, oil spills (e.g., Deepwater Horizon, 2010 — **4.9 million barrels**), and pipeline leaks.

#### 2.1.2 Health Issues
- **WHO estimate:** Ambient air pollution causes **~4.2 million premature deaths/year** globally.
- Respiratory diseases (asthma, COPD), cardiovascular disease, lung cancer, and neurodevelopmental disorders in children.
- Coal miners' pneumoconiosis ("black lung disease").
- **Mercury poisoning** from coal combustion bioaccumulates in fish.

#### 2.1.3 Geopolitical & Economic Issues
- **OPEC+ price volatility** (e.g., 1973 oil crisis, 2022 Russia-Ukraine conflict).
- Energy import dependency: **EU imported ~60% of its energy in 2022**, much from a single supplier.
- Stranded assets: ~USD **1.4–2.4 trillion** in fossil fuel infrastructure at risk under net-zero pathways.

#### 2.1.4 Sustainability & Resource Depletion
- **Peak Oil theory** (Hubbert, 1956): Conventional oil production has plateaued or peaked in many regions.
- Coal reserves may last **~130 years** at current consumption, oil **~50 years**, natural gas **~50 years** (BP Statistical Review).

### 2.2 Why Nuclear Energy is a Potential Solution — The Five Strategic Pillars

1. **Extraordinary Energy Density:** 1 kg of U-235 releases energy equivalent to **~2.7 million kg of coal** (derived in Section 3).
2. **Near-Zero Operational Carbon Emissions:** ~**12 g CO₂-eq/kWh** over lifecycle (vs. **820 g CO₂-eq/kWh** for coal).
3. **Reliable Baseload Power:** Capacity factor of nuclear = **~90%** vs. solar ~25%, wind ~35% (intermittent renewables).
4. **Energy Security & Independence:** Domestic uranium/thorium reduces import dependency.
5. **Scalability & Versatility:** Powers grids, enables hydrogen production, district heating, desalination, and space propulsion.

> [!NOTE]
