# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define isotopes. Give two examples of isotopes used as nuclear fuel.**

**Model Answer:**
Isotopes are atoms of the same element that have the **same atomic number (Z)** but **different mass numbers (A)** due to a different number of neutrons in their nuclei.

Examples of isotopes used as nuclear fuel:
1. **$^{235}_{92}U$** — a fissile isotope used as primary fuel in thermal reactors.
2. **$^{239}_{94}Pu$** — a fissile isotope produced by neutron capture in $^{238}U$, used in MOX fuel.

*(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for U-235, 1 for Pu-239)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is mass defect? How is it related to the binding energy of a nucleus?**

**Model Answer:**
**Mass defect ($\Delta m$)** is the difference between the sum of the rest masses of the constituent nucleons (protons and neutrons taken separately) and the actual mass of the fully assembled nucleus.

$$\Delta m = Z m_p + N m_n - m_{nucleus}$$

**Relation to Binding Energy:** By Einstein's mass-energy equivalence, the lost mass is converted into the energy that holds the nucleus together. This is the **Binding Energy (BE)**:

$$BE = \Delta m \cdot c^2 = \Delta m \times 931.494 \text{ MeV (when } \Delta m \text{ is in u)}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for mass defect definition, 1 for formula, 1 for energy equivalence)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with suitable examples the difference between isotopes, isobars, and isotones. Mention their significance in nuclear reactor design. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The atomic mass of $^{16}_{8}O$ is **15.994915 u**. Calculate the binding energy per nucleon. Given: $m_p = 1.007276$ u, $m_n = 1.008665$ u, $m_e = 0.000549$ u, 1 u = 931.494 MeV/c². **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** *(7 marks)*

| Term | Definition | Example |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Isotopes** | Same Z, different A (and N) | $^{235}_{92}U$ (N=143), $^{238}_{92}U$ (N=146) |
| **Isobars** | Same A, different Z | $^{14}_{6}C$, $^{14}_{7}N$ |
| **Isotones** | Same N, different Z | $^{13}_{6}C$ (N=7), $^{14}_{7}N$ (N=7) |

**Significance in Reactor Design:**
- Isotopes of uranium determine the **fissile content** of fuel and the design of enrichment plants.
- Isobars help in identifying **decay chains** and fission product behavior.
- Isotones guide the choice of **moderator materials** based on neutron-scattering properties.

*(7 marks: 2 for definitions, 1 for each example, 2 for reactor significance)*

**Part (b):** *(7 marks)*

For $^{16}_{8}O$: $Z = 8$, $A = 16$, $N = 16 - 8 = 8$

**Step 1:** Use hydrogen atom mass to account for electrons:
$$m_H = m_p + m_e = 1.007276 + 0.000549 = 1.007825 \text{ u}$$

**Step 2:** Compute mass defect:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= Z \cdot m_H + N \cdot m_n - m_{atom} \\
\Delta m &= (8)(1.007825) + (8)(1.008665) - 15.994915 \\
\Delta m &= 8.06260 + 8.06932 - 15.994915 \\
\Delta m &= 16.13192 - 15.994915 \\
\Delta m &= 0.137005 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to binding energy:

$$BE = 0.137005 \times 931.494 = 127.62 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 4:** Binding energy per nucleon:

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{127.62}{16} = 7.98 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

*(7 marks: 2 for setup, 2 for mass defect, 2 for BE, 1 for BE/A)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Discuss the binding energy per nucleon curve. Explain how it justifies both nuclear fission and nuclear fusion as energy-releasing processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a nuclear reaction, show how $^{238}_{92}U$ (fertile) is converted into $^{239}_{94}Pu$ (fissile) inside a reactor. Compute the energy released if 1 g of $^{235}U$ undergoes complete fission, given that the average energy per fission is **200 MeV**. Avogadro's number = **6.022 × 10²³**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a):** *(7 marks)*

The **binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) curve** is a plot of BE/A (in MeV) versus mass number A. Key features:

1. **Rising region (A < 56):** BE/A increases sharply from ~1 MeV (for ²H) to a peak of **~8.79 MeV at ⁵⁶Fe**.
2. **Peak region (A ≈ 56–62):** Most stable nuclei (Fe, Ni) — neither fission nor fusion releases energy from these.
3. **Declining region (A > 62):** BE/A gradually decreases to ~7.5 MeV for heavy nuclei (U, Pu).

**Why Fission Releases Energy:**
When a heavy nucleus (e.g., $^{235}U$, BE/A ≈ 7.59 MeV) splits into two medium-mass fragments (BE/A ≈ 8.4 MeV), the products are *more tightly bound*. The difference (~0.8 MeV/nucleon × 235 ≈ 200 MeV total) is released as kinetic energy.

**Why Fusion Releases Energy:**
When two very light nuclei (e.g., ²H + ³H, BE/A ≈ 1.1 MeV) fuse into a heavier one (⁴He, BE/A ≈ 7.07 MeV), the product is more tightly bound. The difference is released as energy.

Both processes **move nuclei toward the iron peak** — the most stable configuration.

*(7 marks: 2 for curve description, 2 for fission explanation, 2 for fusion explanation, 1 for conclusion)*

**Part (b):** *(7 marks)*

**Breeding Reaction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
^{238}_{92}U + ^{1}_{0}n &\rightarrow ^{239}_{92}U + \gamma \\
^{239}_{92}U &\xrightarrow{\beta^-} \, ^{239}_{93}Np + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e \quad (t_{1/2} = 23.5 \text{ min}) \\
^{239}_{93}Np &\xrightarrow{\beta^-} \, ^{239}_{94}Pu + e^- + \bar{\nu}_e \quad (t_{1/2} = 2.36 \text{ days})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Energy Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Number of atoms in 1 g of $^{235}U$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of atoms} &= \frac{N_A \times \text{mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} \\
&= \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 1 \text{ g}}{235 \text{ g/mol}} \\
&= 2.563 \times 10^{21} \text{ atoms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Total energy released:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= N \times E_{per\,fission} \\
E_{total} &= 2.563 \times 10^{21} \times 200 \text{ MeV} \\
E_{total} &= 5.126 \times 10^{23} \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Convert to Joules (1 MeV = $1.602 \times 10^{-13}$ J):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{total} &= 5.126 \times 10^{23} \times 1.602 \times 10^{-13} \\
E_{total} &= 8.21 \times 10^{10} \text{ J} \\
E_{total} &\approx 82.1 \text{ GJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is equivalent to the energy from burning **~2,500 kg of coal** — demonstrating the extraordinary energy density of nuclear fuel.

*(7 marks: 2 for breeding reaction, 2 for atom count, 2 for energy, 1 for conversion/reality check)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Using $m_p$ instead of $m_H = m_p + m_e$ when atomic mass is given. This introduces a **26 × 0.000549 = 0.0143 u** error per iron atom — failing the question.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the **N/Z ratio** distinction in stability questions. Always state the ratio range (1 to 1.5).
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not converting to **MeV** properly. 1 u = **931.494 MeV/c²** (NOT 931.5 — the .006 difference matters in long calculations).
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Confusing **fissile** with **fissionable**. Fissile = thermal-neutron fission; fissionable = any-neutron fission (a broader category).
> - **Common Mistake 5:** In breeding reaction, forgetting the **gamma emission** in the first step.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Isotopes** = same Z, different A. Example: $^{235}U$ vs $^{238}U$.
- ✅ **Isobars** = same A, different Z. Example: $^{14}C$ vs $^{14}N$.
- ✅ **Isotones** = same N, different Z. Example: $^{13}C$ (N=7) vs $^{14}N$ (N=7).
- ✅ **Mass defect ($\Delta m$)** = $Z m_H + N m_n - m_{atom}$ (use hydrogen atom mass when atomic mass is given).
- ✅ **Binding Energy (BE)** = $\Delta m \cdot c^2$ in MeV; use **1 u = 931.494 MeV/c²**.
- ✅ **BE/A peak** at $^{56}Fe$ ≈ 8.79 MeV/nucleon — most stable nucleus.
- ✅ **Fissile isotopes:** $^{235}U$, $^{239}Pu$, $^{233}U$ (fission with thermal neutrons).
- ✅ **Fertile isotopes:** $^{238}U$, $^{232}Th$ (convert to fissile via neutron capture).
- ✅ **N/Z ratio:** ~1 for light stable nuclei, ~1.5 for heavy stable nuclei.
- ✅ **Breeding reaction:** $^{238}U + n \rightarrow ^{239}U \xrightarrow{\beta^-} \, ^{239}Np \xrightarrow{\beta^-} \, ^{239}Pu$.
- ✅ **Energy of 1 g of $^{235}U$** complete fission ≈ **82 GJ** (≈ 2,500 kg of coal equivalent).
- ✅ **Most common exam trap:** Forgetting to use hydrogen atom mass (1.007825 u) instead of bare proton mass (1.007276 u) in BE calculations.
- ✅ **Remember:** Isotopes have the **same chemistry** but **vastly different nuclear properties** — this is the foundation of nuclear engineering.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Binding Energy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Binding Energy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Nuclear Binding Energy (BE)** is precisely defined as the **minimum amount of energy** that must be supplied to a fully assembled atomic nucleus to completely disintegrate it into its individual, free constituent **protons (Z)** and **neutrons (N)** at rest and infinitely separated from one another. Equivalently, by Einstein's mass-energy equivalence, it is the amount of energy *released* when Z protons and N neutrons coalesce to form a stable nucleus.

Mathematically, the nuclear binding energy is expressed as:

$$BE = \left[ Z \cdot m_p + N \cdot m_n - M_{nuc}(A,Z) \right] \cdot c^2$$

where:
- $Z$ = atomic number (number of protons)
- $N$ = number of neutrons
- $A = Z + N$ = mass number
- $m_p$ = mass of a free proton ≈ **1.007276 u**
- $m_n$ = mass of a free neutron ≈ **1.008665 u**
- $M_{nuc}$ = rest mass of the assembled nucleus
- $c^2$ = square of the speed of light (the mass-energy conversion factor)

The quantity inside the brackets is called the **mass defect ($\Delta m$)** — a positive number for all stable nuclei.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** For computational ease in board examinations, the conversion factor **1 u (atomic mass unit) = 931.494 MeV/c² ≈ 931.5 MeV/c²** is universally used. So practically, $BE \ (\text{in MeV}) = \Delta m \ (\text{in u}) \times 931.5$.

> [!NOTE]
> A higher binding energy means a more stable nucleus. However, the more meaningful metric is **Binding Energy per Nucleon (BE/A)**, which normalizes the stability with respect to the size of the nucleus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a pile of LEGO bricks scattered on a table — these are the free protons and neutrons. Now imagine assembling them into a perfectly structured, tightly interlocked LEGO castle — this is the nucleus.

**Here is the fascinating part:** If you weigh the loose LEGO bricks on a precise scale, and then weigh the fully assembled castle, the castle will weigh **slightly less**. The "missing mass" did not vanish — it was converted into the **energy of the strong nuclear force** that holds the bricks together. That missing energy is the **binding energy**.

**The "lock and key" intuition for energy release:**

- **NUCLEAR FUSION** (e.g., Hydrogen → Helium in the Sun): Light nuclei with LOW BE/A are forced together. The product nucleus has a HIGHER BE/A, meaning it is *more tightly bound*. The "extra" binding energy has to go somewhere — it is released as **kinetic energy** of the products (heat, light, radiation).
- **NUCLEAR FISSION** (e.g., Uranium-235 splitting in a reactor): A heavy nucleus with moderate BE/A splits into two mid-mass fragments with HIGHER BE/A. Again, the difference is released as **energy**.

Think of it as **rolling down a valley** on a graph of BE/A — nuclei "want" to settle at the peak (Iron-56 region) and any movement toward that peak releases energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Binding Energy per Nucleon (BE/A)** ranges from about **1.1 MeV/nucleon** (for very light nuclei like deuterium) to a maximum of **~8.8 MeV/nucleon** (for Iron-56, Fe), and then slowly decreases for heavier nuclei (U-235 has ~7.6 MeV/nucleon).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Mass Defect: A Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown

The mass defect arises from two facts of nature:

1. **Einstein's Equivalence (1905):** Mass and energy are two forms of the same physical quantity. Energy lost = mass lost (divided by $c^2$).
2. **The Strong Nuclear Force:** The force binding nucleons is mediated by the exchange of **pions** (Yukawa, 1935) and is extremely powerful but short-ranged (~1–3 femtometers).

**Logical step chain for binding energy derivation:**

- **Step 1:** Count the constituents. A nucleus $^{A}_{Z}X$ contains **Z protons** and **A − Z neutrons** in its free, separated state.
- **Step 2:** Calculate the hypothetical "sum mass" — the total mass if the nucleons were floating independently: $Z \cdot m_p + (A-Z) \cdot m_n$.
- **Step 3:** Compare with the *actual* nuclear mass $M_{nuc}(A,Z)$. Experimentally, the assembled nucleus is **lighter** because some mass was converted into the strong-force binding energy.
- **Step 4:** The mass defect is the difference: $\Delta m = [Z \cdot m_p + (A-Z) \cdot m_n] - M_{nuc}(A,Z)$.
- **Step 5:** Apply $E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ to obtain the binding energy.

### 2.2 The Binding Energy per Nucleon (BE/A) Curve

The **BE/A** is the most important single quantity in nuclear physics because:

- It is the **average energy required to extract one nucleon** from the nucleus.
- It quantifies **nuclear stability** in a size-independent way.
- Its shape dictates **where energy can be extracted** (fusion on the rising left, fission on the falling right).

**Key features of the BE/A curve:**

| Region of Curve | Behavior | Physical Implication |
|---|---|---|
| Very low A (A < 12) | Sharp oscillations; rises steeply | FUSION is highly exothermic (e.g., D-T reaction releases 17.6 MeV) |
| A = 50–80 (mid-mass) | Reaches a broad maximum ~8.7–8.8 MeV | Most stable nuclei (Iron, Nickel region) |
| A = 80–200 (heavy) | Gradual decline to ~7.5 MeV | FISSION releases energy by moving toward the peak |
| A > 230 (transuranic) | Lowest BE/A in stable chart | Spontaneous fission may occur; also α-decay |

### 2.3 The Semi-Empirical Mass Formula (SEMF) — Liquid Drop Model

For large-scale calculations, KTU expects familiarity with the **Weizsäcker (1935) Semi-Empirical Mass Formula**, which parameterizes the binding energy in terms of **five physical effects**:

$$BE = a_v A - a_s A^{2/3} - a_c \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A} \pm \delta(A,Z)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Five-term physics breakdown:**
> - **Volume Term ($a_v A$):** Binding is proportional to the *number* of nucleons, since each nucleon interacts with its neighbors (saturation of nuclear force). Coefficient $a_v \approx$ **15.75 MeV**.
> - **Surface Term ($-a_s A^{2/3}$):** Nucleons on the surface have fewer neighbors → less binding. Surface area scales as $A^{2/3}$. Coefficient $a_s \approx$ **17.8 MeV**.
> - **Coulomb Term ($-a_c Z(Z-1)/A^{1/3}$):** Proton-proton electrostatic repulsion *reduces* binding. Scales as $Z(Z-1)$ (pair-wise). Coefficient $a_c \approx$ **0.711 MeV**.
> - **Asymmetry Term ($-a_a (A-2Z)^2/A$):** Pauli's exclusion principle — nuclei prefer $N \approx Z$ to maximize pairing energy. Coefficient $a_a \approx$ **23.7 MeV**.
> - **Pairing Term ($\pm \delta$):** Extra stability for **even-even** nuclei, less for **odd-odd**. $\delta \approx$ **11.18 MeV** (or 0 for odd-A).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $BE = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ | Mass-energy equivalence | MeV |
| 2 | $\Delta m = Z m_p + N m_n - M_{nuc}$ | Mass defect definition | u (atomic mass units) |
| 3 | $1 \text{ u} = 931.5 \text{ MeV}/c^2$ | Conversion constant | MeV/u |
| 4 | $BE/A$ (avg) | Binding energy per nucleon | MeV/nucleon |
| 5 | $Q = (M_{reactants} - M_{products})c^2$ | Q-value of a nuclear reaction | MeV |
| 6 | $f = (M - A)/A$ | Packing fraction (older metric) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $BE_{SEMF} = a_v A - a_s A^{2/3} - a_c \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A} \pm \delta$ | Weizsäcker liquid drop | MeV |
| 8 | $N_{max} = \frac{a_c}{4a_a}(2Z + 1)$ | Most stable isobar (minimum mass) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In nuclear reactor design, BE/A dictates *fuel choice* (high-A nuclei for fission), *shielding requirements* (related to binding energy distribution), and *thermodynamic efficiency* (Q-value determines recoverable energy per reaction). The SEMF powers nearly all nuclear astrophysics calculations — stellar nucleosynthesis, supernova energetics, and r-process modeling.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Pressurized Water Reactors (PWRs):** Use $^{235}$U and $^{239}$Pu. The difference between BE/A of the heavy fuel and the mid-mass fission products (~200 MeV per fission) is the source of thermal power.
- **Thermonuclear Weapons & ITER (Fusion Reactors):** Exploit the D-T reaction, where $\Delta(BE/A)$ between deuterium/tritium and helium is **+24 MeV** per fusion.
- **Stellar Evolution Models:** The BE/A curve is the **road map** of stellar burning stages. A star cannot extract energy by fusion once it reaches Iron, triggering gravitational collapse → supernova.
- **Radioactive Waste Transmutation:** Engineers use BE/A calculations to design transmutation pathways that convert long-lived actinides into shorter-lived or stable isotopes.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Derivation #1: Binding Energy of Deuterium (²H)

**Given data (standard table values):**
- $m_p = 1.007276$ u
- $m_n = 1.008665$ u
- $M_{nuc}(^2H) = 2.013553$ u
- $1 \text{ u} = 931.5 \text{ MeV}/c^2$

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Z, N, and A:**

For deuterium $^{2}_{1}H$: $Z = 1$ proton, $N = 1$ neutron, $A = 2$.

**Step 2 — Sum the free nucleon masses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma m_{nucleons} &= Z \cdot m_p + N \cdot m_n \\
&= (1)(1.007276) + (1)(1.008665) \\
&= 1.007276 + 1.008665 \\
&= 2.015941 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass defect:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= \Sigma m_{nucleons} - M_{nuc} \\
&= 2.015941 - 2.013553 \\
&= 0.002388 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The mass defect is positive — confirming that the assembled nucleus is *lighter* than its components. The "missing" 0.002388 u has been converted into binding energy.

**Step 4 — Convert mass defect into binding energy using $E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BE &= \Delta m \times 931.5 \text{ MeV/u} \\
&= 0.002388 \times 931.5 \\
&= 2.2244 \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute BE/A (binding energy per nucleon):**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{2.2244}{2} = 1.1122 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

**Physical interpretation:** Deuterium has an exceptionally *low* BE/A (1.11 MeV/nucleon), which is why it is so easy to break apart — this is the basis of the **D-T fusion reaction** in stars and thermonuclear devices.

---

### 3.2 Worked Derivation #2: Binding Energy of Helium-4 (α-particle)

**Given data:**
- $m_p = 1.007276$ u, $m_n = 1.008665$ u
- $M_{nuc}(^4He) = 4.001506$ u

**Step-by-step solution:**

**Step 1 — Z, N, A for $^{4}_{2}He$:** $Z = 2$, $N = 2$, $A = 4$.

**Step 2 — Sum of free nucleon masses:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma m &= 2(1.007276) + 2(1.008665) \\
&= 2.014552 + 2.017330 \\
&= 4.031882 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Mass defect:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= 4.031882 - 4.001506 = 0.030376 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Binding energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BE &= 0.030376 \times 931.5 \\
&= 28.296 \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — BE/A:**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{28.296}{4} = 7.07 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the huge jump:** From deuterium (1.11 MeV/nucleon) to helium-4 (7.07 MeV/nucleon). This 6 MeV/nucleon difference is precisely why the **D + T → He-4 + n** reaction releases a colossal 17.6 MeV. This is the foundation of stellar energy and fusion reactor design.

---

### 3.3 Worked Derivation #3: Q-Value of a Fission Event

Consider the prototypical fission of $^{235}$U absorbing a thermal neutron:

$$^{235}_{92}U + ^1_0n \rightarrow ^{141}_{56}Ba + ^{92}_{36}Kr + 3\,^1_0n + Q$$

**Given atomic masses (using atomic, not nuclear — electrons cancel):**
- $M(^{235}U) = 235.043930$ u
- $M(n) = 1.008665$ u
- $M(^{141}Ba) = 140.914411$ u
- $M(^{92}Kr) = 91.926156$ u

**Step-by-step Q-value calculation:**

**Step 1 — Total reactant mass:**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_R &= M(^{235}U) + M(n) \\
&= 235.043930 + 1.008665 \\
&= 236.052595 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Total product mass (include 3 neutrons):**

$$\begin{aligned}
M_P &= M(^{141}Ba) + M(^{92}Kr) + 3 M(n) \\
&= 140.914411 + 91.926156 + 3(1.008665) \\
&= 140.914411 + 91.926156 + 3.025995 \\
&= 235.866562 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Mass difference:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= M_R - M_P = 236.052595 - 235.866562 = 0.186033 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Q-value (energy released):**

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta m \times 931.5 \\
&= 0.186033 \times 931.5 \\
&\approx 173.3 \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering note:** Of this ~173 MeV, about **~185 MeV equivalent** appears in practice, distributed as: ~165 MeV kinetic energy of fragments (heat), ~5 MeV neutrons, ~7 MeV gammas, ~10 MeV betas/antineutrinos. The **antineutrinos (~10 MeV) escape the reactor** — this is why fission reactors cannot be ~100% efficient at converting mass-energy to electricity (Carnot losses also apply).

---

### 3.4 Worked Derivation #4: SEMF Calculation for $^{56}$Fe

**Using coefficients:**
- $a_v = 15.75$ MeV, $a_s = 17.8$ MeV, $a_c = 0.711$ MeV, $a_a = 23.7$ MeV, $\delta = +11.18$ MeV (even-even)
- For $^{56}_{26}Fe$: $A = 56$, $Z = 26$, $N = 30$.

**Step 1 — Volume term:**

$$a_v A = 15.75 \times 56 = 882.00 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 2 — Surface term:**

$$a_s A^{2/3} = 17.8 \times (56)^{2/3} = 17.8 \times 14.59 = 259.7 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 3 — Coulomb term:**

$$a_c \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} = 0.711 \times \frac{26 \times 25}{(56)^{1/3}} = 0.711 \times \frac{650}{3.826} = 0.711 \times 169.9 = 120.8 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 4 — Asymmetry term:**

$$a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A} = 23.7 \times \frac{(56-52)^2}{56} = 23.7 \times \frac{16}{56} = 23.7 \times 0.2857 = 6.77 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 5 — Pairing term (even-even, positive sign):**

$$\delta = +11.18 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 6 — Total binding energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BE &= 882.00 - 259.7 - 120.8 - 6.77 + 11.18 \\
&= 505.9 \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — BE/A:**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{505.9}{56} = 9.03 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Experimental value for $^{56}$Fe is BE/A = 8.79 MeV/nucleon, total BE ≈ 492 MeV. The SEMF gives a slight over-estimate (~3%) — this is acceptable for an empirical model and demonstrates the model's predictive power near the peak of the BE/A curve.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparison Across Nuclei

| Nucleus | Z | N | A | Δm (u) | BE (MeV) | BE/A (MeV) | Stability Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $^{2}$H | 1 | 1 | 2 | 0.0024 | 2.22 | 1.11 | Very weakly bound (loose) |
| $^{4}$He | 2 | 2 | 4 | 0.0304 | 28.30 | 7.07 | Tightly bound, magic |
| $^{12}$C | 6 | 6 | 12 | 0.0990 | 92.16 | 7.68 | Magic Z & N |
| $^{56}$Fe | 26 | 30 | 56 | 0.5285 | 492.3 | 8.79 | **Peak of stability** |
| $^{235}$U | 92 | 143 | 235 | 1.9151 | 1783.9 | 7.59 | Fissionable |
| $^{238}$U | 92 | 146 | 238 | 1.9342 | 1801.7 | 7.57 | Fertile (α-emitter) |
