## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022] Define the term "Binding Energy per nucleon". Sketch the binding energy per nucleon versus mass number curve and explain its salient features.**

**Model Answer:**

**Binding Energy per Nucleon (BE/A):** It is defined as the average energy required to separate one nucleon from the nucleus, or equivalently, the total binding energy of the nucleus divided by its mass number A.

$$BE/A = \frac{[Z m_p + N m_n - M_{nuc}] \cdot c^2}{A}$$

**Salient features of the BE/A vs A curve:**

1. The curve rises sharply for light nuclei (A < 20), reaching ~8 MeV/nucleon around A = 20.
2. It attains a **broad maximum of ~8.8 MeV/nucleon in the Iron-56 region (A ≈ 56)**, which is the most stable configuration.
3. Beyond A ≈ 100, the curve **gradually decreases** to ~7.5 MeV/nucleon for the heaviest nuclei.
4. **Light nuclei** have low BE/A → **fusion** releases energy (curve goes UP).
5. **Heavy nuclei** have lower BE/A than mid-mass fragments → **fission** releases energy (curve goes UP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering implication:** The curve tells us that both fusion of light nuclei and fission of heavy nuclei are *exothermic* processes, while nuclei around Iron-56 are the *end-point* of nuclear energy generation (cannot release further nuclear energy by standard means).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2023] State and explain the semi-empirical mass formula (Weizsäcker formula) for nuclear binding energy. List the five physical terms with their physical significance.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Semi-Empirical Mass Formula (SEMF)**, also known as the **Weizsäcker liquid drop formula** (1935), expresses the binding energy of a nucleus as a sum of five physically motivated terms:

$$BE = a_v A - a_s A^{2/3} - a_c \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A} \pm \delta$$

| Term | Formula | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Volume Term** | $a_v A$ | Strong force saturation; bulk binding proportional to A |
| **Surface Term** | $-a_s A^{2/3}$ | Surface nucleons underbind; correction to volume |
| **Coulomb Term** | $-a_c \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}}$ | Proton-proton electrostatic repulsion |
| **Asymmetry Term** | $-a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A}$ | Pauli exclusion favors N ≈ Z |
| **Pairing Term** | $\pm \delta$ | Even-even extra stability, odd-odd less stable |

> [!NOTE]
> The sign of $\delta$ is **+11.18 MeV for even-even nuclei, −11.18 MeV for odd-odd nuclei, and 0 for odd-A nuclei**. The values of coefficients are fitted empirically from a large set of measured nuclear masses.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Using *atomic* masses in nuclear mass defect formula without accounting for electron masses — or vice versa. Be consistent.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** Forgetting to convert atomic mass units to MeV using the 931.5 factor. Marks are deducted for unit errors.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** In SEMF, the pairing term sign is frequently inverted. **Recall:** + for even-even, − for odd-odd, 0 for odd-A.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that Q-value is **M_reactants − M_products**, NOT the reverse. Mixing up the sign loses 2–3 marks.
> - **Common Mistake #5:** Plotting BE/A curve without labeling axes or units. KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for unlabeled diagrams.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Comprehensive: Definition + Derivation + Application]

**(a)** Define the terms *mass defect* and *binding energy*. Derive an expression for the binding energy of a nucleus $^{A}_{Z}X$ in terms of its constituents' masses.

**(b)** Calculate the binding energy per nucleon of $^{16}_{8}O$ given the following atomic data:
- $m(^1H) = 1.007825$ u
- $m(n) = 1.008665$ u
- $M(^{16}O) = 15.994915$ u
- 1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²

Also comment on the stability of $^{16}$O compared to $^{12}$C (BE/A = 7.68 MeV/nucleon).

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Mass Defect:** The mass defect $\Delta m$ of a nucleus is defined as the *difference* between the sum of the rest masses of its individual, free constituent nucleons and the actual rest mass of the assembled nucleus.

$$\Delta m = [Z m_p + (A-Z) m_n] - M_{nuc}$$

**Binding Energy:** The binding energy BE of a nucleus is the minimum energy required to completely disintegrate the nucleus into its constituent free nucleons. Equivalently, it is the energy released when Z protons and (A−Z) neutrons combine to form the nucleus.

**Derivation:**

Consider a nucleus $^{A}_{Z}X$ containing Z protons and N = (A−Z) neutrons in its free, separated state.

**Step 1 — Total mass of separated nucleons (at rest, infinite separation):**

$$M_{free} = Z \cdot m_p + (A - Z) \cdot m_n$$

**Step 2 — Apply Einstein's mass-energy equivalence principle:**

When nucleons come together under the influence of the strong nuclear force, the system releases an amount of energy equal to the binding energy. By $E = mc^2$, the mass of the system must therefore *decrease* by $\Delta m \cdot c^2$.

**Step 3 — Define mass defect as the "missing mass":**

$$\Delta m = M_{free} - M_{nuc} = Z m_p + (A-Z) m_n - M_{nuc}$$

**Step 4 — Apply $E = \Delta m \cdot c^2$ to obtain the binding energy:**

$$\boxed{BE = \left[ Z m_p + (A-Z) m_n - M_{nuc} \right] c^2}$$

Using atomic masses (with electron mass automatically cancelled for the same Z on both sides), this simplifies to:

$$BE = \left[ Z M(^1H) + (A-Z) m_n - M(^A_ZX) \right] c^2$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $Z = 8$, $N = 8$, $A = 16$.

**Step 1 — Sum of atomic constituents' masses (using $^{1}$H atomic mass for protons):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Sigma m &= 8 \times M(^1H) + 8 \times m_n \\
&= 8(1.007825) + 8(1.008665) \\
&= 8.062600 + 8.069320 \\
&= 16.131920 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Mass defect:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= 16.131920 - 15.994915 = 0.137005 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Total binding energy:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BE &= 0.137005 \times 931.5 \\
&= 127.625 \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Binding energy per nucleon:**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{127.625}{16} = 7.977 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with $^{12}$C:**

| Nucleus | BE/A (MeV) |
|---|---|
| $^{12}$C | 7.68 |
| $^{16}$O | 7.98 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment on stability:** $^{16}$O has a *higher* binding energy per nucleon (7.98 MeV) than $^{12}$C (7.68 MeV). Therefore, **$^{16}$O is more stable per nucleon** than $^{12}$C. Both are doubly magic nuclei (Z, N magic), which accounts for their high stability.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice — SEMF & Applications Focus]

**(a)** What is the Semi-Empirical Mass Formula? Explain each term of the formula and state the values of the standard coefficients.

**(b)** Using the SEMF, estimate the binding energy of $^{238}_{92}$U. Given:
- $a_v = 15.75$ MeV, $a_s = 17.8$ MeV, $a_c = 0.711$ MeV, $a_a = 23.7$ MeV
- $^{238}$U is even-even, so $\delta = +11.18$ MeV.

Compare your result with the experimental value of 7.6 MeV/nucleon and comment on the agreement.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

*(See Q2 of Part A for detailed explanation of each term and physical significance.)*

**Standard Coefficient Values (KTU syllabus values):**

| Coefficient | Value (MeV) | Physical Origin |
|---|---|---|
| $a_v$ (volume) | 15.75 | Strong force saturation |
| $a_s$ (surface) | 17.8 | Reduced binding at surface |
| $a_c$ (Coulomb) | 0.711 | Proton-proton repulsion |
| $a_a$ (asymmetry) | 23.7 | Pauli exclusion |
| $\delta$ (pairing) | +11.18 / 0 / −11.18 | Even-even / odd-A / odd-odd |

**Assumptions of the liquid drop model:**
1. The nucleus behaves as an incompressible fluid of nuclear matter.
2. The strong force is short-ranged and saturates.
3. The nuclear density is approximately constant for all nuclei.
4. The Coulomb force acts throughout the entire nuclear volume.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $A = 238$, $Z = 92$, $N = 146$ (even-even nucleus).

**Step 1 — Volume term:**

$$a_v A = 15.75 \times 238 = 3748.50 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 2 — Surface term:**

$$a_s A^{2/3} = 17.8 \times (238)^{2/3}$$

Computing $(238)^{2/3}$:

$$(238)^{1/3} \approx 6.194, \quad (238)^{2/3} = (6.194)^2 \approx 38.36$$

$$a_s A^{2/3} = 17.8 \times 38.36 = 682.81 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 3 — Coulomb term:**

$$a_c \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} = 0.711 \times \frac{92 \times 91}{6.194} = 0.711 \times \frac{8372}{6.194}$$

$$= 0.711 \times 1351.6 = 960.99 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 4 — Asymmetry term:**

$$a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A} = 23.7 \times \frac{(238 - 184)^2}{238} = 23.7 \times \frac{(54)^2}{238}$$

$$= 23.7 \times \frac{2916}{238} = 23.7 \times 12.25 = 290.33 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 5 — Pairing term (even-even):**

$$\delta = +11.18 \text{ MeV}$$

**Step 6 — Total binding energy from SEMF:**

$$\begin{aligned}
BE &= 3748.50 - 682.81 - 960.99 - 290.33 + 11.18 \\
&= 1825.55 \text{ MeV}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 7 — Binding energy per nucleon:**

$$\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{1825.55}{238} = 7.67 \text{ MeV/nucleon}$$

**Step 8 — Comparison with experiment:**

| Quantity | SEMF (Predicted) | Experimental |
|---|---|---|
| BE/A for $^{238}$U | 7.67 MeV/nucleon | 7.60 MeV/nucleon |
| Total BE | 1825.5 MeV | 1801.7 MeV |

> [!NOTE]
> **Comment:** The SEMF prediction (7.67 MeV/nucleon) agrees with the experimental value (7.60 MeV/nucleon) to **within ~1%** — an excellent agreement for an empirical 5-parameter model. The small overestimation arises because the SEMF neglects **shell effects** (especially for magic numbers like Z = 92, N = 146 which form a deformed but shell-closed configuration). The model is most accurate for mid-mass nuclei (A ≈ 50–150) and least accurate near magic numbers or for very light nuclei.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ⭐ **Binding Energy (BE)** = energy needed to disassemble a nucleus into free nucleons = $\Delta m \cdot c^2$.
- ⭐ **Mass defect ($\Delta m$)** is **always positive** for a bound nucleus: $\Delta m = [Z m_p + (A-Z) m_n] - M_{nuc}$.
- ⭐ **Conversion factor to memorize**: **1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²**.
- ⭐ **BE/A** is the proper measure of stability; **higher BE/A = more stable**.
- ⭐ **Peak of BE/A** is at **Fe-56 (~8.8 MeV/nucleon)**.
- ⭐ **Fusion** (light → mid-mass) and **fission** (heavy → mid-mass) both move *up* the BE/A curve → both release energy.
- ⭐ **SEMF** has **5 terms**: volume (+), surface (−), Coulomb (−), asymmetry (−), pairing (±).
- ⭐ **Coefficient values to remember**: $a_v = 15.75$, $a_s = 17.8$, $a_c = 0.711$, $a_a = 23.7$, $\delta = \pm 11.18$ MeV.
- ⭐ **Pairing sign rule**: + for even-even, − for odd-odd, 0 for odd-A.
- ⭐ **Q-value** of a reaction: $Q = (M_R - M_P) c^2$ — positive Q means exothermic (energy released).
- ⭐ **Antineutrinos in fission** carry away ~10 MeV unrecoverable energy per event.
- ⭐ **D-T fusion** releases 17.6 MeV: $^2H + ^3H \to ^4He + n$.
- ⭐ **SEMF works best for mid-mass nuclei**; deviates near magic numbers and very light/heavy nuclei.
- ⭐ **Stability trends**: even-even nuclei are most abundant in nature; odd-odd are rarest.
- ⭐ **Engineering applications**: reactor fuel selection, fusion reactor design, stellar evolution models, radioactive waste transmutation.
- ⚠️ **Unit trap**: always include MeV (or J) as final unit; mixing u and MeV without the 931.5 factor loses full marks.
- ⚠️ **Sign trap**: in Q-value formula, $M_R - M_P$ (not reverse); a negative Q means endothermic (energy must be supplied).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nuclear Stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Nuclear Stability

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nuclear Stability** is the condition in which a nucleus exists in a long-lived, energetically balanced state without undergoing spontaneous radioactive decay. A nucleus is said to be *stable* when the **strong nuclear force** binding the nucleons together is sufficient to overcome the **electrostatic Coulomb repulsion** between protons, and when its total energy configuration corresponds to a local minimum in the nuclear mass-energy surface.

In formal KTU terminology, nuclear stability is quantitatively expressed through three primary metrics:

1. **Binding Energy (BE)** — the energy required to completely separate a nucleus into its constituent nucleons.
2. **Binding Energy per Nucleon (BE/A)** — the average energy per nucleon, which serves as the universal indicator of nuclear stability.
3. **Mass Defect ($\Delta m$)** — the difference between the sum of individual nucleon masses and the actual mass of the assembled nucleus.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Water-Droplet in a Valley"

Imagine the **mass-energy surface** of nuclei as a hilly terrain. Each nuclide is like a water droplet sitting somewhere on this terrain:

- **Stable nuclei** = droplets resting at the *bottom of valleys* (local minima). They need an external "push" (an energy input or particle bombardment) to climb out.
- **Unstable nuclei (radioactive)** = droplets perched on *hillsides or ridges*. They will spontaneously roll downhill, releasing energy — this is *radioactive decay*.
- **The depth of the valley** = the Binding Energy per Nucleon. The deeper the valley, the more stable the nucleus.

The **Iron-56 ($^{56}$Fe)** nucleus sits at the deepest part of this "valley of stability" — this is why iron is the end product of stellar nucleosynthesis and the most tightly bound nucleus in nature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Module 1):** The 2024 scheme explicitly emphasizes the *Semi-Empirical Mass Formula (SEMF)* — also called the *Weizsäcker-Bethe formula* — as the primary mathematical tool for predicting nuclear stability across the chart of nuclides.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants are essential for all nuclear stability calculations and are accepted under **CODATA 2018 / KTU Standard Reference Values**:

- Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8} \; \text{m/s}$
- $c^{2} = 8.988 \times 10^{16} \; \text{J/kg} = 931.494 \; \text{MeV/u}$
- Atomic mass unit: $1 \; \text{u} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \; \text{kg} = 931.494 \; \text{MeV}/c^{2}$
- Proton rest mass: $m_{p} = 1.007276 \; \text{u}$
- Neutron rest mass: $m_{n} = 1.008665 \; \text{u}$
- Electron rest mass: $m_{e} = 0.000549 \; \text{u}$
- 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \; \text{J}$

### 1.4 The Concept of Mass Defect — Intuitive View

When isolated protons and neutrons come together to form a nucleus, the **measured mass of the nucleus is LESS than the sum of the masses of its free nucleons**. The "missing" mass has been converted into the binding energy that holds the nucleons together (via $E = \Delta m c^{2}$). This missing mass is the **mass defect**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Competing Terms of Nuclear Stability

A nucleus is held together by a balance of **five fundamental effects**, each contributing a term to the total nuclear binding energy:

#### (a) Volume Term (Saturation Effect)
- **Logic:** The strong nuclear force is short-ranged (~1–3 fm). Each nucleon interacts strongly only with its *nearest neighbors*. Therefore, the binding energy is *roughly proportional* to the total number of nucleons A.
- **Form:** $B_{V} = +a_{V} A$
- **Why positive:** Adds to binding energy. The coefficient $a_{V} \approx 15.75 \; \text{MeV}$.

#### (b) Surface Term (Surface Nucleon Penalty)
- **Logic:** Nucleons on the *surface* of the nucleus have fewer neighbors than interior nucleons. They are therefore less tightly bound.
- **Form:** $B_{S} = -a_{S} A^{2/3}$
- **Why negative (subtracted):** Surface nucleons contribute *less* to stability. Coefficient $a_{S} \approx 17.8 \; \text{MeV}$.

#### (c) Coulomb Term (Electrostatic Repulsion)
- **Logic:** Protons repel each other via the long-range Coulomb force. This repulsion *destabilizes* the nucleus and reduces binding.
- **Form:** $B_{C} = -a_{C} \dfrac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}}$
- **Why negative:** Purely electrostatic. Coefficient $a_{C} \approx 0.711 \; \text{MeV}$.

#### (d) Asymmetry Term (Neutron–Proton Imbalance)
- **Logic:** Stable nuclei prefer a balanced $N \approx Z$ ratio (in light nuclei) because the Pauli exclusion principle forces nucleons into higher energy states when $N \neq Z$.
- **Form:** $B_{A} = -a_{A} \dfrac{(A - 2Z)^{2}}{A}$
- **Why negative:** Deviation from symmetry costs energy. Coefficient $a_{A} \approx 23.7 \; \text{MeV}$.

#### (e) Pairing Term (Even-Odd Effect)
- **Logic:** Nucleons of the same type (p-p or n-n) form energetically favorable spin-zero pairs. Even-even nuclei are most stable; odd-odd are least.
- **Form:** $B_{P} = +\delta(A)$, where:
  - $+\delta$ for even-even nuclei (extra stability)
  - $0$ for even-A, odd Z nuclei
  - $-\delta$ for odd-odd nuclei (least stable)
- **Magnitude:** $\delta = \dfrac{a_{P}}{A^{3/4}}$, with $a_{P} \approx 34 \; \text{MeV}$.

### 2.2 The Semi-Empirical Mass Formula (SEMF) — KTU Master Equation

Combining all five terms, the **total binding energy** of a nucleus $(Z, A)$ is:

$$
B(Z, A) = a_{V} A - a_{S} A^{2/3} - a_{C} \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_{A} \frac{(A - 2Z)^{2}}{A} + \delta(A)
$$

The **mass of the nucleus** is then:

$$
M(Z, A) = Z m_{p} + (A - Z) m_{n} - \frac{B(Z, A)}{c^{2}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When asked to compute the *most stable isobar* for a given mass number A, the trick is to minimize $M(Z, A)$ with respect to Z (treat Z as continuous), yielding the most stable atomic number $Z_{0}$:
>
> $$Z_{0} = \dfrac{A}{2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 + \dfrac{a_{C}}{4 a_{A}} A^{2/3}}$$

### 2.3 Magic Numbers and Exceptional Stability

> [!NOTE]
> **Magic Numbers** are specific numbers of protons or neutrons (2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126) that confer extraordinary extra stability. Nuclei with both Z and N magic are called **doubly magic** (e.g., $^{4}$He, $^{16}$O, $^{40}$Ca, $^{208}$Pb) and lie far above the binding energy curve.

These are explained by the **Nuclear Shell Model** as closed shells of nucleon energy levels.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Typical Value / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Mass defect $\Delta m$ | $\Delta m = \left[Z m_{p} + (A-Z) m_{n}\right] - M_{\text{nucleus}}$ | Atomic mass units (u) |
| Binding energy $BE$ | $BE = \Delta m \cdot c^{2}$ | MeV |
| BE per nucleon $f$ | $f = BE / A$ | MeV/nucleon (~8 MeV typical) |
| Total SEMF $B(Z,A)$ | $a_{V} A - a_{S} A^{2/3} - a_{C} \dfrac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_{A} \dfrac{(A-2Z)^{2}}{A} + \delta$ | MeV |
| Volume term | $a_{V} A$ | $a_{V} = 15.75$ MeV |
| Surface term | $-a_{S} A^{2/3}$ | $a_{S} = 17.8$ MeV |
| Coulomb term | $-a_{C} \dfrac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}}$ | $a_{C} = 0.711$ MeV |
| Asymmetry term | $-a_{A} \dfrac{(A-2Z)^{2}}{A}$ | $a_{A} = 23.7$ MeV |
| Pairing term | $+\delta(A)$ | $\delta = a_{P}/A^{3/4}$, $a_{P}=34$ MeV |
| Most stable isobar $Z_{0}$ | $\dfrac{A}{2 + (a_{C}/2a_{A}) A^{2/3}}$ | Dimensionless |
| Mass-energy equivalence | $E = \Delta m c^{2}$ | $931.494$ MeV per u |
| Packing fraction $f_{P}$ | $\dfrac{M - A}{A}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table Syntax Note:** Absolute value bars $\vert x \vert$ have been replaced with $\mid x \mid$ notation to avoid breaking KTU-compatible markdown tables. Use `\mid` in LaTeX deliverables.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding nuclear stability is foundational for:

- **Reactor Fuel Design:** Choosing fissile isotopes ($^{235}$U, $^{239}$Pu) with appropriate BE/A for sustained chain reactions.
- **Waste Management:** Predicting the half-lives of radioactive fission products.
- **Stellar Nucleosynthesis:** Modeling energy release in stars (the BE/A curve explains why fusion dominates in light stars and fission in heavy ones).
- **Medical Isotope Production:** Selecting stable target nuclei for accelerator-driven isotope production.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Binding Energy from Mass Defect

**Starting premise:** When Z protons and (A − Z) neutrons assemble into a nucleus, the assembled nucleus has a smaller mass than the sum of the free nucleons. This mass difference is the *mass defect*:

$$
\Delta m = \left[ Z \cdot m_{p} + (A - Z) \cdot m_{n} \right] - M_{\text{nucleus}}
$$

Using Einstein's mass-energy equivalence:

$$
BE = \Delta m \cdot c^{2}
$$

Substituting $c^{2} = 931.494 \; \text{MeV/u}$:

$$
BE (\text{in MeV}) = \Delta m (\text{in u}) \times 931.494
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Binding Energy of $^{56}$Fe

Given:
- $Z = 26$, $N = 30$, $A = 56$
- $m_{p} = 1.007276 \; \text{u}$
- $m_{n} = 1.008665 \; \text{u}$
- Atomic mass of $^{56}$Fe = $55.934939 \; \text{u}$ (we approximate nuclear mass by atomic mass minus electron masses, but since $BE$ includes electron binding energy effects, we use atomic masses and atomic masses of H instead — this is the standard textbook practice for clean KTU calculations).

**Using atomic masses (H atom for proton mass):**

Mass of separate constituents:
- 26 H atoms: $26 \times 1.007825 = 26.20345 \; \text{u}$
- 30 neutrons: $30 \times 1.008665 = 30.25995 \; \text{u}$
- Sum: $26.20345 + 30.25995 = 56.46340 \; \text{u}$

Mass defect:
$$
\Delta m = 56.46340 - 55.93494 = 0.52846 \; \text{u}
$$

Binding energy:
$$
BE = 0.52846 \times 931.494 = 492.26 \; \text{MeV}
$$

Binding energy per nucleon:
$$
\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{492.26}{56} = 8.79 \; \text{MeV/nucleon}
$$

This is **the maximum BE/A in the entire periodic table** — the signature of $^{56}$Fe as the most stable nucleus.

### 3.3 Derivation 2 — Most Stable Isobar (Minimization of Mass)

For a fixed mass number A, we want to find $Z_{0}$ that minimizes $M(Z, A)$ (equivalently, maximizes $B(Z, A)$). Treat Z as a continuous variable.

The relevant terms in $B(Z, A)$ that depend on Z are:

$$
B(Z, A) = \text{(terms without Z)} - a_{C} \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_{A} \frac{(A - 2Z)^{2}}{A}
$$

To find the maximum of $B$, take $\dfrac{\partial B}{\partial Z} = 0$.

$$
\frac{\partial B}{\partial Z} = -a_{C} \frac{(2Z - 1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_{A} \cdot \frac{2(A - 2Z)(-2)}{A}
$$

$$
\frac{\partial B}{\partial Z} = -\frac{a_{C}(2Z - 1)}{A^{1/3}} + \frac{4 a_{A}(A - 2Z)}{A}
$$

Setting equal to zero:

$$
\frac{a_{C}(2Z - 1)}{A^{1/3}} = \frac{4 a_{A}(A - 2Z)}{A}
$$

Assuming $2Z - 1 \approx 2Z$ for large Z (negligible correction):

$$
\frac{2 a_{C} Z}{A^{1/3}} = \frac{4 a_{A}(A - 2Z)}{A}
$$

Multiplying both sides by $A$:

$$
2 a_{C} Z A^{2/3} = 4 a_{A}(A - 2Z)
$$

$$
2 a_{C} Z A^{2/3} + 8 a_{A} Z = 4 a_{A} A
$$

$$
Z \left[2 a_{C} A^{2/3} + 8 a_{A} \right] = 4 a_{A} A
$$

$$
Z_{0} = \frac{4 a_{A} A}{2 a_{C} A^{2/3} + 8 a_{A}} = \frac{A}{2 + \dfrac{a_{C}}{2 a_{A}} A^{2/3}}
$$

This is the **most stable atomic number** for a given isobar. The second derivative test confirms it is a maximum of BE (minimum of mass).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This formula explains why the *neutron-to-proton ratio* increases with A in stable nuclei. For large A, $Z_{0} < A/2$, meaning more neutrons than protons are needed to dilute the Coulomb repulsion.

### 3.4 Derivation 3 — The Pairing Term Justification

The pairing term $\delta(A)$ reflects the quantum mechanical pairing energy. Empirically, it scales as $A^{-3/4}$ (or sometimes $A^{-1/2}$ in older textbooks) because:

- Pairing affects only nucleons near the Fermi surface.
- The number of such nucleons scales as $A^{1/3}$ (surface nucleons).
- The pairing energy per pair is roughly constant.

Therefore:

$$
\delta(A) = \begin{cases} +\dfrac{a_{P}}{A^{3/4}} & \text{even-even} \\ 0 & \text{odd A} \\ -\dfrac{a_{P}}{A^{3/4}} & \text{odd-odd} \end{cases}
$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Term-by-Term Behavior

| **Term** | **Sign** | **A-dependence** | **Z-dependence** | **Physical Origin** | **Dominant For** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Volume | $+$ | $A^{1}$ | None | Strong force saturation | All nuclei |
| Surface | $-$ | $A^{2/3}$ | None | Surface nucleon underbinding | Light nuclei |
| Coulomb | $-$ | $A^{-1/3}$ | $Z^{2}$ | Proton-proton repulsion | Heavy nuclei |
| Asymmetry | $-$ | $A^{-1}$ | $(A-2Z)^{2}$ | Pauli exclusion | Medium-heavy |
| Pairing | $\pm$ | $A^{-3/4}$ | Depends | Spin-coupling | All nuclei (small) |

### 3.6 Limiting Cases and Sanity Checks

- **For very light nuclei** ($A \le 20$): Surface and asymmetry terms dominate; BE/A rises sharply with A.
- **Around $A \approx 56$**: BE/A peaks at ~8.79 MeV/nucleon (iron peak).
- **For heavy nuclei** ($A > 100$): Coulomb term dominates the decline; BE/A gradually drops to ~7.5 MeV at $^{238}$U.
- **At $A \to \infty$**: BE/A $\to a_{V} - a_{S} A^{-1/3} - a_{C} Z^{2}/A^{4/3} \to a_{V} \approx 15.75$ MeV (asymptotic, never reached due to Coulomb instability).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Architecture — The Five-Term Binding Energy Model

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts how the five SEMF terms combine to determine the net binding energy of a nucleus:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Nuclear Binding Energy B(Z,A)"] --> B["Volume Term: +aV * A"]
    A --> C["Surface Term: -aS * A pow 2/3"]
    A --> D["Coulomb Term: -aC * Z(Z-1) / A pow 1/3"]
    A --> E["Asymmetry Term: -aA * (A-2Z) pow 2 / A"]
    A --> F["Pairing Term: plus or minus delta"]
    B --> G["Net Stabilizing Influence"]
    C --> H["Net Destabilizing Influence"]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> I["Net Modulating Influence"]
    G --> J["Final B Z A value in MeV"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Stability Decision Flowchart

This flowchart shows how to determine nuclear stability given a candidate nuclide $(Z, A)$:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1["Input Nucleus: Z protons, N neutrons, A = Z + N"] --> S2{"Compute BE per nucleon using SEMF"}
    S2 --> S3{"Is BE per nucleon greater than 8 MeV?"}
    S3 -- Yes --> S4{"Is Z and N magic?"}
    S3 -- No --> S5["Marked Unstable"]
    S4 -- Yes --> S6["Doubly Magic: Exceptionally Stable"]
    S4 -- No --> S7["Stable but not Magic"]
    S7 --> S8{"Check Pairing: even-even?"}
    S8 -- Yes --> S9["Extra Stability Bonus"]
    S8 -- No --> S10["Normal Stability"]
    S6 --> JFIN["Final Classification: Most Stable"]
    S9 --> JFIN
    S10 --> JFIN
    S5 --> JFIN
```

### 4.3 Binding Energy Curve Schematic (BE/A vs A)

Since Mermaid cannot physically plot graphs, we render the conceptual shape of the BE/A curve as a process-flow schematic:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Light Nuclei A less than 20: Sharp Rise"] --> P2["Iron Peak Region A equals 56: Maximum BE/A equals 8.79 MeV"]
    P2 --> P3["Medium Nuclei 56 to 120: Plateau around 8.5 MeV"]
    P3 --> P4["Heavy Nuclei A greater than 120: Gradual Decline"]
    P4 --> P5["A greater than 230: BE/A drops to 7.5 MeV at Uranium"]
    P5 --> P6["Beyond A equals 260: Spontaneous Fission Dominates"]
```

### 4.4 Architecture of Magic Number Stability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1["Magic Numbers: 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126"] --> M2{"Apply to Z or N?"}
    M2 -- Z Magic --> M3["Proton Shell Closed"]
    M2 -- N Magic --> M4["Neutron Shell Closed"]
    M3 --> M5{"Is the other also magic?"}
    M4 --> M5
    M5 -- Yes --> M6["Doubly Magic Nuclei: 4He, 16O, 40Ca, 48Ca, 208Pb"]
    M5 -- No --> M7["Singly Magic Nuclei: Enhanced Stability"]
    M6 --> M8["Extra-Large BE/A Anomaly"]
    M7 --> M8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualization Note:** All Mermaid node identifiers are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `S1`, `M6`) with no reserved keywords used as labels. Labels are raw uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting, ensuring smooth Mermaid rendering on KTU-compatible markdown viewers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> *Define mass defect and binding energy per nucleon. Why is the binding energy per nucleon curve considered the signature of nuclear stability?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Mass Defect (1 mark):** The mass defect $\Delta m$ of a nucleus is defined as the difference between the sum of the masses of its individual constituent nucleons (taken in their free state) and the actual measured mass of the assembled nucleus:

$$
\Delta m = \left[ Z \cdot m_{p} + (A - Z) \cdot m_{n} \right] - M_{\text{nucleus}}
$$

**Binding Energy per Nucleon (1 mark):** Binding energy per nucleon $f = BE/A$ is the *average* energy required to remove one nucleon from the nucleus. It is calculated as:

$$
f = \frac{\Delta m \cdot c^{2}}{A}
$$

**Why BE/A indicates stability (1 mark):** A higher $BE/A$ means each nucleon is more tightly bound. Nuclei near the peak of the BE/A curve (around $^{56}$Fe, ~8.79 MeV/nucleon) are the most stable, while nuclei far from the peak (very light or very heavy) are less stable and undergo fusion or fission to move toward the peak.

---

> **Question 2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
> *What are magic numbers in nuclear physics? Name any four magic numbers and explain why they confer extra stability.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Magic numbers are specific values of proton number (Z) or neutron number (N) at which the nucleus exhibits anomalously high stability compared to its neighbors. They correspond to completely filled nuclear shells in the nuclear shell model.

**Four Magic Numbers (1 mark):** The seven accepted magic numbers are: **2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126**.

**Why they confer extra stability (1 mark):** When the number of protons or neutrons equals a magic number, a nuclear shell is completely filled (analogous to noble gases in atomic electron shells). The closed-shell configuration has significantly higher binding energy than the smooth SEMF prediction. Nuclei with both Z and N magic — called *doubly magic* nuclei — are exceptionally stable (e.g., $^{4}$He, $^{16}$O, $^{40}$Ca, $^{208}$Pb).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
> *(a)* Derive the Semi-Empirical Mass Formula (SEMF), explaining the physical origin of each term with its sign and magnitude. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Using the SEMF, find the most stable isobar for mass number $A = 125$. Given: $a_{C} = 0.711$ MeV and $a_{A} = 23.7$ MeV. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — Derivation of SEMF (7 marks):**

The SEMF expresses total nuclear binding energy as a sum of five competing physical terms:

$$
B(Z, A) = a_{V} A - a_{S} A^{2/3} - a_{C} \frac{Z(Z-1)}{A^{1/3}} - a_{A} \frac{(A - 2Z)^{2}}{A} + \delta(A)
$$

**Term 1 — Volume Term (1.5 marks):** The strong nuclear force is *short-ranged* (~1–3 fm). Each nucleon interacts strongly only with its nearest neighbors. By the *saturation property*, the binding energy is therefore *proportional to the number of nucleons* A. This gives a positive contribution $+a_{V} A$, with $a_{V} \approx 15.75$ MeV. The force is attractive → adds to BE.

**Term 2 — Surface Term (1.5 marks):** Nucleons at the nuclear surface have fewer neighboring nucleons than interior nucleons. They are therefore less tightly bound, contributing *less* than the bulk. The number of surface nucleons scales with the surface area, $\propto A^{2/3}$. This subtracts from BE: $-a_{S} A^{2/3}$, with $a_{S} \approx 17.8$ MeV.

**Term 3 — Coulomb Term (1.5 marks):** The Coulomb repulsion between every pair of Z protons is long-ranged. The electrostatic potential energy of a uniformly charged sphere scales as $Z(Z-1)/A^{1/3}$ (the $Z(Z-1)$ accounts for unique pairs; the $A^{1/3}$ accounts for the sphere radius). This is purely destabilizing: $-a_{C} Z(Z-1)/A^{1/3}$, with $a_{C} \approx 0.711$ MeV.

**Term 4 — Asymmetry Term (1.5 marks):** By the Pauli exclusion principle, identical nucleons cannot occupy the same quantum state. If $N \neq Z$, some nucleons must occupy higher energy states. The cost in binding energy scales as $(A - 2Z)^{2}/A$. This destabilizes: $-a_{A} (A - 2Z)^{2}/A$, with $a_{A} \approx 23.7$ MeV.

**Term 5 — Pairing Term (1 mark):** Nucleons of the same type (p-p or n-n) couple into spin-zero pairs with extra binding. Even-even nuclei gain $+\delta$, even-odd nuclei gain $0$, and odd-odd nuclei lose $-\delta$, where $\delta = a_{P}/A^{3/4}$ and $a_{P} \approx 34$ MeV.

**Part (b) — Most Stable Isobar for A = 125 (7 marks):**

Using the most-stable-isobar formula:

$$
Z_{0} = \frac{A}{2 + \dfrac{a_{C}}{2 a_{A}} A^{2/3}}
$$

**Step 1 — Calculate $A^{2/3}$:**
$$
A^{2/3} = 125^{2/3} = (5^{3})^{2/3} = 5^{2} = 25
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the correction factor:**
$$
\frac{a_{C}}{2 a_{A}} A^{2/3} = \frac{0.711}{2 \times 23.7} \times 25 = \frac{0.711 \times 25}{47.4} = \frac{17.775}{47.4} = 0.3750
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the formula:**
$$
Z_{0} = \frac{125}{2 + 0.375} = \frac{125}{2.375} = 52.63
$$

**Step 4 — Round to nearest integer:**
$$
Z_{0} \approx 53 \quad (\text{Iodine-like, } N = 72)
$$

This corresponds approximately to $^{125}$Sb (Z = 51) or $^{125}$Te (Z = 52) or $^{125}$I (Z = 53), all known stable or long-lived isobars.

---

> **Question B.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]
> *(a)* Explain the binding energy per nucleon curve. Discuss how it explains the energy release in (i) nuclear fission and (ii) nuclear fusion. *(7 marks)*
> *(b)* Calculate the binding energy and binding energy per nucleon for $^{16}$O, given the atomic mass of $^{16}$O = 15.994915 u, mass of H atom = 1.007825 u, and mass of neutron = 1.008665 u. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution:

**Part (a) — BE/A Curve and Energy Release (7 marks):**

The binding energy per nucleon ($BE/A$) curve plots $f = BE/A$ versus mass number $A$.

**Curve characteristics (2 marks):**
- Rises sharply from $A = 1$ to $A \approx 56$, reaching a peak at $^{56}$Fe with $BE/A \approx 8.79$ MeV.
- Plateaus between $A = 56$ and $A \approx 120$ at around 8.5 MeV/nucleon.
- Gradually decreases for $A > 120$, reaching ~7.5 MeV/nucleon at $^{238}$U.
- Drops steeply beyond $A = 230$ due to instability against spontaneous fission.

**Fission (2.5 marks):** Heavy nuclei (e.g., $^{235}$U, $BE/A \approx 7.6$ MeV) split into medium-mass fragments (e.g., around $A = 100$–140, $BE/A \approx 8.4$ MeV). The fragments have *higher* BE/A than the parent. The difference (~0.8 MeV/nucleon × ~235 nucleons ≈ 200 MeV) is released as kinetic energy of fragments, gamma rays, and neutrinos.

**Fusion (2.5 marks):** Very light nuclei (e.g., $^{2}$H, $^{3}$H, $BE/A \approx 1$–3 MeV) combine to form a heavier nucleus (e.g., $^{4}$He, $BE/A \approx 7.07$ MeV). The product has a *higher* BE/A than the reactants. The difference (~3 MeV/nucleon × 4 nucleons ≈ 24 MeV per fusion event) is released as kinetic energy of the helium nucleus and neutron.

**Physical principle (1 mark):** Both processes move the system *uphill* on the BE/A curve toward the iron peak — but "uphill on BE/A" actually means *downhill in total energy*, since higher BE/A means more negative (bound) mass-energy.

**Part (b) — Calculation for $^{16}$O (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $A = 16$, $Z = 8$, $N = 8$
- Atomic mass of $^{16}$O = $15.994915$ u
- Mass of H atom = $1.007825$ u
- Mass of neutron = $1.008665$ u

**Step 1 — Calculate sum of constituent masses (2 marks):**
$$
\Sigma = Z \cdot m_{H} + N \cdot m_{n} = 8 \times 1.007825 + 8 \times 1.008665
$$

$$
\Sigma = 8.062600 + 8.069320 = 16.131920 \; \text{u}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass defect (2 marks):**
$$
\Delta m = \Sigma - M(^{16}O) = 16.131920 - 15.994915 = 0.137005 \; \text{u}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate binding energy (2 marks):**
$$
BE = \Delta m \times 931.494 \; \text{MeV} = 0.137005 \times 931.494
$$

$$
BE = 127.619 \; \text{MeV}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate BE per nucleon (1 mark):**
$$
\frac{BE}{A} = \frac{127.619}{16} = 7.976 \; \text{MeV/nucleon}
$$

**Verification:** This is consistent with the experimental value of ~7.98 MeV/nucleon for $^{16}$O. The slightly low value (compared to the peak ~8.79) reflects that $^{16}$O is still on the rising portion of the BE/A curve.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Sign errors in SEMF:** Students often forget the **negative sign** of the surface, Coulomb, and asymmetry terms. Always remember: only Volume and Pairing (when applicable) are *positive*.
> 2. **Using $Z(Z-1)$ instead of $Z^{2}$:** In the Coulomb term, the correct form is $Z(Z-1)$ (number of unique proton pairs), not $Z^{2}$. Although some textbooks simplify to $Z^{2}$ for large Z, the rigorous form uses $Z(Z-1)$.
> 3. **Pairing term sign confusion:** $+ \delta$ for even-even (Z even, N even); $- \delta$ for odd-odd (Z odd, N odd); $0$ for nuclei with odd A.
> 4. **Atomic mass vs. nuclear mass:** In problems using atomic masses (as in Part B above), use the **H atom mass**, not the bare proton mass, to avoid electron-mass bookkeeping errors.
> 5. **Unit conversion trap:** $1 \; \text{u} = 931.494 \; \text{MeV}/c^{2}$. Forgetting the factor of $c^{2}$ when converting mass defect to binding energy is the most common error.
> 6. **Non-integer $Z_{0}$:** Students often panic when the most-stable-isobar formula gives a non-integer (e.g., $Z_{0} = 52.6$). Always round to the *nearest integer*, and check experimental data for actual stable isotopes in that range.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nuclear Stability**

- [x] **Mass Defect** = (sum of free nucleon masses) − (assembled nucleus mass). Always *positive* in stable nuclei.
- [x] **Binding Energy** = $\Delta m \cdot c^{2}$; measured in MeV. $1 \; \text{u} = 931.494$ MeV/$c^{2}$.
- [x] **BE/A** peaks at $^{56}$Fe (~8.79 MeV/nucleon). This is the "iron peak" of stability.
- [x] **SEMF has 5 terms:** Volume (+), Surface (−), Coulomb (−), Asymmetry (−), Pairing (±).
- [x] **Standard coefficients:** $a_{V} \approx 15.75$, $a_{S} \approx 17.8$, $a_{C} \approx 0.711$, $a_{A} \approx 23.7$, $a_{P} \approx 34$ MeV.
- [x] **Most stable isobar:** $Z_{0} = A / \left[ 2 + (a_{C}/2a_{A}) A^{2/3} \right]$. Valid for $A \ge 20$ approximately.
- [x] **Pairing term:** $+ \delta$ (even-even), $0$ (odd A), $- \delta$ (odd-odd).
- [x] **Magic numbers:** 2, 8, 20, 28, 50, 82, 126. *Doubly magic* nuclei (e.g., $^{4}$He, $^{16}$O, $^{40}$Ca, $^{208}$Pb) are exceptionally stable.
- [x] **Fission & Fusion:** Both move the system toward the iron peak of BE/A, releasing energy because the products are *more* tightly bound.
- [x] **KTU 2024 Scheme Weight:** Module 1 (Nuclear Stability) carries high weightage — expect at least one full 14-mark question and one 3-mark short question per exam cycle.
- [x] **Common units:** MeV (binding energies), u (masses), fm (distances), MeV/nucleon (BE/A).

> **End of Notes — Nuclear Stability | PEMET412 Module 1 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Alpha, Beta, Gamma and Neutron Interactions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Alpha, Beta, Gamma and Neutron Interactions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Radiation–Matter Interactions

In nuclear engineering, the behaviour of ionising radiations as they traverse matter dictates reactor shielding design, radiation dosimetry, detector selection, and fuel-cycle safety. The four fundamental radiations emitted during nuclear transformations and their characteristic interaction mechanisms form the bedrock of radiation physics. Each particle (or quantum) deposits its energy through a distinct microscopic mechanism depending on its **mass, charge, and energy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (PEMET412 / Module 1):**
> The student must be able to describe the *interaction of alpha, beta, gamma, and neutron radiation* with matter, classify the dominant interaction regimes, and apply attenuation/range formulas to solve numerical problems in shielding and dosimetry.

### 1.2 The Four Radiations at a Glance

| Radiation | Identity | Charge | Mass (approx.) | Typical Energy | Penetration |
|-----------|----------|:------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:-----------:|
| **Alpha ($\alpha$)** | $^4_2\text{He}$ nucleus | $+2e$ | $4\,\text{u}$ | $4$–$10$ MeV | Very low (paper) |
| **Beta ($\beta$)** | $e^{-}/e^{+}$ (high KE) | $\mp e$ | $m_e$ | $0$–$3$ MeV | Moderate (mm–cm) |
| **Gamma ($\gamma$)** | High-energy photon | $0$ | $0$ | $0.01$–$10$ MeV | High (cm–m) |
| **Neutron (n)** | Free neutron | $0$ | $1\,\text{u}$ | $0.025$ eV – $20$ MeV | Very high |

where $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{C}$ is the elementary charge, $m_e = 9.109 \times 10^{-31}\,\text{kg}$ is the electron rest mass, and $1\,\text{u} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27}\,\text{kg}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy (The "Bowling Ball vs. Bullet" Picture):**
> Imagine throwing different objects at a stack of thin paper sheets:
> - **Alpha particle** = a slow, heavy bowling ball. It smashes the first few sheets and stops — it ionises *enormously* per unit path, but barely penetrates.
> - **Beta particle** = a light, fast tennis ball. It punches through more sheets but scatters randomly, leaving a tortuous zigzag path.
> - **Gamma ray** = an invisible flash of light. It doesn't "hit" the sheets directly; it is *absorbed* or *scattered* probabilistically, behaving like light passing through fog.
> - **Neutron** = a neutral billiard ball. It doesn't interact electromagnetically at all; it collides *nuclearly* with the atomic nuclei of the paper, like marbles bouncing off other marbles.

### 1.3 Formal Definitions of the Interactions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 1 – Charged-Particle Interaction:**
> Charged particles (α, β) interact primarily via the **Coulomb force** with the orbital electrons and nuclei of the absorber, transferring energy through *excitation*, *ionisation*, and (for β only) *radiative (bremsstrahlung) losses*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 2 – Gamma-Ray Interaction:**
> Gamma rays are uncharged photons that interact via *quantum* processes: the **photoelectric effect**, **Compton scattering**, and **pair (and triplet) production**. The relative dominance of each mode is governed by the photon energy $h\nu$ and the absorber atomic number $Z$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition 3 – Neutron Interaction:**
> Because the neutron is electrically neutral, it interacts *only* with atomic nuclei through **strong (nuclear) forces**, manifesting as elastic scattering, inelastic scattering, radiative capture $(n,\gamma)$, charged-particle reactions $(n,p)/(n,\alpha)$, and fission $(n,f)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Constants to Memorise for KTU Exams:**
> - Speed of light: $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}\,\text{m/s}$
> - Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$, $\hbar = 1.055 \times 10^{-34}\,\text{J·s}$
> - Electron rest energy: $m_e c^{2} = 0.511\,\text{MeV}$
> - 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\,\text{J}$
> - 1 barn $= 10^{-28}\,\text{m}^{2}$ (cross-section unit)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Alpha-Particle ($\alpha$) Interactions

Alpha particles are mono-energetic (discrete emission lines) and lose energy predominantly through:

1. **Inelastic Coulomb scattering with bound electrons** → *excitation* and *ionisation*. This dominates the energy loss (≈ 99 %).
2. **Elastic Coulomb scattering with absorber nuclei** → *Rutherford scattering*. Negligible energy loss, but causes small-angle deflections.
3. **Nuclear reactions** — extremely rare due to the Coulomb barrier; only important above ≈ 30 MeV.

**Key Theoretical Framework — Stopping Power:**

The *linear stopping power* $S$ is defined as the average energy lost by the particle per unit path length:

$$S = -\frac{dE}{dx}$$

For a heavy charged particle traversing an absorber of atomic number $Z$ and atomic mass $A$, the **Bethe-Bloch formula** (non-relativistic, heavy-particle limit) reads:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4 \pi k_0^{2} z^{2} e^{4}}{m_e v^{2}} \cdot N Z \left[ \ln \left( \frac{2 m_e v^{2}}{I} \right) \right]$$

where:
- $z = 2$ for the alpha particle (charge number),
- $v$ is the alpha velocity,
- $N$ is the number density of absorber atoms,
- $I \approx 10\,\text{eV} \cdot Z$ is the mean excitation energy of the absorber,
- $k_0 = \tfrac{1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}$.

**Range of Alpha Particles:**

Because alphas travel in nearly straight lines with continuously increasing $S$ as they slow down (the famous **$1/v^{2}$ Bragg behaviour**), the *range* $R$ is well-defined and obtained by integrating $1/S$:

$$R = \int_{0}^{E_0} \left(-\frac{dE}{dx}\right)^{-1} dE$$

A widely-used empirical approximation (Geiger's rule) is:

$$R_{\text{air}} \,(\text{cm}) \approx 0.318 \, E^{3/2}$$

with $E$ in MeV (valid for $4 \le E \le 7$ MeV).

### 2.2 Beta-Particle ($\beta$) Interactions

Beta particles (electrons/positrons) lose energy through three competing mechanisms:

1. **Ionisation (collision loss)** — dominant at low β-energies; classical Rutherford–Bohr treatment.
2. **Radiative loss (bremsstrahlung)** — the β is decelerated in the Coulomb field of a nucleus, emitting a continuous X-ray spectrum. The fraction of energy radiated scales as $\propto Z E_\beta / 800$.
3. **Elastic scattering** with electrons and nuclei → produces the characteristic tortuous, *zigzag* path.

**Stopping-Power Partition:**

$$-\left(\frac{dE}{dx}\right)_{\text{total}} = -\left(\frac{dE}{dx}\right)_{\text{coll}} - \left(\frac{dE}{dx}\right)_{\text{rad}}$$

The **Bethe-Bloch formula for electrons** (relativistic, Møller/Bhabha corrections):

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{2 \pi k_0^{2} e^{4}}{m_e v^{2}} \cdot N Z \left[ \ln \left( \frac{m_e v^{2} E}{2 I^{2} (1 - \beta^{2})} \right) - \beta^{2} \right]$$

**Critical Energy ($\epsilon_0$):** Energy at which radiative loss equals ionisation loss:

$$\epsilon_0 \approx \frac{800}{Z} \quad \text{(MeV)}$$

**Range of Beta Particles:** Because β spectra are *continuous* (from 0 to $E_{\max}$), the *extrapolated* range is used:

$$R_\beta \approx 0.412 \, E^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E} \quad \text{(g/cm}^{2}\text{, with } E \text{ in MeV)}$$

### 2.3 Gamma-Ray ($\gamma$) Interactions

Gamma photons interact via **three quantum processes**, each dominant in a different $(E_\gamma, Z)$ region.

**A. Photoelectric Effect (low $E_\gamma$, high $Z$):**

The photon transfers *all* its energy to a *bound* electron, which is ejected with kinetic energy:

$$T_e = h\nu - \phi - E_b$$

where $\phi$ is the work function and $E_b$ is the binding energy of the shell. The mass attenuation coefficient varies approximately as:

$$\tau \propto \frac{Z^{n}}{E_\gamma^{3.5}}$$

with $n \approx 4$–$5$ for $E_\gamma \approx 0.1$–$1$ MeV.

**B. Compton Scattering (intermediate $E_\gamma$):**

The photon scatters off a *free* (loosely-bound) electron. Using conservation of relativistic 4-momentum, the **Compton wavelength-shift formula** is:

$$\lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c} (1 - \cos\theta)$$

The energy of the *scattered* photon is:

$$h\nu' = \frac{h\nu}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}, \qquad \alpha \equiv \frac{h\nu}{m_e c^{2}}$$

where $\theta$ is the scattering angle. The recoil electron kinetic energy is:

$$T_e = h\nu - h\nu' = h\nu \cdot \frac{\alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}$$

**C. Pair Production ($E_\gamma \ge 1.022\,\text{MeV}$):**

In the Coulomb field of a nucleus, the photon converts to an **electron–positron pair**:

$$h\nu \rightarrow e^{-} + e^{+}$$

The threshold is $1.022\,\text{MeV} = 2 m_e c^{2}$ (energy needed to create the pair at rest in the centre-of-mass frame). Excess energy appears as kinetic energy of the pair. The annihilation of the positron yields two $0.511\,\text{MeV}$ *annihilation quanta* (secondary photons).

The mass attenuation coefficient:

$$\kappa \propto Z^{2} \, f(E_\gamma)$$

rises from threshold, reaches a maximum, and then decreases logarithmically.

**Total Linear Attenuation Coefficient:**

$$\mu = \tau + \sigma_C + \kappa$$

The **Beer–Lambert law** governs the attenuation of a collimated γ-beam:

$$I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$$

The **half-value layer (HVL)** is:

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}$$

The **mean free path** is $\lambda = 1/\mu$.

The **tenth-value layer (TVL)** is $x_{1/10} = \ln 10 / \mu \approx 3.32 \, x_{1/2}$.

### 2.4 Neutron (n) Interactions

Neutrons are classified by energy:

| Class | Energy Range | Dominant Application |
|-------|:------------:|----------------------|
| Cold | $< 0.001$ eV | Fundamental research |
| Thermal | $\approx 0.025$ eV | Reactor moderation |
| Epithermal | $0.5$ eV – $1$ keV | Resonance absorption |
| Fast | $> 1$ keV | Fast reactors, fission |
| Relativistic | $> 20$ MeV | Cosmic-ray spallation |

The principal interaction modes are:

**1. Elastic Scattering (n, n):** Neutron scatters off a nucleus; total kinetic energy conserved; *nucleus recoils* receiving energy:

$$E_R = E_n \cdot \frac{4 A}{(1+A)^{2}} \, \cos^{2}\theta_{\text{CM}}$$

The average energy loss per collision (in lab frame) is:

$$\overline{\Delta E} = E_n \cdot \frac{1 - \alpha}{2}, \qquad \alpha = \left(\frac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^{2}$$

The number of collisions $N$ to thermalise from energy $E_0$ to $E$:

$$N = \frac{1}{\xi} \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E}\right), \qquad \xi = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

($\xi$ = *lethargy increment*, the average fractional energy loss per collision.)

**2. Inelastic Scattering (n, n'):** Neutron excites the target nucleus to a higher level; nucleus de-excites by emitting a γ-ray. Threshold:

$$E_{\text{th}} = \frac{E^* (A+1)}{A}$$

where $E^*$ is the first excited-state energy. Important for fast neutrons ($E > 1$ MeV).

**3. Radiative Capture (n, $\gamma$):** Compound nucleus formed, de-excites by γ-emission:

$$^{A}_{Z}\text{X} + n \rightarrow ^{A+1}_{Z}\text{X}^{*} \rightarrow ^{A+1}_{Z}\text{X} + \gamma$$

**4. Charged-Particle Reactions (n, p), (n, $\alpha$):** Threshold reactions; e.g., $^3\text{He}(n,p)\,^3\text{H}$ is the basis of $^3$He detectors.

**5. Fission (n, f):** Heavy nuclei ($^{235}$U, $^{239}$Pu, $^{233}$U) absorb a thermal neutron and split, releasing ≈ 200 MeV per fission.

**6. Multiplication Factor (infinite medium):**

$$k_\infty = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f$$

where $\eta$ = neutrons per absorption in fuel, $\varepsilon$ = fast-fission factor, $p$ = resonance escape probability, $f$ = thermal utilisation factor.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following compact formula sheet is the single most-examined portion of Module 1. Memorise the symbol, the equation, and the typical numerical value.

| Phenomenon | Master Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Caveat |
|------------|-----------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Beer–Lambert attenuation | $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $\mu$ in m$^{-1}$, $x$ in m | Narrow-beam geometry only |
| Half-value layer | $x_{1/2} = \ln 2 / \mu$ | Same as above | HVL |
| Mean free path | $\lambda = 1/\mu$ | m | For photons/neutrons |
| Build-up factor | $I = B \, I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ | $B \ge 1$ | Wide-beam / shielding |
| Compton wavelength shift | $\Delta\lambda = \lambda_c (1 - \cos\theta)$, $\lambda_c = h/m_e c$ | $\lambda_c = 2.43$ pm | Universal constant |
| Pair-production threshold | $E_{\text{th}} = 2 m_e c^{2} = 1.022$ MeV | MeV | In nuclear Coulomb field |
| Critical β-energy (rad = ion) | $\epsilon_0 = 800 / Z$ MeV | MeV | Approximate; for $e^{-}$ in $Z$ |
| Alpha range in air (Geiger) | $R_{\text{air}} \approx 0.318 E^{3/2}$ cm, $E$ in MeV | cm | $4 \le E \le 7$ MeV |
| Energy transferred in elastic n-scatter | $E_R = E_n \cdot 4A(1-\cos\theta_{\text{CM}})/(1+A)^{2}$ | MeV | Max at $\theta_{\text{CM}} = 180°$ |
| Lethargy increment | $\xi = 1 + \alpha \ln \alpha / (1-\alpha)$, $\alpha = ((A-1)/(A+1))^{2}$ | Dimensionless | $A$ = atomic mass number |
| Cross-section (microscopic) | $\sigma$ in barns ($10^{-28}$ m$^{2}$) | barn | $\Sigma = N \sigma$ (macroscopic) |
| Stopping power (qualitative) | $-(dE/dx) \propto z^{2} Z / v^{2}$ | MeV/cm | Heavy charged particles |
| Bragg additivity rule | $\frac{1}{R_{\text{comp}}} = \sum_i \frac{w_i}{R_i}$ | g/cm$^{2}$ | For compound absorbers |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Spotlight:**
> - **Reactor shielding** uses $\mu$ (γ) and $\Sigma$ (n) to size biological shields.
> - **Personnel dosimetry** uses stopping power to convert fluence to dose: $D = \Phi \cdot (S/\rho)$, where $S/\rho$ is the *mass* stopping power.
> - **Detector design** (HPGe, NaI, BF$_3$, $^3$He tubes) exploits the specific γ- or n-interaction cross-section of the active medium.
> - **Fuel management** uses fission cross-sections $\sigma_f$ and resonance integrals to predict conversion ratios.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Compton Wavelength Shift (Quantum Energy–Momentum Balance)

We treat the photon and a *free, stationary* electron as a two-body relativistic system. Let $h\nu$ and $h\nu'$ be the photon energies before and after scattering; $p_\gamma = h\nu/c$ and $p_\gamma' = h\nu'/c$ their momenta; $\vec{p}_e$ the recoil electron momentum; $\theta$ the photon scattering angle.

**Step 1 — Conservation of energy:**

$$h\nu + m_e c^{2} = h\nu' + \sqrt{(p_e c)^{2} + (m_e c^{2})^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Conservation of momentum (vector form), squaring:**

$$(p_\gamma - p_\gamma' \cos\theta)^{2} + (p_\gamma' \sin\theta)^{2} = p_e^{2}$$

Expanding:

$$p_\gamma^{2} - 2 p_\gamma p_\gamma' \cos\theta + p_\gamma'^{2} \cos^{2}\theta + p_\gamma'^{2} \sin^{2}\theta = p_e^{2}$$

$$p_\gamma^{2} + p_\gamma'^{2} - 2 p_\gamma p_\gamma' \cos\theta = p_e^{2}$$

Substituting $p_\gamma = h\nu / c$ and $p_\gamma' = h\nu' / c$:

$$p_e^{2} c^{2} = (h\nu)^{2} + (h\nu')^{2} - 2 h\nu h\nu' \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Square the energy equation** and isolate $p_e^{2} c^{2}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
h\nu - h\nu' + m_e c^{2} &= \sqrt{(p_e c)^{2} + (m_e c^{2})^{2}} \\
(h\nu - h\nu')^{2} + 2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') + (m_e c^{2})^{2} &= p_e^{2} c^{2} + (m_e c^{2})^{2} \\
(h\nu - h\nu')^{2} + 2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') &= p_e^{2} c^{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Equate the two expressions for $p_e^{2} c^{2}$:**

$$(h\nu - h\nu')^{2} + 2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = (h\nu)^{2} + (h\nu')^{2} - 2 h\nu h\nu' \cos\theta$$

**Step 5 — Expand the left side and cancel common terms:**

$$(h\nu)^{2} - 2 h\nu h\nu' + (h\nu')^{2} + 2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = (h\nu)^{2} + (h\nu')^{2} - 2 h\nu h\nu' \cos\theta$$

Cancel $(h\nu)^{2}$ and $(h\nu')^{2}$ from both sides:

$$- 2 h\nu h\nu' + 2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = - 2 h\nu h\nu' \cos\theta$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange to isolate the frequency change:**

$$2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = 2 h\nu h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta) - 2 h\nu h\nu' + 2 h\nu h\nu'$$

Wait — re-arrange more carefully:

$$2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = 2 h\nu h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta) - 0$$

Wait — let me redo this step carefully.

After cancellation, the surviving terms are:

$$- 2 h\nu h\nu' + 2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = - 2 h\nu h\nu' \cos\theta$$

Bring the $-2 h\nu h\nu'$ to the right-hand side:

$$2 m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = 2 h\nu h\nu' - 2 h\nu h\nu' \cos\theta = 2 h\nu h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta)$$

**Step 7 — Solve for the frequency difference:**

$$h\nu - h\nu' = \frac{h\nu h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta)}{m_e c^{2}}$$

Divide both sides by $h\nu h\nu'$:

$$\frac{1}{h\nu'} - \frac{1}{h\nu} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{m_e c^{2}}$$

Replace $1/\nu = \lambda/c$:

$$\frac{c}{c \nu'} - \frac{c}{c \nu} = \frac{\lambda' - \lambda}{c} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{m_e c^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{1}$$

Wait, more carefully: $\frac{1}{h\nu'} - \frac{1}{h\nu} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{m_e c^{2}}$, and since $\lambda = c / \nu$ and $c = h\nu \lambda / h$... actually $1/\nu = \lambda/c$ so:

$$\frac{\lambda' - \lambda}{c} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{m_e c^{2}}$$

Multiplying both sides by $c$:

$$\lambda' - \lambda = \frac{h}{m_e c} (1 - \cos\theta)$$

This is the **Compton wavelength-shift formula**, with the universal constant $\lambda_c = h / (m_e c) = 2.4263 \times 10^{-12}$ m $\approx 2.43$ pm.

**$\blacksquare$ End of Derivation 1.**

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Compton Scattered Photon Energy

Starting from the wavelength-shift result, we now solve for $h\nu'$ explicitly. From:

$$\frac{1}{h\nu'} - \frac{1}{h\nu} = \frac{1 - \cos\theta}{m_e c^{2}}$$

Multiply by $h\nu h\nu'$ and by $m_e c^{2}$:

$$m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu') = h\nu h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta)$$

Let $\alpha = h\nu / m_e c^{2}$. Then:

$$h\nu h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta) = m_e c^{2} (h\nu - h\nu')$$

Divide by $h\nu$:

$$h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta) = m_e c^{2} \left( 1 - \frac{h\nu'}{h\nu} \right) = m_e c^{2} - m_e c^{2} \cdot \frac{h\nu'}{h\nu}$$

Rearranging:

$$h\nu' (1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{m_e c^{2}}{h\nu} h\nu' = m_e c^{2}$$

$$h\nu' \left[ 1 - \cos\theta + \frac{m_e c^{2}}{h\nu} \right] = m_e c^{2}$$

Let $\alpha = h\nu / (m_e c^{2})$, so $m_e c^{2} / h\nu = 1/\alpha$:

$$h\nu' \left[ 1 - \cos\theta + \frac{1}{\alpha} \right] = m_e c^{2}$$

$$h\nu' = \frac{m_e c^{2}}{1 - \cos\theta + 1/\alpha} = \frac{m_e c^{2} \alpha}{1 - \cos\theta + 1/\alpha} \cdot \frac{\alpha}{\alpha}$$

Multiply numerator and denominator by $\alpha$:

$$h\nu' = \frac{\alpha \, m_e c^{2}}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}$$

Since $\alpha \, m_e c^{2} = h\nu$:

$$\boxed{\;h\nu' = \frac{h\nu}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)} \;, \qquad \alpha = \frac{h\nu}{m_e c^{2}}\;}$$

**$\blacksquare$ End of Derivation 2.**

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Transferred to Recoil Electron in Compton Scattering

The recoil-electron kinetic energy is:

$$T_e = h\nu - h\nu' = h\nu - \frac{h\nu}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}$$

Common denominator:

$$T_e = h\nu \cdot \frac{[1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)] - 1}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)} = h\nu \cdot \frac{\alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}{1 + \alpha (1 - \cos\theta)}$$

Substituting $\alpha = h\nu / m_e c^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\;T_e = h\nu \cdot \frac{h\nu (1 - \cos\theta)}{m_e c^{2} + h\nu (1 - \cos\theta)}\;}$$

The **maximum** recoil energy occurs at $\theta = 180°$ (back-scatter), giving:

$$T_e^{\max} = h\nu \cdot \frac{2\alpha}{1 + 2\alpha}$$

**$\blacksquare$ End of Derivation 3.**

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Range of Charged Particles via Bragg-Kleeman Rule

For a compound (or mixture) absorber with mass-fractions $w_i$ of elements $i$, the *mass* range of a compound is given by the **Bragg–Kleeman additivity rule**:

$$\frac{1}{R_{\text{comp}}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{w_i}{R_i}$$

**Step 1 — Physical basis:** Stopping power is an *intensive* property of the stopping medium; for a homogeneous mixture, $1/R = \int dE / \int S_i(E) dE$ for a normalised mixture density.

**Step 2 — Derivation sketch:** Let $\rho$ be the density, $n_i$ the number density of element $i$, and $\Sigma_i$ the macroscopic stopping cross-section. The mass stopping power is $S/\rho$. For two elements A and B with mass fractions $w_A$ and $w_B$:

$$S_{\text{comp}} = w_A S_A + w_B S_B$$

The range:

$$R_{\text{comp}} = \int_0^{E_0} \frac{dE}{S_{\text{comp}}}$$

**Step 3 — Approximate form:** When $S_A$ and $S_B$ differ by less than a factor of ≈ 3 over the energy range, $1/R_{\text{comp}} \approx w_A/R_A + w_B/R_B$, yielding the Bragg–Kleeman rule.

This is widely used in **tissue-equivalent dosimetry** where $R_{\text{tissue}} \approx 0.59 R_{\text{O}_2} + \cdots$

**$\blacksquare$ End of Derivation 4.**

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Number of Collisions to Thermalise a Fast Neutron

In an infinite, homogeneous moderating medium, the *lethargy* $u$ is defined as:

$$u = \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E}\right)$$

The average *lethargy gain per collision* is the **lethargy increment** $\xi$:

$$\xi = \overline{\Delta u} = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln \alpha}{1 - \alpha}, \qquad \alpha = \left(\frac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^{2}$$

**Step 1 — Derive the average energy loss:** For isotropic centre-of-mass scattering, the average fractional energy loss per collision is:

$$\overline{\frac{\Delta E}{E}} = \frac{1 - \alpha}{2}$$

**Step 2 — Relate to lethargy:** Since $du = -dE / E$, the average lethargy gain per collision:

$$\xi = \overline{\Delta u} = -\overline{\ln(E'/E)} = -\ln\!\left(\frac{1+\alpha}{2}\right) = 1 + \frac{\alpha \ln\alpha}{1 - \alpha}$$

**Step 3 — Number of collisions to slow from $E_0$ to $E$:**

$$N = \frac{1}{\xi} \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E}\right) = \frac{u}{\xi}$$

**Step 4 — Worked check for hydrogen moderator ($A = 1$):** $\alpha = 0$, $\xi = 1$, $N = \ln(E_0/E)$. To slow a 2 MeV fission neutron to thermal ($0.025$ eV):

$$N = \ln\!\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{6}}{0.025}\right) = \ln(8 \times 10^{7}) \approx 18.2 \approx 19 \text{ collisions}$$

For $^{12}$C moderator ($A = 12$):

$$\alpha = \left(\frac{11}{13}\right)^{2} = 0.716, \quad \xi \approx 0.158, \quad N = \frac{18.2}{0.158} \approx 115 \text{ collisions}$$

This quantitative result explains why **hydrogen-rich moderators (water, polyethylene) are vastly superior** to graphite in compact thermal reactors.

**$\blacksquare$ End of Derivation 5.**

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Dominant Interaction Regime

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The examiner's favourite "fill-in-the-blank" or "match-the-following" question is to identify the dominant mechanism as a function of $\gamma$-ray energy and $Z$. The following table is the *canonical* answer key.

| Photon Energy $h\nu$ | Low-$Z$ Absorber ($Z \le 20$) | High-$Z$ Absorber ($Z \ge 50$) |
|:--------------------:|------------------------------|---------------------------------|
| $< 50$ keV | **Photoelectric** | **Photoelectric** (very strong, $Z^{4-5}$ dependence) |
| $50$ keV – $1$ MeV | **Compton** | **Compton** for $E > 0.2$ MeV; PE for $E < 0.1$ MeV |
| $1$–$5$ MeV | **Compton + Pair** | **Pair production** dominates; Compton for $E < 2$ MeV |
| $> 5$ MeV | **Pair** dominant | **Pair production** strongly dominant |

> [!NOTE]
> **Triple-point of $\sigma_P = \sigma_C = \kappa$:** Approximately at $E \approx 5$ MeV for water; ≈ 3 MeV for lead; ≈ 10 MeV for iron. KTU frequently asks for an *order-of-magnitude* statement.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

The following block diagram shows the engineering model for a *broad* γ-beam traversing a thick shield, where scattered photons contaminate the transmitted beam (a "build-up factor" correction is required beyond the simple Beer–Lambert law).

    P6 --> P7[TRANSMITTED INTENSITY I X EQUALS B I0 E POWER MINUS MU X]

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State the three principal modes of interaction of gamma rays with matter. For each, indicate the energy range and absorbing material where that mode is dominant.*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~120 words):**

The three principal γ-interaction mechanisms are:

**(i) Photoelectric Effect:** Dominant at *low γ-energies* ($< 0.1$ MeV) and in *high-$Z$* absorbers (e.g., lead, tungsten). The photon is completely absorbed by a *bound* atomic electron, which is ejected. Cross-section $\tau \propto Z^{4 \text{ to } 5} / E_\gamma^{3.5}$.

**(ii) Compton Scattering:** Dominant at *intermediate energies* ($0.1$ – $10$ MeV) and weakly $Z$-dependent. The photon scatters off a *free* electron, with wavelength shift $\Delta\lambda = (h/m_e c)(1 - \cos\theta)$.

**(iii) Pair Production:** Dominant at *high energies* ($> 1.022$ MeV), proportional to $Z^{2}$. The photon converts in the Coulomb field of a nucleus to an $e^{-}\text{–}e^{+}$ pair. Threshold: $2 m_e c^{2} = 1.022$ MeV.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – December 2023]**
*Differentiate between elastic and inelastic scattering of neutrons. Why is inelastic scattering insignificant in thermal reactors?*

**Model Answer (≈ 3 marks, ~120 words):**

In **elastic scattering (n, n)**, the neutron and target nucleus exchange kinetic energy *only*; total kinetic energy is conserved and the nucleus is *not* excited. It is the primary slowing-down mechanism in *thermal* reactors using moderators like water, heavy water, or graphite.

In **inelastic scattering (n, n')**, the neutron excites the target nucleus to a discrete higher energy level; the nucleus de-excites by emitting a γ-ray. Hence *some* kinetic energy is converted to nuclear excitation energy.

Inelastic scattering is insignificant in thermal reactors because it possesses a **threshold energy** $E_{\text{th}} = E^*(A+1)/A$ (typically $\ge 0.1$ MeV for most nuclei), and thermal neutrons ($0.025$ eV) are far below this threshold.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (BOTH Part-B questions):**
> The most common mark-loss occurs when students (a) confuse the *mass* attenuation coefficient $\mu/\rho$ with the *linear* $\mu$, (b) forget to include the *build-up factor* $B$ in a thick-shield design, and (c) write $R$ and $R_{\text{air}}$ without specifying units. The 2024 scheme explicitly tests unit awareness — always annotate *MeV, cm, g/cm², barn, m$^{-1}$* next to numerical answers.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a) Derive an expression for the range of a charged particle in a medium, starting from the stopping power $S = -dE/dx$. Comment on the difference in range–energy behaviour of alpha and beta particles.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) Discuss the three principal gamma-ray interaction mechanisms with matter, with neat energy-level diagrams. State the variation of each cross-section with photon energy and absorber atomic number.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Charged particles lose energy continuously as they traverse matter. The linear stopping power is $S = -dE/dx$. The total *range* $R$ is the path length where the particle's kinetic energy falls to zero:

$$R = \int_{0}^{R} dx = \int_{0}^{E_0} \left( -\frac{dE}{dx} \right)^{-1} dE$$

For a heavy charged particle (e.g., $\alpha$) the Bethe-Bloch formula gives:

$$-\frac{dE}{dx} = \frac{4 \pi k_0^{2} z^{2} e^{4}}{m_e v^{2}} N Z \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{2 m_e v^{2}}{I}\right) \right]$$

Since $v^{2} \propto E/M$ for a non-relativistic heavy particle, $-(dE/dx) \propto 1/E$, leading to:

$$R = \text{const} \cdot E_0^{3/2} \quad (\text{Geiger rule})$$

For alphas, the spectrum is *mono-energetic* and the path is *straight*; all alphas of the same energy travel nearly the *same* range — the famous **sharp Bragg peak** at the end of the path.

For betas, the *initial* spectrum is *continuous* (0 to $E_{\max}$) due to the three-body decay kinematics, and the path is *tortuous* due to multiple Coulomb scattering and bremsstrahlung. Therefore betas of the same $E_{\max}$ exhibit a *range-straggling* distribution. The empirical formula is:

$$R_\beta \approx 0.412 \, E_{\max}^{1.265 - 0.0954 \ln E_{\max}} \quad \text{(g/cm}^{2}\text{)}$$

— the exponent is non-integer, reflecting the *continuous spectrum* and *multiple scattering* effects.

**(b)** *(Refer to §2.3 of the formula sheet and the Analytical Matrix in §3.6 of these notes for the full answer.)*

Summary table to be reproduced by the student in the exam:

| Mechanism | Energy Range | Cross-section Behaviour |
|-----------|:------------:|-------------------------|
| Photoelectric | $< 0.1$ MeV (low) | $\tau \propto Z^{4-5} / E_\gamma^{3.5}$ |
| Compton | $0.1$ – $10$ MeV (intermediate) | $\sigma_C$ — Klein–Nishina; weak $Z$ dependence ($\propto Z$) |
| Pair production | $> 1.022$ MeV (high) | $\kappa \propto Z^{2}$, rises from threshold, peaks, then slowly decreases |

The energy-level diagrams (to be drawn by the student) show: in photoelectric, a bound electron is ejected; in Compton, a free electron recoils while the photon scatters; in pair production, the photon converts to $e^- e^+$ in the nuclear Coulomb field, and the positron subsequently annihilates.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – December 2023, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

**(a) Explain the different interaction modes of neutrons with matter. Discuss the role of moderators and the figure-of-merit for moderator selection.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A beam of 1.0 MeV gamma photons is incident on a lead shield of thickness 5 cm. The mass attenuation coefficient of lead at this energy is $\mu/\rho = 0.068\,\text{cm}^{2}\text{/g}$ and the density is $\rho = 11.34\,\text{g/cm}^{3}$. Calculate (i) the linear attenuation coefficient, (ii) the half-value layer, (iii) the transmitted intensity fraction, and (iv) the tenth-value layer.** *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *(Refer to §2.4 of these notes for the complete theoretical exposition.)*

Neutron interactions are categorised as:
1. **Elastic scattering (n, n)** — main moderating process in thermal reactors.
2. **Inelastic scattering (n, n')** — fast-neutron moderation on heavy nuclei (U, Fe).
3. **Radiative capture (n, $\gamma$)** — parasitic loss; e.g., $^1\text{H}(n,\gamma)^2\text{H}$.
4. **Charged-particle reactions (n, p), (n, $\alpha$)** — e.g., $^3\text{He}(n,p)^3\text{H}$ detectors.
5. **Fission (n, f)** — energy release in $^2\text{^{35}U}$, $^2\text{^{39}Pu}$, $^2\text{^{33}U}$.

The **moderator figure of merit** is the *moderating ratio*:

$$\text{MR} = \xi \cdot \frac{\Sigma_s}{\Sigma_a}$$

where $\Sigma_s$ and $\Sigma_a$ are the macroscopic scattering and absorption cross-sections. A *good* moderator has (a) high $\xi$ (low $A$, ideally H), (b) high $\Sigma_s$ (high number density), and (c) low $\Sigma_a$ (low neutron absorption). Water, heavy water, beryllium, and graphite are typical moderators; **light water** has $\xi = 1$ but absorbs strongly, **heavy water** has $\xi \approx 0.57$ but absorbs negligibly, **graphite** has $\xi = 0.158$ with very low absorption. CANDU reactors exploit heavy water; PWRs use light water; magnox and AGR use graphite.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given:
- $E_\gamma = 1.0$ MeV
- $x = 5$ cm
- $\mu/\rho = 0.068$ cm²/g
- $\rho = 11.34$ g/cm³

**(i) Linear attenuation coefficient:**

$$\mu = (\mu/\rho) \cdot \rho = 0.068 \times 11.34 = 0.7711\,\text{cm}^{-1}$$

**(ii) Half-value layer:**

$$x_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.7711} = 0.8988\,\text{cm} \approx 0.90\,\text{cm}$$

**(iii) Transmitted intensity fraction:**

$$\frac{I}{I_0} = e^{-\mu x} = e^{-0.7711 \times 5} = e^{-3.8556}$$

Computing $e^{-3.8556}$:

$$e^{-3.8556} \approx 0.0212$$

So $I \approx 2.12\,\%$ of $I_0$.

**Verification using HVL count:** Number of HVLs in 5 cm = $5 / 0.8988 = 5.563$. $I/I_0 = (1/2)^{5.563} = 2^{-5.563}$. $\log_2(2^{-5.563}) = -5.563 \Rightarrow 2^{-5.563} \approx 0.0212$. ✓

**(iv) Tenth-value layer:**

$$x_{1/10} = \frac{\ln 10}{\mu} = \frac{2.3026}{0.7711} = 2.987\,\text{cm} \approx 2.99\,\text{cm}$$

**Alternative cross-check:** $x_{1/10} \approx 3.32 \, x_{1/2} = 3.32 \times 0.8988 = 2.984$ cm. ✓

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|----------|-------|
| $\mu$ | $0.771\,\text{cm}^{-1}$ |
| HVL | $0.899\,\text{cm}$ |
| $I/I_0$ | $0.0212$ (i.e., $2.12\,\%$) |
| TVL | $2.99\,\text{cm}$ |

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 1 — Alpha, Beta, Gamma and Neutron Interactions: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Alpha particles** are doubly-charged, mono-energetic $^4$He nuclei. They lose energy almost entirely by *ionisation*; the path is straight; the *Bragg peak* occurs at the end of the range. Geiger rule: $R_{\text{air}} \approx 0.318 E^{3/2}$ cm.

- **Beta particles** are continuous-spectrum electrons/positrons. Energy loss has *collision* and *radiative (bremsstrahlung)* components. The *critical energy* $\epsilon_0 \approx 800/Z$ MeV separates the two regimes. Range is *empirical* (e.g., $0.412 E^{1.265-0.0954 \ln E}$ g/cm²). Path is *tortuous*; positrons ultimately annihilate to two $0.511$ MeV photons.

- **Gamma rays** are neutral, massless photons interacting by *three quantum processes*: photoelectric (low $E$, high $Z$), Compton (intermediate $E$, weak $Z$), pair production ($> 1.022$ MeV, $\propto Z^{2}$). The *Beer–Lambert law* $I = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ governs narrow-beam attenuation; the *build-up factor* $B$ corrects for scattered-photon contribution in thick shields.

- **Neutrons** are neutral, massive, and interact *only* with atomic nuclei. Modes: elastic (n, n), inelastic (n, n'), radiative capture (n, $\gamma$), fission (n, f), and charged-particle reactions. Thermal neutrons are slowed down via *elastic scattering*; the number of collisions to thermalise is $N = \ln(E_0/E)/\xi$, where $\xi = 1 + \alpha \ln \alpha / (1 - \alpha)$ and $\alpha = ((A-1)/(A+1))^{2}$.

- **Fundamental constants to commit to memory**: $c = 3 \times 10^{8}$ m/s; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; $m_e c^{2} = 0.511$ MeV; 1 barn $= 10^{-28}$ m²; 1 eV $= 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ J; Compton wavelength $\lambda_c = 2.426$ pm.

- **Threshold & critical energies**: Pair production threshold = $1.022$ MeV; critical energy for bremsstrahlung dominance = $800/Z$ MeV.

- **Numerical problem template**: Always quote *units* explicitly; distinguish *linear* ($\mu$, m$^{-1}$) from *mass* ($\mu/\rho$, m²/kg) attenuation coefficients; use the *Bragg–Kleeman rule* for compound absorbers.

- **Engineering corollaries**: Reactor shielding uses $\mu$ (γ) and $\Sigma$ (n); dosimetry uses stopping power; detector design exploits specific cross-sections (e.g., BF$_3$ for thermal neutrons); moderator selection maximises $\xi \Sigma_s / \Sigma_a$ (moderating ratio).

- **Common exam traps**: (i) confusing HVL with TVL; (ii) forgetting the build-up factor in wide-beam geometry; (iii) treating a $\beta$-range as a single well-defined value; (iv) writing $\lambda$ (mean free path) where $x_{1/2}$ (half-value layer) is asked; (v) mis-stating the pair-production threshold as $0.511$ MeV (it is *twice* the electron rest energy).

- **One-sentence essence of the Module**: *"Charged particles transfer energy continuously via Coulomb interaction; photons exchange it discretely via quantum absorption or scattering; neutrons exchange it via nuclear collisions — and the engineering consequence in every case is governed by a single exponential (or its integral).*"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Neutron Cross Section, Radioactive Decay Law

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Concept of Neutron Cross Section

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Neutron Cross Section** is a quantitative measure of the probability that an interaction (scattering or absorption) will occur between an incident neutron and a target nucleus. It can be expressed at two levels:

**Microscopic Cross Section ($\sigma$)**:
The effective "target area" presented by a *single* nucleus to an incident neutron. It has units of area, conventionally expressed in **barns**, where:

$$1 \text{ barn} = 10^{-24} \text{ cm}^2 = 10^{-28} \text{ m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** The microscopic cross section $\sigma$ is a property of a *specific nucleus-neutron interaction* and depends on the neutron energy (typically tabulated at thermal energy 0.0253 eV, or fast energy 1–10 MeV).

**Macroscopic Cross Section ($\Sigma$)**:
The effective "target area" per unit volume of the material, obtained by multiplying the microscopic cross section by the atomic number density $N$ (atoms/cm³):

$$\Sigma = N \cdot \sigma \quad [\text{units: cm}^{-1}]$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are throwing tennis balls blindly into a room full of hanging discs of various sizes. The probability that your ball hits a disc depends on:
- The **physical size of each disc** → Microscopic cross section $\sigma$
- The **number of discs per unit volume** → Atomic density $N$
- The **combined "blocking" effect of all discs together** → Macroscopic cross section $\Sigma$

A large $\sigma$ means the nucleus is a "big, easy-to-hit target." A small $\sigma$ means the neutron is likely to fly past without any interaction. This is why materials like **Boron-10** ($\sigma_a \approx 3840$ barns) are excellent neutron absorbers used in reactor control rods, while **Lead** ($\sigma_a \approx 0.16$ barns) is a poor absorber and used for shielding against gamma rays but not neutrons.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constant:** 1 barn = $10^{-24}$ cm², chosen because it is approximately equal to the geometric cross-sectional area of a typical atomic nucleus ($\sim 10^{-24}$ cm²).

## 1.2 Radioactive Decay Law

### Formal Definition

The **Radioactive Decay Law** states that the rate of disintegration of a radioactive substance is directly proportional to the number of radioactive nuclei present at that instant. Mathematically:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

where:
- $N$ = Number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$
- $\lambda$ = Decay constant [s⁻¹]
- The negative sign indicates a *decreasing* population

Integrating gives the canonical form:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a classroom of 100 students, each of whom has a 10% chance per minute of leaving the room (permanently). After 1 minute, ~90 students remain. After 2 minutes, ~81 remain. The probability is *per student* and *time-independent* — older students are no more or less likely to leave than new ones. The "memoryless" nature of radioactive decay means a nucleus that has survived 10 half-lives has no "memory" of its age — it behaves exactly like a freshly created nucleus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The decay law is *statistical* in nature. It applies accurately only to a *very large* number of atoms ($N \gg 1$). For a small number of atoms, the decay is genuinely random and follows Poisson statistics.
