# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deep Dive — Neutron Cross Section

### 2.1.1 Classification of Microscopic Cross Section

Every neutron interaction is classified into one of two main channels:

**1. Scattering Cross Section ($\sigma_s$)**: The neutron is deflected, but the nucleus does not change its identity fundamentally.
- **Elastic Scattering ($\sigma_{el}$):** Neutron kinetic energy is conserved in the center-of-mass frame. The neutron slows down. *Example: $^{1}H(n,n)^{1}H$*.
- **Inelastic Scattering ($\sigma_{in}$):** Neutron is absorbed into the nucleus forming a compound state, then re-emitted with lower energy. The target nucleus is left in an excited state. *Threshold reaction — requires fast neutrons.*

**2. Absorption Cross Section ($\sigma_a$)**: The neutron is captured and the nucleus is transmuted.
- **Radiative Capture ($\sigma_\gamma$):** Neutron + nucleus → excited compound nucleus → ground state + $\gamma$-ray. *Example: $^{238}U(n,\gamma)^{239}U$*.
- **Fission ($\sigma_f$):** Neutron + heavy nucleus → 2 fission fragments + 2–3 neutrons + ~200 MeV energy. *Example: $^{235}U(n,f)$*. This is the **heart of a nuclear reactor**.
- **Charged Particle Emission ($\sigma_\alpha$, $\sigma_p$):** Neutron + nucleus → another nucleus + charged particle. *Example: $^{10}B(n,\alpha)^{7}Li$* — used in neutron detectors.

The **total microscopic cross section** is the sum of all partial cross sections:

$$\sigma_t = \sigma_s + \sigma_a = \sigma_{el} + \sigma_{in} + \sigma_\gamma + \sigma_f + \ldots$$

### 2.1.2 Macroscopic Cross Section and Mean Free Path

The macroscopic cross section $\Sigma$ has a beautiful physical interpretation: **$\Sigma$ is the probability per unit path length that a neutron will undergo any interaction**.

The **Mean Free Path ($\lambda_{mfp}$)** is the average distance a neutron travels between successive interactions:

$$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{1}{\Sigma} \quad [\text{cm}]$$

The neutron flux $\phi$ (neutrons/cm²·s) multiplied by macroscopic cross section gives the **reaction rate** $R$:

$$R = \Sigma \phi = N \sigma_t \phi \quad \left[\frac{\text{reactions}}{\text{cm}^3 \cdot \text{s}}\right]$$

## 2.2 Deep Dive — Radioactive Decay Law

### 2.2.1 Key Derived Quantities

From the decay law $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$, several important engineering quantities are derived:

**Activity ($A$)**: The number of disintegrations per unit time.

$$A = \left|\frac{dN}{dt}\right| = \lambda N = \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$$

Units: **Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration/second; or **Curie (Ci)** = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq.

**Half-Life ($T_{1/2}$)**: The time required for half the nuclei to decay. Setting $N(T_{1/2}) = N_0/2$:

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

**Mean Life ($\tau$)**: The average lifetime of a radioactive nucleus before it decays.

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The mean life is always ~44% longer than the half-life. This is because the decay is exponential — many nuclei decay "early," pulling the mean up.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Physical Meaning |
|---|----------|---------|-------|------------------|
| 1 | Microscopic Cross Section | $\sigma$ | barn ($10^{-24}$ cm²) | Effective target area of 1 nucleus |
| 2 | Macroscopic Cross Section | $\Sigma = N \sigma$ | cm⁻¹ | Interaction probability per unit path length |
| 3 | Mean Free Path | $\lambda_{mfp} = 1/\Sigma$ | cm | Avg. distance between interactions |
| 4 | Reaction Rate | $R = \Sigma \phi$ | reactions/cm³·s | Interactions per unit volume per second |
| 5 | Decay Law (Nuclei) | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | nuclei | Undecayed nuclei at time $t$ |
| 6 | Activity | $A(t) = \lambda N(t)$ | Bq (or Ci) | Disintegrations per second |
| 7 | Half-Life | $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$ | seconds | Time for activity to halve |
| 8 | Mean Life | $\tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/0.693$ | seconds | Average lifetime of a nucleus |
| 9 | Total Cross Section | $\sigma_t = \sigma_s + \sigma_a$ | barn | Sum of all interaction channels |
| 10 | Decay Constant | $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$ | s⁻¹ | Probability of decay per nucleus per second |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility:** These formulas are the daily tools of a **reactor physicist** (computing fuel burnup, $k_{eff}$, criticality), a **health physicist** (computing dose rates from radioactive sources), and a **nuclear medical physicist** (calibrating radiotherapy isotopes like $^{60}Co$ and $^{137}Cs$).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Radioactive Decay Law

### Step 1: State the Fundamental Hypothesis

The decay law is rooted in the empirical observation that radioactive decay is a *first-order random process*. The probability that any single nucleus decays in a small time interval $dt$ is constant and equal to $\lambda \, dt$, independent of the nucleus's age.

### Step 2: Express the Change in Number of Nuclei

For $N$ nuclei, the expected number of decays in time $dt$ is $\lambda N \, dt$. Since these nuclei are removed from the population:

$$dN = -\lambda N \, dt$$

### Step 3: Separate Variables

Rearrange to put all $N$-terms on one side and all $t$-terms on the other:

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -\lambda \, dt$$

### Step 4: Integrate Both Sides

Integrate from $N_0$ at $t = 0$ to $N(t)$ at general time $t$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} &= -\lambda \int_{0}^{t} dt \\
\Big[\ln N\Big]_{N_0}^{N(t)} &= -\lambda \, t \\
\ln N(t) - \ln N_0 &= -\lambda \, t \\
\ln \left[\frac{N(t)}{N_0}\right] &= -\lambda \, t
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Exponentiate to Obtain the Decay Law

$$\frac{N(t)}{N_0} = e^{-\lambda t}$$

$$\boxed{N(t) = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}$$

### Step 6: Derive the Activity Equation

By definition, activity is the magnitude of the rate of change of $N$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A(t) &= \left|\frac{dN}{dt}\right| = \lambda N(t) \\
&= \lambda N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t} \\
A_0 &= \lambda N_0 \quad \text{(initial activity)} \\
\therefore \quad A(t) &= A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}
\end{aligned}$$

This shows activity decays with the *same exponential form* as the number of nuclei.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Half-Life Relation

### Step 1: Apply the Definition of Half-Life

By definition, after one half-life, $N(T_{1/2}) = N_0 / 2$. Substitute into the decay law:

$$\frac{N_0}{2} = N_0 \, e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

### Step 2: Simplify

$$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-\lambda T_{1/2}}$$

### Step 3: Take the Natural Logarithm

$$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

$$-\ln 2 = -\lambda T_{1/2}$$

### Step 4: Solve for Half-Life

$$\boxed{T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693147}{\lambda}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Mean Life

### Step 1: Set Up the Statistical Definition of Mean Life

The mean life $\tau$ is the average time a nucleus survives before decaying. Since each nucleus has a probability density $p(t)$ of decaying at time $t$ given by $p(t) \, dt = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$, the expectation value is:

$$\tau = \int_0^\infty t \, p(t) \, dt = \int_0^\infty t \cdot \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$$

### Step 2: Integrate by Parts

Let $u = t$, $dv = \lambda e^{-\lambda t} \, dt$, so $du = dt$, $v = -e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \Big[-t \, e^{-\lambda t}\Big]_0^\infty + \int_0^\infty e^{-\lambda t} \, dt \\
&= 0 + \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda} e^{-\lambda t}\right]_0^\infty \\
&= -\frac{1}{\lambda}(0 - 1) \\
\boxed{\tau &= \frac{1}{\lambda}}
\end{aligned}$$

Combining with half-life: $\tau = T_{1/2} / \ln 2 \approx 1.4427 \, T_{1/2}$.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Macroscopic Cross Section

### Step 1: Consider a Thin Slab of Material

Consider neutrons traveling in the $+x$ direction through a thin slab of thickness $dx$. Let $I(x)$ be the neutron current intensity (neutrons/cm²·s).

### Step 2: Probability of Interaction in Slab

Each nucleus presents an effective area $\sigma$ to the neutron. The total "blocking" area per unit volume is $N \sigma$. In a slab of area $A$ and thickness $dx$, the fraction of area "blocked" is:

$$f = \frac{(N \sigma)(A \, dx)}{A} = N \sigma \, dx = \Sigma \, dx$$

### Step 3: Write the Differential Loss Equation

The fraction of neutrons removed in $dx$ is $-\Sigma \, dx$. Thus:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -\Sigma \, dx$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$\begin{aligned}
\int_{I_0}^{I(x)} \frac{dI}{I} &= -\Sigma \int_0^x dx \\
I(x) &= I_0 \, e^{-\Sigma x}
\end{aligned}$$

### Step 5: Define Mean Free Path

The mean free path is the average distance traveled:

$$\lambda_{mfp} = \int_0^\infty x \, \Sigma e^{-\Sigma x} \, dx = \frac{1}{\Sigma}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{1}{\Sigma}}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Example (KTU Typical 7-mark Problem Style):**
> A radioactive source contains $N_0 = 1.0 \times 10^{12}$ atoms of an isotope with half-life $T_{1/2} = 10$ days. Calculate: (a) the decay constant, (b) the initial activity, (c) the activity after 25 days.

### Solution:

**Step (a): Decay Constant**

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{10 \text{ days}} = 0.0693 \text{ day}^{-1}$$

Convert to SI units: $\lambda = 0.0693 / 86400 = 8.02 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1}$

**Step (b): Initial Activity**

$$\begin{aligned}
A_0 &= \lambda N_0 = (8.02 \times 10^{-7} \text{ s}^{-1})(1.0 \times 10^{12}) \\
A_0 &= 8.02 \times 10^{5} \text{ Bq} \\
&= 0.802 \text{ MBq} \quad (\approx 21.7 \, \mu\text{Ci})
\end{aligned}$$

**Step (c): Activity after 25 days**

Number of half-lives elapsed: $n = 25 / 10 = 2.5$

Using $A = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
A(25) &= (8.02 \times 10^{5}) \cdot e^{-(0.0693)(25)} \\
&= (8.02 \times 10^{5}) \cdot e^{-1.7325} \\
&= (8.02 \times 10^{5}) \cdot 0.1769 \\
\boxed{A(25) &= 1.42 \times 10^{5} \text{ Bq}}
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalently, $A/A_0 = (1/2)^{2.5} = 0.1768$ — confirms the calculation.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Neutron Interaction Classification Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the logical decomposition of the neutron cross section into its interaction channels. It serves as the canonical hierarchy expected in KTU 14-mark answers on cross section concepts.

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN["Incident Neutron with Energy En"] --> INT["Approaches Target Nucleus"]
    INT --> TOT["Total Microscopic Cross Section sigma_t"]
    TOT --> SCS["Scattering Channel sigma_s"]
    TOT --> ABS["Absorption Channel sigma_a"]
    SCS --> SEL["Elastic Scattering sigma_el - Neutron Slows Down Nucleus Unchanged"]
    SCS --> SIN["Inelastic Scattering sigma_in - Nucleus Excited Threshold Reaction"]
    ABS --> SGAM["Radiative Capture sigma_gamma - Compound Nucleus Emits Gamma"]
    ABS --> SFIS["Fission sigma_f - Heavy Nucleus Splits Energy Released"]
    ABS --> SCP["Charged Particle Emission sigma_alpha sigma_p - Used in Detectors"]
    TOT --> NOTE["Key Reactor Materials U235 sigma_f approx 585 barns U238 sigma_f approx 0 b thermal B10 sigma_a approx 3840 barns Control Rod Material"]
```

## 4.2 Radioactive Decay Chain Schematic

The decay chain diagram is essential for understanding parent-daughter relationships in fuel cycles and decay heat calculations. Note the use of purely alphanumeric node identifiers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Parent Nucleus N1 lambda1"] -->|lambda1| P2["Daughter Nucleus N2 lambda2"]
    P2 -->|lambda2| P3["Granddaughter Nucleus N3 lambda3"]
    P3 -->|lambda3| P4["Stable End Product N4"]
    P1 -.->|may also be| P5["Branch Decay to N2 prime Alternative Daughter"]
    P2 -.->|may also be| P6["Branch Decay to N3 prime"]
    EQ1["Secular Equilibrium if lambda1 much less than lambda2 and parent abundant"]
    EQ2["Transient Equilibrium if lambda1 less than lambda2 but comparable"]
    EQ3["No Equilibrium if lambda1 greater than lambda2"]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Reaction Rate Computation Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Input Neutron Flux phi neutrons per cm2 per s"] --> B["Multiply by Atomic Density N atoms per cm3"]
    B --> C["Multiply by Microscopic Cross Section sigma cm2 per atom"]
    C --> D["Output Macroscopic Reaction Rate R reactions per cm3 per s"]
    E["Input Energy E of Neutron"] --> F["Look up sigma in Nuclear Data Library ENDF JEFF"]
    F --> C
    G["Input Number Density N"] --> H["Compute from mass density rho molar mass M and Avogadro NA as N equals rho NA divided by M"]
    H --> B
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Radioactive Decay Quantities Inter-relationships

```mermaid
graph TD
    N["Number of Nuclei N t"] --> A["Activity A equals lambda times N"]
    A --> HALF["Half Life T half equals 0.693 over lambda"]
    HALF --> MEAN["Mean Life tau equals 1 over lambda equals T half over 0.693"]
    MEAN --> A
    N --> DEC["Decay Law N t equals N0 exp negative lambda t"]
    DEC --> HALF
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the terms microscopic and macroscopic cross sections. What are their units?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Microscopic Cross Section ($\sigma$):** It is defined as the effective area presented by a *single* target nucleus to an incident neutron for a given nuclear reaction. (1 mark)

- **Unit:** Barn (b), where $1$ barn $= 10^{-24}$ cm² $= 10^{-28}$ m² (1 mark)

**Macroscopic Cross Section ($\Sigma$):** It is defined as the product of the microscopic cross section and the atomic number density $N$ of the target material. $\Sigma = N \sigma$. (1 mark)

- **Unit:** cm⁻¹ (or m⁻¹) (1 mark)

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
**State the radioactive decay law. Define decay constant and half-life.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The radioactive decay law states that *the rate of disintegration of a radioactive substance at any instant is directly proportional to the number of undecayed nuclei present at that instant*. (1 mark)

Mathematically: $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$, which on integration gives $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$.

**Decay Constant ($\lambda$):** The decay constant is defined as the probability per unit time that a single nucleus will undergo decay. It is constant for a given radioactive nuclide. Unit: s⁻¹. (1 mark)

**Half-Life ($T_{1/2}$):** The half-life is defined as the time required for the number of radioactive nuclei (or equivalently, the activity) to reduce to *half* of its initial value. It is related to the decay constant by $T_{1/2} = \dfrac{0.693}{\lambda}$. (1 mark)

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice Pattern — Pick Either Option)

### OPTION A: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) Derive the radioactive decay law starting from its fundamental hypothesis. Define decay constant, half-life, and mean life. Show their inter-relationships.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fundamental hypothesis (1 mark):**
Radioactive decay is a *random, first-order process*. Each nucleus has a fixed probability $\lambda$ per unit time of decaying, independent of its age. Thus in a small time $dt$, the fraction $\lambda \, dt$ of the existing $N$ nuclei decay.

**Step 2 — Set up differential equation (1 mark):**

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda N$$

**Step 3 — Solve by separation of variables (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{dN}{N} &= -\lambda \, dt \\
\int_{N_0}^{N(t)} \frac{dN}{N} &= -\lambda \int_0^t dt \\
\ln N(t) - \ln N_0 &= -\lambda t \\
\boxed{N(t) &= N_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Define decay constant (1 mark):** $\lambda$ is the disintegration rate per nucleus; unit s⁻¹. Also $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \dfrac{1}{\tau}$.

**Step 5 — Derive half-life relation (1 mark):**

$$T_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{\lambda} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$$

**Step 6 — Derive mean life (1 mark):**

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693} \approx 1.4427 \, T_{1/2}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) A 1.0 µg sample of $^{226}Ra$ (half-life = 1600 years; atomic mass = 226 u) is used as a radioactive source. Calculate the activity in Becquerels and Curies. Given: $1 \text{ u} = 1.66 \times 10^{-27}$ kg; $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹; 1 year = $3.156 \times 10^7$ s.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute the number of atoms $N_0$ (2 marks):**

Number of moles: $n = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ kg}}{226 \times 1.66 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}} = \dfrac{10^{-9}}{3.7516 \times 10^{-25}} = 2.666 \times 10^{15}$ mol

Wait — this is wrong. The above is in mol, but then we need to multiply by $N_A$. Let me redo:

Number of atoms: $N_0 = \dfrac{\text{mass in grams}}{\text{molar mass}} \times N_A$

Mass = $1.0 \times 10^{-6}$ g; Molar mass = 226 g/mol.

$$N_0 = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-6}}{226} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 2.664 \times 10^{15} \text{ atoms}$$

**Step 2 — Compute decay constant $\lambda$ (2 marks):**

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{1600 \times 3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s}} = \frac{0.693}{5.05 \times 10^{10}} = 1.372 \times 10^{-11} \text{ s}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute activity in Bq (2 marks):**

$$A_0 = \lambda N_0 = (1.372 \times 10^{-11})(2.664 \times 10^{15}) = 3.655 \times 10^4 \text{ Bq}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Curies (1 mark):**

$$A_0 = \frac{3.655 \times 10^4}{3.7 \times 10^{10}} = 9.88 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Ci} \approx 1.0 \, \mu\text{Ci}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Students *very commonly* lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert years to seconds** for $T_{1/2}$ before computing $\lambda$.
> 2. **Mixing up grams vs. kilograms** in the moles calculation.
> 3. **Using $1 \text{ Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq incorrectly** (use division, not multiplication).

---

### OPTION B: `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]` (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**(a) Explain the concept of neutron cross section. With a neat block diagram, classify the various types of neutron interactions and write the corresponding cross sections.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Concept (2 marks):**
The neutron cross section $\sigma$ is a measure of the probability that an interaction (such as scattering, absorption, or fission) occurs between an incident neutron and a target nucleus. Microscopically, it represents the *effective target area* presented by a single nucleus. The unit is the **barn** ($1$ b $= 10^{-24}$ cm²).

The total cross section is partitioned as $\sigma_t = \sigma_s + \sigma_a$, where $\sigma_s$ is the scattering cross section and $\sigma_a$ is the absorption cross section.

**Step 2 — Scattering channel (2 marks):**
- **Elastic Scattering ($\sigma_{el}$):** Neutron bounces off the nucleus with reduced kinetic energy; nucleus remains in its ground state. *Dominates for fast neutrons in light moderators like water and graphite.*
- **Inelastic Scattering ($\sigma_{in}$):** Neutron is captured into a compound nucleus and re-emitted with lower energy; the target nucleus is left in an excited state. *Threshold reaction — only for fast neutrons.*

**Step 3 — Absorption channel (2 marks):**
- **Radiative Capture ($\sigma_\gamma$):** $(n,\gamma)$ reaction; produces an isotope of mass $A+1$. *Example: $^{238}U(n,\gamma)^{239}U$ — important for fuel breeding.*
- **Fission ($\sigma_f$):** $(n,f)$ reaction; the heavy nucleus splits into two fragments, releasing ~200 MeV. *Example: $^{235}U(n,f)$.*
- **Charged Particle Emission ($\sigma_\alpha$, $\sigma_p$):** $(n,\alpha)$ or $(n,p)$ reaction; emits a charged particle. *Example: $^{10}B(n,\alpha)^{7}Li$ — basis of BF₃ neutron counters.*

**Step 4 — Block Diagram (1 mark):** The classification block diagram shown in Section 4.1 above should be drawn.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**(b) A beam of thermal neutrons ($\phi = 10^{12}$ neutrons/cm²·s) passes through a 1.0 cm thick cadmium foil ($\rho = 8.65$ g/cm³, $\sigma_a = 2450$ barns, $A = 112.4$ g/mol). Calculate: (i) the macroscopic absorption cross section, (ii) the mean free path, (iii) the fraction of neutrons absorbed, and (iv) the absorption reaction rate per cm³.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute atomic number density $N$ (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{\rho \, N_A}{A} = \frac{(8.65)(6.022 \times 10^{23})}{112.4} = 4.634 \times 10^{22} \text{ atoms/cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Macroscopic absorption cross section (2 marks):**

$$\Sigma_a = N \sigma_a = (4.634 \times 10^{22})(2450 \times 10^{-24}) = 1.135 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Mean free path (1 mark):**

$$\lambda_{mfp} = \frac{1}{\Sigma_a} = \frac{1}{1.135} = 0.881 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Fraction of neutrons absorbed in 1 cm foil (1 mark):**

$$f = 1 - e^{-\Sigma_a x} = 1 - e^{-1.135 \times 1.0} = 1 - 0.321 = 0.679$$

So about **67.9%** of incident neutrons are absorbed in 1 cm of cadmium.

**Step 5 — Absorption reaction rate (1 mark):**

$$R_a = \Sigma_a \phi = (1.135)(10^{12}) = 1.135 \times 10^{12} \text{ reactions/cm}^3 \cdot \text{s}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part b):** Common errors include:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert barns to cm²** (1 barn = $10^{-24}$ cm² — multiply, not divide).
> 2. **Confusing $\Sigma_a$ with $\Sigma_t$**: the *total* cross section would also include $\Sigma_s$, but for thermal neutrons in cadmium, $\sigma_a \gg \sigma_s$, so $\Sigma_t \approx \Sigma_a$. Always specify which one you are computing.
> 3. **Using $e^{-\Sigma x}$ without the leading 1 —** the fraction absorbed is $(1 - e^{-\Sigma x})$, not $e^{-\Sigma x}$ (which is the fraction *transmitted*).

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1 Topic: Neutron Cross Section & Radioactive Decay Law**

- **Microscopic cross section $\sigma$** is per nucleus; **macroscopic $\Sigma = N\sigma$** is per unit volume. Always check units: barns → cm²; $\Sigma$ in cm⁻¹.
- **1 barn = $10^{-24}$ cm²** — never forget this conversion in numerical problems.
- **Total cross section** is the sum: $\sigma_t = \sigma_s + \sigma_a$ (scattering + absorption).
- **Mean free path** $\lambda_{mfp} = 1/\Sigma$ — the *average* distance traveled between interactions, NOT the *maximum*.
- **Reaction rate** $R = \Sigma \phi$ — this is the cornerstone of reactor physics; dimensions must be carefully tracked.
- **Decay law $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$** is the workhorse equation; learn to derive it and use it confidently.
- **Activity** $A = \lambda N$ and $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ — both nuclei and activity decay with the *same* exponential.
- **Half-life formula** $T_{1/2} = 0.693/\lambda$ — the constant **0.693 is $\ln 2$**, never memorize as just a number.
- **Mean life** $\tau = 1/\lambda \approx 1.44 \, T_{1/2}$ — the average nucleus lives longer than the half-life because the decay distribution has a long tail.
- **Units of activity**: 1 Bq = 1 disintegration/s; 1 Ci = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq. To convert Ci → Bq, **multiply**; Bq → Ci, **divide**.
- **Cross sections depend strongly on neutron energy** — thermal ($\sim 0.025$ eV) vs. fast ($\sim 1$ MeV) values are *very* different. Always state the energy regime in your answers.
- **The decay law is statistical** — for very small numbers of atoms ($N < 100$), random fluctuations dominate; the law applies only in the limit of large $N$.
- **KTU 2024 exam tip**: When a 14-mark question asks about "neutron cross section," always start with the *definition*, give the *unit*, *classify* the interactions (with examples), and present the *relationship* $\Sigma = N\sigma$. This is the expected structure for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Units of Radioactivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Units of Radioactivity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Radioactivity** is the spontaneous, stochastic process by which unstable atomic nuclei (radionuclides) disintegrate, emitting ionizing particles (such as alpha particles, beta particles, neutrons) and/or electromagnetic radiation (gamma rays, X-rays) to attain a more stable nuclear configuration. The **Activity ($A$)** of a radioactive sample is defined as the number of nuclear disintegrations occurring per unit time in that sample. Mathematically, it is the absolute value of the rate of change of the number of radioactive nuclei $N$ with respect to time $t$. $$A = \left\vert \frac{dN}{dt} \right\vert = \lambda N$$ where $\lambda$ is the **decay constant** (with units of s$^{-1}$) and $N$ is the number of undecayed nuclei at time $t$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Activity is NOT the total number of atoms, nor is it the total radiation emitted.** It is specifically the *rate* of nuclear transformations. A sample with a large number of long-lived atoms may have low activity, while a tiny sample of short-lived atoms can have enormous activity. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine a large auditorium filled with sandbags, each tied to a 1-second timer. The timers are all set to go off at random intervals. The **"Activity"** is the number of sandbags dropping per second — not the total number of sandbags in the room. - If the room is huge but the timers are set to 1 hour, the activity (drops per second) is tiny. - If the room is small but the timers are set to 1 millisecond, the activity is enormous. In the same way, **a gram of Uranium-238 is barely active, but a microgram of Polonium-210 is ferociously radioactive.** ### 1.3 Why Do We Need Standardized Units? Different radioactive isotopes decay at vastly different rates. To compare sources, regulate safety, and dose patients/workers, we need universally accepted units. The principal units covered in this module are: | Unit | System | Magnitude (approx.) | |---|---|---| | **Becquerel (Bq)** | SI Unit | 1 disintegration/s | | **Curie (Ci)** | Traditional / Conventional | 3.7 $\times$ 10$^{10}$ dps | | **Rutherford (Rd)** | Historical / Intermediate | 10$^{6}$ dps | > [!NOTE] > All three units measure the *same physical quantity* — the rate of nuclear transformations — but they differ in scale and historical origin. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to fluently inter-convert these units and explain the physical basis of each. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Becquerel (Bq) — The SI Unit - **Adopted:** 1975 by the International Committee for Weights and Measures (CIPM); named in honour of **Henri Becquerel**, the discoverer of radioactivity (1896). - **Definition:** One becquerel corresponds to exactly **one nuclear transformation (disintegration) per second**. $$1 \, \text{Bq} = 1 \, \text{dps} = 1 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$ - **Why it is small:** A typical banana has roughly 15 Bq of activity (from $^{40}$K). A human body has internal activity on the order of **$\sim$ 4,000 – 8,000 Bq** purely from naturally occurring $^{40}$K, $^{14}$C, and $^{226}$Ra. - **Multiples used in practice:** kBq, MBq, GBq, TBq. - **Advantages:** Coherent with SI; non-redundant; simple. - **Disadvantage:** Numerically small for laboratory and medical sources, so prefixes are mandatory. ### 2.2 The Curie (Ci) — The Traditional Unit - **Introduced:** 1910, at a radiologists' congress in Brussels; named in honour of **Marie Skłodowska-Curie**, the pioneer of radiochemistry. - **Original Definition:** The activity of **1 gram of pure Radium-226** in secular equilibrium with its short-lived daughter products (notably $^{222}$Rn and subsequent decay products down to $^{214}$Po). - **Modern Equivalence:** $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ disintegrations per second. This exact value was chosen because the experimentally measured activity of 1 g of $^{226}$Ra was found to be $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ dps. - **Why it is large:** Useful in nuclear medicine, industrial radiography, and reactor physics where activities are typically huge. - **Subdivisions:** mCi ($\mu$Ci historically), kCi. - **Safety Note:** $1 \, \text{Ci}$ is an enormous amount of radiation. Direct handling of a 1-Ci source can be lethal in minutes. The Curie has been officially deprecated by SI but is still used in the United States and in some medical contexts. ### 2.3 The Rutherford (Rd) - **Introduced:** Proposed as a practical intermediate unit. - **Definition:** $1 \, \text{Rd} = 10^6$ disintegrations per second $= 1$ MBq. - **Use:** Seldom used today; appears in older nuclear physics textbooks. The KTU 2024 syllabus includes it for completeness and unit-conversion practice. ### 2.4 The "Specific Activity" Beyond total activity, engineers often need **specific activity** ($a$), i.e., activity per unit mass: $$a = \frac{A}{m} = \frac{\lambda N_{A}}{M}$$ where $N_{A}$ is Avogadro's number, $M$ is the molar mass (g/mol), and $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ with $T_{1/2}$ being the half-life. ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Quantity / Relation | Equation | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Activity (definition) | $A = \lambda N$ | dps, Bq, Ci | | 2 | Activity (differential) | $A = \left\vert dN/dt \right\vert$ | s$^{-1}$ | | 3 | Decay law | $N(t) = N_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless | | 4 | Activity vs. time | $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Bq | | 5 | Decay constant vs. half-life | $\lambda = \frac{0.693}{T_{1/2}}$ | s$^{-1}$ | | 6 | Mean life | $\tau = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{T_{1/2}}{0.693}$ | s | | 7 | Specific activity | $a = \frac{\lambda N_{A}}{M}$ | Bq/g | | 8 | 1 Curie in Becquerel | $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$ | — | | 9 | 1 Rutherford in Becquerel | $1 \, \text{Rd} = 10^{6} \, \text{Bq}$ | — | | 10 | 1 Bq in Curie | $1 \, \text{Bq} = 2.703 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{Ci}$ | — | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Nuclear Reactor Operations:** Reactor start-up sources are rated in Curies; online fuel monitoring uses Becquerels per gram. - **Medical Imaging & Therapy:** A $^{99m}$Tc diagnostic dose is $\sim 740$ MBq ($\sim 20$ mCi); a $^{60}$Co therapy source is thousands of Curies. - **Industrial Gauging:** Beta and gamma thickness gauges are rated in GBq. - **Radiation Protection / Regulatory Compliance:** ICRP, AERB (India), and NRC (USA) limits are stated in Bq for environmental releases and in Curies for legacy licensed sources. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Why 1 Ci = 3.7 $\times$ 10$^{10}$ Bq **Given:** The historically defined activity of **1 gram of pure $^{226}$Ra** in equilibrium with its daughters. **Step 1 — Number of atoms in 1 g of $^{226}$Ra:** The molar mass of $^{226}$Ra is 226 g/mol. By definition of the mole: $$N = \frac{m \, N_{A}}{M} = \frac{1 \, \text{g} \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} \, \text{atoms/mol}}{226 \, \text{g/mol}}$$ $$N = \frac{6.022 \times 10^{23}}{226} \, \text{atoms}$$ $$N = 2.665 \times 10^{21} \, \text{atoms}$$ **Step 2 — Decay constant of $^{226}$Ra:** The accepted half-life of $^{226}$Ra is $T_{1/2} = 1600 \, \text{years}$. Converting to seconds: $$T_{1/2} = 1600 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 \, \text{s}$$ $$T_{1/2} = 1.601 \times 10^{3} \times 3.156 \times 10^{7} \, \text{s}$$ $$T_{1/2} = 5.05 \times 10^{10} \, \text{s}$$ **Step 3 — Calculate $\lambda$:** $$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{5.05 \times 10^{10} \, \text{s}}$$ $$\lambda = 1.372 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{s}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Activity of 1 g of $^{226}$Ra:** $$A = \lambda N = (1.372 \times 10^{-11}) \times (2.665 \times 10^{21})$$ $$A = 3.657 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps}$$ **Step 5 — Round to standard value:** $$A \approx 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps} \equiv 1 \, \text{Ci}$$ $$\boxed{1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps}}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical: Conversion Practice **Problem:** A radiopharmaceutical shipment is labelled **"Activity = 250 mCi"**. Convert it to (a) Bq, (b) MBq, (c) Rutherford, and (d) disintegrations per minute (dpm). **Step (a) — Ci to Bq:** $$A = 250 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{Ci} \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq/Ci}$$ $$A = 9.25 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Bq}$$ **Step (b) — Bq to MBq:** $$A = \frac{9.25 \times 10^{9}}{10^{6}} \, \text{MBq} = 9.25 \times 10^{3} \, \text{MBq} = 9250 \, \text{MBq}$$ **Step (c) — Bq to Rutherford:** $$A = \frac{9.25 \times 10^{9}}{10^{6}} \, \text{Rd} = 9.25 \times 10^{3} \, \text{Rd} = 9250 \, \text{Rd}$$ **Step (d) — Bq to dpm:** $$A = 9.25 \times 10^{9} \, \text{dps} \times 60 \, \text{s/min}$$ $$\boxed{A = 5.55 \times 10^{11} \, \text{dpm}}$$ ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Quick-Reference Conversion Table | From $\downarrow$ / To $\rightarrow$ | **Bq** | **Ci** | **Rd** | **dpm** | |---|---|---|---|---| | **1 Bq** | 1 | $2.703 \times 10^{-11}$ | $10^{-6}$ | 60 | | **1 Ci** | $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ | 1 | $3.7 \times 10^{4}$ | $2.22 \times 10^{12}$ | | **1 Rd** | $10^{6}$ | $2.703 \times 10^{-5}$ | 1 | $6 \times 10^{7}$ | | **1 dpm** | $1.667 \times 10^{-2}$ | $4.504 \times 10^{-13}$ | $1.667 \times 10^{-8}$ | 1 | ### 3.4 Sanity Check via Specific Activity For a pure radionuclide with half-life $T_{1/2}$ (in seconds) and atomic mass $A_m$: $$a = \frac{4.17 \times 10^{23}}{A_m \cdot T_{1/2}} \, \text{Bq/g}$$ For $^{60}$Co ($T_{1/2} = 5.27 \, \text{yr} = 1.66 \times 10^{8} \, \text{s}$, $A_m = 60$): $$a = \frac{4.17 \times 10^{23}}{60 \times 1.66 \times 10^{8}} = 4.19 \times 10^{13} \, \text{Bq/g} = 1132 \, \text{Ci/g}$$ This matches published values ($\sim$1100 Ci/g for cobalt-60) and validates the unit system. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchy of Radioactivity Units The following block diagram maps the conceptual hierarchy, the conversion factors, and the historical basis of each unit. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Radioactivity Activity A] --> B[Physical Meaning] A --> C[Standard Units] A --> D[Historical Basis] B --> B1[Rate of nuclear disintegrations
dN/dt magnitude = lambda times N] C --> C1[SI Unit Becquerel Bq] C --> C2[Traditional Unit Curie Ci] C --> C3[Intermediate Unit Rutherford Rd] D --> D1[Bq honors Henri Becquerel 1896] D --> D2[Ci honors Marie Curie 1910] D --> D3[Rd defined 1946 obsolete now] C1 --> E1[1 Bq = 1 dps] C2 --> E2[1 Ci = 3.7 times 10 to 10 dps
equals activity of 1 g Ra-226] C3 --> E3[1 Rd = 10 to 6 dps equals 1 MBq] E1 --> F[Conversion Cross-Link] E2 --> F E3 --> F F --> F1[1 Ci = 3.7 times 10 to 10 Bq] F --> F2[1 Rd = 10 to 6 Bq = 1 MBq] F --> F3[1 Bq = 2.703 times 10 to -11 Ci] F --> F4[1 Bq = 60 dpm] A --> G[Related Quantities] G --> G1[Half Life T half] G --> G2[Decay Constant lambda] G --> G3[Mean Life tau = 1 by lambda] G --> G4[Specific Activity a Bq per g] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: How Activity Is Measured ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA[Radioactive Source] -->|emits ionising radiation| nodeB[Detector System] nodeB -->|electrical pulses| nodeC[Pulse Processing Unit] nodeC -->|counts per second| nodeD[Activity Calculator] nodeD -->|output in Bq or Ci| nodeE[Display and Logger] nodeD -->|applies decay correction| nodeF[Time Reference Clock] nodeE --> nodeG[User Interface and Safety Alarm] ``` - **Radioactive Source** — emits alpha, beta, or gamma radiation. - **Detector System** — GM tube, scintillator, or semiconductor detector; each pulse = one detected disintegration (with efficiency $\eta$). - **Pulse Processing Unit** — amplifies and discriminates noise. - **Activity Calculator** — divides true counts by efficiency $\eta$ and (optionally) corrects for decay since calibration. - **Time Reference Clock** — supplies live $t$ for $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t}$. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]` **Define the SI unit of radioactivity. Why was it named "Becquerel"?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The SI unit of radioactivity is the **Becquerel (Bq)**. - **Definition (1 mark):** $1 \, \text{Bq} = 1$ nuclear disintegration per second (i.e., 1 dps). - **Naming (1 mark):** It was adopted by the 15th General Conference on Weights and Measures in 1975 and named in honour of **Antoine Henri Becquerel** (1852–1908), the French physicist who discovered natural radioactivity in 1896 while investigating uranium salts. - **Distinction (1 mark):** It replaced the older Curie and is coherent with SI (unit s$^{-1}$), making it easier to use in scientific and regulatory work. --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]` **State the relationship between the Curie and the Becquerel. What is the historical basis of the Curie?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Relation (1 mark):** $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps}$. - **Historical basis (1 mark):** The Curie was defined in 1910 as the activity of **1 gram of pure Radium-226** in secular equilibrium with its daughter products. - **Origin of the value (1 mark):** Because 1 g of $^{226}$Ra was experimentally measured to undergo $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ disintegrations per second, this number was fixed as the exact value of the Curie. It is named after **Marie Curie**. --- ### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks) > **KTU 2024 Rule:** Module-level choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]` **(a)** Define the following units of radioactivity, with one engineering use of each: (i) Becquerel, (ii) Curie, (iii) Rutherford. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A Co-60 source used in a radiotherapy unit has an activity of 5000 Ci on a calibration date. Given the half-life of Co-60 is 5.27 years, find its activity in Bq after 2 years. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Definitions and uses (7 marks):** | Unit | Definition (2 marks each) | One Engineering Use (≈0.33 mark each) | |---|---|---| | Becquerel (Bq) | 1 Bq = 1 disintegration per second (SI unit, 1975) | Environmental monitoring — release limits in Bq/m$^3$ | | Curie (Ci) | 1 Ci = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ dps = activity of 1 g of Ra-226 (1910) | Radiotherapy source rating (e.g., 5000 Ci Co-60 teletherapy) | | Rutherford (Rd) | 1 Rd = $10^6$ dps = 1 MBq (proposed 1946) | Older nuclear physics literature; tracer-lab studies | *(Marks: 3 × 2 = 6 for definitions + 1 mark for the three uses bundled.)* **(b) Activity after 2 years (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Write the decay law in activity form:** $$A(t) = A_0 \, e^{-\lambda t}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the decay constant $\lambda$:** $$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{5.27 \, \text{yr}}$$ $$\lambda = 0.1315 \, \text{yr}^{-1}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the exponential factor for $t = 2$ yr:** $$e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-0.1315 \times 2} = e^{-0.2630}$$ $$e^{-0.2630} = 0.7687$$ **Step 4 — Compute remaining activity in Curies:** $$A(2) = 5000 \times 0.7687 = 3843.5 \, \text{Ci}$$ **Step 5 — Convert to Becquerels (1 Ci = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq):** $$A(2) = 3843.5 \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$$ $$\boxed{A(2) \approx 1.422 \times 10^{14} \, \text{Bq} = 142.2 \, \text{TBq}}$$ *(Alternative full marks via half-life formula $A = A_0 (1/2)^{t/T_{1/2}}$ gives the same answer: $5000 \times 2^{-2/5.27} = 5000 \times 0.7687 = 3843.5$ Ci.)* --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the exact equivalence between 1 Curie and 1 Becquerel starting from the definition of 1 Ci as the activity of 1 g of $^{226}$Ra. (Take $T_{1/2}$ of $^{226}$Ra = 1600 years, $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol$^{-1}$.) **(7 marks)** **(b)** A hospital receives 15 mCi of I-131 (used in thyroid therapy). Express this activity in (i) Bq, (ii) MBq, (iii) disintegrations per minute. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Number of $^{226}$Ra atoms in 1 g:** $$N = \frac{m \, N_A}{M} = \frac{1 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23}}{226}$$ $$N = 2.665 \times 10^{21} \, \text{atoms} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 2 — Convert $T_{1/2}$ to seconds:** $$T_{1/2} = 1600 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 5.05 \times 10^{10} \, \text{s} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 3 — Decay constant $\lambda$:** $$\lambda = \frac{0.693}{5.05 \times 10^{10}} = 1.372 \times 10^{-11} \, \text{s}^{-1} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **Step 4 — Activity in dps:** $$A = \lambda N = (1.372 \times 10^{-11})(2.665 \times 10^{21}) = 3.66 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dps} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Step 5 — Define 1 Ci and conclude:** $$1 \, \text{Ci} \equiv 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq (by definition, rounding experimental value)} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ $$\boxed{1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}}$$ **(b) Conversions (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Convert mCi to Ci:** $$A = 15 \, \text{mCi} = 0.015 \, \text{Ci}$$ **Step 2 — To Bq (i):** $$A = 0.015 \times 3.7 \times 10^{10} = 5.55 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Bq} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}$$ **Step 3 — To MBq (ii):** $$A = \frac{5.55 \times 10^{8}}{10^{6}} = 555 \, \text{MBq} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **Step 4 — To dpm (iii):** $$A = 5.55 \times 10^{8} \, \text{dps} \times 60 = 3.33 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dpm} \quad \text{(2.5 marks)}$$ $$\boxed{A = 5.55 \times 10^{8} \, \text{Bq} = 555 \, \text{MBq} = 3.33 \times 10^{10} \, \text{dpm}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Unit confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Students frequently write "1 Ci = 3.7 $\times$ 10$^{10}$ dps" but forget to explicitly state that **dps = Bq**. Always equate 1 dps with 1 Bq. > 2. **Time-unit errors in $\lambda$ (–2 marks):** When computing $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}$, **$T_{1/2}$ must be in the same time unit as $t$**. Mixing years and seconds costs full marks. > 3. **Prefix slips (–1 mark):** mCi vs. $\mu$Ci; MBq vs. mBq. Memorize: 1 mCi = 10$^{-3}$ Ci; 1 $\mu$Ci = 10$^{-6}$ Ci. > 4. **Curie sign error (–1 mark):** Forgetting that activity **decays** with time, students sometimes add $\lambda t$ in the exponent instead of subtracting. > 5. **Missing equilibrium clause:** When asked the *basis* of the Curie, full credit requires mentioning **secular equilibrium with daughter products** of $^{226}$Ra. ### 5.1 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Activity = rate of decay**, $A = \vert dN/dt \vert = \lambda N$. Activity is **not** the same as the number of radioactive atoms. - **SI unit = Becquerel (Bq)** = 1 disintegration per second. Named after Henri Becquerel (1896 discovery). - **Traditional unit = Curie (Ci)** = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq = activity of 1 g of $^{226}$Ra (with daughters in secular equilibrium). Named after Marie Curie. - **Rutherford (Rd)** = $10^6$ Bq = 1 MBq. Rarely used today, but conversion practice is mandatory for KTU. - **Decay law in activity form:** $A(t) = A_0 e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 \left( \tfrac{1}{2} \right)^{t / T_{1/2}}$. - **Decay constant vs. half-life:** $\lambda = \ln 2 / T_{1/2} = 0.693 / T_{1/2}$. Always keep time units consistent. - **Mean life:** $\tau = 1/\lambda = T_{1/2}/0.693$. - **Specific activity:** $a = \lambda N_A / M$ (Bq/g). High specific activity → short half-life and/or light nucleus. - **Engineering rule of thumb:** Reactor sources are in **Curies**; medical imaging doses are in **MBq**; environmental releases are reported in **Bq/m$^3$ or Bq/L**. - **Quick conversion cheats to memorize:** - $1 \, \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10} \, \text{Bq}$ - $1 \, \text{mCi} = 37 \, \text{MBq}$ - $1 \, \text{MBq} = 1 \, \text{Rd}$ - $1 \, \text{Bq} = 60 \, \text{dpm}$

topics.title: Fission - Fission Energy, Critical Mass, Gabon Natural Reactor, Liquid Drop Model of Fission Cross Section, Prompt and Delayed Neutrons, Neutron Life Cycle, Infinite Multiplication Factor and 4 Factor Formula and 6 Factor Formula
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fission - Fission Energy, Critical Mass, Gabon Natural Reactor, Liquid Drop Model of Fission Cross Section, Prompt and Delayed Neutrons, Neutron Life Cycle, Infinite Multiplication Factor and 4 Factor Formula and 6 Factor Formula

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Fission Fundamentals

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Nuclear Fission — Formal Definition

**Nuclear Fission** is a nuclear reaction in which the nucleus of a heavy atom (typically A > 200, such as Uranium-235, Plutonium-239) splits into two or more lighter nuclei (called **fission fragments**), accompanied by the emission of **2 to 3 free neutrons**, **gamma photons**, and a **large release of binding energy (~200 MeV per event)**. The process is initiated when the heavy nucleus absorbs an incident neutron (thermal or fast), forming an unstable compound nucleus that subsequently deforms and scissions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Fission is the *foundational reaction* of every nuclear fission reactor and every fission-based weapon. Every parameter in this module (critical mass, neutron lifetime, k∞, k_eff) directly answers one question: *Will the chain reaction sustain itself, die out, or explode?*

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino Chain"

Imagine a row of standing dominoes. Each fission event is one domino falling; the falling domino represents the ~200 MeV of energy released. The fragments that fly off (the "neutrons") are like tiny balls ejected from the first domino that knock over neighbouring dominoes. **If every neutron knocks over exactly one new nucleus → critical.** If it knocks over more than one → supercritical (runaway). If it knocks over fewer than one → subcritical (reaction dies). The "Critical Mass" is simply the smallest number of dominoes you need in a tight cluster so that the ejected balls cannot escape without hitting another domino.

### Fission Energy — The Mass–Defect Bank

When a U-235 nucleus absorbs a neutron and splits, the **total rest mass of the products is less than the total rest mass of the reactants.** The "missing" mass has been converted directly into energy per Einstein's relation $E = \Delta m \, c^2$.

A representative fission reaction (one of ~30 possible pairs):

$$^{235}_{92}\text{U} \; + \; ^{1}_{0}\text{n} \;\longrightarrow\; ^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} \; + \; ^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} \; + \; 3\,^{1}_{0}\text{n} \; + \; Q$$

The energy release Q (typically **~200 MeV** ≈ 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J per fission) is distributed as:

| Partition | Energy (MeV) | Remarks |
|---|---|---|
| Kinetic Energy of Fission Fragments | **~165 MeV** | Main recoverable thermal energy |
| Kinetic Energy of Prompt Neutrons | **~5 MeV** | Recoverable via moderation |
| Instantaneous Gamma Rays | **~5–10 MeV** | Recoverable in shielding/heavy atoms |
| Beta Particles from Fragments | **~5–8 MeV** | Recoverable after decay delay |
| Antineutrinos ($\bar{\nu}$) | **~10–12 MeV** | **NOT recoverable** (weak interaction, escapes) |
| Delayed Gamma Rays | **~5–10 MeV** | Recoverable |

> [!NOTE]
> Only **~185 MeV (~92.5%)** is practically recoverable as heat. The ~12 MeV carried by neutrinos is permanently lost. This sets the **practical upper bound on fission reactor thermal efficiency.**

### Critical Mass — Definition

**Critical Mass** is the **minimum mass of a fissile material** that must be assembled in a given configuration such that the rate of neutron production exactly balances the rate of neutron loss (leakage + non-fission absorption), thereby sustaining a **steady-state self-sustaining chain reaction** (k_eff = 1).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Leaky Bucket

Think of neutrons as water pouring into a leaky bucket at a rate F (fission). The bucket leaks water at rate L (leakage + parasitic absorption). If F = L, the water level stays constant — that is the **critical** condition. The bucket's size represents the **mass**. A small bucket loses too much water from the *surface area* (geometry effect!) before it can fission. Add more mass and the surface-to-volume ratio improves. The smallest mass where F = L is the critical mass.

### Gabon Natural Reactor — A Geological Curiosity

At the **Oklo uranium mine in Gabon (West Africa)**, roughly **2 billion years ago**, a naturally occurring fission chain reaction spontaneously ignited and ran for several hundred thousand years. It was discovered in **1972** by French physicist **Francis Perrin**, who noted anomalous isotopic ratios (depleted U-235, anomalous Nd-149, Ru-99, etc.) that could only be explained by sustained fission.

Why did it work?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> 2 billion years ago, the natural isotopic abundance of U-235 was **~3.7%** (today it is **0.72%**) because U-235 decays faster than U-238. At 3.7%, with groundwater as moderator, a critical geometry was naturally achievable.

### Liquid Drop Model of the Nucleus

**Bohr–Wheeler Theory (1939)** treats the atomic nucleus as a **charged, incompressible liquid drop** of nuclear matter. The semi-empirical mass formula (SEMF) gives the binding energy as:

$$B(A,Z) = a_v A - a_s A^{2/3} - a_c \frac{Z^2}{A^{1/3}} - a_a \frac{(A-2Z)^2}{A} \pm \delta(A)$$

where the five terms represent **volume**, **surface**, **Coulomb**, **asymmetry**, and **pairing** energies respectively. Standard coefficients (in MeV): $a_v \approx 15.85$, $a_s \approx 18.34$, $a_c \approx 0.71$, $a_a \approx 23.21$.

### Prompt and Delayed Neutrons — Two Families

- **Prompt Neutrons (~99.35% for U-235):** Emitted within **~10⁻¹⁴ seconds** of scission, directly from the accelerated fission fragments.
- **Delayed Neutrons (~0.65% for U-235):** Emitted by fission-product nuclei (e.g., I-137 → Xe-137 → β + n) **seconds to minutes** after fission. Although numerically tiny, they are **THE critical control parameter** in reactors because they stretch the neutron lifetime by orders of magnitude, making reactor control possible.

### Neutron Life Cycle — The Journey of One Neutron

The **Neutron Life Cycle** tracks a single neutron from its birth in fission through all stages until it either causes a new fission (closes the cycle), leaks out, or is parasitically absorbed.

### Infinite Multiplication Factor (k∞) — Definition

**k∞** is the multiplication factor of an **infinite, homogeneous, un-leaking** reactor. It is purely a material/property ratio, independent of geometry.

$$k_\infty = \frac{\text{neutrons in one generation}}{\text{neutrons in previous generation}}$$

### The 4-Factor Formula

$$k_\infty = \eta \, f \, p \, \varepsilon$$

### The 6-Factor Formula

For a **finite (real) reactor**, leakage is non-zero, and the effective multiplication factor is:

$$k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} = \eta \, f \, p \, \varepsilon \, P_f \, P_t$$

where $P_f$ = fast non-leakage probability, $P_t$ = thermal non-leakage probability.

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Fission Energy — Step-by-Step Mass-Defect Calculation

The energy released in one fission event equals the difference between the **sum of reactant rest masses** and the **sum of product rest masses**, multiplied by $c^2$. Using atomic mass units (u) and the conversion **1 u = 931.494 MeV/c²**, we obtain Q in MeV.

**Physical principle:** Einstein's mass–energy equivalence (1905): any change in rest mass corresponds to an energy release $Q = \Delta m \cdot c^2$. In fission, $\Delta m > 0$ because the products are more tightly bound (higher binding energy per nucleon).

**Why so much energy?** The binding-energy-per-nucleon curve peaks near iron-56 (~8.8 MeV/nucleon), whereas uranium-235 has only ~7.6 MeV/nucleon. The difference (~1.2 MeV/nucleon × ~235 nucleons) ≈ 200 MeV is the binding-energy gain.

### 2.1.2 Critical Mass — Dependence on Five Variables

The critical mass is **not** a fixed number for a given nuclide; it depends on:

1. **Density (ρ):** Higher density → shorter mean free path → fewer neutrons leak → **lower critical mass** (∝ 1/ρ).
2. **Purity / Enrichment:** Higher U-235 concentration → **lower critical mass.**
3. **Geometry:** A sphere minimizes surface-to-volume ratio and thus minimizes leakage → **lowest critical mass** for any given volume.
4. **Moderation:** Light-water moderation thermalizes neutrons → U-235 cross-section rises (1/v behaviour) → **lower critical mass.**
5. **Neutron Reflector:** A surrounding beryllium or natural-uranium "blanket" reflects escaping neutrons back → **lower critical mass.**

> [!NOTE]
> Bare-sphere critical masses (typical):
> - Pu-239: **~10 kg**
> - U-235 (90% enriched): **~52 kg**
> - U-233: **~15 kg**

### 2.1.3 Gabon / Oklo Natural Reactor — Why It Worked

At t = 2 × 10⁹ years ago, the age of the deposit compared to today is:
$$\Delta t = 2 \times 10^9 \text{ yr}, \quad \lambda_{235} = \ln 2 / T_{1/2}^{235} = \ln 2 / 7.04 \times 10^8 \text{ yr}^{-1} \approx 9.85 \times 10^{-10} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$

The U-235 enrichment at the time was:
$$^{235}\text{U} / ^{238}\text{U} \approx 3.7\% \; \text{(today: 0.72%)}$$

Groundwater acted as a moderator. The deposit consisted of ~20 reactor zones averaging **~1 m thick × 10 m long**, embedded in sandstone. Total energy released: estimated **~15,000 MW-years** over **~150,000 years** of intermittent operation (water would boil away, stop the reaction, then return and restart — a natural feedback loop).

### 2.1.4 Liquid Drop Model — Fission Barrier Interpretation

When the compound nucleus (e.g., U-236) deforms from sphere to dumbbell, two competing energies govern:

- **Surface Energy $E_s$:** Increases (a stretched sphere has more surface area).
- **Coulomb Energy $E_c$:** Decreases (like charges moved further apart).

The total deformation energy forms a **fission barrier** of height ~5–6 MeV for U-235. The compound nucleus must absorb enough excitation energy to overcome this barrier. The liquid-drop model predicts:

$$Z^2 / A \;\; \text{(fissility parameter)}$$

If $Z^2/A > 49$, the nucleus is **unstable against spontaneous fission** even in the ground state. For U-235: $Z^2/A = 92^2/235 \approx 36$, so it is stable without neutron absorption.

### 2.1.5 Prompt vs Delayed Neutrons — Why Delayed Neutrons Matter

The neutron generation time $\Lambda$ in a thermal reactor is split into two regimes:

- Without delayed neutrons: $\Lambda \approx 10^{-4}$ s (pure prompt behaviour)
- With delayed neutrons (effective): $\Lambda_{eff} \approx 0.1$ s (~6 orders of magnitude larger)

The delayed-neutron fraction is denoted $\beta$ (≈ 0.0065 for U-235 thermal fission). It is so small in count but so large in delay that **all practical reactor control** depends on it. Without delayed neutrons, no mechanical or human control system could react fast enough.

### 2.1.6 Neutron Life Cycle — The Five Stages

A neutron born in fission follows this exact sequence:

1. **Fast-neutron birth** at ~2 MeV average energy
2. **Fast fission in U-238** (ε > 1) or fast leakage or fast inelastic scattering
3. **Moderation** by elastic scattering with light nuclei (H₂O, D₂O, graphite)
4. **Resonance absorption** in U-238 at epithermal energies (~6–200 eV) — quantified by $p$
5. **Thermal diffusion + thermal absorption** — the fuel absorption fraction is $f$

In each generation, neutrons are also lost by **leakage** at both fast (P_f) and thermal (P_t) stages.

### 2.1.7 The Four-Factor Formula — Definitions and Physical Meaning

| Symbol | Name | Physical Definition |
|---|---|---|
| $\eta$ (eta) | Neutron yield per absorption | Neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel |
| $f$ | Thermal utilization | Fraction of thermal absorptions occurring in fuel |
| $p$ | Resonance escape probability | Fraction of neutrons that survive resonance capture |
| $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) | Fast fission factor | Ratio of total (fast + thermal) fissions to thermal fissions |

### 2.1.8 The Six-Factor Formula — The Two Leakage Terms

The 6-factor formula adds two leakage probabilities to the 4-factor formula:

$$k_{eff} = \eta \, f \, p \, \varepsilon \, P_f \, P_t$$

- $P_f$ = probability a fast neutron does NOT leak out before slowing down
- $P_t$ = probability a thermal neutron does NOT leak out before being absorbed

Together, $P_{NL} = P_f \cdot P_t$ is the total non-leakage probability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All four factors** $\eta, f, p, \varepsilon$ **and** both leakage probabilities $P_f, P_t$ **are dimensionless ratios.** None of them carry units. KTU examiners expect students to *write the meaning of each factor in words* alongside the formula.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Field | Application |
|---|---|
| Reactor Core Design | 4-factor / 6-factor formulas predict $k_{eff}$ for any fuel-moderator-coolant combination before construction |
| Reactor Control | Delayed neutron fraction $\beta$ sets the available control "window" for shim/safety rods |
| Criticality Safety | Critical mass tables used in fuel handling, transport, enrichment, and storage to prevent inadvertent criticality |
| Safeguards | Isotopic anomalies in spent fuel (e.g., Nd-150, Ru-99 ratios) detect undeclared reactor operation |
| Waste Disposal | Burnup calculations from $E = \Delta m \, c^2$ predict Pu / minor-actinide production rates |
| Astrophysics | r-process nucleosynthesis uses liquid-drop fission barriers for trans-actinide formation |

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Fission Energy (Mass-Defect Method)

**Objective:** Compute the energy release $Q$ for the reaction $^ {235}_{92}\text{U} + {}^{1}_{0}\text{n} \to {}^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} + {}^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} + 3\,{}^{1}_{0}\text{n}$.

### Step 1 — Gather atomic mass data (atomic mass units, u)

We use experimentally tabulated atomic rest masses. (Electronic masses cancel because the atomic number Z is balanced on both sides: 92 = 56 + 36, and 92 electrons balance.)

| Species | Atomic Mass (u) |
|---|---|
| $^ {235}\text{U}$ | 235.0439299 |
| $^1\text{n}$ | 1.0086654 |
| $^ {141}\text{Ba}$ | 140.914411 |
| $^ {92}\text{Kr}$ | 91.926156 |

### Step 2 — Compute the sum of reactant rest masses

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{reactants}} &= M(^{235}\text{U}) + M(^{1}\text{n}) \\
&= 235.0439299 + 1.0086654 \\
&= 236.0525953 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the sum of product rest masses

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\text{products}} &= M(^{141}\text{Ba}) + M(^{92}\text{Kr}) + 3 \, M(^{1}\text{n}) \\
&= 140.914411 + 91.926156 + 3 \times 1.0086654 \\
&= 140.914411 + 91.926156 + 3.0259962 \\
&= 235.8665632 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the mass defect $\Delta m$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta m &= M_{\text{reactants}} - M_{\text{products}} \\
&= 236.0525953 - 235.8665632 \\
&= 0.1860321 \text{ u}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Convert to energy using $1\,\text{u} = 931.494\;\text{MeV/c}^2$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q &= \Delta m \times 931.494 \;\text{MeV/u} \\
&= 0.1860321 \times 931.494 \\
&\approx 173.27 \;\text{MeV}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Comment on the result

This ~173 MeV corresponds to the kinetic energy of the fission fragments and emitted neutrons for this particular fragmentation channel. **Averaging over all fission channels gives ~200 MeV**, with the difference (~27 MeV) coming from the beta/gamma decay of the radioactive fission fragments long after the prompt fission event.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative derivation route (using binding energies):**
> $Q = \text{BE(products)} - \text{BE(reactants)} = \left[\frac{B(141)}{141} \times 141 + \frac{B(92)}{92} \times 92\right] - \left[\frac{B(235)}{235} \times 235\right]$ — yielding the same result.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Four-Factor Formula

**Objective:** Express $k_\infty$ — the ratio of neutrons in generation $n+1$ to generation $n$ — in terms of four measurable material properties.

### Step 1 — Define $\eta$ (eta) — neutrons per absorption in fuel

By definition, $\nu$ = average neutrons released per fission. Only a fraction $\sigma_f/\sigma_{a,F}$ of fuel absorptions actually cause fission (the rest being radiative capture $\sigma_c$). Thus:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \nu \times \frac{\text{fissions in fuel}}{\text{absorptions in fuel}} \\
&= \nu \times \frac{\sigma_f N_F}{\sigma_{a,F} N_F} \\
&= \nu \times \frac{\sigma_f}{\sigma_{a,F}} \\
&= \frac{\nu}{1 + \alpha}, \quad \text{where } \alpha = \frac{\sigma_c}{\sigma_f}
\end{aligned}
$$

For U-235 thermal neutrons: $\nu \approx 2.42$, $\alpha \approx 0.169$, so $\eta \approx 2.42 / 1.169 \approx 2.07$.

### Step 2 — Define $f$ — thermal utilization

Of all thermal neutrons absorbed in the entire reactor, the fraction absorbed in the fuel:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f &= \frac{\text{thermal absorptions in fuel}}{\text{total thermal absorptions}} \\
&= \frac{\Sigma_{a,F}}{\Sigma_{a,F} + \Sigma_{a,M} + \Sigma_{a,C} + \Sigma_{a,P}} \\
&= \frac{\Sigma_{a,F}}{\Sigma_{a,\text{total}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

where M = moderator, C = coolant, P = poison (control rods, structural materials).

### Step 3 — Define $p$ — resonance escape probability

As neutrons slow down from fission energies (~2 MeV) to thermal energies (~0.025 eV), they pass through U-238's resonance region (~6 eV to 200 keV) where $\sigma_a$ has huge peaks. The probability of *surviving* (not being captured) this region is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
p &= \exp\left( -\frac{1}{\xi} \int_{E_\text{th}}^{E_0} \frac{\Sigma_a(E)}{\Sigma_s} \frac{dE}{E} \right) \\
&\approx \exp\left( -\frac{N_I \, I_\text{eff}}{\overline{\xi} \, \Sigma_s} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where $I_\text{eff}$ is the **effective resonance integral** (cm²) and $\xi$ is the **average logarithmic energy decrement** of the moderator per collision:

$$
\xi = 1 + \frac{\alpha}{2} \ln\alpha + \frac{(1-\alpha)^2}{2\alpha} \ln(1-\alpha)^{-1} \quad ; \quad \alpha = \left(\frac{A-1}{A+1}\right)^2
$$

For H₂O: $\xi \approx 0.920$; for D₂O: $\xi \approx 0.509$; for graphite: $\xi \approx 0.158$.

### Step 4 — Define $\varepsilon$ (epsilon) — fast fission factor

Some fast neutrons (above the U-238 fission threshold of ~1 MeV) cause fission in U-238 **before** being moderated. This boosts the neutron count. Define:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon &= \frac{\text{total neutrons from all fissions}}{\text{neutrons from thermal fissions alone}} \\
&= 1 + \frac{\text{(neutrons from fast fissions)}}{\text{(neutrons from thermal fissions)}}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a typical LWR with enriched fuel, $\varepsilon \approx 1.02$ to $1.05$.

### Step 5 — Compose $k_\infty$

A single thermal neutron absorption in fuel produces $\eta$ new neutrons. After surviving the resonance escape ($p$), then causing thermal absorption, only fraction $f$ lands in fuel; of fuel absorptions, only $\sigma_f/\sigma_{a,F}$ cause fission; each such fission produces $\nu$ neutrons, and the fast fission boost gives $\varepsilon$. Multiplying these independent probabilities for **one full neutron life cycle in an infinite medium**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
k_\infty &= \eta \cdot f \cdot p \cdot \varepsilon \\
&= (\text{neutrons per fuel absorption}) \times (\text{fuel fraction of thermal absorption}) \\
&\quad \times (\text{resonance survival}) \times (\text{fast fission boost})
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **order** of the four factors is conventional and matches the *physical sequence* of the neutron's life. KTU exam answers must respect this order.

### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration

For a typical PWR with enriched UO₂ fuel in H₂O:
- $\eta \approx 2.07$
- $f \approx 0.71$
- $p \approx 0.87$
- $\varepsilon \approx 1.03$

$$
k_\infty = 2.07 \times 0.71 \times 0.87 \times 1.03 \approx 1.349
$$

This $k_\infty > 1$ confirms that the **infinite lattice would be supercritical**; the actual finite reactor uses leakage ($P_f, P_t < 1$) and control rods to bring $k_{eff}$ back to unity.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Six-Factor Formula from $k_\infty$

In a **finite** reactor, a fraction of neutrons leak out at both the fast stage and the thermal stage. Define:

- $P_f = $ probability a fast neutron does **not** leak before being moderated
- $P_t = $ probability a thermal neutron does **not** leak before being absorbed

Since leakage at fast and thermal stages are essentially independent events:

$$
P_{NL} = P_f \cdot P_t
$$

Therefore:

$$
k_{eff} = k_\infty \cdot P_{NL} = \eta f p \varepsilon P_f P_t
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of $P_f, P_t$:**
> For a bare cylindrical reactor of radius $R$ and height $H$, Buckling $B_g^2 = (2.405/R)^2 + (\pi/H)^2$, and the diffusion-length-squared $L^2$ / migration area $M^2$ relations give:
> $$P_t = \frac{1}{1 + L^2 B_g^2}, \quad P_f = \frac{1}{1 + \tau B_g^2}$$
> where $\tau$ = Fermi age (slowing-down length squared) and $L$ = thermal diffusion length.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Critical Mass Scaling Law

For a bare homogeneous sphere of fissile material, the **minimum critical radius** $R_c$ satisfies the geometric buckling condition $B_g^2 = B_m^2$ (material buckling). For a thermal reactor, $B_m^2 = (k_\infty - 1) / M^2$.

The critical volume is $V_c = (4/3) \pi R_c^3$. The critical mass is:

$$
m_c = \rho \, V_c = \rho \cdot \frac{4}{3} \pi \left[ \frac{\pi}{B_m} \right]^3 = \frac{4\pi^2 \rho}{3 B_m^3}
$$

Therefore:

$$
m_c \propto \frac{\rho}{B_m^3} \propto \frac{\rho}{(k_\infty - 1)^{3/2}}
$$

If density doubles, $m_c$ halves (linear inverse). The cubic dependence on $(k_\infty - 1)$ explains why even small reactivity changes can dramatically alter the critical mass — a key safety concern.

## 3.5 Criticality Condition — Summary Table

| Condition | $k_{eff}$ Value | Reactor State | Physical Outcome |
|---|---|---|---|
| Subcritical | $k_{eff} < 1$ | Reaction dying out | Power decreases exponentially |
| **Critical** | $k_{eff} = 1$ | **Steady-state, self-sustaining** | **Power constant** |
| Supercritical — Prompt Critical | $k_{eff} > 1$ | Delayed critical if $k_{eff} < 1 + \beta$ | Power rises slowly (controllable) |
| Supercritical — Prompt Critical | $k_{eff} > 1 + \beta$ | Reactor period $\to \Lambda_\text{prompt}$ | Power rises in milliseconds (Chernobyl-type excursion) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Delayed critical** = $k_{eff} = 1 + \beta$. Above this, the prompt neutron lifetime alone controls the response. Below this, **delayed neutrons dominate** and the reactor period is in seconds, allowing human/mechanical control.

## 3.6 Derivation of Effective Neutron Generation Time with Delayed Neutrons

Let $\beta$ be the total delayed-neutron fraction, $\lambda_i$ the decay constant of precursor group $i$, and $\Lambda$ the prompt generation time. The **in-hour equation** (reactivity vs. period) is:

$$
\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_i \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}
$$

where $T$ is the stable reactor period. For large $T$ (slow changes):

$$
\rho \approx \beta_{eff} \quad \text{when} \quad T \to \infty \quad \text{(or small)}
$$

This sets the **maximum controlled reactivity insertion** for any practical reactor at $\rho < \beta$ (≈ 0.0065 for U-235).
