> **The Inhour Equation** (KTU High-Yield Result): This equation rigorously relates reactivity to the stable reactor period when delayed neutrons are included:
>
> $$\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$
>
> where $\beta_i$ is the delayed neutron fraction of group $i$ and $\lambda_i$ is its decay constant. **One "inhour"** is the reactivity that produces a period of **1 hour (3600 s)** — hence the equation's name.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Comprehensive Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Save this table — these formulas appear in nearly every KTU Module 1 numerical problem.**

| # | Formula | Name | Typical Units | Domain of Validity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k_\infty = \eta \varepsilon p f$ | Four-factor formula | dimensionless | Infinite homogeneous reactor |
| 2 | $k_{eff} = k_\infty P_{NL}^{fast} P_{NL}^{th}$ | Effective multiplication | dimensionless | Finite reactor |
| 3 | $\rho = (k_{eff}-1)/k_{eff}$ | Reactivity | dimensionless (or $\Delta k/k$) | All regimes |
| 4 | $\rho_{excess} = k_{eff} - 1$ | Excess reactivity | dimensionless | Near criticality |
| 5 | $\rho_{cents} = (\rho/\beta_{eff}) \times 100$ | Cents of reactivity | cents | Reactor control |
| 6 | $T = \Lambda / \rho$ | Prompt period | seconds | $\rho > 0$, no delayed neutrons |
| 7 | $\rho = \Lambda/T + \sum \beta_i/(1+\lambda_i T)$ | Inhour equation | dimensionless | Full kinetics |
| 8 | $k_{eff} = k_\infty/(1+L^2 B^2 + \tau B^2)$ | Buckling-corrected $k$ | dimensionless | Diffusive reactor |
| 9 | $B_c^2 = (k_\infty - 1)/(\tau + L^2)$ | Critical buckling | cm$^{-2}$ | Homogeneous bare reactor |
| 10 | $\Lambda = \ell / k_{eff}$ | Effective generation time | seconds | All reactors |
| 11 | $\eta = \nu \Sigma_f / \Sigma_a^{fuel}$ | Neutrons per absorption | dimensionless | Thermal spectrum |
| 12 | $P(t) = P_0 e^{t/T}$ | Exponential power rise | W | Supercritical reactor |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The concepts of criticality and reactivity are not just academic — they are the **heart of reactor safety analysis** and operational licensing:

- **Reactor Startup Physics Tests:** Operators use control rods and a startup neutron source (e.g., Cf-252 or Am-Be) to approach criticality in carefully measured steps, recording reactivity worth.
- **Reactivity Coefficients:** The reactor's inherent safety relies on **negative reactivity feedback coefficients** — if temperature rises, reactivity automatically decreases, creating a self-stabilizing system.
- **Criticality Safety in Fuel Handling:** Spent fuel storage pools, fresh fuel transport casks, and enrichment facilities must remain subcritical even under worst-case flooding (water is both a moderator and reflector) — governed by **subcritical limits** like $k_{eff} \le 0.95$ with administrative margin.
- **Reactor Kinetics Simulators:** Programs like **PARCS, SIMULATE, and MCNP** solve the point kinetics equations (including the inhour equation) to simulate transients.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Reactivity from the Multiplication Factor

**Objective:** Starting from the definition of the multiplication factor, rigorously derive the reactivity expression $\rho = (k_{eff} - 1)/k_{eff}$.

**Step 1 — Define the neutron multiplication factor:**
The neutron multiplication factor $k$ is defined as the ratio of the neutron production rate in generation $n+1$ to that in generation $n$:

$$k = \frac{N_{n+1}}{N_n}$$

**Step 2 — Express the change in neutron population per generation:**
The *change* in neutron population between successive generations is:

$$\Delta N = N_{n+1} - N_n = k N_n - N_n = (k - 1) N_n$$

**Step 3 — Normalize by the production rate to obtain a dimensionless fractional change:**
The fractional change in neutron population, normalized per neutron produced, is:

$$\rho = \frac{\Delta N}{N_{n+1}} = \frac{(k-1) N_n}{k N_n} = \frac{k-1}{k}$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**
This is the formal KTU definition of reactivity:

$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}}$$

**Step 5 — Approximation for reactors near criticality:**
When $k_{eff} \approx 1$ (the normal operating regime), the denominator $k_{eff} \approx 1$, so:

$$\rho \approx k_{eff} - 1 \equiv \Delta k$$

This approximation is universally used in board problems because operating reactors are designed to operate within a few hundred pcm of criticality.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Critical Size from the Buckling Equation

**Objective:** Derive the critical dimension of a bare, homogeneous, spherical thermal reactor using the buckling equation.

**Step 1 — Neutron balance in a finite reactor:**
For a finite reactor, neutron losses occur via both **non-fission absorption** and **leakage**. The balance is written as:

$$k_\infty = k_{eff}\left(1 + L^2 B^2 + \tau B^2\right)$$

where:
- $L^2$ = diffusion area (for thermal neutrons)
- $\tau$ = Fermi age (for fast neutrons)
- $B^2$ = geometric buckling (shape-dependent)

**Step 2 — Apply the criticality condition $k_{eff} = 1$:**
At criticality, the equation becomes:

$$k_\infty = 1 + L^2 B_c^2 + \tau B_c^2 = 1 + (L^2 + \tau) B_c^2$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the critical buckling:**
Rearranging for $B_c^2$:

$$(L^2 + \tau) B_c^2 = k_\infty - 1$$

$$B_c^2 = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2 + \tau}$$

**Step 4 — For a spherical bare reactor of radius $R$**, the geometric buckling is:

$$B_g^2 = \left(\frac{\pi}{R + \delta}\right)^2$$

where $\delta$ is the extrapolated distance. Setting $B_c^2 = B_g^2$ at criticality:

$$\left(\frac{\pi}{R_c}\right)^2 = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2 + \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the critical radius:**

$$R_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{L^2 + \tau}{k_\infty - 1}}$$

This is the **fundamental result** of reactor design: the critical size is determined entirely by the nuclear data ($L^2$, $\tau$, $k_\infty$). The smaller $(k_\infty - 1)$ becomes, the larger the reactor must be — a critical safety constraint.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Reactor Period from Point Kinetics

**Objective:** Derive the prompt-neutron-only reactor period from the point kinetics equation.

**Step 1 — Start with the prompt-neutron kinetics equation:**
Ignoring delayed neutrons, the point kinetics equation for neutron population $n$ is:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho}{\Lambda} n$$

where $\Lambda$ is the effective neutron generation time and $\rho$ is the reactivity.

**Step 2 — Separate variables and integrate:**

$$\frac{dn}{n} = \frac{\rho}{\Lambda} dt$$

Integrating from $n_0$ at $t=0$ to $n(t)$ at time $t$:

$$\ln\left(\frac{n(t)}{n_0}\right) = \frac{\rho t}{\Lambda}$$

**Step 3 — Exponentiate to obtain the solution:**

$$n(t) = n_0 \exp\left(\frac{\rho t}{\Lambda}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Define the reactor period $T$:**
By definition, the reactor period is the time for the population to grow by a factor of $e$. Setting $n(T)/n_0 = e$:

$$e^1 = \exp\left(\frac{\rho T}{\Lambda}\right) \implies \frac{\rho T}{\Lambda} = 1$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $T$:**

$$\boxed{T = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho}}$$

**Numerical illustration for KTU board problems:**
Suppose $\rho = 0.001$ (i.e., $k_{eff} = 1.001$) and $\Lambda = 10^{-4}\,s$ for a thermal reactor:

$$T = \frac{10^{-4}}{10^{-3}} = 0.1\,s$$

A period of 0.1 s is **extremely fast** and dangerous — this is why real reactors limit $\rho$ to values much less than $\beta_{eff}$ so that **delayed neutrons dominate** the response.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example — Reactivity in Cents

**Problem:** A reactor is operating with $k_{eff} = 1.00085$. The effective delayed neutron fraction is $\beta_{eff} = 0.0065$. Express the reactivity in:
(a) dimensionless units,
(b) pcm,
(c) dollars,
(d) cents.

**Solution:**

**(a) Dimensionless reactivity:**
Using $\rho = (k_{eff} - 1)/k_{eff}$:

$$\rho = \frac{1.00085 - 1}{1.00085} = \frac{8.5 \times 10^{-4}}{1.00085} \approx 8.493 \times 10^{-4}$$

**(b) In pcm (per cent mille):**

$$\rho_{pcm} = 8.493 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^5 = 84.93 \approx 85\,\text{pcm}$$

**(c) In dollars:**
One dollar = $\beta_{eff} = 0.0065$:

$$\rho_{\$} = \frac{8.493 \times 10^{-4}}{0.0065} = 0.1307\,\$ \approx 13.07\,\text{cents}$$

**(d) In cents:** Already computed above.

$$\boxed{\rho \approx 85\,\text{pcm} \approx 13\,\text{cents}}$$

This is a small, controllable reactivity insertion — typical of a routine control rod withdrawal.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reactor Criticality State Machine — Mermaid Flowchart

This diagram maps the **logical control states** of a nuclear reactor based on the value of $k_{eff}$ and the corresponding reactivity insertion mechanisms.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Shutdown\nk_eff less than 0.99\nrho negative] -->|Withdraw Control Rods| B[Approach Criticality\nk_eff less than 1\nrho slightly negative]
    B -->|Criticality Achieved\nrho equals 0\nk_eff equals 1| C[Critical State\nSteady Power Output\nSelf Sustaining Chain Reaction]
    C -->|Insert Control Rods\nOr Boron Dilution| A
    C -->|Small Reactivity Insertion\nrho less than beta_eff| D[Delayed Supercritical\nPower Rises Slowly\nControlled by Delayed Neutrons]
    C -->|Large Reactivity Insertion\nrho greater than beta_eff| E[Prompt Supercritical\nDangerous Power Excursion\nRisk of Fuel Damage]
    D -->|Reactivity Reduced| C
    E -->|SCRAM Emergency Insertion\nAll Rods Drop| F[Subcritical Shutdown\nrho strongly negative\nCooling Phase]
    F -->|Remove Poisons\nRestart Sequence| A
```

---

## 4.2 Neutron Lifecycle Block Diagram — Criticality Mapping

This block diagram traces **one complete neutron lifecycle** from birth in fission through to its fate, identifying where each four-factor term acts.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fission Neutron Birth\nTotal nu neutrons per fission] --> B[Fast Fission in U238\nMultiplication by epsilon]
    B --> C[Moderation Phase\nNeutrons Slow Down\nResonance Escape Probability p]
    C --> D[Thermalization\nNeutrons Reach Thermal Energy]
    D --> E{Fate of Thermal Neutron}
    E -->|Absorbed in Fuel| F[Fission Event\nProduces nu new neutrons\neta factor applies]
    E -->|Absorbed in Moderator\nOr Structure| G[Parasitic Loss\nReduces Thermal Utilization f]
    E -->|Leakage Out of Core| H[Geometric Loss\nAccounted by P Non Leakage]
    F --> A
    G --> I[Dead Neutron\nChain Interrupted]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Required for Criticality\neta times epsilon times p times f\ntimes P_NL must equal 1]
```

---

## 4.3 Reactivity Insertion Mechanism Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Reactivity Insertion Sources] --> S1[Fast Mechanisms\nMillisecond Response]
    R --> S2[Intermediate Mechanisms\nSeconds to Minutes]
    R --> S3[Slow Mechanisms\nHours to Months]

    S1 --> T1[Control Rod Movement\nWorth in Dollars]
    S1 --> T2[Boron Injection\nPWR Emergency Shutdown]

    S2 --> U1[Moderator Temperature\nDensity Effect]
    S2 --> U2[Fuel Temperature\nDoppler Broadening]
    S2 --> U3[Void Formation\nBWR Transient]

    S3 --> V1[Fuel Burnup\nU235 Depletion]
    S3 --> V2[Fission Product Buildup\nXe135 and Sm149]
    S3 --> V3[Coolant Boron Dilution\nPWR Load Follow]
```

---

## 4.4 Criticality Safety Block Architecture — Fuel Handling

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Fresh Fuel Assembly\nEnriched to 3 to 5 percent U235] --> Q{Storage Configuration}
    Q -->|Dry Storage in Vault| R1[Subcritical by Geometry\nSpacing greater than 30 cm]
    Q -->|Wet Storage in Pool| R2[Subcritical by Borated Water\nBoron 2000 ppm minimum]
    Q -->|In Core Position| R3[Critical Under Design Conditions\nControlled by Control Rods]

    R1 --> S[Administrative Limit\nk_eff less than 0.95]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S

    S --> T[Double Contingency Principle\nTwo Independent Failures Required\nfor Criticality Excursion]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Criticality and Reactivity in a Nuclear Reactor System
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

**Model Answer:**

**Criticality** is the operating condition of a nuclear reactor in which the rate of neutron production from fission exactly equals the rate of neutron loss through leakage and non-fission absorption. This results in a **self-sustaining, steady-state** chain reaction with $k_{eff} = 1$.

**Reactivity** ($\rho$) is a dimensionless parameter quantifying the fractional deviation of the neutron multiplication factor from unity:

$$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}}$$

When $\rho = 0$, the reactor is exactly critical. When $\rho > 0$, the reactor is supercritical (power rising). When $\rho < 0$, it is subcritical (power falling). Reactivity is the primary controlled variable in reactor operation.

**Mark Distribution (per KTU scheme):**
- Criticality definition with $k_{eff} = 1$ → **1.5 marks**
- Reactivity formula and physical meaning → **1.5 marks**

---

### Question 2: State and Explain the Significance of the Delayed Neutron Fraction $\beta_{eff}$
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The **effective delayed neutron fraction** $\beta_{eff}$ is the total fraction of fission neutrons that emerge from fission products (precursors) rather than directly from the fission event. For U-235 thermal fission, $\beta_{eff} \approx 0.0065$ (650 pcm).

**Significance:**
1. **Reactor Controllability:** Reactivity insertions less than $\beta_{eff}$ produce power rises governed by delayed-neutron timescales (~minutes), making the reactor inherently controllable. Insertions exceeding $\beta_{eff}$ trigger prompt-critical response (~milliseconds), which is far too fast for mechanical control.
2. **Safety Threshold:** The **dollar** unit is defined such that $\rho = 1\,\$ = \beta_{eff}$. Reactivity insertions are limited to a small fraction of a dollar in normal operation.
3. **Reactor Period:** The inhour equation explicitly contains $\beta_i$ terms — without delayed neutrons, reactor periods would be too short for human or mechanical response.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of $\beta_{eff}$ → **1 mark**
- Three significance points → **2 marks (≈0.67 each)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A: Criticality, Reactivity & Reactor Period (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Comprehensive Module 1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Derive the Four-Factor Formula for $k_\infty$ and Discuss the Criticality Condition for a Finite Reactor

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Define each term in the four-factor formula $k_\infty = \eta \varepsilon p f$.

**(i) Neutrons per absorption in fuel:**
$$\eta = \nu \frac{\Sigma_f^{fuel}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}}$$
where $\nu \approx 2.43$ for U-235 thermal fission.

**(ii) Fast fission factor:** $\varepsilon > 1$ accounts for fast-neutron fission in U-238 (typically 1.03–1.07 for thermal reactors).

**(iii) Resonance escape probability:** $p < 1$ gives the fraction of neutrons surviving U-238 resonance absorption during slowing down (typically 0.7–0.9).

**(iv) Thermal utilization:** $f = \Sigma_a^{fuel}/(\Sigma_a^{fuel} + \Sigma_a^{mod} + \Sigma_a^{struct})$ gives the fraction of thermal neutrons absorbed in fuel (typically 0.7–0.9).

**Step 2:** Combine: $k_\infty = \eta \varepsilon p f$. For thermal reactors, $k_\infty \approx 1.2$–$1.6$.

**Step 3:** Apply finite-reactor correction using non-leakage probabilities:
$$k_{eff} = k_\infty P_{NL}^{fast} P_{NL}^{th}$$

**Step 4:** The criticality condition $k_{eff} = 1$ yields:
$$k_\infty P_{NL}^{fast} P_{NL}^{th} = 1$$

This implicitly defines the **critical geometry** through the buckling equation:
$$B_c^2 = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2 + \tau}$$

The critical radius of a bare spherical reactor is therefore:
$$R_c = \pi \sqrt{\frac{L^2 + \tau}{k_\infty - 1}}$$

**Step 5:** Discussion — Increasing enrichment raises $k_\infty$, decreasing $R_c$. Adding a reflector reduces $L^2 + \tau$ effective term, also decreasing $R_c$. These are the principal design handles for achieving criticality.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definitions of four factors (1 mark each, total 4 marks)
- Criticality condition derivation (2 marks)
- Critical radius result (1 mark)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Derive the Inhour Equation Relating Reactivity to the Stable Reactor Period

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Start with point kinetics equations including six delayed-neutron precursor groups:

$$\frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} n + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i$$

$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} n - \lambda_i C_i$$

**Step 2:** Assume exponential solution $n(t) = n_0 e^{t/T}$ and $C_i(t) = C_i^0 e^{t/T}$.

**Step 3:** Substitute into the precursor equation:
$$\frac{C_i^0}{n_0} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda(1/T + \lambda_i)} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} \cdot \frac{1}{1/T + \lambda_i}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the neutron equation:
$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \lambda_i}{\Lambda(1/T + \lambda_i)}$$

Multiply by $\Lambda$ and rearrange:
$$\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}$$

**Step 5 — The Inhour Equation:**
$$\boxed{\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T}}$$

**Special Case Verification:**
- For very large $T$ (long period): $\rho \approx \sum \beta_i = \beta_{eff}$ (delayed-critical reactivity)
- For very small $T$: $\rho \approx \Lambda/T$ (prompt-neutron approximation)

**Numerical Example:** For U-235 with $\Lambda = 10^{-4}$ s, $\beta = 0.0065$, $\lambda = 0.08$ s$^{-1}$ (single group), and $T = 100$ s:
$$\rho = \frac{10^{-4}}{100} + \frac{0.0065}{1 + 8} = 10^{-6} + 7.22 \times 10^{-4} \approx 7.22 \times 10^{-4} = 72.2\,\text{pcm}$$

**Mark Distribution:**
- Point kinetics equations (2 marks)
- Exponential ansatz and substitution (2 marks)
- Final inhour equation (2 marks)
- Special cases / numerical example (1 mark)

---

### Question B: Reactivity Coefficients & Reactor Control (14 Marks) — **ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**
`[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Alternative Module 1]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Explain the Four Primary Reactivity Coefficients and Their Role in Reactor Safety

**Model Solution:**

A **reactivity coefficient** $\alpha_X$ is defined as the change in reactivity per unit change in parameter $X$:

$$\alpha_X = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial X}$$

**Coefficient 1: Moderator Temperature Coefficient (MTC)**
$$\alpha_{T_{mod}} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T_{mod}}$$

Increasing moderator temperature reduces water density, decreasing the slowing-down power. This reduces $p$ and hence $k_{eff}$. For LWRs, $\alpha_{T_{mod}}$ is **negative**, providing inherent safety.

**Coefficient 2: Fuel Temperature Coefficient (Doppler Coefficient)**
$$\alpha_{T_{fuel}} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T_{fuel}}$$

Increased fuel temperature broadens U-238 resonance absorption peaks via the **Doppler effect**, increasing parasitic capture and reducing $p$. This is **strongly negative** and acts within milliseconds — the fastest inherent safety mechanism.

**Coefficient 3: Void Coefficient (BWR-specific)**
$$\alpha_{void} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial V_{void}}$$

In a BWR, steam voids displace moderator. The sign depends on the moderation ratio: in typical BWR designs, it is **negative** for safe operation.

**Coefficient 4: Boron Concentration Coefficient (PWR-specific)**
$$\alpha_{B} = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial C_{B}}$$

Higher dissolved boron reduces $k_{eff}$ by absorbing thermal neutrons. PWRs use this for **long-term reactivity control** and load following.

**Safety Role:** For inherent safety, ALL coefficients must be **negative** — any temperature rise causes automatic reactivity reduction, creating a self-stabilizing system without operator intervention.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Definition of reactivity coefficient (1 mark)
- Description of each of the four coefficients (4 marks = 1 each)
- Safety implication (2 marks)

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: A Thermal Reactor Operates at $k_{eff} = 1.0015$ with $\beta_{eff} = 0.0065$ and $\Lambda = 5 \times 10^{-5}$ s. Calculate:
- (i) Reactivity in pcm and dollars
- (ii) Prompt-neutron-only period $T$
- (iii) Comment on the safety implications

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Reactivity conversion:**
$$\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}} = \frac{0.0015}{1.0015} = 1.4978 \times 10^{-3}$$

In pcm:
$$\rho_{pcm} = 1.4978 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^5 = 149.78 \approx 150\,\text{pcm}$$

In dollars:
$$\rho_{\$} = \frac{1.4978 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0065} = 0.2304\,\$ \approx 23.04\,\text{cents}$$

**(ii) Prompt-neutron-only period:**
$$T = \frac{\Lambda}{\rho} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{1.4978 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0334\,s \approx 33.4\,\text{ms}$$

**(iii) Safety Implications:**
- A 33 ms period is **extremely fast** — power would multiply by $e$ every 33 ms. Without delayed neutrons, this would be uncontrollable.
- The reactivity (23 cents) is significantly less than $1\,\$$ (= $\beta_{eff}$ = 65 cents). Hence the reactor is in the **delayed supercritical** regime, where the actual stable period is much longer (typically 50–100 s).
- **Operative Insight:** Real reactor control systems ensure $\rho \ll \beta_{eff}$. If $\rho$ ever exceeded $\beta_{eff}$ (1 dollar), the reactor would become **prompt critical** — a dangerous condition approached only in nuclear weapons or severe accidents (e.g., Chernobyl).
- **Conclusion:** The reactor is safely controllable because $\rho/\beta_{eff} \approx 0.23$, well below prompt criticality.

**Mark Distribution:**
- Reactivity calculations (3 marks)
- Period calculation (2 marks)
- Safety discussion (2 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting to divide by $k_{eff}$ in $\rho$:** Students often write $\rho = k_{eff} - 1$ when $k_{eff}$ is significantly above 1. Always use $\rho = (k_{eff}-1)/k_{eff}$ unless the problem specifies the approximation.
> 2. **Confusing $\Lambda$ and $\ell$:** The prompt generation time $\ell$ (about $10^{-4}$ s for thermal) differs from the effective value $\Lambda = \ell/k_{eff}$. The inhour equation uses $\Lambda$.
> 3. **Mixing up delayed neutron fraction with total neutron fraction:** $\beta_{eff}$ is about 0.65% for U-235 — *not* a large fraction. Writing $\beta = 0.65$ instead of $0.0065$ leads to 100× errors.
> 4. **Forgetting units in inhour equation:** $\lambda_i$ is in s$^{-1}$, $T$ in s, and the products must be dimensionless.
> 5. **Treating criticality as a single state:** Criticality is a continuous balance — reactivity can be slightly negative, zero, or slightly positive, all near critical.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Criticality & Reactivity**

- **Criticality = $k_{eff} = 1$.** Three regimes: subcritical ($k_{eff} < 1$), critical ($k_{eff} = 1$), supercritical ($k_{eff} > 1$).
- **Reactivity** $\rho = (k_{eff} - 1)/k_{eff}$. Approximated as $\rho \approx k_{eff} - 1$ near criticality.
- **Four-factor formula:** $k_\infty = \eta \varepsilon p f$. Each factor has a precise physical meaning tied to a stage of the neutron lifecycle.
- **Finite reactor correction:** $k_{eff} = k_\infty P_{NL}^{fast} P_{NL}^{th}$.
- **Critical size from buckling:** $R_c = \pi\sqrt{(L^2 + \tau)/(k_\infty - 1)}$ for a bare sphere.
- **Reactivity units:** pcm ($10^{-5}$), dollars ($\beta_{eff}$), cents ($\beta_{eff}/100$).
- **Delayed neutron fraction:** $\beta_{eff} \approx 0.0065$ for U-235 thermal fission. **One dollar of reactivity = $\beta_{eff}$.**
- **Inhour equation:** $\rho = \Lambda/T + \sum \beta_i/(1 + \lambda_i T)$ — relates reactivity to stable period.
- **Prompt period:** $T = \Lambda/\rho$ — only valid for $\rho > \beta_{eff}$ (prompt critical).
- **Reactivity coefficients** must be **negative** for inherent safety (Doppler, moderator temperature, void in BWR).
- **Xenon-135** has the highest thermal absorption cross-section of any nuclide ($\approx 2.6 \times 10^6$ barns) and dominates post-shutdown reactivity behavior.
- **Criticality safety limit:** $k_{eff} \le 0.95$ in fuel storage (administrative margin against uncertainties).
- **Most-tested KTU concept:** The transition from $k_\infty$ through leakage corrections to $k_{eff}$, followed by the reactivity definition and its units.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Kinetics and Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reactor Kinetics and Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Reactor Kinetics and Control

**Reactor Kinetics** is the branch of nuclear reactor physics that studies the **time-dependent behaviour** of the neutron population, power output, and precursor concentrations in a nuclear reactor following a perturbation in reactivity. It is governed by a coupled set of first-order non-linear differential equations known as the **Point Reactor Kinetics Equations (PRKE)**, which simultaneously track the neutron density *N(t)* and the concentrations *Cᵢ(t)* of the *i*-th group of **delayed neutron precursors**.

**Reactor Control** is the engineering discipline concerned with maintaining the neutron population, reactor power, and reactivity within prescribed safe operating limits using a combination of **control rods**, **chemical shim (boron)**, **burnable absorbers**, and **moderator temperature feedback** mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The point kinetics equations with delayed neutrons (typically the **six-group precursor model** for U-235 fission), the concept of **reactivity (ρ)**, **reactor period (T)**, the **in-hour equation**, and the various **reactivity coefficients** (temperature, void, pressure) form the core high-weightage content of this module.

## 1.2 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following are the standard neutron-physics constants used throughout KTU board examinations:

- **Effective delayed neutron fraction (β)**: **0.0065 (≈ 0.65 %)** for U-235 thermal fission.
- **Mean neutron generation time (Λ)**: **~10⁻⁴ s** (thermal reactor) and **~10⁻⁶ s** (fast reactor).
- **One dollar of reactivity ($1)**: defined as **ρ = β**.
- **One cent (1¢)**: defined as **ρ = 0.01 β**.
- **Prompt critical condition**: **ρ = β** (i.e., k = 1 using prompt neutrons alone).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bonfire on a Leash

Imagine you are tending a large bonfire in a windy field. The wood and oxygen supply represent the **fissile fuel** and the **neutron multiplication factor (k)**. If the fire starts getting too hot (excess power), you don't rip away half the wood at once — that would smother it. Instead, you push a metal plate (a **control rod**) slowly to absorb neutrons and dampen the reaction.

Now, here is the crucial twist: when a neutron slams into a U-235 nucleus and causes fission, **99.35 % of the new neutrons appear instantly (prompt neutrons)**, but the remaining **0.65 % emerge seconds later from the radioactive decay of fission products (delayed neutron precursors)**.

Think of **prompt neutrons** as the immediate flare-up of the fire, and **delayed neutrons** as the slow, smouldering embers that keep feeding it. This tiny 0.65 % acts as a built-in **"governor"** — it gives the control rods a precious few seconds to mechanically move into place. Without delayed neutrons, reactor control would be practically impossible because power would double in **microseconds**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Board-Exam Wording):** *Reactivity (ρ) is the dimensionless fractional departure of the effective multiplication factor from unity, expressed as* **ρ = (k − 1) / k**.

## 1.4 Scope of This Module

The following sub-topics are covered in sequence:

1. The **Point Kinetics Equations** with and without delayed neutrons.
2. The **Reactor Period (T)** and its relation to reactivity.
3. The **In-Hour Equation** (Six-group and One-delayed-group approximations).
4. **Reactivity Measurement** in dollars, cents, and per-cent-mille (pcm).
5. **Reactivity Coefficients**: temperature, void, and power coefficients.
6. **Control Rods**: their worth, differential and integral.
7. **Reactor Control Strategies**: shim, regulating, and scram systems.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Foundation — Neutron Cycle and Multiplication

In a steady-state critical reactor, the **effective multiplication factor (k)** is exactly unity, meaning the neutron population is constant. Any small deviation from unity produces a time-varying behaviour described by kinetics.

The **four-factor formula** gives k as:

$$k = \eta \cdot \varepsilon \cdot p \cdot f \cdot P_{NL} \cdot P_{A}$$

where:
- **η** = neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in fuel
- **ε** = fast fission factor
- **p** = resonance escape probability
- **f** = thermal utilization factor
- **P_NL** = non-leakage probability (fast)
- **P_A** = non-leakage probability (thermal)

## 2.2 The Delayed Neutron Phenomenon

A small fraction of neutrons are **not emitted instantly** during fission but are released later when certain fission products (precursors, e.g., Br-87, I-137) undergo **beta-minus decay**. These are called **delayed neutrons**.

Key properties of delayed neutrons (for U-235):
- Total delayed fraction **β ≈ 0.0065**
- Six distinct energy/half-life groups, with mean lifetimes ranging from **~0.1 s to ~80 s**.
- The longest-lived group has a half-life of **~55.6 s** (Br-87 precursor).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The delayed neutrons are the reason reactor control is feasible. Their fraction β is small but their effect on the reactor period is **enormous** — they stretch the time scale from microseconds to seconds.

## 2.3 The Point Reactor Kinetics Equations (PRKE)

The complete PRKE with **six delayed-neutron groups** is:

$$\frac{dN(t)}{dt} = \left[\frac{\rho(t) - \beta}{\Lambda}\right] N(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i \, C_i(t)$$

$$\frac{dC_i(t)}{dt} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} N(t) - \lambda_i \, C_i(t) \quad ; \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6$$

where:
- **N(t)** = neutron density (neutrons/cm³)
- **Cᵢ(t)** = precursor concentration of the *i*-th group
- **ρ(t)** = reactivity
- **β** = total effective delayed neutron fraction = Σβᵢ
- **βᵢ** = delayed fraction of *i*-th group
- **λᵢ** = decay constant of *i*-th precursor
- **Λ** = effective mean neutron generation time

## 2.4 The In-Hour Equation (Six-Group Form)

By assuming an **exponential solution** *N(t) = N₀ e^(ωt)* for constant reactivity, the PRKE reduces to the algebraic **in-hour (or reactivity) equation**:

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \, \omega}{\omega + \lambda_i}$$

Solving this transcendental equation for ω given ρ (or vice versa) is the central computational task of reactor kinetics.

**Special case — One Delayed Group (ODG) approximation** (used for hand-derivation in KTU board):

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \frac{\beta \omega}{\omega + \lambda}$$

## 2.5 Reactivity Definitions and Conversions

| Quantity | Symbol | Definition | Typical Value (KTU) |
|----------|--------|------------|---------------------|
| Reactivity | ρ | (k − 1)/k | dimensionless |
| Excess reactivity | ρ_ex | Reactivity available above critical | 0.05 to 0.15 |
| Reactivity in dollars | $ | ρ / β | 1 $ ≡ β |
| Reactivity in cents | ¢ | 100 ρ / β | 100 ¢ = 1 $ |
| Per cent mille | pcm | ρ × 10⁵ | 1 pcm = 10⁻⁵ Δk/k |
| Reactor period | T | 1 / ω (stable roots) | seconds |
| Prompt critical | ρ = β | k_prompt = 1 | critical condition |

## 2.6 Reactivity Coefficients

These are **feedback parameters** that make the reactor inherently self-stabilising:

$$\alpha_T = \frac{1}{k} \frac{\partial k}{\partial T} \quad \text{(Temperature coefficient, pcm/°C)}$$

$$\alpha_V = \frac{1}{k} \frac{\partial k}{\partial V} \quad \text{(Void coefficient)}$$

$$\alpha_P = \frac{1}{k} \frac{\partial k}{\partial P} \quad \text{(Power coefficient)}$$

A **negative temperature coefficient** is a mandatory safety feature — it makes the reactor self-regulating.

## 2.7 Control Rod Worth

The **reactivity worth** of a control rod is the change in reactivity produced when it is fully inserted:

$$\Delta\rho_{rod} = \rho_{in} - \rho_{out}$$

- **Differential rod worth**: dρ/dx (per unit insertion depth).
- **Integral rod worth**: ∫(dρ/dx) dx from out to in.

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | ρ = (k − 1) / k | Reactivity definition | dimensionless |
| 2 | dN/dt = [(ρ − β)/Λ] N + Σ λᵢCᵢ | Point kinetics — neutron | neutrons/cm³·s |
| 3 | dCᵢ/dt = (βᵢ/Λ) N − λᵢCᵢ | Precursor equation | atoms/cm³·s |
| 4 | ρ = ωΛ + Σ βᵢω/(ω + λᵢ) | Six-group in-hour equation | dimensionless |
| 5 | T = 1/ω | Stable reactor period | seconds |
| 6 | T_p = Λ / (ρ − β) | Prompt period (for ρ > β) | seconds |
| 7 | ρ_dollars = ρ / β | Reactivity in dollars | $ |
| 8 | α_T = (1/k)(dk/dT) | Temperature coefficient | Δk/k per °C |
| 9 | Λ = ℓ / k̄ | Mean generation time | seconds |
| 10 | dρ/dx | Differential control rod worth | Δk/k per cm |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — From Neutron Balance to Point Kinetics Equation

### Step 1: Net Rate of Change of Neutron Density

The net rate of change of neutron density in an infinitesimal volume is the sum of **production** minus **losses**:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \text{Production rate} - \text{Loss rate}$$

**Production rate** includes:
- Neutrons from **prompt fission**: each absorbed neutron in fuel produces ν neutrons; only (1 − β) of these are prompt.
- Neutrons from **delayed precursors**: Σᵢ λᵢCᵢ (decay of *i*-th group precursor).

**Loss rate** includes:
- **Absorption** in fuel, moderator, structure, coolant.
- **Leakage** out of the reactor core.

Using the **effective multiplication factor k** and **mean generation time Λ**, this is compactly written as:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} N(t) + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i(t)$$

### Step 2: Net Rate of Change of Precursor Concentration

Each fission event in fuel produces a total of **βᵢ** precursors per neutron absorbed (in the *i*-th group). The precursors **decay** with constant **λᵢ**:

$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} N(t) - \lambda_i C_i(t)$$

These are the canonical **point kinetics equations** used universally in KTU board derivations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — The In-Hour Equation (One-Delayed-Group Approximation)

This is the **most-asked derivation** in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations.

### Step 1: Assume an Exponential Solution

For a **constant reactivity ρ**, assume the neutron density grows (or decays) exponentially:

$$N(t) = N_0 \, e^{\omega t}$$

Therefore:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \omega N_0 \, e^{\omega t} = \omega N(t)$$

### Step 2: Assume Equilibrium for the Precursor

For an exponentially varying N, the precursor also varies exponentially:

$$C(t) = C_0 \, e^{\omega t}$$

So:

$$\frac{dC}{dt} = \omega C(t)$$

### Step 3: Substitute into the Precursor Equation

$$\omega C_0 e^{\omega t} = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda} N_0 e^{\omega t} - \lambda C_0 e^{\omega t}$$

Dividing throughout by e^(ωt):

$$\omega C_0 = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda} N_0 - \lambda C_0$$

### Step 4: Solve for C₀ in Terms of N₀

Rearranging:

$$C_0 (\omega + \lambda) = \frac{\beta}{\Lambda} N_0$$

$$C_0 = \frac{\beta \, N_0}{\Lambda (\omega + \lambda)}$$

### Step 5: Substitute into the Neutron Equation

The neutron equation in ODG form:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} N + \lambda C$$

Substituting the assumed forms:

$$\omega N_0 e^{\omega t} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} N_0 e^{\omega t} + \lambda C_0 e^{\omega t}$$

Divide by e^(ωt):

$$\omega N_0 = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} N_0 + \lambda C_0$$

### Step 6: Insert the C₀ Expression

$$\omega N_0 = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} N_0 + \lambda \cdot \frac{\beta N_0}{\Lambda (\omega + \lambda)}$$

Factor out N₀/Λ:

$$\omega N_0 = \frac{N_0}{\Lambda} \left[ (\rho - \beta) + \frac{\lambda \beta}{\omega + \lambda} \right]$$

### Step 7: Cancel and Rearrange

Dividing both sides by N₀/Λ:

$$\omega \Lambda = \rho - \beta + \frac{\lambda \beta}{\omega + \lambda}$$

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \beta - \frac{\lambda \beta}{\omega + \lambda}$$

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \beta \left[ 1 - \frac{\lambda}{\omega + \lambda} \right]$$

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \beta \left[ \frac{\omega + \lambda - \lambda}{\omega + \lambda} \right]$$

### Step 8: Final In-Hour Equation (ODG)

$$\boxed{\rho = \omega \Lambda + \frac{\beta \, \omega}{\omega + \lambda}}$$

For **six groups**, simply sum over the *i*-th group:

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i \, \omega}{\omega + \lambda_i}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — Reactor Period

The **stable reactor period T** is the inverse of the dominant positive root ω of the in-hour equation. For the special case of **negative reactivity** and **delayed critical operation (ρ ≪ β)**:

### Step 1: Neglect Λω (since Λ is very small)

The in-hour equation reduces to:

$$\rho \approx \frac{\beta \, \omega}{\omega + \lambda}$$

### Step 2: Solve for ω

$$\rho (\omega + \lambda) = \beta \omega$$

$$\rho \omega + \rho \lambda = \beta \omega$$

$$\rho \lambda = \omega (\beta - \rho)$$

$$\omega = \frac{\rho \lambda}{\beta - \rho}$$

### Step 3: Invert to Obtain Period

$$T = \frac{1}{\omega} = \frac{\beta - \rho}{\rho \lambda}$$

This is the famous **stable period** formula for small delayed-supercritical or subcritical operation.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A thermal reactor has β = 0.0065, Λ = 10⁻⁴ s, λ = 0.08 s⁻¹. Reactivity is ρ = 0.0013 (i.e., 20 ¢). Compute the stable reactor period.

**Solution:**

Using the ODG in-hour equation:

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \frac{\beta \omega}{\omega + \lambda}$$

Since Λω ≪ β for small ω, approximate:

$$\omega = \frac{\rho \lambda}{\beta - \rho} = \frac{0.0013 \times 0.08}{0.0065 - 0.0013}$$

$$\omega = \frac{1.04 \times 10^{-4}}{5.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.02 \, \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$T = \frac{1}{\omega} = 50 \, \text{seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** When ρ is small (≪ β), the period T = (β − ρ) / (ρλ) is the standard KTU 2024 board result.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Prompt vs Delayed Critical

| Condition | Reactivity Range | Dominant Period | Time to Double Power |
|-----------|------------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **Delayed subcritical** | ρ < 0, ∣ρ∣ > 0 | Asymptotically decays | Infinity (power → 0) |
| **Critical (steady)** | ρ = 0 | Infinite (stable) | Power constant |
| **Delayed supercritical** | 0 < ρ < β | Stable T = (β − ρ)/ρλ | Seconds to minutes |
| **Prompt critical** | ρ = β | Period ≈ Λ / (ρ − β) → ∞ at exactly ρ = β | Uncontrolled jump |
| **Prompt supercritical** | ρ > β | T_p = Λ / (ρ − β) | **Milliseconds** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse *prompt critical* (ρ = β) with *delayed critical* (ρ = 0). The reactor is critical in the **engineering sense** at ρ = 0, but only **delayed critical**. At ρ = β, it is *prompt critical* — the point beyond which control rods cannot respond fast enough.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Reactor Kinetics Feedback Control Loop

This is the **functional architecture** showing how the neutron population, precursors, and reactivity feedback interact in a controlled nuclear reactor.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fission Event in Fuel] --> B[Neutron Production]
    B --> C{Prompt vs Delayed Split}
    C --> D[Prompt Neutrons 99.35 percent]
    C --> E[Precursor Atoms 0.65 percent]
    D --> F[Neutron Density N of t]
    E --> G[Precursor Concentration C of t]
    G -->|Beta Decay| F
    F --> H[Reactor Power P of t]
    H --> I[Reactivity Feedback rho]
    I -->|Negative Temp Coeff| F
    J[Control Rod Insertion] --> I
    K[Chemical Shim Boron] --> I
    L[Burnable Absorbers] --> I
    M[Reactor Period T] --> N[Safety and Control Logic]
    H --> N
    N --> O[Scram Trip Signal]
    O --> J
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- **A → B → C**: Each fission in U-235 produces neutrons, which split into prompt and delayed components.
- **D → F**: Prompt neutrons contribute immediately to the neutron density N(t).
- **E → G → F**: Precursors decay with rate λᵢ, feeding delayed neutrons into N(t) over seconds.
- **F → H → I**: Power P = Σ_f Φ produces reactivity feedback α_T·ΔT.
- **J, K, L → I**: External reactivity inputs from rods, shim, and burnable poison.
- **H → N → O → J**: The safety logic monitors power, period T, and trips the scram.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Reactivity Control System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Reactor Core] --> Q[Neutron Flux Sensor]
    Q --> R[Reactivity Computer]
    R --> S{Is rho within band}
    S -->|Yes| T[Steady State Operation]
    S -->|No| U[Control Action Required]
    U --> V[Regulating Rod Drive]
    U --> W[Shim Rod Drive]
    U --> X[Emergency Scram]
    V --> P
    W --> P
    X --> P
```

---

## 4.3 Delayed Neutron Group Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[U-235 Fission] --> B1[Prompt Neutrons instant]
    A1 --> C1[Precursor Nuclei 6 groups]
    C1 --> D1[Group 1 Half Life 55.6 s]
    C1 --> D2[Group 2 Half Life 22.7 s]
    C1 --> D3[Group 3 Half Life 6.2 s]
    C1 --> D4[Group 4 Half Life 2.3 s]
    C1 --> D5[Group 5 Half Life 0.6 s]
    C1 --> D6[Group 6 Half Life 0.2 s]
    D1 --> E1[Delayed Neutron Emission]
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E1
    D6 --> E1
    E1 --> F1[Combined Delayed Neutron Flux]
    B1 --> G1[Total Neutron Flux]
    F1 --> G1
```

---

## 4.4 Control Rod Worth Curve (S-Curve Behaviour)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Rod Fully Out] -->|Insertion| B[Linear Region]
    B -->|Insertion| C[High Worth Region]
    C -->|Insertion| D[Rod Fully In Maximum Negative rho]
    D -->|Reactivity Peak| E[Rod Shadowing Effect]
    E --> F[Effective Worth Lowered]
```

**Interpretation:**
- The control rod is most effective in the **middle of the core** (highest flux).
- Near the **edges**, the rod is "shadowed" by fuel — its incremental worth is less.
- This gives the characteristic **S-shaped** integral worth curve.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define reactivity. Explain its significance in nuclear reactor operation.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Reactivity (ρ)** is defined as the fractional deviation of the effective multiplication factor *k* from unity:
>
> $$\rho = \frac{k - 1}{k}$$
>
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** that measures how far the reactor is from criticality. Its significance:
> - **ρ = 0** → critical, power constant.
> - **ρ > 0** → supercritical, power rising.
> - **ρ < 0** → subcritical, power falling.
>
> The magnitude of ρ, expressed in **dollars** (ρ/β) or **pcm** (10⁵ ρ), directly determines the **reactor period** and is the primary control parameter manipulated by control rods and chemical shim.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What are delayed neutrons? Why are they important for reactor control?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Delayed neutrons** are neutrons emitted **seconds to minutes after fission**, not directly during the fission event. They originate from the **beta decay of fission-product precursors** such as Br-87, I-137, etc.
>
> For U-235 thermal fission, the total delayed fraction is **β ≈ 0.0065 (0.65 %)**, distributed among **six groups** with half-lives ranging from **0.2 s to 55.6 s**.
>
> **Importance for reactor control:**
> 1. They extend the reactor period from **microseconds to seconds**, giving control rods time to respond mechanically.
> 2. They provide an inherent **negative feedback** through precursor decay lag.
> 3. Without them, all reactors would be **prompt critical** at k = 1 — practically uncontrollable.
> 4. They define the **delayed-critical** condition (ρ = 0) and the **prompt-critical** threshold (ρ = β).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model Paper)]

#### (a) Derive the point reactor kinetics equations with six delayed-neutron groups. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

The point reactor kinetics equations are derived from the **neutron balance** in an infinitesimal volume of the reactor.

**(i) Neutron Density Equation:**

Consider an element of fuel where the neutron production rate is kΣ_aΦV and the loss rate is Σ_aΦV (absorption) plus leakage D∇²ΦV.

For a point (homogenised) reactor, this simplifies to:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{k(1-\beta) - 1}{\ell} N + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i$$

where ℓ is the mean neutron lifetime. Using the mean generation time Λ = ℓ/k and the reactivity definition ρ = (k−1)/k, this becomes:

$$\frac{dN}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta}{\Lambda} N + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \lambda_i C_i \quad \cdots (1)$$

**(ii) Precursor Equation:**

For the *i*-th delayed group, the production rate per unit volume is (βᵢ/Λ)N and the decay rate is λᵢCᵢ:

$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = \frac{\beta_i}{\Lambda} N - \lambda_i C_i \quad ; \quad i = 1, 2, \ldots, 6 \quad \cdots (2)$$

Equations (1) and (2) are the **Point Reactor Kinetics Equations**.

#### (b) Derive the in-hour equation in the one-delayed-group approximation and discuss the concept of prompt criticality. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

Assuming exponential solutions N(t) = N₀e^(ωt) and C(t) = C₀e^(ωt), substitution yields:

$$\rho = \omega \Lambda + \frac{\beta \omega}{\omega + \lambda}$$

**(Derivation steps in detail — see Section 3.2 of this note.)**

**Prompt criticality discussion:**

- When **ρ < β**, the dominant root of the in-hour equation gives a **stable, finite positive period** T = (β − ρ)/(ρλ). Control is feasible.
- When **ρ = β**, the period T → ∞ at the threshold — the reactor is at the boundary of **prompt criticality**.
- When **ρ > β**, the period becomes the **prompt period** T_p = Λ/(ρ − β), which is on the order of **milliseconds**. Control rods (with insertion times of 1–2 s) cannot respond in time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write ρ = β as "critical." The correct KTU terminology is: **ρ = 0 is delayed critical**; **ρ = β is prompt critical**. Failing to distinguish these costs **2 marks** in valuation.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – June 2023 (Model Paper)]

#### (a) Explain the concept of reactivity coefficients. Discuss the importance of a negative temperature coefficient of reactivity. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Reactivity coefficients** quantify how the reactivity ρ changes with a state parameter (T, V, P):

$$\alpha_T = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial T} = \frac{1}{k} \frac{\partial k}{\partial T} \quad [\Delta k/k \text{ per } ^\circ C]$$

**Mechanisms that give α_T its sign:**
- **Doppler broadening** of U-238 resonances → **negative** contribution (more absorption with T).
- **Moderator density decrease** with T → reduces moderation → fewer thermal fissions → **negative** for light-water reactors.
- For **pressurised heavy-water reactors (PHWR)**, void formation gives a complex behaviour.

**Importance of a negative α_T:**
1. **Inherent safety** — a power excursion raises T, which reduces ρ, automatically shutting the reactor down.
2. **Self-regulating** — no external intervention needed for small transients.
3. **Stable operation** — eliminates the need for continuous control movement.
4. **Required by regulatory bodies** for all commercial power reactors (positive α_T reactors are excluded from grid operation).

#### (b) A thermal reactor has β = 0.0065, Λ = 10⁻⁴ s, and a mean delayed-neutron precursor decay constant λ = 0.08 s⁻¹. Calculate (i) the stable reactor period when ρ = 0.0013, and (ii) the reactivity in dollars and pcm. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Stable Reactor Period:**

For small delayed-supercritical reactivity, the stable period is given by:

$$T = \frac{\beta - \rho}{\rho \lambda}$$

Substituting:

$$T = \frac{0.0065 - 0.0013}{0.0013 \times 0.08} = \frac{0.0052}{1.04 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$T = 50 \, \text{seconds}$$

**(ii) Reactivity in dollars and pcm:**

In dollars:

$$\rho_{\$} = \frac{\rho}{\beta} = \frac{0.0013}{0.0065} = 0.20 \, \$\, (20\,\text{cents})$$

In pcm:

$$\rho_{pcm} = \rho \times 10^5 = 0.0013 \times 10^5 = 130 \, \text{pcm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** A common error in this problem is **forgetting to multiply by 10⁵ for pcm conversion**, or mixing up dollars and cents. KTU valuation keys penalise 1 mark for unit errors and 2 marks for conversion mistakes. Always **state the unit explicitly** in your final answer line.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Reactivity (ρ) = (k − 1)/k** — the single most important parameter in reactor control.
- **Delayed neutron fraction β = 0.0065** for U-235 thermal fission — small fraction, enormous consequence.
- The **Point Kinetics Equations** are **two coupled ODEs**: one for N(t), one for each Cᵢ(t).
- The **In-Hour Equation** is the algebraic form of the kinetics equations under exponential assumption.
- **Reactor period T = 1/ω** — for delayed operation, T = (β − ρ)/(ρλ); for prompt critical, T_p = Λ/(ρ − β).
- **Prompt criticality** occurs at **ρ = β**; **delayed criticality** at **ρ = 0** — never confuse these.
- **1 dollar = β**; **1 cent = 0.01 β**; **1 pcm = 10⁻⁵ Δk/k**.
- A **negative temperature coefficient** is a mandatory safety requirement.
- **Control rod worth** is the reactivity change produced by full insertion; its differential worth dρ/dx varies with position.
- **Six-group delayed neutron data** for U-235 is typically provided in KTU exam data sheets — memorise the *form*, not the numbers.
- The reactor **scram** (emergency shutdown) is triggered when period T becomes too short or power exceeds the safety threshold.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Fuel Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reactor Fuel Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Reactor Fuel Cycle** (also called the **Nuclear Fuel Cycle**) is the complete, cradle-to-grave sequence of industrial, chemical, and physical processes involved in producing electrical (or thermal) energy from nuclear fuel. It encompasses every stage the fuel undergoes — from the extraction of uranium ore from the earth, through its purification, isotopic enrichment, fabrication into fuel assemblies, irradiation inside a nuclear reactor core, temporary storage, possible chemical reprocessing, and finally the permanent disposal of high-level radioactive waste.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (verbatim style):** The Reactor Fuel Cycle refers to the totality of activities associated with the supply of fresh fuel to a nuclear reactor, its in-core residence and burn-up, followed by spent-fuel handling, reprocessing (if any), recycling of fissile/fertile isotopes, and the management and disposal of radioactive waste.

The fuel cycle is conventionally divided into two logical halves:

| Sub-Cycle | Also Known As | Core Activities |
|---|---|---|
| **Front-End** | Pre-reactor / "Head-End" | Mining → Milling → Conversion → Enrichment → Fuel Fabrication |
| **Back-End** | Post-reactor / "Tail-End" | Spent-Fuel Storage → Reprocessing → Waste Conditioning → Geological Disposal |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Journey of a Coal Nugget"

Imagine a coal-fired power plant where each piece of coal has to be mined, washed, pressed into a briquette, burned in a furnace, and the resulting ash buried safely for **hundreds of thousands of years** in a lead-lined vault. Also, while the coal is burning, the furnace simultaneously *manufactures new, even more powerful fuel inside itself* (plutonium from uranium-238) — and produces lethal poisons (fission products like iodine-131, cesium-137) that must be removed. That is essentially the **Reactor Fuel Cycle** in plain English.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-line student mnemonic:** *"Mine it, refine it, enrich it, shape it, burn it, cool it, recycle it (or not), and bury it forever."*

## 1.3 Two Strategic Archetypes of the Cycle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Open Fuel Cycle (Once-Through Cycle):** Spent fuel is *never* reprocessed. After discharge, it is stored and eventually disposed of directly as waste. The USA historically adopted this strategy.
> - **Closed Fuel Cycle (Reprocessing Cycle):** Spent fuel is chemically treated to recover fissile Uranium (U) and Plutonium (Pu), which are then re-fabricated into Mixed Oxide (MOX) fuel and re-inserted into the reactor. France, Russia, Japan, and India follow variants of this strategy.

The choice between these two archetypes is one of the most consequential **energy-policy decisions** a nation can make, balancing economics, energy security, proliferation risk, and long-term waste burden.

## 1.4 Key Engineering Metrics Introduced in the Topic

> [!NOTE]
> - **Enrichment Assay (w):** Mass fraction of U-235 in the total uranium mass of the fresh fuel (typically **3–5 %** for Light Water Reactors, abbreviated as LEU).
> - **Burnup (BU):** Total energy released per unit mass of initial heavy-metal charge, expressed in **MW·d/MTU** (megawatt-days per metric ton of initial uranium) or **GWd/MTU**. Typical LWR burnups are **45,000 MWd/MTU** (i.e., 45 GWd/MTU).
> - **Conversion Ratio (CR):** The rate at which fertile material (U-238) is converted into fissile material (Pu-239) relative to the rate at which fissile material is destroyed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-Wise Operational Logic of the Front-End

The **front-end** is governed by mass and isotopic economics. Each stage progressively *concentrates* the fissile content and *purifies* the chemical form so that the fuel can withstand extreme in-core conditions.

### Stage 1 — Uranium Mining
- **Open-Pit Mining:** Used for near-surface deposits (e.g., Ranger, Australia).
- **Underground Mining:** Used for deep, narrow vein deposits.
- **In-Situ Leaching (ISL):** Acidic or alkaline solutions are pumped underground to dissolve UO₂²⁺, which is then extracted via ion-exchange resins. This is now the *lowest-cost* method and accounts for >50 % of world production.
- **By-product Recovery:** Uranium is also recovered as a by-product of phosphate fertilizer, copper, and gold mining.

### Stage 2 — Milling (Ore Beneficiation)
- The mined ore (typically **0.03 – 0.2 % U₃O₈** by mass) is crushed, ground, and leached with sulfuric acid.
- The leachate is purified via solvent extraction (using amines like Alamine 336) or ion exchange.
- The product is **"Yellowcake"** (U₃O₈), a yellow-brown powder with purity ≥ 95 %.

### Stage 3 — Conversion to UF₆
- Yellowcake is calcined to UO₃, then reduced to UO₂ with H₂.
- UO₂ is hydrofluorinated to UF₄, and finally fluorinated with F₂ gas to produce **UF₆** (uranium hexafluoride), the only uranium compound that is gaseous at moderate temperatures (**sublimes at 56.5 °C at 1 atm**). UF₆ is essential for the enrichment step.

### Stage 4 — Isotopic Enrichment
Natural uranium contains only **0.711 % U-235**; the remainder is mostly U-238. Most commercial reactors require **3–5 % U-235**, so enrichment is mandatory.
- **Gaseous Diffusion:** UF₆ is forced through porous membranes. Lighter U-235 molecules diffuse slightly faster. *Obsolete in Western nations due to high energy consumption (~2,400 kWh per SWU).*
- **Gas Centrifuge:** UF₆ gas is spun at ~60,000 rpm in vacuum. The heavier U-238 concentrates at the wall; U-235 concentrates near the axis. The dominant modern method (consumes ~50–200 kWh/SWU).
- **Laser Isotope Separation (SILEX, AVLIS, MLIS):** Photons selectively ionize U-235 atoms. Emerging technology.

### Stage 5 — Fuel Fabrication
- Enriched UF₆ is converted back to UO₂ powder via **ADU (Ammonium Diuranate)** or **AUC (Ammonium Uranyl Carbonate)** routes.
- UO₂ is cold-pressed into cylindrical pellets (≈ **10.5 mm diameter, 13 mm height**) and sintered at **1,750 °C** in H₂ atmosphere to achieve **~95 % Theoretical Density (TD)**.
- Pellets are loaded into **Zircaloy-4 cladding tubes** (for PWRs) or Zircaloy-2 (for BWRs), back-filled with **He at ~3 atm**, and welded.
- Rods are bundled into **fuel assemblies** (e.g., 17×17 PWR assembly contains 264 fuel rods + 25 guide tubes).

## 2.2 In-Reactor (Service) Stage

During the 18–24 month residence time inside the core, the fuel undergoes:

- **U-235 Fission:** Primary energy source. The number of U-235 atoms decreases exponentially.
- **U-238 Neutron Capture → Pu-239:** The fertile-to-fissile breeding process, which forms the basis of the **Closed Cycle** and **Breeder Reactors**.
- **Fission Product Buildup:** Xenon-135 (the strongest neutron poison, σ_a ≈ 2.6 × 10⁶ barns), Samarium-149, and other neutron absorbers.
- **Fuel Swelling and Fission Gas Release:** Xe, Kr, I, Cs accumulate in the fuel-cladding gap, affecting thermal conductivity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Burnup Equation (under constant flux approximation):**
> The remaining fissile atom density follows a first-order exponential law because absorption is the dominant loss mechanism.

## 2.3 Stage-Wise Operational Logic of the Back-End

### Stage 6 — Spent Fuel Storage
- **Spent Fuel Pool (SFP):** Immediate storage underwater for **≥ 5 years** to allow short-lived fission products to decay and reduce decay heat. Water provides both cooling and radiation shielding.
- **Dry Cask Storage:** After pool cooling, fuel is transferred to sealed steel/concrete casks (e.g., **HI-STORM**, **TN-24**, **CASTOR** systems) for interim storage of **50–100+ years**.

### Stage 7 — Reprocessing (Purex Process)
- **PUREX = Plutonium Uranium Redox EXtraction.**
- Spent fuel rods are chopped, dissolved in **hot nitric acid (≈ 8 M HNO₃)**, and the U and Pu are selectively extracted into an organic phase (30 % Tributyl Phosphate in kerosene), leaving >99 % of fission products in the aqueous raffinate.
- Pu is then stripped with a **reducing agent (Fe²⁺ or U⁴⁺)**, separating it from U.
- The recovered U is re-enriched and re-fabricated; the recovered Pu is mixed with depleted U to form **MOX (Mixed Oxide, UO₂–PuO₂)** fuel.

### Stage 8 — Radioactive Waste Management
- **High-Level Waste (HLW):** Vitrified into borosilicate glass logs and stored in shielded vaults.
- **Intermediate-Level Waste (ILW):** Solidified in cement/bitumen.
- **Low-Level Waste (LLW):** Compacted and near-surface disposed.
- **Spent Fuel considered as Waste** (Open Cycle) is itself a form of HLW.

### Stage 9 — Geological Disposal
- Multi-barrier concept: waste form (glass) → metal canister (e.g., copper) → bentonite buffer → stable host rock (granite, salt, or clay) at **> 500 m depth**.
- Repositories under development: **Onkalo (Finland)**, **Yucca Mountain (USA, halted)**, **Cigéo (France)**, and analogous sites in Sweden, Canada, and Switzerland.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are examinable in ESE (End Semester Examination) Module 2 questions.

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Form | Definition / Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of fresh fuel | $m_f = P_{th} \, t_{cycle} / BU$ | Fuel mass needed to sustain a thermal power $P_{th}$ for one cycle | kg or t (metric tons) |
| 2 | Initial U-235 atom density | $N_{5,0} = \dfrac{\rho_{UO_2} N_A w}{M_{UO_2}}$ | Fissile atoms per unit volume in fresh pellet | atoms / cm³ |
| 3 | U-235 consumption (exponential) | $N_5(t) = N_{5,0} \, e^{-\sigma_{a5}\phi t}$ | Fissile atoms left after irradiation time $t$ | atoms / cm³ |
| 4 | Specific power | $P_{sp} = P_{th} / m_{HM}$ | Power per unit heavy-metal mass | MW / MTU |
| 5 | Burnup | $BU = P_{sp} \cdot T_{res}$ | Total energy extracted per mass of initial U | MWd / MTU |
| 6 | Conversion Ratio (instantaneous) | $CR = \dfrac{\Sigma_{c,238}}{\Sigma_{a,235} + \Sigma_{a,239}+\dots}$ | New fissile atoms produced / fissile atoms destroyed | dimensionless |
| 7 | Decay heat (ANSI/ANS-5.1, $t$ in seconds) | $P_d(t) = 0.0622 \, P_0 \, t^{-0.2}$ | Long-term residual thermal power after shutdown | MW |
| 8 | Separative Work (ideal) | $\Delta U = P \, V(w_p) + W \, V(w_t) - F \, V(w_f)$ | Minimum thermodynamic work to enrich feed $F$ into product $P$ and tails $W$ | kg SWU |
| 9 | Value function | $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln\dfrac{x}{1-x}$ | Stage efficiency parameter in isotope separation | dimensionless |
| 10 | Fuel Residence Time | $T_{res} = m_{HM} BU / P_{th}$ | Time the fuel stays in the core | days or years |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The Conversion Ratio determines whether a reactor is a *burner* (CR < 1, LWRs), *converter* (CR ≈ 1), or *breeder* (CR > 1, Fast Breeder Reactors). This single number dictates the entire back-end strategy of a nation's fuel cycle.

## 2.5 Mass Flow Logic of an Open (Once-Through) Cycle

For a typical **1 GWe PWR** operating for one year with a burnup of **45 GWd/MTU**:

- Annual electricity output: $1{,}000 \text{ MW} \times 8{,}760 \text{ h} \times 0.33 \text{ (capacity factor)} = 2.89 \text{ TWh}$.
- Annual thermal energy: $2.89 / 0.33 = 8.76 \text{ TWh} = 8.76 \times 10^{6} \text{ MWh} = 3.15 \times 10^{10} \text{ MJ}$.
- Required fresh uranium: $3.15 \times 10^{10} / (45 \times 86.4) \approx 8.1 \text{ tonnes of enriched U}$.
- Natural uranium feed required (for 4.5 % enriched product and 0.3 % tails): approximately **160 tonnes of natural U per year per GWe**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Exponential Fissile Burndown Under Constant Flux

The number of U-235 atoms at any time $t$ is governed by the absorption (mostly fission + capture) rate. The governing differential equation is:

$$
\frac{dN_5(t)}{dt} = -\sigma_{a5}\,\phi\,N_5(t)
$$

where:
- $N_5(t)$ = number density of U-235 at time $t$ (atoms / cm³)
- $\sigma_{a5}$ = microscopic absorption cross-section of U-235 (barns) = **585 barns for thermal neutrons**
- $\phi$ = thermal neutron flux (n / cm²·s)
- $t$ = time of irradiation (seconds)

### Step-by-Step Integration

**Step 1.** Separate the variables:

$$
\frac{dN_5}{N_5} = -\sigma_{a5}\phi\,dt
$$

**Step 2.** Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$:

$$
\int_{N_{5,0}}^{N_5(t)} \frac{dN_5}{N_5} = -\sigma_{a5}\phi \int_{0}^{t} dt
$$

**Step 3.** Evaluate the integrals:

$$
\ln\left(\frac{N_5(t)}{N_{5,0}}\right) = -\sigma_{a5}\phi\,t
$$

**Step 4.** Exponentiate both sides:

$$
N_5(t) = N_{5,0}\,e^{-\sigma_{a5}\phi t}
$$

**Step 5.** The **fluence** is defined as $\Phi = \phi\,t$, so equivalently:

$$
N_5(t) = N_{5,0}\,e^{-\sigma_{a5}\Phi}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The decay constant analog is $\lambda_{eff} = \sigma_{a5}\phi$, in units of s⁻¹. The half-life analog ("fissile half-life") is $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / (\sigma_{a5}\phi)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Burnup as a Function of Specific Power and Residence Time

The **burnup** $BU$ (MWd / MTU) is defined as the total recoverable thermal energy per unit mass of initial heavy metal:

$$
BU = \frac{1}{m_{HM}}\int_{0}^{T_{res}} P_{sp}(t)\,dt
$$

Assuming **constant specific power** $P_{sp}$ (a reasonable approximation for large reactors with continuous refueling at equilibrium):

$$
BU = P_{sp}\cdot T_{res}
$$

### Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A 3,400 MWth PWR core contains 100 metric tons of initial uranium (MTU). The fuel cycle length is 18 months. Calculate the discharge burnup.

**Given:**
- $P_{th} = 3{,}400$ MW
- $m_{HM} = 100$ MTU
- $T_{res} = 18$ months $= 18 \times 30 \times 24 = 12{,}960$ hours $= 540$ days

**Step 1.** Compute specific power:

$$
P_{sp} = \frac{P_{th}}{m_{HM}} = \frac{3{,}400 \text{ MW}}{100 \text{ MTU}} = 34 \text{ MW/MTU}
$$

**Step 2.** Compute burnup:

$$
BU = P_{sp}\times T_{res} = 34 \text{ MW/MTU} \times 540 \text{ days} = 18{,}360 \text{ MWd/MTU}
$$

**Step 3.** Convert to GWd/MTU:

$$
BU = 18{,}360 / 1000 = 18.36 \text{ GWd/MTU}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Comment:** 18.36 GWd/MTU is on the **low end** for modern LWRs (which now target 45–60 GWd/MTU). This was typical of **1960s–1970s** designs. Modern high-burnup fuel requires improved cladding (e.g., M5®, ZIRLO™) and more corrosion-resistant pellets (e.g., doped UO₂ with Cr₂O₃, Al₂O₃).

## 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Ratio (Simplified Thermal Reactor)

For a thermal reactor, the **instantaneous conversion ratio** is approximated as:

$$
CR = \frac{\text{rate of fissile atom production in U-238}}{\text{rate of fissile atom (U-235 + Pu-239) destruction}}
$$

The U-238 capture rate is $\Sigma_{c,238}\,\phi$. The total fissile destruction rate is $(\Sigma_{a,235}+\Sigma_{a,239}+\dots)\phi$. Thus:

$$
CR = \frac{\Sigma_{c,238}}{\Sigma_{a,235}+\Sigma_{a,239}}
$$

For a fresh PWR with **3.2 % enriched UO₂**:

- $\Sigma_{c,238} = N_{238}\,\sigma_{c,238} \approx 1.5 \text{ cm}^{-1}\cdot 0.003 \text{ barns} \cdot 10^{-24} \approx 4.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cm}^{-1}$ (scaled appropriately)
- $\Sigma_{a,235} \approx N_{235}\,\sigma_{a,235}$

After evaluating the actual cross-section values, the equilibrium CR for a PWR lies in the range **0.55 – 0.65**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Breeding Condition:** $CR \geq 1$ requires a fast spectrum where $\eta_{239}$ (neutrons produced per neutron absorbed in Pu-239) exceeds 2.0, which happens for $E_n > 0.6$ MeV. Hence, **Fast Breeder Reactors (FBRs)** like India's PFBR achieve $CR \approx 1.05 - 1.20$.

## 3.4 Derivation: Decay Heat Power (Long-Term, ANSI/ANS-5.1 Approximation)

After a long irradiation of duration $T_0$ at power $P_0$, the decay heat power $P_d(t)$ at shutdown time $t$ follows the **Way-Wigner empirical law** for $t \gg T_0$:

$$
P_d(t) \approx 0.0622\,P_0\,t^{-0.2}
$$

where $t$ is in **seconds** and $P_0$ is the operating thermal power in MW.

### Step-by-Step Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A 3,000 MWth reactor is suddenly scrammed. Estimate the decay heat power (a) 1 minute, (b) 1 hour, and (c) 1 day after shutdown.

**Step 1.** Convert times to seconds:
- 1 min = 60 s
- 1 h = 3,600 s
- 1 d = 86,400 s

**Step 2.** Apply the formula:

$$
P_d(60) = 0.0622 \times 3{,}000 \times 60^{-0.2}
$$

Computing $60^{-0.2}$:
$$
60^{-0.2} = e^{-0.2 \ln 60} = e^{-0.2 \times 4.094} = e^{-0.819} = 0.441
$$

Therefore:
$$
P_d(60) = 0.0622 \times 3{,}000 \times 0.441 = 82.3 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 3.** At 1 hour:
$$
3{,}600^{-0.2} = e^{-0.2 \ln 3600} = e^{-0.2 \times 8.189} = e^{-1.638} = 0.194
$$
$$
P_d(3600) = 0.0622 \times 3{,}000 \times 0.194 = 36.2 \text{ MW}
$$

**Step 4.** At 1 day:
$$
86{,}400^{-0.2} = e^{-0.2 \ln 86400} = e^{-0.2 \times 11.37} = e^{-2.274} = 0.103
$$
$$
P_d(86400) = 0.0622 \times 3{,}000 \times 0.103 = 19.2 \text{ MW}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering implication:** Even one day after shutdown, a 3,000 MWth reactor still generates ~19 MW of thermal power. This is why spent fuel pools must remain operational for **years**, and dry casks need active or passive cooling fins for **decades**.

## 3.5 Enrichment Mass Balance — Feed, Product, Tails

For enrichment, define:
- $F$ = mass of feed (natural U), with assay $w_f$ = 0.00711
- $P$ = mass of product (enriched U), with assay $w_p$ (e.g., 0.045)
- $W$ = mass of tails (depleted U), with assay $w_t$ (e.g., 0.003)

**Conservation of total mass:**
$$
F = P + W
$$

**Conservation of U-235 mass:**
$$
F w_f = P w_p + W w_t
$$

Solving simultaneously:

$$
P = F \cdot \frac{w_f - w_t}{w_p - w_t}
$$

$$
W = F \cdot \frac{w_p - w_f}{w_p - w_t}
$$

### Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A centrifuge plant must produce 100 kg of 4.5 % enriched uranium. The tails assay is 0.3 %. Find the feed (natural U) required.

**Step 1.** Apply the formula with $w_f = 0.00711$, $w_t = 0.003$, $w_p = 0.045$:

$$
P = F \cdot \frac{0.00711 - 0.003}{0.045 - 0.003} = F \cdot \frac{0.00411}{0.042} = 0.0979\,F
$$

**Step 2.** Solve for $F$:

$$
F = \frac{P}{0.0979} = \frac{100}{0.0979} = 1{,}021.5 \text{ kg of natural U}
$$

**Step 3.** Tails mass:

$$
W = F - P = 1{,}021.5 - 100 = 921.5 \text{ kg of depleted U (0.3 \% assay)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This feed-to-product ratio (~10:1) is the reason uranium enrichment is the **most mass-intensive** stage of the front-end and why the **tails assay** is a critical economic knob. Lowering $w_t$ to 0.002 increases feed needs but produces more U-235 in tails (a strategic stockpile).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block: Complete Open Fuel Cycle (Once-Through)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uranium Ore Mining] --> B[U3O8 Milling and Yellowcake Production]
    B --> C[Conversion to UF6 Gas]
    C --> D[Isotopic Enrichment U235 from 0.711 to 3 to 5 percent]
    D --> E[Fuel Fabrication UO2 Pellets and Zircaloy Rods]
    E --> F[Reactor Core Irradiation 18 to 24 months]
    F --> G[Spent Fuel Pool Storage 5 to 10 years]
    G --> H[Interim Dry Cask Storage 50 to 100 years]
    H --> I[Permanent Geological Repository 500 m depth]
    I --> J[Long Term Isolation over 100,000 years]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** Follow the arrows from top to bottom. Notice that in the **Open Cycle**, the spent fuel path never branches back to fresh fuel fabrication — it proceeds in a straight line from reactor to final disposal.

## 4.2 Mermaid Block: Complete Closed Fuel Cycle (with Reprocessing)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uranium Mining] --> B[Milling to Yellowcake]
    B --> C[Conversion to UF6]
    C --> D[Enrichment]
    D --> E1[Fresh UO2 Fuel Fabrication]
    E1 --> F[Reactor Irradiation]
    F --> G[Spent Fuel Pool Storage]
    G --> H{Reprocessing Decision}
    H -- Reprocess --> I[PUREX Chemical Separation]
    I --> I1[Recovered Uranium Recycle]
    I --> I2[Recovered Plutonium Recycle]
    I1 --> E2[Re-enrichment or Direct Refabrication]
    I2 --> K[MOX Fuel Fabrication UO2 plus PuO2]
    E2 --> F
    K --> F
    H -- Do Not Reprocess --> L[Direct Spent Fuel Disposal]
    I --> M[High Level Waste Vitrification]
    M --> N[Geological Repository]
    L --> N
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the chart:** The two "recycle" arrows (E2 → F and K → F) close the loop, creating a circular material economy. The decision diamond `{H}` is the **proliferation and policy gate** — it is the most consequential node in the entire fuel cycle, both technically and geopolitically.

## 4.3 Mermaid Block: Block-Level Functional Architecture — Front-End vs Back-End Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FRONT[FRONT END FUEL CYCLE MODULE]
        direction TB
        F1[Mining and Milling] --> F2[Conversion]
        F2 --> F3[Enrichment]
        F3 --> F4[Fuel Fabrication]
    end

    subgraph CORE[IN REACTOR SERVICE]
        direction TB
        R1[Fresh Fuel Assemblies] --> R2[Neutron Irradiation]
        R2 --> R3[Spent Fuel Discharged]
    end

    subgraph BACK[BACK END FUEL CYCLE MODULE]
        direction TB
        B1[Cooling and Storage] --> B2[Reprocessing or Direct Disposal]
        B2 --> B3[Waste Conditioning]
        B3 --> B4[Geological Disposal]
    end

    F4 --> R1
    R3 --> B1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reading:** This block diagram shows the **three functional modules** that KTU examiners love to test. The arrows crossing the subgraph boundaries represent the **physical hand-off** of the fuel between responsibility domains (mining company → enrichment company → utility operator → storage agency → repository operator). Each hand-off is a **regulatory and quality-control checkpoint**.

## 4.4 Mermaid Block: PUREX Reprocessing Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Spent Fuel Chopped into 1 inch segments] --> P2[Dissolution in Hot 8M HNO3]
    P2 --> P3[Feed Clarification and Adjustment]
    P3 --> P4[Co extraction Column 30 percent TBP in Kerosene]
    P4 --> P5[U plus Pu in Organic Phase]
    P4 --> P6[Fission Products in Aqueous Raffinate]
    P6 --> P7[Vitrification into Borosilicate Glass]
    P7 --> P8[Canister Storage and Disposal]
    P5 --> P9[Partition Column with Reducing Agent]
    P9 --> P10[Pu Product Aqueous Phase]
    P9 --> P11[U Product Aqueous Phase]
    P10 --> P12[PuO2 Powder for MOX]
    P11 --> P13[U Product for Re enrichment]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Purex design philosophy:** The PUREX process exploits the *valence-state chemistry* of plutonium. Pu⁴⁺ is co-extracted with UO₂²⁺ into TBP, but Pu is later *reduced* to Pu³⁺ (which is *not* extractable by TBP), cleanly separating it from uranium. The TBP organic phase is recycled, the nitric acid is recovered by distillation, and the only true waste stream is the **raffinate → glass log** pathway on the left.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
> **Define the term "Reactor Fuel Cycle". Distinguish between an "Open Fuel Cycle" and a "Closed Fuel Cycle" with one example country for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** The Reactor Fuel Cycle is the complete sequence of industrial processes involved in preparing nuclear fuel for use in a reactor, irradiating it, and managing the resulting spent fuel and radioactive waste.
- **Open Cycle (1 mark):** In an open (once-through) cycle, the spent fuel discharged from the reactor is *not* reprocessed; it is stored and eventually disposed of directly as high-level waste. *Example: United States of America.*
- **Closed Cycle (1 mark):** In a closed cycle, the spent fuel undergoes chemical reprocessing (e.g., PUREX) to recover fissile U-235 and Pu-239, which are then re-fabricated into fresh fuel (e.g., MOX) and re-loaded. *Example: France (La Hague plant).*

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
> **What is "yellowcake"? Why is uranium converted into UF₆ before enrichment, and what enrichment level is typically required for a commercial Pressurized Water Reactor (PWR)?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Yellowcake (1 mark):** Yellowcake is the product of uranium milling, chemically identified as **U₃O₈** (triuranium octoxide). It is a yellow-brown powder with purity typically ≥ 95 %, obtained by solvent extraction or ion exchange from the ore leachate.
- **Why UF₆ (1 mark):** UF₆ is the only uranium compound that is **gaseous at moderate temperatures** (sublimes at 56.5 °C at atmospheric pressure). Gaseous state is essential for isotope separation processes like gaseous diffusion and gas centrifugation.
- **PWR Enrichment (1 mark):** Commercial PWRs typically require **3 – 5 % U-235** enrichment (Low Enriched Uranium, LEU), compared to the natural assay of 0.711 %.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks — Full Question with Internal Sub-Parts)

> **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]`**
>
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **complete front-end** of the nuclear fuel cycle. Explain the role and importance of isotopic enrichment in this context. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the exponential law governing the consumption of U-235 atoms in a thermal neutron flux. A PWR fuel assembly is irradiated to a burnup of **30,000 MWd/MTU**. If the average thermal flux is **3 × 10¹³ n/cm²/s**, estimate the fraction of initial U-235 atoms remaining. Use $\sigma_{a,235} = 585$ barns and neglect capture-only losses. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Block diagram (2 marks):**
Draw (or describe) the sequence: **Mining → Milling → Conversion → Enrichment → Fuel Fabrication** as five sequential boxes.

**Step 2 — Detailed stages (4 marks):**

- **Mining (1 mark):** Open-pit, underground, or in-situ leaching (ISL) methods produce uranium ore with very low U concentration (0.03–0.2 % U₃O₈).
- **Milling (1 mark):** Crushed ore is leached with sulfuric acid; solvent extraction (amines) yields yellowcake (U₃O₈).
- **Conversion (1 mark):** Yellowcake is fluorinated through UO₃ → UO₂ → UF₄ → UF₆.
- **Enrichment (1 mark):** UF₆ is enriched by gas diffusion or centrifuge to ~3–5 % U-235.
- **Fabrication (1 mark, optional consolidation):** UF₆ → UO₂ powder → pellets → Zircaloy-clad rods → assemblies.

**Step 3 — Importance of Enrichment (1 mark):**
Natural uranium contains only 0.711 % fissile U-235. Most commercial reactors (PWR, BWR) need 3–5 % to sustain a chain reaction with low-enriched fuel; without enrichment, only **Heavy Water Reactors (PHWRs)** like India's can operate on natural uranium due to their superior neutron economy.

### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Derive the exponential law (3 marks):**

Rate of U-235 loss = absorption rate:

$$
\frac{dN_5}{dt} = -\sigma_{a5}\,\phi\,N_5
$$

Separating and integrating:

$$
\int_{N_{5,0}}^{N_5(t)}\frac{dN_5}{N_5} = -\sigma_{a5}\phi\int_0^t dt
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \ln\!\left(\frac{N_5(t)}{N_{5,0}}\right) = -\sigma_{a5}\phi t
$$

$$
\Rightarrow N_5(t) = N_{5,0}\,e^{-\sigma_{a5}\phi t}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the fluence $\Phi$ (2 marks):**

Burnup definition: $BU = P_{sp} \times T_{res} = 30{,}000$ MWd/MTU.

Convert burnup into total fissions per initial U atom. The energy per fission is ~**200 MeV = 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J**, equivalent to ~**0.948 MWd/MTU per one fission per initial U-235 atom** (more precisely, 1 MWd = 3.6 × 10⁹ J released per MTU of initial heavy metal; dividing by 200 MeV = 3.2 × 10⁻¹¹ J/fission gives number of fissions).

Number of fissions per initial U atom:
$$
F = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ MWd/MTU}}{0.948 \text{ MWd/MTU per fission}} \approx 31{,}646 \text{ fissions per initial U atom}
$$

For an initial enrichment of ~3.2 % (typical for 30 GWd/MTU), the number of initial U-235 atoms is $0.032 \times N_{total}$, so each U-235 atom experiences on average $\sim 1.0$ absorption. We use the fluence directly:

Equivalently, total fluence is:
$$
\Phi = \frac{BU \times N_A \times 10^{-6}}{E_f \times m_{HM}}
$$

Using the energy per fission $E_f = 200$ MeV = $3.2 \times 10^{-11}$ J, and 1 MTU = 1,000 kg:

$$
\Phi = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ MWd/MTU} \times 1{,}000 \text{ kg} \times 3.6 \times 10^9 \text{ J/MWd}}{3.2 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J} \times 1{,}000 \text{ kg}}
$$

$$
\Phi = \frac{1.08 \times 10^{17}}{3.2 \times 10^{-8}} = 3.375 \times 10^{21} \text{ n/cm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the fraction remaining (2 marks):**

$$
\sigma_{a5}\Phi = 585 \times 10^{-24} \times 3.375 \times 10^{21} = 1.974
$$

$$
\frac{N_5(t)}{N_{5,0}} = e^{-1.974} = 0.139
$$

**Result: ~13.9 % of the initial U-235 atoms remain.** This corresponds to ~86 % consumption, which is consistent with realistic discharge compositions of PWR spent fuel at 30 GWd/MTU.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

> **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]`**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a flowchart, describe the **PUREX reprocessing process**. State the role of TBP and the reducing agent. **(7 marks)**
>
