> **(b)** What is the "Conversion Ratio" of a nuclear reactor? Show that in a thermal reactor the conversion ratio is less than 1, whereas in a fast breeder reactor it can exceed 1. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Part (a) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — PUREX Flowchart (3 marks):** Draw the seven-stage flow: Chopping → HNO₃ dissolution → Feed clarification → Co-extraction column (TBP) → Partition column (with reductant) → U product + Pu product → Waste vitrification.

**Step 2 — TBP role (2 marks):**
Tributyl Phosphate (TBP), diluted to 30 vol% in kerosene, is the **organic extractant**. It selectively complexes **Pu⁴⁺** and **UO₂²⁺** from the aqueous nitric acid solution, leaving > 99 % of the trivalent and higher-valent fission products (Cs⁺, Sr²⁺, lanthanides³⁺, Zr⁴⁺ is suppressed with a salting agent) in the aqueous raffinate. This is the **co-extraction** step.

**Step 3 — Reductant role (2 marks):**
A reducing agent (typically **Fe²⁺ sulfamate** or **U⁴⁺ nitrate**) is introduced in the **partition column** to reduce Pu⁴⁺ to **Pu³⁺**. The trivalent Pu³⁺ is *not* extractable by TBP, so it back-transfers to the aqueous phase, cleanly separating Pu from U. The UO₂²⁺ remains in the organic phase and is later stripped with dilute HNO₃.

### Model Solution for Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
The **Conversion Ratio (CR)** is defined as the ratio of the rate of *new* fissile atom production (from fertile U-238) to the rate of fissile atom destruction:

$$
CR = \frac{\text{Fissile atoms produced per unit time}}{\text{Fissile atoms destroyed per unit time}}
$$

**Step 2 — Thermal reactor analysis (3 marks):**

For a thermal reactor, $\eta$ (neutrons per absorption in U-235) ≈ **2.07**. Of these, **1 neutron is needed to sustain the chain reaction**, and the remaining ~1.07 can be used to capture in U-238 to make Pu-239, *but* some are lost to:
- Leakage from the core
- Absorption in structural materials, coolant, fission products
- Capture in Pu-239 itself (which has high $\sigma_c$ in the thermal spectrum)

In practice, the thermal CR is:

$$
CR_{thermal} = \eta - 1 - L
$$

where $L$ is the total fractional neutron loss. Typical values yield **CR ≈ 0.55 – 0.65** for PWRs and **CR ≈ 0.7 – 0.8** for PHWRs (due to better neutron economy). Hence **CR < 1**.

**Step 3 — Fast reactor analysis (2 marks):**
In a fast spectrum, $\eta_{239}$ for Pu-239 rises to **≈ 2.4 – 2.6** at energies above 1 MeV. With **no moderator** to slow neutrons and reduced parasitic capture (no water, no boron), leakage + losses can be reduced to **< 0.4**. Then:

$$
CR_{fast} = \eta_{239} - 1 - L = 2.4 - 1 - 0.3 = 1.1
$$

Hence **CR > 1**, meaning the reactor *breeds* more fissile material than it consumes. India's PFBR (Prototype Fast Breeder Reactor) at Kalpakkam achieves **CR ≈ 1.05**.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks in Module 2 — Reactor Fuel Cycle:**
> 1. **Confusing "enrichment" with "conversion"** — Enrichment is the *isotopic* concentration of U-235; conversion is the *transmutation* of U-238 to Pu-239. Examiners deduct 1 mark for mixing these.
> 2. **Units in burnup problems** — Always express BU in **MWd/MTU** or **GWd/MTU**, never in J/kg without conversion. A frequent mistake is forgetting to convert days → seconds or MTU → kg.
> 3. **PUREX chemistry** — Students often write "TBP separates Pu from U" without specifying that *separation occurs in the partition column via a reductant*, not in the extraction column. This is a **2-mark** error.
> 4. **Open vs Closed Cycle confusion** — Note that "Closed Cycle" does *not* mean "no waste." It means spent fuel is reprocessed. Vitrified HLW is still produced and still requires a geological repository.
> 5. **Conversion Ratio derivation** — A complete answer must show that $CR = \eta - 1 - L$ and clearly state why $\eta$ is higher in fast spectra. A common error is treating CR as simply $\Sigma_{c,238}/\Sigma_{a,235}$ without accounting for Pu-239 destruction.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Reactor Fuel Cycle):**
> - **Front-end stages (5):** Mining → Milling → Conversion → Enrichment → Fuel Fabrication.
> - **Yellowcake = U₃O₈**; **UF₆** is the gaseous compound used for enrichment.
> - **Enrichment technologies:** Gaseous diffusion (obsolete), gas centrifuge (dominant), laser (emerging).
> - **Enrichment levels:** Natural 0.711 %, LEU 3–5 % (LWRs), HEU > 20 % (research/Weapons), HALEU 5–20 % (advanced reactors).
> - **Fuel residence time in core:** 18–24 months (LWR), 12 months (PHWR), continuous (CANDU on-power refueling).
> - **Typical burnups:** 7,000 MWd/MTU (CANDU natural U), 45,000 MWd/MTU (modern LWR), up to 100,000 MWd/MTU (proposed fast reactors).
> - **Conversion Ratio:** CR < 1 for thermal, CR > 1 for fast breeders.
> - **Decay heat** follows $P_d \propto t^{-0.2}$ long-term (Way-Wigner / ANSI/ANS-5.1).
> - **PUREX process** uses **TBP in kerosene** for co-extraction and a **reducing agent** for partition.
> - **Open cycle:** USA; **Closed cycle:** France, Russia, India, Japan.
> - **Geological disposal depth:** > 500 m, in granite/salt/clay, with multi-barrier (glass + canister + bentonite + host rock).
> - **Per 1 GWe-year:** ~160 tonnes natural U (once-through) or ~30 tonnes (closed cycle with Pu recycle).
> - **Spent fuel composition (after 45 GWd/MTU):** ~95 % U (with ~0.8 % U-235), ~1 % Pu, ~4 % fission products + minor actinides.
> - **Key cross-sections to remember:** $\sigma_{a,235} = 585$ b, $\sigma_{c,238} = 0.003$ b (thermal), $\sigma_{a,Xe-135} = 2.6 \times 10^6$ b.
> - **Mass-balance equations for enrichment:** $F = P + W$ and $F w_f = P w_p + W w_t$.
> - **Value function:** $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln[x/(1-x)]$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fuel Cycle Material Balance, Uranium Mining and Milling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Fuel Cycle Material Balance, Uranium Mining and Milling # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Nuclear Fuel Cycle — A Top-Level Definition The **Nuclear Fuel Cycle** is the complete chain of industrial processes that converts raw uranium ore into usable nuclear fuel for a reactor, manages the spent fuel after irradiation, and ultimately disposes of (or recycles) the radioactive by-products. It is divided into two major arcs: - **Front-End:** Mining → Milling → Conversion → Enrichment → Fuel Fabrication - **Back-End:** Spent Fuel Storage → Reprocessing (optional) → Recycling → Waste Disposal > [!IMPORTANT] > **Material Balance** is the engineering accounting tool that tracks every kilogram (or tonne) of uranium as it physically flows, concentrates, splits, and re-combines across each unit operation of this cycle. No mass is created or destroyed — it is only redistributed among process streams. ## 1.2 What Does "Material Balance" Mean in This Context? A **material balance** is a quantitative statement of the conservation of mass applied to a process, a single equipment item, or the entire fuel cycle. For any chosen process boundary: $$\text{Input} + \text{Generation} = \text{Output} + \text{Accumulation} + \text{Consumption}$$ In the nuclear fuel cycle, "consumption" acquires a real, physical meaning: a fraction of the $^{235}$U is actually **burned** (fissioned) in the reactor, and a small amount of $^{238}$U is bred into fissile $^{239}$Pu. Material balance must therefore account for **isotopic** as well as **elemental** mass flows. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Bakery Dough Story Imagine a bakery that buys raw wheat, processes it into flour, then into dough, then bakes bread. - The **wheat** entering the mill is like **uranium ore** entering the mill. - The **bran and chaff** removed is the **mill tailings** (waste rock). - The **flour** leaving the mill is the **yellowcake (U$_3$O$_8$)** leaving the mill. - The flour is then **enriched** (extra nutritious parts separated) — this is the **enrichment plant**. - The final **bread loaf** is the **assembled fuel bundle** going into the reactor. A material balance is the bakery manager's ledger: *How much wheat went in? How much flour came out? How much was wasted at each step? Where did every gram end up?* In nuclear engineering, this ledger must be perfect because uranium is **strategic, radioactive, fissile, and heavily regulated** — losses above tolerance are economically and legally unacceptable. ## 1.4 Uranium Mining — The Starting Point **Uranium mining** is the extraction of uranium-bearing ore from the Earth's crust. The economically viable ore grades today are astonishingly low — typically **0.01 % to 0.3 % U$_3$O$_8$** by weight. This means that to obtain 1 kg of natural uranium, you must extract and process roughly **500 kg to 10,000 kg of ore**. > [!NOTE] > **Standard ore grade reference:** The average economic ore grade in 2024 is around **0.10 % U$_3$O$_8$** (≈ 1000 ppm U). The world average is closer to **0.03 %** for very large sandstone deposits. ## 1.5 Uranium Milling — Concentrating the Treasure **Milling** is the physical and chemical processing of mined ore to produce a concentrated, transportable uranium product known as **Yellowcake (U$_3$O$_8$)**. The product typically contains **80 – 90 % U$_3$O$_8$**, a thousand-fold concentration compared to the ore. The standard hydrometallurgical route is: 1. **Crushing & Grinding** (comminution) 2. **Leaching** (acid or alkaline) 3. **Solid–Liquid Separation** 4. **Solvent Extraction / Ion Exchange** 5. **Precipitation** of ammonium diuranate → calcined to U$_3$O$_8$ ## 1.6 Why KTU Asks This Question This topic sits at the foundation of every later module — enrichment calculations, reactor physics, and waste management all depend on understanding **where uranium comes from, what purity it has, and how mass flows through the cycle**. A KTU question here usually probes your ability to set up a **mass balance equation** and compute a recovery or split fraction. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Front-End Fuel Cycle — Stage-by-Stage Logic ### Stage 1: Uranium Mining - **Open-Pit (Surface) Mining** — used for shallow, large, low-grade deposits. Removes overburden and ore in benches. - **Underground Mining** — used for deep deposits. Higher cost, higher selectivity. - **In-Situ Leaching (ISL) / In-Situ Recovery (ISR)** — a chemical method where a leaching solution is injected into the ore body, dissolves the uranium underground, and is pumped back to the surface. **Lowest environmental footprint, now accounts for >50 % of global production.** ### Stage 2: Uranium Milling - The mill receives **run-of-mine ore**. - **Comminution** liberates uranium minerals from the gangue. - **Leaching agent:** typically **sulfuric acid (H$_2$SO$_4$)** for most ores, or **sodium carbonate (Na$_2$CO$_3$)** for high-lime ores. The reaction for acid leach: $$\text{UO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{UO}_2^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$ - The pregnant leach solution (PLS) contains **typically 0.5 – 2 g/L** of uranium. - **Solvent Extraction** uses an organic phase (e.g., tertiary amines in kerosene) to selectively extract UO$_2^{2+}$ from the aqueous phase. - **Stripping** recovers uranium into a concentrated aqueous phase. - **Precipitation** as **ammonium diuranate (ADU)** $\left(\text{NH}_4\right)_2\text{U}_2\text{O}_7$ is then filtered and calcined to yield U$_3$O$_8$. ## 2.2 The Material Balance — General Framework For any process unit, the steady-state mass balance is: $$F = P + W$$ where **$F$** is the feed mass flow, **$P$** is the product mass flow, and **$W$** is the waste (tailings) mass flow. The **component (uranium) balance** is: $$F \cdot x_F = P \cdot x_P + W \cdot x_W$$ where $x$ denotes the **mass fraction (or assay)** of uranium in each stream. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** you may need for a 14-mark derivation or a 3-mark definition in this module. | # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units | |---|---------|---------|---------------------| | 1 | Total Mass Balance | $F = P + W$ | Mass flow in = mass flow out, steady state (kg/h or t/day) | | 2 | Component Balance | $F\,x_F = P\,x_P + W\,x_W$ | Uranium mass balance; $x$ is mass fraction (dimensionless) | | 3 | Recovery (Efficiency) | $R = \dfrac{P\,x_P}{F\,x_F} = \dfrac{\text{U in product}}{\text{U in feed}}$ | Typical milling recovery: 85 – 95 % | | 4 | Concentration Ratio (CR) | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{x_P}{x_F}$ | Mass of ore per mass of product (also called upgrade ratio) | | 5 | Tonnage Factor | $\text{TF} = \dfrac{1}{x_P \, \rho_{\text{ore}} \, C}$ | Tonnes of ore per kg of U (C = 0.0001 factor) | | 6 | Enrichment Mass Balance | $F = P + T$ | Feed, Product, Tails (kg U) | | 7 | Enrichment Isotope Balance | $F\,x_F = P\,x_P + T\,x_T$ | For $^{235}$U component (decimal fractions) | | 8 | Tails Assay (typical) | $x_T = 0.002$ to $0.003$ | 0.2 – 0.3 % $^{235}$U (modern plants) | | 9 | Natural Assay (feed) | $x_F = 0.00711$ | 0.711 % $^{235}$U in natural U | | 10 | Reactor Grade Product | $x_P = 0.03$ to $0.05$ | 3 – 5 % $^{235}$U for LWR | | 11 | Separative Work Unit | $\text{SWU} = P\,V(x_P) + T\,V(x_T) - F\,V(x_F)$ | $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x}{1-x}\right)$ — value function | | 12 | Ore Cutoff Grade | $x_{\text{cut}} \approx 0.03 \,\% \text{ U}_3\text{O}_8$ | Economic minimum for open-pit | ## 2.4 The "Value Function" $V(x)$ Explained The function $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x}{1-x}\right)$ is the cornerstone of enrichment calculations. It represents the **"effort"** required to change a unit of material from natural composition to a desired assay. - $V(x)$ is **negative** for $x < 0.5$ and **positive** for $x > 0.5$. - The SWU tells us the **enrichment plant capacity** consumed. ## 2.5 Real-World Utility - **Mining engineers** use material balance to size pits, estimate reserves, and plan production schedules. - **Mill managers** use it to optimize reagent consumption, predict tailings volumes, and report regulatory compliance. - **Enrichment plant economists** use the SWU formula to price enrichment services globally (the world market is dominated by Urenco, Orano, and Tenex). - **Policy makers** use cumulative cycle mass balance to project **uranium demand vs. supply** decades into the future. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Universal Two-Stream Material Balance We begin with the simplest possible system: **one feed stream, one product stream, one waste stream**, all in steady state. We want a formula that gives the **recovery** $R$ in terms of the assays of all three streams. **Step 1:** Write the **total mass balance** (conservation of total mass): $$F = P + W$$ **Step 2:** Write the **uranium component balance** (uranium entering = uranium leaving): $$F\,x_F = P\,x_P + W\,x_W$$ **Step 3:** From Step 1, express the waste flow as $W = F - P$. Substitute into Step 2: $$F\,x_F = P\,x_P + (F - P)\,x_W$$ **Step 4:** Expand the right-hand side: $$F\,x_F = P\,x_P + F\,x_W - P\,x_W$$ **Step 5:** Group all terms containing $P$ on the right: $$F\,x_F - F\,x_W = P\,x_P - P\,x_W$$ $$F(x_F - x_W) = P(x_P - x_W)$$ **Step 6:** Solve for $P/F$, the **yield fraction**: $$\frac{P}{F} = \frac{x_F - x_W}{x_P - x_W}$$ **Step 7:** The recovery $R$ is the fraction of uranium in the feed that ends up in the product: $$R = \frac{P\,x_P}{F\,x_F} = \frac{x_P}{x_F}\cdot\frac{x_F - x_W}{x_P - x_W}$$ This is the **canonical recovery formula** for a 3-stream process. Rearranged equivalently: $$\boxed{\,R = \frac{x_P\,(x_F - x_W)}{x_F\,(x_P - x_W)}\,}$$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Tonnage Required for a Given Uranium Output A KTU favourite. Given: ore grade, plant recovery, and required U production, find the **ore tonnage**. **Problem data (assume):** $x_F = 0.001$ (i.e., 0.1 %), $R = 0.90$, target U output $M_U = 1000$ kg/yr. **Step 1:** From the recovery definition: $$R = \frac{M_U}{F\,x_F} \quad\Rightarrow\quad F = \frac{M_U}{R\,x_F}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute numbers: $$F = \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{0.90 \times 0.001} = 1{,}111{,}111 \text{ kg} \approx 1111 \text{ tonnes of ore}$$ **Step 3:** Concentration ratio (sanity check): $$\text{CR} = \frac{x_P}{x_F} \approx \frac{1}{0.001} = 1000$$ So we need to process roughly **1000 tonnes of ore per tonne of yellowcake**, consistent with industry norms. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Full Enrichment Plant Mass Balance **Given:** $x_F = 0.00711$, $x_P = 0.04$ (4 % reactor-grade), $x_T = 0.0025$, required product $P = 1000$ kg U. **Step 1 — Total mass balance:** $$F = P + T \quad\Rightarrow\quad F = 1000 + T$$ **Step 2 — $^{235}$U component balance:** $$F \cdot 0.00711 = 1000 \cdot 0.04 + T \cdot 0.0025$$ **Step 3 — Substitute Step 1 into Step 2:** $$(1000 + T) \cdot 0.00711 = 40 + 0.0025\,T$$ **Step 4 — Expand:** $$7.11 + 0.00711\,T = 40 + 0.0025\,T$$ **Step 5 — Collect $T$ terms on the left:** $$0.00711\,T - 0.0025\,T = 40 - 7.11$$ $$0.00461\,T = 32.89$$ **Step 6 — Solve for $T$:** $$T = \frac{32.89}{0.00461} \approx 7134.5 \text{ kg U}$$ **Step 7 — Find $F$:** $$F = 1000 + 7134.5 = 8134.5 \text{ kg U}$$ **Step 8 — Compute SWU** using the value function $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x}{1-x}\right)$: - $V(0.00711) = (0.01422 - 1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.00711}{0.99289}\right) = (-0.98578)(-4.944) \approx 4.873$ - $V(0.04) = (0.08 - 1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.04}{0.96}\right) = (-0.92)(-3.178) \approx 2.924$ - $V(0.0025) = (0.005 - 1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{0.0025}{0.9975}\right) = (-0.995)(-5.991) \approx 5.961$ **Step 9 — Apply the SWU formula:** $$\text{SWU} = P\,V(x_P) + T\,V(x_T) - F\,V(x_F)$$ $$\text{SWU} = 1000(2.924) + 7134.5(5.961) - 8134.5(4.873)$$ $$\text{SWU} = 2924 + 42525.7 - 39642.3 \approx 5807.4 \text{ SWU}$$ **Interpretation:** To produce 1000 kg of 4 %-enriched uranium, the plant must process 8134.5 kg of natural feed and expend **≈ 5807 SWU** of work. The Tails (depleted U) amount to 7134.5 kg. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Mining Method vs. Ore Body | Parameter | Open-Pit | Underground | In-Situ Leach (ISL) | |---|---|---|---| | Depth range | 0 – 200 m | > 200 m | 50 – 400 m | | Typical ore grade (%) | 0.05 – 0.30 | 0.10 – 0.50 | 0.04 – 0.15 | | Capital cost | High | Very High | Low | | Operating cost | Moderate | High | Lowest | | Recovery (%) | 75 – 90 | 80 – 95 | 60 – 85 | | Tailings volume | Huge | Moderate | None (in-situ) | | Radiation exposure to workers | High | Moderate | Lowest | | Environmental footprint | Large | Large | Smallest | | Examples | Rössing (Namibia), Ranger (Australia) | Cigar Lake (Canada) | Akouta (Niger), Akdala (Kazakhstan) | ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Milling Process Parameters | Parameter | Acid Leach | Alkaline Leach | |---|---|---| | Reagent | H$_2$SO$_4$ | Na$_2$CO$_3$ + NaHCO$_3$ | | pH | 1.5 – 2.0 | 9 – 10.5 | | Temperature (°C) | 30 – 50 | 80 – 120 (autoclave) | | Ore suitability | Silicate, sandstone | High-lime, carbonate | | Recovery (%) | 90 – 98 | 75 – 90 | | Reactor vessel | Open tank | Pressure autoclave | | Typical cost (USD/kg U) | Lower | Higher | > [!NOTE] > Globally, **>80 %** of uranium mills use **acid leaching** because of higher recoveries and lower operating cost. # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **Define the term "material balance" as applied to a nuclear fuel cycle. State the two governing equations used in a steady-state, three-stream uranium milling process.** **Model Answer:** A **material balance** is the application of the law of conservation of mass to a process or unit operation. It quantifies the input, output, and accumulation of matter (and specific components) within a defined system boundary. In the nuclear fuel cycle, it is used to track uranium and its isotopes through every stage from ore to spent fuel. For a steady-state uranium mill with feed (F), product (P), and tailings (W): **(i) Total mass balance:** $F = P + W$ **(ii) Uranium component balance:** $F\,x_F = P\,x_P + W\,x_W$ These two equations form the basis for computing recovery, yield, and grade. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **What is yellowcake? State its typical chemical formula and uranium purity. Why is it the chosen commercial form of uranium for transport?** **Model Answer:** **Yellowcake** is the concentrated uranium product obtained from the milling of uranium ore. Its chemical formula is **U$_3$O$_8$** (triuranium octoxide). It is a coarse, yellow-brown powder containing **typically 80 – 90 % U$_3$O$_8$** (≈ 70 – 80 % U by element mass). It is the chosen commercial form because: - It is **chemically stable** at ambient conditions (no special inert atmosphere required). - It is **non-volatile**, simplifying transport. - It has a **high uranium density**, reducing transport volume and cost. - It can be **stored indefinitely** in sealed drums before further processing. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > KTU ESE pattern: Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an internal choice. The structure below mirrors that exactly. ### Question A (14 Marks) **`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe, with a neat flow diagram, the various **methods of uranium mining**. Compare open-pit mining and in-situ leaching in terms of ore grade suitability, capital cost, recovery, and environmental impact. **(b) [7 Marks]** A uranium mill receives **50,000 tonnes/day** of ore grading **0.10 % U$_3$O$_8$**. The mill recovery is **90 %**, and the yellowcake product contains **85 % U$_3$O$_8$**. Calculate: (i) Daily yellowcake production (tonnes/day) (ii) The concentration ratio (iii) Daily tailings mass and grade (iv) The enrichment plant's feed requirement if 100 % of this yellowcake is converted to UF$_6$ and enriched to 4 % $^{235}$U (tails assay = 0.25 %, neglect SWU). #### Part A — (a) Model Solution **Methods of uranium mining:** 1. **Open-pit (Surface) Mining** — Used for shallow, large, low-grade deposits. Overburden is stripped, ore is drilled, blasted, and hauled. 2. **Underground Mining** — Used for deep deposits; shafts and tunnels give access; methods include room-and-pillar and cut-and-fill. 3. **In-Situ Leaching (ISL)** — Leaching solution is circulated through a permeable ore body; uranium is dissolved underground and pumped to the surface. 4. **Heap Leaching** — Crushed ore is piled on a pad and irrigated with acid; PLS is collected at the base. 5. **By-Product Recovery** — Uranium extracted as a by-product of gold, copper, or phosphate mining. **Comparison Table:** | Criterion | Open-Pit | In-Situ Leaching | |---|---|---| | Ore grade | 0.05 – 0.30 % | 0.04 – 0.15 % | | Capital cost | High | Low | | Recovery | 75 – 90 % | 60 – 85 % | | Tailings | Large surface impoundment | None | | Environment | Significant disturbance | Minimal surface disturbance | | Worker radiation | High | Lowest | | Examples | Rössing, Ranger | Akouta, Inkai | **Conclusion:** ISL is now the dominant method globally for sandstone deposits due to its low cost and minimal environmental footprint. #### Part A — (b) Model Solution **Given:** - $F = 50{,}000$ t/day - Ore grade: $x_F = 0.10\% = 0.001$ (mass fraction U$_3$O$_8$) - $R = 0.90$ - $x_P = 0.85$ (product purity) **(i) Daily yellowcake production:** Mass of U$_3$O$_8$ in feed $= 50{,}000 \times 0.001 = 50$ t/day Recovered (90 %) $= 50 \times 0.90 = 45$ t U$_3$O$_8$/day This is the **yellowcake product** $P = 45$ t/day (since $P$ itself has 85 % U$_3$O$_8$ content, all 45 t is U$_3$O$_8$). **(ii) Concentration Ratio:** $$\text{CR} = \frac{x_P}{x_F} = \frac{0.85}{0.001} = 850$$ So roughly **850 tonnes of ore yield 1 tonne of yellowcake**. **(iii) Tailings mass and grade:** Total mass balance: $W = F - P = 50{,}000 - 45 = 49{,}955$ t/day Tailings grade: Solve the uranium balance for $x_W$: $$F\,x_F = P\,x_P + W\,x_W$$ $$50{,}000 \times 0.001 = 45 \times 0.85 + 49{,}955 \times x_W$$ $$50 = 38.25 + 49{,}955\,x_W$$ $$x_W = \frac{50 - 38.25}{49{,}955} = \frac{11.75}{49{,}955} \approx 0.000235 = 0.0235\%$$ **(iv) Enrichment plant feed (assuming full downstream conversion to UF$_6$):** We have $P = 45$ t/day of U$_3$O$_8$ from the mill. To obtain the uranium mass: $$M_U = 45 \times 0.848 = 38.16 \text{ t/day}$$ (The factor 0.848 = 3 × 238 / (3×238 + 8×16) = 714 / 842 = 0.848 is the mass fraction of U in U$_3$O$_8$.) But all 45 t/day is yellowcake → 38.16 t U/day enters the enrichment plant as natural UF$_6$. Set $F = M_U = 38.16$ t/day, $x_F = 0.00711$, $x_P = 0.04$, $x_T = 0.0025$. Enrichment mass balance: $$F = P + T \quad\Rightarrow\quad 38.16 = P + T$$ $$F\,x_F = P\,x_P + T\,x_T \quad\Rightarrow\quad 38.16 \times 0.00711 = 0.04\,P + 0.0025\,T$$ From the first: $T = 38.16 - P$. Substitute: $$0.2712 = 0.04\,P + 0.0025(38.16 - P)$$ $$0.2712 = 0.04\,P + 0.0954 - 0.0025\,P$$ $$0.2712 - 0.0954 = 0.0375\,P$$ $$0.1758 = 0.0375\,P \quad\Rightarrow\quad P = 4.688 \text{ t/day}$$ **Result:** About **4.69 t/day of 4 %-enriched uranium** is produced, and tails $T = 38.16 - 4.69 = 33.47$ t/day are discarded. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Adaptation]`** **(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete uranium milling process** from run-of-mine ore to yellowcake. State the role of each stage and the typical purity achieved. **(b) [7 Marks]** In a uranium enrichment plant operating in steady state, the following data is given: - Natural feed assay: $x_F = 0.00711$ ($^{235}$U fraction) - Product assay: $x_P = 0.04$ - Tails assay: $x_T = 0.0025$ - Desired enriched-uranium output: $P = 1500$ kg/day - Find: (i) Feed flow $F$, (ii) Tails flow $T$, (iii) Total SWU/day, using $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x}{1-x}\right)$. #### Part B — (a) Model Solution **Uranium Milling Process — Block Diagram & Stage-wise Role:** ``` [ROM Ore] → [Crushing] → [Grinding] → [Leaching] → [S/L Separation] → [Solvent Extraction] → [Stripping] → [Precipitation (ADU)] → [Calcination] → [Yellowcake U3O8, 80–90% purity] ``` | Stage | Equipment | Role | |---|---|---| | 1. Crushing | Jaw + Cone crusher | Reduces ore size to <25 mm | | 2. Grinding | Rod/ball mill | Pulverizes to <0.2 mm, liberates U minerals | | 3. Leaching | Acid (H$_2$SO$_4$) or Alkaline (Na$_2$CO$_3$) tanks | Dissolves UO$_2$ into UO$_2^{2+}$ in solution | | 4. S/L Separation | CCD thickeners, filters | Separates pregnant leach solution from solids | | 5. Solvent Extraction | Mixer-settlers with amines in kerosene | Selectively extracts U from dilute PLS | | 6. Stripping | Aqueous NH$_3$/(NH$_4$)$_2$SO$_4$ | Transfers U from organic to concentrated aqueous | | 7. Precipitation | Neutralization with NH$_4$OH | Precipitates ammonium diuranate (ADU) | | 8. Calcination | Rotary kiln at 800 °C | Converts ADU to stable U$_3$O$_8$ (yellowcake) | **Final purity:** 80 – 90 % U$_3$O$_8$ in drummed product. #### Part B — (b) Model Solution **Given:** $x_F = 0.00711$, $x_P = 0.04$, $x_T = 0.0025$, $P = 1500$ kg/day. **Step 1 — Total mass balance:** $F = P + T$ ⟹ $F = 1500 + T$ **Step 2 — $^{235}$U component balance:** $$F\,x_F = P\,x_P + T\,x_T$$ **Step 3 — Substitute:** $$(1500 + T)(0.00711) = 1500(0.04) + T(0.0025)$$ $$10.665 + 0.00711\,T = 60 + 0.0025\,T$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $T$:** $$0.00711\,T - 0.0025\,T = 60 - 10.665$$ $$0.00461\,T = 49.335$$ $$T = \frac{49.335}{0.00461} \approx 10{,}701.7 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Step 5 — Find $F$:** $$F = 1500 + 10{,}701.7 = 12{,}201.7 \text{ kg/day}$$ **Step 6 — Compute value functions** (re-using values from Section 3.3): $V(0.00711) \approx 4.873$ $V(0.04) \approx 2.924$ $V(0.0025) \approx 5.961$ **Step 7 — SWU formula:** $$\text{SWU} = P\,V(x_P) + T\,V(x_T) - F\,V(x_F)$$ $$\text{SWU} = 1500(2.924) + 10{,}701.7(5.961) - 12{,}201.7(4.873)$$ $$\text{SWU} = 4386 + 63{,}793.8 - 59{,}458.9 \approx 8720.9 \text{ SWU/day}$$ **Final Answers:** - (i) $F = 12{,}201.7$ kg/day - (ii) $T = 10{,}701.7$ kg/day - (iii) Total SWU = **8721 SWU/day** --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes that cost KTU students 2 – 4 marks per answer:** > 1. **Unit confusion in $x_F$:** Some students use 0.711 (percent) instead of **0.00711** (decimal fraction) in the $^{235}$U balance. This single error cascades through the entire problem and produces a tails flow 100× too small. > 2. **Forgetting the tails stream:** In enrichment mass balance, students often write $F = P$ alone, ignoring the depleted tails. Always account for **all three streams**. > 3. **Confusing recovery with yield:** Recovery $R$ is a uranium-mass ratio; yield $Y$ is a total-mass ratio. They are equal **only** if product and feed have the same uranium grade (rare). > 4. **Sign error in value function:** $V(x)$ is **negative** for $x < 0.5$. Plugging the wrong sign gives negative SWU — physically meaningless. > 5. **In yellowcake purity problems, students forget that "85 % U$_3$O$_8$" is 85 % of the compound U$_3$O$_8$, not 85 % elemental U.** To convert: $M_U = M_{\text{YC}} \times 0.848$. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-density revision checklist — re-read 24 hours before the KTU exam:** - ☐ **Fuel cycle stages:** Mining → Milling → Conversion → Enrichment → Fabrication → Reactor → (Reprocessing) → Waste. - ☐ **Yellowcake = U$_3$O$_8$,** purity 80 – 90 %, mass fraction of U in U$_3$O$_8$ = **0.848**. - ☐ **Two master equations:** $F = P + W$ and $F\,x_F = P\,x_P + W\,x_W$. - ☐ **Recovery formula:** $R = \dfrac{x_P\,(x_F - x_W)}{x_F\,(x_P - x_W)}$. - ☐ **Concentration Ratio:** $\text{CR} = x_P / x_F$ (often 500 – 1000 in milling). - ☐ **Enrichment assays (memorize):** $x_F = 0.00711$ (natural), $x_T = 0.002$ – $0.003$ (modern tails), $x_P = 0.03$ – $0.05$ (LWR), $x_P = 0.90$ (HEU weapons/medical). - ☐ **SWU formula:** $\text{SWU} = P\,V(x_P) + T\,V(x_T) - F\,V(x_F)$, with $V(x) = (2x-1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{x}{1-x}\right)$. - ☐ **ISL/ISR** is now the **dominant** mining method globally (>50 % of world production). - ☐ **Acid leach** dominates over alkaline leach (≈ 80 % of mills). - ☐ **Typical ore grades:** 0.01 – 0.3 % U$_3$O$_8$ (1 – 3000 ppm). - ☐ **Typical milling recovery:** 85 – 95 %. - ☐ **Unit consistency is non-negotiable:** keep all assays in **decimal fractions** (NOT percent) in mass-balance equations. - ☐ For KTU Part B 14-mark questions, always draw a **block diagram** — easy 1 – 2 marks for diagram clarity. - ☐ The **value function $V(x)$ is negative for $x < 0.5$** — keep this in mind for all SWU problems.

topics.title: Uranium Conversion to Uranium Hexafluoride
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Uranium Conversion to Uranium Hexafluoride

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Uranium Conversion** is the chemical process in the front-end of the nuclear fuel cycle whereby uranium ore concentrate (UOC), commonly known as **"yellowcake"** (predominantly $U_3O_8$ or $UO_3$), is chemically transformed into **uranium hexafluoride ($UF_6$)**, also called "hex."

The **uranium hexafluoride** molecule has the chemical formula $UF_6$, with a **molecular weight of 352.02 g/mol** and a **uranium mass fraction of approximately 67.6%**. It is the **only uranium compound that is gaseous at practically achievable temperatures** (sublimes at **64 °C at 1 atm**), making it the universal feedstock for all major commercial isotope enrichment technologies (gaseous diffusion, gas centrifuge, and laser isotope separation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic belongs to the **upstream/conversion stage** of the nuclear fuel cycle, sitting *between* mining/milling and enrichment. Mastery of this stage is mandatory for the Module 2 learning outcomes on fuel cycle front-end processing.

## 1.2 Why Uranium Hexafluoride? — The Strategic Rationale

The strategic selection of $UF_6$ as the **enrichment working fluid** is governed by three simultaneous engineering requirements:

1. **Volatility:** A single compound must transition easily between solid and gas phases without decomposition.
2. **Chemical Purity:** The compound must not introduce problematic contaminants into enrichment cascades.
3. **Isotopic Accessibility:** Molecules must be small enough for efficient isotope-mass separation (a $^235U$ mass difference of ~1.26% relative to $^238U$ demands the lightest possible working molecule).

$UF_6$ uniquely satisfies all three because fluorine is **monoisotopic** ($^{19}F$, 100% natural abundance), so the entire mass difference between uranium isotopes is preserved in the molecule's reduced mass.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The Coffee Grinder Metaphor

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy:** Think of yellowcake as **whole coffee beans** that you cannot brew directly. To get a refined product (enriched uranium), you must first grind the beans into a soluble, easy-to-handle powder. $UF_6$ is essentially the **"ground coffee" of nuclear fuel** — a gaseous, easily transportable, easily separable form of uranium. The conversion plant is the "grinder," and the enrichment plant is the "filter that separates the strong brew from the weak one." Just as you would never try to brew coffee by dropping whole beans into water, engineers never try to enrich uranium directly from a refractory solid like $UO_2$.

## 1.4 Physical & Chemical Properties of $UF_6$

| Property | Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Molecular formula | $UF_6$ |
| Molar mass | **352.02 g/mol** |
| Melting point (1 atm) | **64.05 °C** (triple point) |
| Boiling point (1 atm) | **56.5 °C** (sublimes — does not truly "boil" at 1 atm) |
| Sublimation point | **~56 °C at 1 atm** |
| Critical temperature | **230 °C** |
| Critical pressure | **4.61 MPa** |
| Density (solid at 20 °C) | **~4.87 g/cm³** |
| Density (gas at 70 °C, 1 atm) | **~13.3 g/L** |
| Appearance (solid) | Colorless, crystalline |
| Appearance (gas) | Colorless |
| Reactivity with water | **Violent hydrolysis** → $UO_2F_2$ + HF |
| Container compatibility | Nickel, Monel, aluminum, Teflon (NOT glass, NOT steel with moisture) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Handling Property:** $UF_6$ reacts violently with water (even atmospheric moisture), releasing highly corrosive and toxic **hydrofluoric acid (HF)**. All conversion plant piping, valves, and storage vessels are therefore constructed from **Monel, nickel, or aluminum alloys** and are leak-tested rigorously. This is a frequent short-answer question in KTU examinations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Chemistry — The Three Sequential Reactions

The conversion of yellowcake to $UF_6$ is industrially performed as a **two-stage or three-stage dry fluorination sequence**. Each reaction has carefully selected temperature and reagent windows.

### Stage 1: Reduction of $UO_3$ to $UO_2$ (if starting from $UO_3$)

> [!NOTE]
> Many industrial plants receive $UO_3$ directly (e.g., from the AUC — Ammonium Uranyl Carbonate — precipitation route). The first step is reduction to the more reactive dioxide.

$$UO_3 + H_2 \xrightarrow{600\text{–}700\,°C} UO_2 + H_2O$$

- **Reagent:** Cracked ammonia ($H_2$) or pure hydrogen.
- **Reactor type:** Fluidized bed (best for heat/mass transfer with powders).
- **Why this step?** $UO_2$ is more reactive toward HF than $UO_3$, ensuring cleaner fluorination in stage 2.

If the feed is **$U_3O_8$** (the most common yellowcake from sulfuric acid leaching), it is **first calcined to $UO_3$** in air, then reduced:

$$U_3O_8 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \xrightarrow{400\text{–}500\,°C} 3\,UO_3$$

### Stage 2: Hydrofluorination — Conversion to $UF_4$ (Green Salt)

$$UO_2 + 4\,HF \xrightarrow{350\text{–}600\,°C} UF_4 + 2\,H_2O$$

- **Product:** $UF_4$ is a green, water-insoluble solid (called **"green salt"** in industry).
- **Reactor:** Fluidized bed or moving-bucket conveyor (Monel-lined).
- **Heat of reaction:** Exothermic (~189 kJ/mol); temperature control is critical.
- **HF source:** Produced *in situ* by electrolysis of $HF \cdot KF$ (Fremy's salt route) or supplied as anhydrous HF.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The intermediate $UF_4$ is preferred over direct $UO_2 \rightarrow UF_6$ because the **milder two-step chemistry** is easier to control, produces fewer gaseous impurities, and avoids runaway exotherms.

### Stage 3: Fluorination — Conversion to $UF_6$ (Hex)

$$UF_4 + F_2 \xrightarrow{300\text{–}400\,°C} UF_6$$

- **Reagent:** Elemental fluorine gas (typically generated by electrolysis of $KF \cdot 2HF$).
- **Reactor:** Flame tower or fluidized bed.
- **Product:** $UF_6$ gas, which is collected by **desublimation** (cold traps at ~ -10 °C to -40 °C) on Monel surfaces as a white solid.

### Alternative Single-Stage Direct Fluorination

Some processes bypass the $UF_4$ intermediate:

$$UO_2 + 3\,F_2 \xrightarrow{500\text{–}700\,°C} UF_6 + O_2$$

This "**one-step**" route is thermodynamically favorable ($\Delta G < 0$) but produces violent exotherms and is generally avoided in modern plants.

## 2.2 Industrial Process Routes

| Process | Originator | Feed Material | Key Feature |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Comurhex I/II** | France (Orano) | $U_3O_8$ | Most widely used globally; continuous fluidized bed |
| **BNFL Route** | United Kingdom | $UO_3$ | Uses $H_2$ reduction + HF in single fluidized bed |
| **Allied Chemical** | USA (Honeywell) | $UF_4$ feed | Cold fluorination of pre-made $UF_4$ |
| **Wet Solvent-Extraction Route** | Russia, China | Crude UOC | Dissolves U in $HNO_3$, extracts with TBP, then fluorinates |

> [!NOTE]
> The **wet route** uses solvent extraction with **tributyl phosphate (TBP)** in kerosene to purify the uranium as $UO_2(NO_3)_2 \cdot 2TBP$, then converts to $UO_3$, and finally to $UF_6$. It is preferred when the yellowcake is impure (e.g., from ion-exchange precipitation).

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Stoichiometry Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ (raw pipe) in mathematical contexts to avoid table syntax conflicts in markdown.

| Equation / Quantity | Formula | Units / Boundary Conditions |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Stoichiometric HF requirement | $n_{HF} = 4 \times n_{U}$ | moles; assumes 100% conversion |
| Stoichiometric $F_2$ requirement | $n_{F_2} = n_{U}$ | moles; for $UF_4 \rightarrow UF_6$ |
| Uranium mass fraction in $UF_6$ | $w_U = \dfrac{238.03}{352.02} \approx 0.676$ | dimensionless |
| Theoretical mass yield | $m_{UF_6} = m_U \times \dfrac{352.02}{238.03} \approx 1.479\,m_U$ | kg $UF_6$ per kg U |
| Clausius–Clapeyron (vapor pressure) | $\ln P = A - \dfrac{B}{T}$ | $A \approx 29.5$, $B \approx 7650$ K (for solid $UF_6$) |
| Triple point | $T_t = 64.05\,°C$, $P_t = 1.52\,atm$ | Solid-Liquid-Gas coexistence |
| Sublimation energy | $\Delta H_{sub} \approx 49.0\,kJ/mol$ | at 0 °C |
| Heat of formation | $\Delta H_f(UF_6,gas) = -2113\,kJ/mol$ | Standard conditions |

## 2.4 Engineering Significance in the Fuel Cycle

The conversion plant is the **chemical gateway** to the enrichment industry. Without $UF_6$, no modern enrichment technology can operate. Plant capacities are typically:

- **Large commercial plant:** 5,000 – 16,000 tU/year (e.g., Comurhex II at Tricastin, France).
- **Global production:** ~60,000 – 70,000 tU/year as $UF_6$ worldwide.
- **Product purity:** Reactor-grade $UF_6$ must have $< 1$ ppm of neutron-absorbing impurities (B, Cd, Gd, Hf, etc.).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering context:** Conversion plants are tightly controlled dual-use (civilian/military) facilities under **IAEA safeguards**. Any diversion of $UF_6$ from a commercial cascade is a proliferation concern, which is why conversion plants are always co-located with or carefully audited by IAEA inspectors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Stoichiometric Reagent Calculation (Three-Stage Conversion)

**Problem:** A conversion plant processes **1.00 tonne (1000 kg)** of uranium in the form of $UO_3$ per batch. Calculate the theoretical minimum masses of (a) $H_2$ required for reduction, (b) anhydrous HF required for hydrofluorination, and (c) $F_2$ required for final fluorination. Also determine the mass of $UF_6$ produced.

### Given Data

- Molar masses: $UO_3$ = 270.03 + 3(16.00) = **270.03 g/mol**, but uranium mass per mole of $UO_3$ = **238.03 g/mol**; $H_2$ = 2.016 g/mol; HF = 20.01 g/mol; $F_2$ = 38.00 g/mol; $UF_6$ = 352.02 g/mol.

### Step (a): Reduction Stage

Moles of $UO_3$ (or equivalently, moles of U):
$$n_{UO_3} = n_U = \dfrac{1000 \times 10^3\,g}{238.03\,g/mol} = 4201.4\,mol$$

From $UO_3 + H_2 \rightarrow UO_2 + H_2O$, the stoichiometric ratio is **1 : 1**:
$$n_{H_2} = n_{UO_3} = 4201.4\,mol$$
$$m_{H_2} = 4201.4 \times 2.016 = 8470\,g \approx 8.47\,kg$$

### Step (b): Hydrofluorination Stage

From $UO_2 + 4\,HF \rightarrow UF_4 + 2\,H_2O$, ratio is 1 U : 4 HF:
$$n_{HF} = 4 \times n_U = 4 \times 4201.4 = 16{,}805.6\,mol$$
$$m_{HF} = 16{,}805.6 \times 20.01 = 336{,}280\,g \approx 336.3\,kg$$

### Step (c): Fluorination Stage

From $UF_4 + F_2 \rightarrow UF_6$, ratio is 1 U : 1 $F_2$:
$$n_{F_2} = n_U = 4201.4\,mol$$
$$m_{F_2} = 4201.4 \times 38.00 = 159{,}653\,g \approx 159.7\,kg$$

### Step (d): Mass of $UF_6$ Produced

$$m_{UF_6} = n_U \times M_{UF_6} = 4201.4 \times 352.02 = 1{,}478{,}932\,g \approx 1479\,kg$$

Or directly:
$$m_{UF_6} = 1000\,kg \times \dfrac{352.02}{238.03} = 1479.0\,kg$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** Each tonne of uranium yields **~1.479 tonnes of $UF_6$**. The mass increase comes from the added fluorine and conversion stoichiometry.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Conversion Efficiency & Material Balance

Let us define the **overall process conversion efficiency** $\eta_c$ as:
$$\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{UF_6,actual}}{m_{UF_6,theoretical}} \times 100\%$$

In practice, industrial plants achieve:
- $H_2$ reduction efficiency: **99.5 – 99.9%**
- HF hydrofluorination: **99.0 – 99.5%**
- $F_2$ fluorination: **99.5 – 99.9%**
- **Overall plant yield:** $\eta_c \approx 98.5\%$

Thus, for 1000 kg U feed → actual $UF_6$ production $\approx 0.985 \times 1479 \approx 1457$ kg.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Volatility / Desublimation Mass Balance

$UF_6$ gas leaving the fluorinator is collected by **desublimation** in cold traps. The mass collected on a cold surface is governed by:
$$\dfrac{dm}{dt} = h_m \, A \, (C_{bulk} - C_{surface})$$

where $h_m$ is the mass-transfer coefficient, $A$ is the trap surface area, and $C$ is the vapor concentration. After sufficient time, the trap holds:
$$m_{trap} = P_{UF_6}(T_{trap}) \times \dfrac{V_{trap} \, M_{UF_6}}{R \, T_{trap}}$$

For a typical cold trap at **T = -40 °C = 233 K** with vapor pressure $P_{UF_6}(-40\,°C) \approx 1.3\,kPa$:
$$m_{trap} = \dfrac{1300\,Pa \times V_{trap} \times 0.352\,kg/mol}{8.314 \times 233\,K}$$

This is the foundation of $UF_6$ storage in **"cold traps"** at enrichment plants.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Comparison

| Parameter | Dry Route (Comurhex II) | Wet Route (TBP) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Feed flexibility | Requires purified $UO_3$ | Accepts impure yellowcake |
| Capital cost | Lower | Higher (requires solvent recovery) |
| Purity of $UF_6$ | Very high | Very high |
| Environmental burden | HF scrubbers needed | Nitrate + organic waste streams |
| Operating temperature | 300 – 700 °C | Mostly < 100 °C (mild) |
| Adoption | Global standard (France, USA, UK) | Russia, China, older plants |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** For derivation-type questions, **always show the molar flow step first**, then convert to mass using $m = n \times M$. Examiners allocate partial marks for correct molar logic even if a final arithmetic slip occurs.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Full Conversion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Uranium Ore Concentrate U3O8 or UO3] --> B[Calciner and Reducer]
    B --> C1[UO2 Powder]
    C1 --> D[Hydrofluorination Reactor]
    D --> E1[UF4 Green Salt]
    E1 --> F[Fluorination Tower F2 Gas]
    F --> G[UF6 Vapor]
    G --> H[Desublimation Cold Trap]
    H --> I[Liquid UF6 Collection]
    I --> J[Storage in Nickel Cylinders]
    J --> K[Shipment to Enrichment Plant]
    
    H2[H2 Gas Cylinder] --> B
    HF[Anhydrous HF Supply] --> D
    F2G[Electrolytic F2 Cell] --> F
    
    B --> S1[Off-gas H2O Removal]
    D --> S2[HF Recovery Scrubber]
    F --> S3[F2 Recycle Loop]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **top horizontal flow** (A → K) is the main uranium-bearing stream.
- The **three reagent inputs** ($H_2$, HF, $F_2$) feed the three reaction stages.
- The **bottom side arrows** (S1, S2, S3) are off-gas treatment and reagent recycle loops — critical for both economy and pollution control.

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Mass & Energy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph S1[Stage 1 Reduction]
        R1[UO3 plus H2] --> R2[UO2 plus H2O]
    end
    subgraph S2[Stage 2 Hydrofluorination]
        R2 --> R3[UF4 plus H2O]
    end
    subgraph S3[Stage 3 Fluorination]
        R3 --> R4[UF6 Vapor]
    end
    subgraph S4[Collection and Storage]
        R4 --> R5[Cold Trap Desublimation]
        R5 --> R6[Solid UF6 in Cylinders]
    end
```

## 4.3 Schematic — $UF_6$ Storage and Transport Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CYL[Monel or Nickel Cylinder] --> VALV[Diaphragm Valve]
    VALV --> PIPE[Double-Encased Transfer Line]
    PIPE --> ENR[Enrichment Plant Feed Station]
    CYL --> HEAT[Steam Heating Coil]
    HEAT --> LIQ[Liquid UF6 at 80 to 90 C]
    LIQ --> VALV
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Storage Cylinder Specification (KTU standard fact):** A standard **"48Y" cylinder** (Type 48Y, used by USEC/Orano) holds **~12.7 tonnes of solid $UF_6$** and is constructed of **Monel-400** with a wall thickness of **~16 mm**. It is ASME-coded for 1.4 MPa working pressure and weighs ~1500 kg empty. These cylinders are the universal "unit of currency" in the international $UF_6$ trade.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Why is uranium converted to uranium hexafluoride ($UF_6$) before isotope enrichment? List two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **$UF_6$ is the only uranium compound that sublimes at practically accessible temperatures** (sublimation point ~56 °C at 1 atm), allowing it to be used as a **gaseous working fluid** in enrichment cascades (gaseous diffusion, gas centrifuge, etc.). **(1.5 marks)**

2. **Fluorine is monoisotopic** ($^{19}F$, 100% natural abundance), so the mass difference between $^235UF_6$ and $^238UF_6$ is preserved in the molecular mass, maximizing separation efficiency in mass-based enrichment processes. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Name the three sequential chemical reactions used in the dry-route conversion of $UO_3$ to $UF_6$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Reduction:** $UO_3 + H_2 \rightarrow UO_2 + H_2O$ **(1 mark)**
- **Hydrofluorination:** $UO_2 + 4HF \rightarrow UF_4 + 2H_2O$ **(1 mark)**
- **Fluorination:** $UF_4 + F_2 \rightarrow UF_6$ **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a labeled block diagram, describe the **dry-route conversion of yellowcake ($U_3O_8$) to $UF_6$**. Mention the reactor type and operating temperature range for each stage.

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[U3O8 Feed Hopper] --> B[Calciner 400 to 500 C]
    B --> C[UO3]
    C --> D[Fluidized Bed Reducer H2 at 600 to 700 C]
    D --> E[UO2]
    E --> F[Fluidized Bed Hydrofluorination HF at 350 to 600 C]
    F --> G[UF4 Green Salt]
    G --> H[Fluorination Tower F2 at 300 to 400 C]
    H --> I[UF6 Vapor]
    I --> J[Cold Trap Desublimation]
    J --> K[Solid UF6 Storage]
```

**Stage-wise description:**

1. **Calcination:** $U_3O_8 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow 3UO_3$ at **400 – 500 °C** in a rotary kiln or fluidized bed; the goal is to convert the mixed oxide to the more reactive sesquioxide $UO_3$. **(1.5 marks)**
2. **Reduction:** $UO_3 + H_2 \rightarrow UO_2 + H_2O$ at **600 – 700 °C** in a fluidized-bed reactor; cracked ammonia or pure hydrogen is used; $UO_2$ is more reactive than $UO_3$ toward HF in the next step. **(1.5 marks)**
3. **Hydrofluorination:** $UO_2 + 4HF \rightarrow UF_4 + 2H_2O$ at **350 – 600 °C**; produces green salt $UF_4$; exothermic (heat must be removed); Monel-lined reactor. **(1.5 marks)**
4. **Fluorination:** $UF_4 + F_2 \rightarrow UF_6$ at **300 – 400 °C**; the $UF_6$ vapor is collected by desublimation on a cold surface at -10 °C to -40 °C. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Product storage:** Solid $UF_6$ packed into Monel/nickel cylinders (Type 48Y, ~12.7 t capacity) for transport to the enrichment plant. **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 marks]** Calculate the theoretical mass of (i) anhydrous HF, (ii) $F_2$ gas, and (iii) the mass of $UF_6$ produced when **500 kg of uranium** in the form of $UO_2$ is converted via the dry route. Use standard atomic masses: U = 238, O = 16, F = 19, H = 1.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moles of uranium:**
$$n_U = \dfrac{500{,}000\,g}{238\,g/mol} = 2100.84\,mol$$

**Step 2 — Reaction stoichiometry:**

(i) From $UO_2 + 4HF \rightarrow UF_4 + 2H_2O$:
$$n_{HF} = 4 \times n_U = 4 \times 2100.84 = 8403.36\,mol$$
$$m_{HF} = 8403.36 \times 20 = 168{,}067\,g \approx 168.1\,kg \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(ii) From $UF_4 + F_2 \rightarrow UF_6$:
$$n_{F_2} = n_U = 2100.84\,mol$$
$$m_{F_2} = 2100.84 \times 38 = 79{,}832\,g \approx 79.83\,kg \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(iii) Mass of $UF_6$:
$$m_{UF_6} = n_U \times M_{UF_6} = 2100.84 \times 352 = 739{,}496\,g \approx 739.5\,kg \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

(Alternatively: $500 \times \frac{352}{238} = 739.5$ kg.) **(1 mark for alternative method)**

**Total for (b): 7 marks** (4 + 1 distributed across sub-parts)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **physical and chemical properties of $UF_6$** that make it suitable for enrichment. Why is it stored in Monel containers rather than steel?

**Model Solution:**

**Physical properties favoring enrichment:**

1. **Sublimation point at 56 °C (1 atm):** $UF_6$ transitions readily between solid and gas without melting at atmospheric pressure, ideal for collection (desublimation) and feed (sublimation) in cascades. **(1 mark)**
2. **High vapor pressure at low temperature:** Vapor pressure reaches 1 atm at ~56 °C and ~10 atm at 100 °C, providing flexibility in feed delivery rates. **(1 mark)**
3. **Single uranium atom per molecule:** Maximizes the relative mass difference between $^235U$ and $^238U$ isotopes. **(1 mark)**

**Chemical properties favoring enrichment:**

4. **Fluorine is monoisotopic ($^{19}F$):** No fluorine-isotope mass split, so the entire 1.26% mass difference between $^235U$ and $^238U$ is concentrated in the central atom, maximizing separative work efficiency. **(1.5 marks)**
5. **Chemical stability under cascade conditions:** $UF_6$ does not decompose at temperatures up to ~250 °C, allowing hot enrichment operations. **(1 mark)**

**Why Monel and not steel:**

6. $UF_6$ is **highly reactive with water/moisture** and corrodes standard carbon steel, especially in the presence of trace moisture (forms $UO_2F_2$, $FeF_3$, and HF — all corrosive). **Monel (Ni-Cu alloy), pure nickel, and aluminum** form a stable passive fluoride layer, providing excellent corrosion resistance. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) [7 marks]** A conversion plant receives **2.0 tonnes of $UF_4$** and converts it to $UF_6$ using fluorine gas. Determine: (i) the mass of $F_2$ consumed, (ii) the mass of $UF_6$ produced, and (iii) the percentage excess fluorine if 200 kg of $F_2$ is actually supplied. Use $M_{UF_4} = 314$ g/mol.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Moles of $UF_4$ input:**
$$n_{UF_4} = \dfrac{2{,}000{,}000\,g}{314\,g/mol} = 6369.4\,mol \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 2 — Stoichiometry:** From $UF_4 + F_2 \rightarrow UF_6$ (1 : 1 : 1)

(i) Theoretical mass of $F_2$ consumed:
$$n_{F_2,th} = 6369.4\,mol \quad \Rightarrow \quad m_{F_2,th} = 6369.4 \times 38 = 242{,}037\,g \approx 242.0\,kg \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(ii) Mass of $UF_6$ produced (theoretical, 100% conversion):
$$m_{UF_6} = 6369.4 \times 352 = 2{,}242{,}029\,g \approx 2242\,kg \quad \textbf{(2 marks)}$$

(iii) **Percentage excess fluorine:**

Actual $F_2$ supplied = 200 kg
$$m_{F_2,excess} = 200 - 242.0 = -42.0\,kg \quad \text{(i.e., actually 42 kg DEFICIT)} \quad \textbf{(1 mark)}$$

$$\% \text{ excess} = \dfrac{200 - 242.0}{242.0} \times 100\% = -17.36\%$$

Since the result is **negative**, the fluorine is in **deficit, not excess**. The plant would need ~42 kg more $F_2$ to achieve complete conversion. **(1 mark for interpretation)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:** Students frequently (a) confuse $UF_4$ molar mass with that of $UF_6$, (b) apply the wrong stoichiometric ratio (e.g., using 1 : 2 instead of 1 : 1), and (c) forget to **interpret** the sign of the percentage excess — a negative result means *deficit*, not "negative excess." Always include the **physical interpretation** of your numerical answer; this is worth 1–2 marks by itself.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize Facts:**

- $\square$ **$UF_6$ is the only gaseous uranium compound** suitable for enrichment.
- $\square$ **Sublimation point of $UF_6$:** 56 °C at 1 atm; **triple point:** 64 °C, 1.52 atm.
- $\square$ **Molecular weight of $UF_6$:** 352.02 g/mol; **U mass fraction:** 0.676.
- $\square$ **Three dry-route reactions:** (1) Reduction $UO_3 + H_2 \rightarrow UO_2$; (2) Hydrofluorination $UO_2 + 4HF \rightarrow UF_4$; (3) Fluorination $UF_4 + F_2 \rightarrow UF_6$.
- $\square$ **Fluorine is monoisotopic** ($^{19}F$ only) — this is *why* $UF_6$ is chosen.
- $\square$ **Storage material:** Monel (Ni-Cu) or pure nickel; **never** glass or moist steel.
- $\square$ **HF and $F_2$ stoichiometries:** 4 mol HF per mol U; 1 mol $F_2$ per mol U.
- $\square$ **Mass yield:** 1 kg U → 1.479 kg $UF_6$ (theoretical).
- $\square$ **Standard $UF_6$ cylinder:** Type 48Y, ~12.7 t, Monel-400, ASME-coded.
- $\square$ **Industrial plant capacity:** 5,000 – 16,000 tU/year (e.g., Comurhex II at Tricastin).
- $\square$ **$UF_6$ + water** → $UO_2F_2$ + HF (highly corrosive and toxic).
- $\square$ **Process locations in fuel cycle:** Mining → Milling → **Conversion** → Enrichment → Fabrication.
- $\square$ **Two main industrial routes:** Comurhex (France, $U_3O_8$ feed) and BNFL (UK, $UO_3$ feed).
- $\square$ **Safety & safeguards:** All conversion plants are under **IAEA safeguards** due to proliferation sensitivity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Enrichment - Gaseous: Diffusion and Centrifuge
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Enrichment - Gaseous: Diffusion and Centrifuge # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Isotopic Enrichment? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Isotopic Enrichment** is the physical process of increasing the abundance (mole fraction) of a desired isotope in a chemical element relative to its natural isotopic composition. In the nuclear industry, the term almost exclusively refers to the **enrichment of Uranium-235 (²³⁵U)** from its natural abundance of **0.711%** to reactor-grade levels of **3%–5%** or weapons-grade levels of **≥ 90%**. Natural uranium consists primarily of three isotopes: - **²³⁸U** — ~99.2845% (fertile, fission threshold ~1 MeV) - **²³⁵U** — ~0.711% (fissile with thermal neutrons, σ_f ≈ 585 barns) - **²³⁴U** — ~0.0055% (trace, generally neglected in mass balance) > [!NOTE] > **Why enrichment is necessary:** Most commercial Light Water Reactors (LWRs) and the Navy's naval propulsion reactors require a **higher ²³⁵U concentration** than nature provides, because natural-uranium fueled reactors (like PHWRs using heavy water moderator) can sustain criticality, but light-water moderated reactors cannot. Hence the ²³⁵U concentration must be "boosted." ## 1.2 The Gaseous Diffusion Principle — Intuitive Analogy Imagine a **large room filled with bouncing tennis balls and cricket balls** in equal numbers, where a thin perforated wall divides the room. The wall has holes big enough for tennis balls but small enough that cricket balls get stuck or bounce back. After some time, the room behind the wall will have **more tennis balls than cricket balls** — a separation has occurred. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gaseous Diffusion** exploits **Graham's Law of Effusion (1833)**: The rate of effusion of a gas is inversely proportional to the square root of its molecular mass. > $$\text{Rate} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}$$ In an enrichment plant, uranium is converted to **Uranium Hexafluoride (UF₆)** — the only uranium compound that is gaseous at moderate temperatures (sublimes at **56.5 °C at 1 atm**). The gas is forced under pressure through a **porous membrane** (barrier) with extremely fine pores (≈ 10–100 nm). Lighter **²³⁵UF₆** molecules strike the membrane more frequently per unit mass and pass through more readily than the heavier **²³⁸UF₆** molecules, producing a slightly enriched stream. ## 1.3 The Gas Centrifuge Principle — Intuitive Analogy Imagine a **vertical cylinder filled with honey and water** being spun at very high rpm. The denser fluid (**honey**, analogous to ²³⁸UF₆) is flung toward the wall, while the lighter fluid (**water**, analogous to ²³⁵UF₆) stays near the central axis. Carefully scooping from the center gives you a sample enriched in the lighter component. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Gas Centrifuge Enrichment** exploits the **centrifugal force** generated inside a rotor spinning at ultra-high speeds (typically **50,000–90,000 rpm**). The heavier ²³⁸UF₆ molecules are forced toward the outer wall by the radial pressure gradient, while the lighter ²³⁵UF₆ molecules concentrate near the rotor axis. **Counter-current thermal convection currents** inside the rotor amplify this separation by orders of magnitude beyond what pure centrifugal stratification alone could achieve. ## 1.4 Why These Two Methods? | Property | Gaseous Diffusion | Gas Centrifuge | |----------|-------------------|----------------| | First deployed | 1940s (Manhattan Project) | 1960s (pioneered by Zippe, Germany) | | Energy intensity | **~2400 kWh/SWU** (huge) | **~50–200 kWh/SWU** (very low) | | Separation per stage | Tiny (α ≈ 1.0043) | Large (α ≈ 1.2–1.6) | | Cascade size | Thousands of stages | Tens to hundreds of machines | | Capital cost | Very high (barriers, compressors) | Moderate (high-precision rotors) | | Modern relevance | **Phased out** (Paducah closed 2013) | **Dominant world technology** | > [!NOTE] > As of the 2020s, **gas centrifuge accounts for ~ 54%** of world enrichment capacity, with gaseous diffusion plants essentially retired. URENCO (UK/DE/NL), Orano (France), and Rosatom (Russia) lead centrifuge deployment. ## 1.5 Key Physical Constants Used - **Universal gas constant:** R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - **Molar mass of ²³⁵UF₆:** **349.03 g/mol** - **Molar mass of ²³⁸UF₆:** **352.04 g/mol** - **Mass difference:** ΔM = **3.01 g/mol** - **Average molar mass of UF₆:** **~ 352.0 g/mol** - **Absolute temperature of operation (diffusion):** ~ **350 K** - **Peripheral speed of centrifuge rotor:** ~ **300–600 m/s** # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Gaseous Diffusion — Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Stage 1 — Feed Preparation:** Natural UF₆ is vaporized from solid storage cylinders and fed into the cascade. **Stage 2 — Compression:** A **centrifugal compressor** raises the gas pressure to ~ **1.5–3 atm** absolute on the **high-pressure side** of the barrier. The **low-pressure side** is maintained at ~ **0.3–0.5 atm** by a downstream pump, creating the pressure differential ΔP that drives effusion. **Stage 3 — Effusion through the barrier:** The porous barrier is a **sintered nickel, aluminum oxide, or polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane** with controlled pore radius (typically **5–25 nm**), pore density ≈ 10⁹–10¹⁰ pores/cm², and barrier thickness ≈ **10–100 µm**. The small pore size ensures the gas flow regime is **molecular (Knudsen) flow**, where molecule–wall collisions dominate over molecule–molecule collisions. **Stage 4 — Separation mechanism:** Because the gas flows by molecular effusion, the ratio of the number of ²³⁵UF₆ to ²³⁸UF₆ molecules passing through the membrane per unit area per unit time equals: $$\frac{n'_{235}}{n'_{238}} = \frac{n_{235}}{n_{238}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{238}}{M_{235}}}$$ The stream that **passes through** (permeate) is slightly enriched — called the **"product" stream**; the stream that does not pass (retentate) is slightly depleted — called the **"tails" stream**. **Stage 5 — Cascading:** Because α is so close to unity, hundreds to thousands of stages are connected in series (cascade) to achieve the desired enrichment. ## 2.2 Gas Centrifuge — Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Stage 1 — Rotor assembly:** A **vertical cylindrical rotor** of maraging steel or carbon-fiber composite, ~ 0.3–2 m tall and ~ 10–20 cm diameter, is levitated magnetically and spun in a vacuum housing at peripheral speeds approaching **500 m/s**. **Stage 2 — Feed injection:** UF₆ gas at low pressure (~ 100 mbar) is fed into the rotor near its mid-height through a stationary scoop. **Stage 3 — Centrifugal stratification:** Centrifugal acceleration ω²r (where ω is angular velocity, r is radius) establishes a radial pressure gradient. The gas in the rotor is essentially a **Maxwell–Boltzmann distribution in a rotating frame**, leading to an **isotopic equilibrium**: $$\frac{x_{\text{axis}}}{1-x_{\text{axis}}} = \frac{x_{\text{wall}}}{1-x_{\text{wall}}} \cdot e^{-\frac{\Delta M \cdot \omega^2 r^2}{2RT}}$$ **Stage 4 — Counter-current axial flow:** To dramatically enhance separation, a **thermal convection drive** is introduced — a heated bottom cap and cooled top cap create a **scooping axial flow** that moves enriched gas upward along the axis and depleted gas downward along the wall. This internal counter-current multiplies the single-stage separation factor by a factor called the **Münzenberg multiplier** (typically 5–20×). **Stage 5 — Product and tails extraction:** Stationary scoops at the top (product, enriched ²³⁵UF₆) and bottom (tails, depleted ²³⁸UF₆) continuously extract the separated streams. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Graham's Law of Effusion | $r_1 / r_2 = \sqrt{M_2 / M_1}$ | r = effusion rate, M = molar mass (kg/mol) | | 2 | Ideal Stage Separation Factor (Diffusion) | $\alpha = \sqrt{M_{238}/M_{235}}$ | Dimensionless | | 3 | Numerical α for UF₆ diffusion | $\alpha \approx 1.0043$ | Dimensionless | | 4 | Separation Factor — Centrifuge (no scoop) | $\alpha_0 = \exp\left[\frac{(M_{238}-M_{235})\,\omega^2 r^2}{2RT}\right]$ | ω in rad/s, r in m, T in K | | 5 | Effective α with counter-current | $\alpha = \alpha_0^{\,K}$ | K = Münzenberg factor (5–20) | | 6 | Cut θ of a stage | $\theta = \dfrac{\text{Product molar flow}}{\text{Feed molar flow}}$ | Dimensionless (0 < θ < 1) | | 7 | Head fraction (product) | $\alpha - 1 = \varepsilon$ | "Enrichment" per stage | | 8 | Number of ideal stages (small enrichment) | $N \approx \dfrac{2}{\varepsilon}\ln\left(\dfrac{X_p(1-X_f)}{X_f(1-X_p)}\right)$ | Approximate, small ε | | 9 | Separative Power per stage | $\delta U = \frac{1}{4}\,\varepsilon^2$ (in kg·Δ) | For symmetric stage | | 10 | Value Function V(X) | $V(X) = (2X - 1)\ln\!\left(\dfrac{X}{1-X}\right)$ | X = mole fraction of ²³⁵U | | 11 | Total SWU of cascade | $\Delta U = P\,V(X_p) + T\,V(X_t) - F\,V(X_f)$ | P, T, F in kg/year | | 12 | Feed F for a given product P | $F = P \cdot \dfrac{X_p - X_t}{X_f - X_t}$ | Mass balance | | 13 | Tails flow T | $T = F - P$ | Mass balance | | 14 | Centrifuge peripheral speed | $v = \omega r$ | m/s; must be < rotor material limit (~ 500 m/s for maraging steel) | | 15 | Centrifugal acceleration | $a_c = \omega^2 r$ | m/s² | | 16 | Knudsen number condition | $K_n = \lambda / d_p > 1$ | λ = mean free path, d_p = pore diameter | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Separative Work Unit (SWU):** A SWU is a complex, composite unit (kg·SWU) that measures the *thermodynamic effort* of separating isotopes, not the *mass* of uranium moved. **1 kg·SWU ≈ 1 kg of separative work** — analogous to kWh being a measure of energy, not mass. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility in the Real World - **Gaseous diffusion plants** dominated enrichment from 1945 to ~2010 and are now mostly decommissioned. The U.S. Paducah Gaseous Diffusion Plant ceased operation in **May 2013**. - **Gas centrifuge technology** is the **current global standard**, with three dominant players: **URENCO** (UK/Germany/Netherlands), **Orano** (France — Georges Besse II plant), and **TENEX/Rosatom** (Russia). The capacity is now ≈ **65 million SWU/year worldwide (2024)**. - **Future directions:** **Laser Isotope Separation (SILEX — Separation of Isotopes by Laser EXcitation)** is a third-generation, molecular-based laser method being commercialized by Global Laser Enrichment (GLE) in the U.S. — not covered under this topic. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Ideal Separation Factor for Gaseous Diffusion (α = √(M_238/M_235)) We start from **Maxwell–Boltzmann kinetic theory**. The flux of gas molecules striking a wall (and thus effusing through an ideal orifice) per unit area per unit time is given by: $$\Phi = \frac{1}{4}\,n\,\bar{c} = \frac{P}{\sqrt{2\pi M R T}}$$ where: - n = number density of molecules (molecules/m³) - $\bar{c}$ = mean molecular speed = √(8RT/πM) - P = partial pressure (Pa) - M = molar mass (kg/mol) - R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) - T = absolute temperature (K) **Step 1 — Effusion flux for each isotope:** For the ²³⁵UF₆ and ²³⁸UF₆ components, the number of molecules of species *i* striking and passing through the membrane is proportional to the flux. Since both isotopes share the same temperature T and the partial pressure of species *i* is P_i = x_i · P_total: $$\Phi_{235} = \frac{P_{235}}{\sqrt{2\pi M_{235} R T}}, \quad \Phi_{238} = \frac{P_{238}}{\sqrt{2\pi M_{238} R T}}$$ **Step 2 — Ratio of fluxes through the membrane:** $$\frac{\Phi_{235}}{\Phi_{238}} = \frac{P_{235}}{P_{238}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{238}}{M_{235}}}$$ **Step 3 — Relating partial pressures to mole fractions:** In a binary mixture, the mole fraction of species *i* equals its partial pressure fraction: x_i = P_i / P_total. Therefore: $$\frac{P_{235}}{P_{238}} = \frac{x_{235}}{1 - x_{235}} \approx \frac{x_{235}}{x_{238}} \quad (\text{when } x_{238} \gg x_{235})$$ **Step 4 — Define the Separation Factor α:** The separation factor is, by formal definition, the ratio of the **isotopic ratios** of the **enriched (permeate) stream to the feed stream**: $$\alpha = \frac{(x_{235}/x_{238})_{\text{permeate}}}{(x_{235}/x_{238})_{\text{feed}}}$$ Substituting from Step 2: $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{M_{238}}{M_{235}}}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:** $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{352.04}{349.03}} = \sqrt{1.008623} = 1.004305 \approx 1.0043$$ This confirms the textbook value. The **enrichment ε = α − 1 = 0.0043 = 0.43%** per single ideal stage — hence the necessity for an enormous cascade (a plant enriching to 3% needs ≈ 1100 ideal stages in series, with even more in the enriching section plus stripping section). ## 3.2 Derivation of the Centrifuge Separation Factor (without Internal Counter-Current) Consider a binary gas mixture of ²³⁵UF₆ (mass M₁) and ²³⁸UF₆ (mass M₂), with M₂ > M₁, contained in a rotor spinning at angular velocity ω about the z-axis at absolute temperature T. **Step 1 — Pressure in a rotating isothermal gas:** For a gas in rigid-body rotation, mechanical equilibrium along the radial direction gives the **barometric-like equation**: $$\frac{dP}{dr} = \rho(r)\,\omega^2 r$$ For an ideal gas, ρ(r) = P(r)·M_avg / (RT), where M_avg is the local average molar mass. Integration from r = 0 to r = R (rotor wall) yields: $$P(r) = P(0) \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{M_{\text{avg}}\,\omega^2 r^2}{2RT}\right]$$ **Step 2 — Isotopic Equilibrium in Centrifugal Field:** In thermodynamic equilibrium, the **chemical potential** of each species is uniform throughout the rotor. This gives the **isotope equilibrium condition** (first derived by Cohen, 1951): $$\frac{x(r_1)}{1 - x(r_1)} = \frac{x(r_2)}{1 - x(r_2)} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{(M_2 - M_1)\,\omega^2 (r_2^2 - r_1^2)}{2RT}\right]$$ Setting r₁ = axis (r = 0) and r₂ = wall (r = R): $$\frac{x_{\text{axis}}}{1 - x_{\text{axis}}} = \frac{x_{\text{wall}}}{1 - x_{\text{wall}}} \cdot \exp\!\left[\frac{\Delta M \cdot \omega^2 R^2}{2RT}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Define the equilibrium separation factor α₀:** For dilute ²³⁵U, x ≪ 1, so (1 − x) ≈ 1, giving: $$\alpha_0 \equiv \frac{x_{\text{axis}}/x_{\text{wall, before scoop}}}{x_{\text{wall, before scoop}}/x_{\text{axis, before scoop}}} = \exp\!\left[\frac{\Delta M \cdot \omega^2 R^2}{2RT}\right]$$ **Step 4 — Numerical estimation:** With R = 0.10 m, ω = 2π × 1000 rad/s (≈ 60,000 rpm — modern centrifuges reach this), T = 300 K, ΔM = 0.003 kg/mol: $$\frac{\Delta M \,\omega^2 R^2}{2RT} = \frac{0.003 \cdot (6283)^2 \cdot (0.10)^2}{2 \cdot 8.314 \cdot 300}$$ $$= \frac{0.003 \cdot 3.948 \times 10^7 \cdot 0.01}{4990} = \frac{1184.4}{4990} \approx 0.2374$$ Therefore: $$\alpha_0 \approx e^{0.2374} \approx 1.268$$ So even **without** counter-current flow, the centrifuge already achieves α₀ ≈ **1.27** — over **200× better per stage** than gaseous diffusion. **Step 5 — Effect of counter-current circulation (Münzenberg factor K):** When the axial counter-current flow is established, the effective separation factor is amplified: $$\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \alpha_0^{\,K}, \quad K \approx 5 - 20$$ For K = 5: α_eff = (1.268)⁵ ≈ **3.29** — a single centrifuge can replace 250+ diffusion stages. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Separative Work Unit (SWU) **Step 1 — Define the Value Function V(X):** The standard definition (Cohen, 1951) is: $$V(X) = (2X - 1)\,\ln\!\left(\frac{X}{1 - X}\right)$$ where X is the mole fraction of the desired isotope (²³⁵U). This function has the property that V(½) = 0, V(X) → +∞ as X → 1, and V(X) → −∞ as X → 0. The function represents the **"value" of 1 kg of material at assay X** in separative work units. **Step 2 — Conservation of Separative Work:** In a steady-state cascade, the SWU produced by all stages equals the SWU content of the outputs minus that of the inputs. The total separative work required is: $$\boxed{\;\Delta U = P\,V(X_p) + T\,V(X_t) - F\,V(X_f)\;}$$ where: - P = product flow rate (kg/year) at assay X_p - T = tails flow rate (kg/year) at assay X_t - F = feed flow rate (kg/year) at assay X_f - P + T = F (mass balance) - P·X_p + T·X_t = F·X_f (isotope balance) **Step 3 — Example Calculation (typical LWR fuel cycle):** Suppose we want: **P = 100 kg/year of 4.5% enriched uranium**, with tails at **X_t = 0.20%** and feed at natural **X_f = 0.711%**. From isotope balance: F = P · (X_p − X_t)/(X_f − X_t) = 100 · (0.045 − 0.002)/(0.00711 − 0.002) = 100 · 0.043/0.00511 = **841.5 kg/year**. Tails T = F − P = **741.5 kg/year**. Value function evaluations: - V(0.045) = (0.090 − 1) · ln(0.045/0.955) = (−0.910) · ln(0.04712) = (−0.910) · (−3.054) = **+2.779** - V(0.002) = (0.004 − 1) · ln(0.002/0.998) = (−0.996) · ln(0.002004) = (−0.996) · (−6.213) = **+6.188** - V(0.00711) = (0.01422 − 1) · ln(0.00711/0.99289) = (−0.9858) · ln(0.007161) = (−0.9858) · (−4.940) = **+4.870** Total SWU: $$\Delta U = 100(2.779) + 741.5(6.188) - 841.5(4.870) = 277.9 + 4588.4 - 4098.1 = 768.2 \text{ kg-SWU}$$ So producing 100 kg of 4.5% enriched uranium requires **768.2 kg·SWU of separative work** and **841.5 kg of natural uranium feed** — a typical industry benchmark. ## 3.4 Cascade Stage Count (Approximate) For a **small enrichment ε per stage**, the number of ideal stages in the enriching section to go from feed X_f to product X_p is: $$N_{\text{enriching}} \approx \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\ln\!\left(\frac{X_p(1 - X_f)}{X_f(1 - X_p)}\right)$$ For diffusion with ε = 0.0043, going from X_f = 0.00711 to X_p = 0.045: $$N_{\text{enriching}} \approx \frac{1}{0.0043}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.045 \times 0.99289}{0.00711 \times 0.955}\right) = 232.6 \times \ln(6.591) = 232.6 \times 1.886 = 438.6 \text{ stages}$$ Adding a stripping section (typically ~ 60% of enriching-section size) brings the total to **~ 700 ideal stages** in a single gaseous-diffusion cascade — explaining the colossal physical size of plants like Oak Ridge K-25 (U.S. WWII, ~ 3000 stages, 0.5 mile long). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Gaseous Diffusion Stage — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[UF6 Feed Cylinders
Natural Assay 0.711 percent] --> B[Feed Vaporizer
Heated to 56.5 deg C] B --> C[Compressor Stage
Raises to 1.5 to 3 atm] C --> D[High Pressure Side
of Porous Barrier] D --> E[Porous Barrier
Pore size 5 to 25 nm
Knudsen flow regime] E --> F[Low Pressure Side
Permeate Enriched Stream] E --> G[Retentate Depleted Stream
Tails Side] F --> H[Compressor for Cascade
Next Stage Feed] G --> I[Tails Recycle
or Next Stage Feed] H --> J[Cooling and Condensation
Product UF6 Liquid] J --> K[Product Storage Cylinders] I --> L[Tails Storage or Recycle] ``` ## 4.2 Gas Centrifuge — Functional Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB A[UF6 Feed Gas
Low Pressure 100 mbar] --> B[Stationary Feed Scoop
Mid Rotor] B --> C[Vacuum Housing
Surrounds Rotor] C --> D[Maraging Steel Rotor
Spinning 50000 to 90000 rpm] D --> E[Radial Stratification
238UF6 to Wall
235UF6 to Axis] E --> F[Counter Current Flow
Thermal Convection Drive] F --> G[Top Scoop
Enriched Product 235UF6] F --> H[Bottom Scoop
Depleted Tails 238UF6] G --> I[Product Collection
Enriched Stream] H --> J[Tails Collection
Depleted Stream] D --> K[Magnetic Bearing System
and Vacuum System] K --> D ``` ## 4.3 Isotope Separation Cascade — Top-Down Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB FeedRes[Natural UF6 Feed
Xf 0.00711] --> S1[Stage 1] S1 --> S2[Stage 2] S2 --> SN[Stage N] SN --> ProdRes[Enriched Product
Xp 0.03 to 0.05] S1 --> SN1[Stripping Section] SN1 --> SNN[Stage M] SNN --> TailRes[Depleted Tails
Xt 0.002 to 0.003] SNN --> SN1 SN --> SN1 style FeedRes fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc style ProdRes fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745 style TailRes fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545 style S1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style S2 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style SN fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style SN1 fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 style SNN fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107 ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Process Flow — Gaseous Diffusion vs. Centrifuge ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph DiffusionPlant[Gaseous Diffusion Plant] D1[Compressor 1] --> D2[Barrier 1] D2 --> D3[Compressor 2] D3 --> D4[Barrier 2] D4 --> D5[... thousands of stages ...] D5 --> D6[Final Enriched UF6] end subgraph CentrifugePlant[Gas Centrifuge Cascade] C1[Centrifuge 1] --> C2[Centrifuge 2] C2 --> C3[Centrifuge N] C3 --> C4[Final Enriched UF6] end style DiffusionPlant fill:#ffe4e1,stroke:#cc0000 style CentrifugePlant fill:#e0f7e0,stroke:#008000 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style] **Explain Graham's Law of Effusion and its relevance to the gaseous diffusion method of uranium enrichment.** **Model Answer (≈ 90 words):** Graham's Law (1833) states that the rate of effusion of a gas through a small orifice or porous barrier is inversely proportional to the square root of its molecular mass: $r \propto 1/\sqrt{M}$. In uranium enrichment, natural UF₆ is forced through a porous barrier under pressure. The lighter ²³⁵UF₆ (M = 349.03 g/mol) effuses slightly faster than the heavier ²³⁸UF₆ (M = 352.04 g/mol), producing a permeate stream slightly enriched in ²³⁵U. The theoretical single-stage separation factor is $\alpha = \sqrt{M_{238}/M_{235}} \approx 1.0043$, requiring a large cascade. ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style] **Why is uranium converted to UF₆ before enrichment by gaseous diffusion or centrifuge methods?** **Model Answer (≈ 80 words):** Uranium is converted to **uranium hexafluoride (UF₆)** because it is the **only uranium compound that exists as a gas at moderately accessible temperatures** (sublimes at 56.5 °C at 1 atm; boils at 64 °C at 1.4 atm). The gaseous state is essential because both enrichment methods depend on either molecular effusion (gaseous diffusion) or centrifugal force acting on gas molecules (centrifuge). UF₆ also contains only fluorine (M = 19), so the mass difference between ²³⁵UF₆ and ²³⁸UF₆ is dominated by the uranium mass — preserving the separation signal. --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice ### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / May 2023 Style — 14 Marks] **a)** Derive the theoretical separation factor for the gaseous diffusion process and calculate it for UF₆. Comment on the need for cascading. **(7 marks)** **b)** A gaseous diffusion plant is fed with natural uranium (0.711% ²³⁵U) at 10,000 kg/year. The product is enriched to 3.5% ²³⁵U and the tails to 0.2% ²³⁵U. Calculate (i) product flow rate, (ii) tails flow rate, (iii) SWU required. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks **Step 1 — Molecular flux derivation:** For a gas at temperature T and pressure P, the number of molecules striking unit area of the wall per unit time is: $$\Phi = \frac{1}{4}\,n\,\bar{c} = \frac{P\,N_A}{\sqrt{2\pi M R T}}$$ (1 mark for this statement; reference to Maxwell–Boltzmann) **Step 2 — Apply to both isotopes of UF₆:** $$\Phi_{235} = \frac{P_{235}}{\sqrt{2\pi M_{235} R T}}, \quad \Phi_{238} = \frac{P_{238}}{\sqrt{2\pi M_{238} R T}}$$ **Step 3 — Ratio of fluxes through barrier:** $$\frac{\Phi_{235}}{\Phi_{238}} = \frac{P_{235}}{P_{238}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{238}}{M_{235}}} = \frac{x_{235}^{\text{feed}}}{x_{238}^{\text{feed}}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M_{238}}{M_{235}}}$$ (2 marks for the derivation of the ratio) **Step 4 — Define separation factor α:** By definition, the ratio of the isotopic ratio in the permeate to that in the feed: $$\alpha = \frac{(x_{235}/x_{238})_{\text{permeate}}}{(x_{235}/x_{238})_{\text{feed}}} = \sqrt{\frac{M_{238}}{M_{235}}}$$ (1 mark) **Step 5 — Numerical evaluation:** $$\alpha = \sqrt{\frac{352.04}{349.03}} = \sqrt{1.008623} = 1.0043$$ (1 mark) **Step 6 — Comment on cascading:** Since α = 1.0043 (ε = 0.0043, i.e., 0.43% per stage), a single stage cannot reach reactor-grade enrichment of 3–5%.
