Going from 0.711% to 3.5% requires: $$N \approx \frac{1}{0.0043}\ln\!\left(\frac{0.035 \times 0.99289}{0.00711 \times 0.965}\right) \approx 232.6 \times \ln(5.058) \approx 232.6 \times 1.622 \approx 377 \text{ ideal stages in enriching section}$$ A full cascade (enriching + stripping) needs ≈ 600–800 stages, hence the enormous size of Oak Ridge K-25, Paducah, etc. (2 marks) #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks **Given:** F = 10,000 kg/year, X_f = 0.00711, X_p = 0.035, X_t = 0.002. **(i) Product flow rate P:** From isotope mass balance: P·X_p + (F − P)·X_t = F·X_f $$P = F \cdot \frac{X_f - X_t}{X_p - X_t} = 10000 \cdot \frac{0.00711 - 0.002}{0.035 - 0.002} = 10000 \cdot \frac{0.00511}{0.033} = 10000 \times 0.15485$$ $$\boxed{P = 1548.5 \text{ kg/year}}$$ (2 marks) **(ii) Tails flow rate T:** $$T = F - P = 10000 - 1548.5 = \boxed{8451.5 \text{ kg/year}}$$ (1 mark) **(iii) SWU required:** Value function $V(X) = (2X - 1)\ln[X/(1-X)]$ - $V(X_f = 0.00711) = (0.01422 - 1)\ln(0.00711/0.99289) = (-0.9858)(-4.940) = 4.870$ - $V(X_p = 0.035) = (0.070 - 1)\ln(0.035/0.965) = (-0.930)(-3.323) = 3.090$ - $V(X_t = 0.002) = (0.004 - 1)\ln(0.002/0.998) = (-0.996)(-6.213) = 6.188$ $$\Delta U = P \cdot V(X_p) + T \cdot V(X_t) - F \cdot V(X_f)$$ $$= 1548.5 \times 3.090 + 8451.5 \times 6.188 - 10000 \times 4.870$$ $$= 4784.9 + 52{,}302.0 - 48{,}700.0$$ $$\boxed{\Delta U = 8386.9 \text{ kg-SWU/year}}$$ (4 marks) --- ### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2022 / Dec 2023 Style — 14 Marks] **a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the working of a gas centrifuge for uranium isotope separation. Derive the equilibrium separation factor expression. **(7 marks)** **b)** Compare gaseous diffusion and gas centrifuge methods of enrichment on the basis of: (i) energy consumption per SWU, (ii) separation factor per stage, (iii) cascade size, (iv) present-day industrial relevance, (v) barriers/challenges in each method. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks **Working of Gas Centrifuge (3 marks):** The gas centrifuge consists of a **vertically oriented cylindrical rotor** of maraging steel or carbon-fiber composite, typically 0.3–2 m tall and 10–20 cm diameter, spinning in a vacuum housing at **50,000–90,000 rpm** (peripheral speed 300–600 m/s). UF₆ gas is fed into the rotor at low pressure via a stationary feed scoop. Inside the rotor, two effects act simultaneously: 1. **Radial stratification** due to centrifugal force — heavier ²³⁸UF₆ is forced toward the wall, lighter ²³⁵UF₆ concentrates near the axis. 2. **Axial counter-current circulation** driven by thermal convection (heated bottom baffle, cooled top baffle) — this **scooping flow** multiplies the radial separation by sweeping enriched gas up the axis and depleted gas down the wall. Product (enriched ²³⁵UF₆) is extracted from a stationary top scoop, and tails (depleted ²³⁸UF₆) from a bottom scoop. **Derivation of Equilibrium Separation Factor (4 marks):** For a binary gas in a rotor at angular velocity ω and temperature T, thermodynamic equilibrium demands uniform chemical potential. This gives (Cohen, 1951): $$\frac{x_a}{1 - x_a} = \frac{x_w}{1 - x_w} \cdot \exp\!\left[-\frac{\Delta M \cdot \omega^2 (r_w^2 - r_a^2)}{2RT}\right]$$ where subscripts a and w denote axis and wall. For dilute ²³⁵U, (1 − x) ≈ 1, so the equilibrium separation factor is: $$\alpha_0 = \exp\!\left[\frac{(M_{238} - M_{235}) \omega^2 R^2}{2RT}\right]$$ where R is the rotor radius and T is the absolute gas temperature. With counter-current flow, the effective separation factor is amplified to: $$\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \alpha_0^{\,K}, \quad K \approx 5 - 20 \text{ (Münzenberg factor)}$$ (4 marks for the derivation with sub-steps as shown in Section 3.2 of these notes) #### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks | S.No | Parameter | Gaseous Diffusion | Gas Centrifuge | |------|-----------|-------------------|----------------| | (i) | Energy per SWU | **~ 2400 kWh/SWU** (very high) | **~ 50–200 kWh/SWU** (very low) | | (ii) | α per stage | ≈ 1.0043 (very small) | ≈ 1.2 – 1.6 (large, with scoop) | | (iii) | Cascade size | 800 – 3000 stages, plants of 0.5–1 mile length | 10 – 50 centrifuges in series, much smaller footprint | | (iv) | Industrial relevance (2024) | Largely **decommissioned** (last U.S. plant closed 2013) | **Dominant world technology** (~ 54% of capacity) | | (v) | Major challenges | Massive electric power requirement, fragile porous barriers, very high capital cost | Ultra-precision rotor balance, magnetic bearings, vacuum system, **proliferation sensitivity** (technology is restricted) | (7 marks — 1 mark for each parameter × 5 + 2 marks for conclusion/proliferation note) --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls to Avoid:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse α with the cut (θ).** The cut is the mass fraction of feed appearing as product (0 < θ < 1). The separation factor α is the *ratio of isotopic ratios*, dimensionless, and is always slightly greater than 1 for an enriching stage. Mixing these up is a guaranteed 1–2 mark loss. > 2. **Always specify the "permeate" and "retentate"** in gaseous diffusion, and the "axis" and "wall" in the centrifuge. KTU evaluators specifically look for these technical terms. > 3. **The formula V(X) = (2X − 1) ln[X/(1−X)] is the single most-cluttered equation** in this module. A sign error in (2X − 1) will wipe out a 4-mark sub-part in one stroke. Double-check by noting V(0.5) = 0. > 4. **Stage count N ≈ (1/ε) ln(...) is an approximation valid for small ε.** If the question gives you large ε (e.g., centrifuge), do not blindly use this; use the exact stage formula or refer to Cohen's method. > 5. **For SWU, the formula ΔU = P·V(X_p) + T·V(X_t) − F·V(X_f)** has the *negative* sign for the feed term. Many students incorrectly write all positive terms, giving a negative SWU. > 6. **Do not confuse rotor peripheral speed (v = ωr in m/s) with angular velocity (ω in rad/s).** Examiners will mark this distinction. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ⭐ **Enrichment** is increasing the ²³⁵U mole fraction above its natural 0.711% — needed for LWR fuel (3–5%) and weapons (≥ 90%). - ⭐ **UF₆ is the universal working gas** — chosen because it is the only gaseous uranium compound, and fluorine has M = 19, so the isotopic mass difference dominates. - ⭐ **Gaseous diffusion** uses **Graham's Law**: lighter ²³⁵UF₆ effuses through a porous barrier slightly faster. - ⭐ **Ideal α for diffusion** = $\sqrt{M_{238}/M_{235}} \approx$ **1.0043**; this dictates the need for **hundreds to thousands of stages**. - ⭐ **Gas centrifuge** spins UF₆ at 50,000–90,000 rpm; heavier ²³⁸UF₆ moves to wall, lighter ²³⁵UF₆ to axis. - ⭐ **Centrifuge α** (equilibrium) = $\exp[\Delta M \cdot \omega^2 R^2 / (2RT)]$; with **counter-current scoop**, $\alpha_{\text{eff}} = \alpha_0^K$, K ≈ 5–20. - ⭐ **Energy:** Centrifuge uses **~ 1/15th** the energy of gaseous diffusion per SWU — the reason it has replaced diffusion. - ⭐ **SWU** (Separative Work Unit) is **NOT mass**; it is a measure of the *thermodynamic effort* of separation. - ⭐ **Value function:** $V(X) = (2X-1)\ln[X/(1-X)]$; always evaluate it to **3 significant figures** in numericals. - ⭐ **Total SWU equation:** $\Delta U = P V(X_p) + T V(X_t) - F V(X_f)$, with $F = P + T$. - ⭐ **Mass balance shortcuts:** $P = F \cdot (X_f - X_t)/(X_p - X_t)$ and $T = F - P$ — memorize these. - ⭐ **Knudsen flow** must be ensured (K_n > 1) by keeping the **barrier pore size < mean free path of UF₆** (~ 10 nm at 1 atm). - ⭐ **Modern era (2024):** Gaseous diffusion is essentially **obsolete**; gas centrifuge dominates, with **SILEX (laser) emerging** as the next-generation method. - ⭐ **Proliferation concern:** Centrifuge technology is on the **NSG (Nuclear Suppliers Group) trigger list** — a key geopolitical issue KTU may test in 2-mark conceptual questions. - ⭐ **Master the example calculation** in Section 3.3 — it covers ~ 80% of typical KTU 14-mark numericals.

topics.title: Fuel Fabrication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fuel Fabrication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!NOTE]
> **Fuel Fabrication** is the meticulously controlled industrial process of converting nuclear-grade fissile material (primarily enriched uranium dioxide, $\text{UO}_2$, or mixed oxide $\text{MOX}$) into high-density, dimensionally precise ceramic pellets, sealing them inside hermetic metallic cladding tubes, and assembling these rods into structural fuel bundles/assemblies suitable for loading into a nuclear reactor core.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, fuel fabrication is the **bridge process** between the front-end fuel cycle (mining, milling, conversion, enrichment) and the in-core operational life of the fuel. The output is a **Fuel Assembly (FA)** engineered to withstand **~1600 K peak temperatures**, **~15 MPa coolant pressure**, intense neutron flux ($\sim 10^{14}$ n/cm²·s), and radiation doses of **> 100 GWd/MTU** without failure.

### 1.2 Key Fuel Forms in Commercial Reactors

| Fuel Type | Chemical Form | Primary Reactor Use |
|---|---|---|
| LWR Oxide | Enriched $\text{UO}_2$ | PWR, BWR, VVER |
| MOX | $(\text{U,Pu})\text{O}_2$ | PWR, BWR (recycle) |
| PHWR Fuel | Natural $\text{UO}_2$ | CANDU, Indian PHWRs |
| Metal Fuel | U-Zr, U-Pu-Zr | Fast Breeder Reactors (FBTR, PFBR) |
| TRISO | $\text{UO}_2$/$\text{UC}_2$ in pyrocarbon/SiC | HTGR, HTR-PM |

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: "The High-Performance Engine Piston"**
> Think of a nuclear fuel pellet as an **extremely high-performance engine piston**, but for a 40,000-hour continuous operation under extreme heat and radiation:
> 1. **The Fuel Pellet** ≈ The piston head — it must be dense, dimensionally perfect, and able to release enormous energy uniformly without cracking.
> 2. **The Cladding Tube** ≈ The cylinder wall — a hermetic metallic sleeve that keeps the radioactive fission products contained and transfers heat to the coolant.
> 3. **The Fuel Assembly** ≈ The complete engine block — structurally rigid, precisely spaced, and interchangeable within the reactor core grid.
>
> Just as a tiny defect in a piston can destroy a car engine, a microscopic crack or impurity in a fuel pellet can cause **pellet-cladding interaction (PCI)** failure or **hot-spot formation**, which is why **tolerances are held within ± 0.01 mm** in modern fabrication plants.

### 1.4 Core Engineering Tolerances & Quality Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Critical Fabrication Specs**
> - Pellet diameter tolerance: **± 0.01 mm to ± 0.025 mm**
> - Pellet density: **95 % – 97.5 % of Theoretical Density (TD)**
> - $\text{UO}_2$ Theoretical Density: **10.96 g/cm³**
> - Surface roughness (grinding): **< 0.8 µm Ra**
> - Cladding material: **Zircaloy-4 (PWR/PHWR) / Zircaloy-2 (BWR)**
> - Cladding wall thickness: **0.57 mm – 0.72 mm** (typical for 9.5 mm OD rods)

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 7-Stage Process Logic of LWR Fuel Fabrication

> [!NOTE]
> The modern LWR fuel fabrication chain is a **dry, powder-metallurgical process** (with one wet conversion step upstream). Each stage has a measurable KPI.

1. **Stage 1 — UF₆ to ADU Conversion (Wet Route)**
   - Gaseous $\text{UF}_6$ is reacted with water to form $\text{UO}_2\text{F}_2$ solution.
   - Precipitation with $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ produces **Ammonium Di-Uranate (ADU)** — "yellowcake" of fuel grade.
   - ADU is filtered, dried, and calcined to $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8$.

2. **Stage 2 — Reduction to $\text{UO}_2$ Powder**
   - $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8$ is reduced in a **hydrogen atmosphere** at $\sim 700\text{°C}$:
     $$\text{U}_3\text{O}_8 + 2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{UO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
   - Produces a **free-flowing, sinterable $\text{UO}_2$ powder** with controlled particle size (~10–50 µm) and surface area (2–6 m²/g).

3. **Stage 3 — Powder Conditioning & Pre-Compaction**
   - Lubricant (**Zinc stearate**, ~0.3 % by weight) is added to prevent die-wall friction.
   - Powder is pre-compacted into "green" granules (~0.5 g/cm³) for flowability.

4. **Stage 4 — Cold Pressing (Pelletization)**
   - Granules are pressed in **hydraulic/mechanical presses** at **200 – 400 MPa**.
   - Achieves "green" density of **~50–60 % TD**.
   - Pellet shape: cylindrical, typically **8.0–9.5 mm diameter, 10–15 mm height** with dished ends.

5. **Stage 5 — High-Temperature Sintering**
   - Green pellets are sintered in **pusher-type or walking-beam furnaces**.
   - Atmosphere: **Cracked ammonia (75 % H₂ + 25 % N₂)** or pure $\text{H}_2$.
   - Temperature: **1650 °C – 1750 °C** for **4–8 hours**.
   - Result: sintered density **95–97.5 % TD**, with grain size of **8–12 µm**.

6. **Stage 6 — Centerless Grinding**
   - Sintered pellets are ground to **± 0.01 mm** diameter tolerance.
   - Surface finish: **< 0.8 µm Ra** (mirror-like) to minimize **fretting wear** on cladding.
   - **Dishing & chamfering** of ends reduces axial stresses.

7. **Stage 7 — Fuel Rod & Assembly Fabrication**
   - Pellets are stacked into **Zircaloy cladding tubes** under **helium fill gas** (1–3 atm).
   - End plugs welded (TIG or laser).
   - Rods are assembled with **spacer grids**, **end fittings**, and ** nozzles** to form the complete fuel assembly.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Equations for KTU 2024 End-Semester Exam (PEMET412 Module 2)**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Variables & Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theoretical Density of $\text{UO}_2$ | $\rho_{TD} = 10.96 \text{ g/cm}^3$ | Constant | Reference for %TD calculation |
| 2 | Sintered (Geometric) Density | $\%TD = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{sintered}}}{\rho_{TD}} \times 100$ | $\rho$ in g/cm³ | Quality control of pellets |
| 3 | Geometric Density of Pellet | $\rho_{pellet} = \dfrac{m_{pellet}}{\pi r^2 h}$ | $r$ = radius (cm), $h$ = height (cm) | Mass-per-pellet loading |
| 4 | Smear Density | $\rho_{smear} = \dfrac{m_{\text{uo}_2}}{V_{\text{free}} - V_{pellet}}$ | g/cm³ | Gap design, PCI prevention |
| 5 | U-235 Linear Loading | $m_{25} = x \times \rho_{TD} \times V_{pellet} \times 0.881$ | $x$ = enrichment (wt%), 0.881 = U fraction in $\text{UO}_2$ | Enrichment verification |
| 6 | Enrichment Mass Fraction | $x = \dfrac{m_{U-235}}{m_{U}} \times 100\%$ | wt% | Fuel qualification |
| 7 | Heat Output per Pellet | $Q_p = q''' \times V_{pellet}$ | $q'''$ = volumetric heat rate (W/cm³) | Thermal-hydraulic design |
| 8 | Linear Heat Rate | $q' = q''' \times A_{pellet}$ | W/cm | Pellet centre temp estimate |
| 9 | Burnup Equation | $BU = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{m_{\text{initial U}}}$ | MWd/MTU | Fuel life assessment |
| 10 | Volumetric Heat Rate | $q''' = \dfrac{P_{\text{core}}}{V_{\text{fuel}}}$ | W/cm³ | Reactor physics verification |
| 11 | Free Volume in Rod | $V_{free} = \pi (r_o^2 - r_i^2) L$ | cm³ | Helium plenum calculation |
| 12 | Stoichiometry of $\text{UO}_2$ | $O/U = 2.00 \pm 0.01$ | dimensionless | Chemistry control |
| 13 | Grain Growth (Arrhenius) | $D^2 - D_0^2 = K_0 t \exp(-Q/RT)$ | $Q$ = activation energy, $T$ = K | Sintering kinetics |
| 14 | Open Porosity Limit | $P_{\text{open}} < 0.5\% \text{ TD}$ | — | Hermeticity test |
| 15 | Stack Length (Pellets in Rod) | $L_{stack} = N \times h_{pellet}$ | cm | Fuel rod design |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Table Safety**: All absolute values and divisions use `\dfrac` / `\frac` / `\exp` to avoid breaking markdown with the pipe `|` symbol.

### 2.3 Why Each Parameter Matters — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **% TD control (95–97.5 %)** — Below 95 % → poor thermal conductivity (overheating); above 97.5 % → poor fission gas retention.
- **Grain size (8–12 µm)** — Larger grains **trap fission gases** (Xe, Kr) inside the pellet, reducing cladding stress from gas pressure buildup.
- **Surface finish < 0.8 µm** — Prevents **fretting corrosion** where pellet-cladding mechanical contact occurs due to vibration.
- **Dished ends** — Allow axial thermal expansion (pellet grows ~1 % in volume during burnup) without cracking the cladding.
- **Helium backfill (1–3 atm)** — Helium is **inert, high-thermal-conductivity gas** that improves heat transfer and resists water-vapour corrosion.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Density Verification of a Sintered $\text{UO}_2$ Pellet

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (KTU-Style)**: A cylindrical $\text{UO}_2$ pellet is sintered. It has a measured mass of **7.80 g**, diameter **9.50 mm**, and height **12.00 mm**. The theoretical density of $\text{UO}_2$ is **10.96 g/cm³**. Determine (a) the geometric density of the pellet, (b) the %TD achieved, and (c) whether it passes the KTU quality standard (95–97.5 % TD).

#### (a) Geometric Density of the Pellet

**Step 1** — Convert dimensions to cm:
$$d = 9.50 \text{ mm} = 0.950 \text{ cm}$$
$$h = 12.00 \text{ mm} = 1.200 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2** — Compute the radius:
$$r = \frac{d}{2} = \frac{0.950}{2} = 0.475 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 3** — Compute the pellet volume (cylinder formula):
$$V_{pellet} = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times (0.475)^2 \times 1.200$$

**Step 4** — Square the radius:
$$(0.475)^2 = 0.225625 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 5** — Multiply by height:
$$0.225625 \times 1.200 = 0.27075 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 6** — Multiply by $\pi$:
$$V_{pellet} = \pi \times 0.27075 = 0.8506 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 7** — Compute the geometric density:
$$\rho_{pellet} = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{7.80 \text{ g}}{0.8506 \text{ cm}^3} = 9.169 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

#### (b) Percentage of Theoretical Density

**Step 8** — Apply the %TD formula:
$$\%TD = \frac{\rho_{pellet}}{\rho_{TD}} \times 100$$

**Step 9** — Substitute values:
$$\%TD = \frac{9.169}{10.96} \times 100$$

**Step 10** — Evaluate:
$$\%TD = 0.8366 \times 100 = 83.66\%$$

#### (c) Quality Assessment

> [!WARNING]
> **Result:** 83.66 % TD is **BELOW the 95 % minimum** acceptance criterion.
> **Engineering Conclusion:** This is an **unsintered "green" pellet** — either sintering temperature or time was insufficient, OR the powder had poor sinterability. The pellet would fail QA and must be re-processed.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — U-235 Mass Loading Per Pellet

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement**: Calculate the U-235 mass in a $\text{UO}_2$ pellet (4 % enriched, sintered to 95 % TD, $d = 9.5$ mm, $h = 12$ mm).

**Step 1** — Pellet volume (from Example 1, Step 6):
$$V_{pellet} = 0.8506 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 2** — Sintered density at 95 % TD:
$$\rho_{\text{sinter}} = 0.95 \times 10.96 = 10.412 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3** — Mass of $\text{UO}_2$ pellet:
$$m_{\text{UO}_2} = \rho \times V = 10.412 \times 0.8506 = 8.857 \text{ g}$$

**Step 4** — Mass of Uranium in $\text{UO}_2$ (U mass fraction = 0.8814):
$$m_U = 0.8814 \times 8.857 = 7.807 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5** — Mass of U-235 (4 % enrichment):
$$m_{U-235} = 0.04 \times 7.807 = 0.3123 \text{ g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Each pellet contains **~0.312 g of fissile U-235**. For a typical 17×17 PWR assembly with **264 fuel rods × ~300 pellets/rod ≈ 79,200 pellets**, the U-235 inventory per assembly is **~24.7 kg**, which justifies the strict IAEA safeguards and tamper-proof packaging protocols.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Burnup Limit Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem**: A 17×17 PWR fuel assembly contains **461 kg of initial U**, operates for **3 years (1095 days)** at an average power of **17.0 MW**. Find the discharge burnup.

**Step 1** — Total energy produced:
$$E_{\text{out}} = P \times t = 17.0 \text{ MW} \times 1095 \text{ days} = 18{,}615 \text{ MWd}$$

**Step 2** — Burnup formula:
$$BU = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{m_{\text{initial U}}}$$

**Step 3** — Convert mass to metric tons of uranium (MTU):
$$m_U = 461 \text{ kg} = 0.461 \text{ MTU}$$

**Step 4** — Compute burnup:
$$BU = \frac{18{,}615 \text{ MWd}}{0.461 \text{ MTU}} = 40{,}380 \text{ MWd/MTU}$$

**Step 5** — Convert to GWd/MTU:
$$BU = 40.38 \text{ GWd/MTU}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is **above the typical licensing limit of ~60 GWd/MTU peak rod**, confirming the assembly still has life. Standard PWR discharge burnups are **45–55 GWd/MTU**.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Common Fabrication Defects

| Defect | Root Cause | Effect in Reactor | Mitigation in Fabrication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low density (<95 % TD) | Insufficient sintering temp/time | Centre melting | Raise sinter temp to 1700°C |
| High density (>97.5 % TD) | Excessive sintering | Fission gas release | Lower dwell time |
| Cracked pellet | Pressing pressure too high | Fretting failure | Reduce press force to 250 MPa |
| Off-diameter pellet | Die wear / grinding drift | Cladding damage | Tool replacement schedule |
| High O/M ratio (>2.05) | Poor $\text{H}_2$ atmosphere | Lower thermal conductivity | Check furnace leak rate |
| Open porosity (>0.5 %) | Inadequate sintering | Fission gas release | Increase sinter time |

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete LWR Fuel Fabrication Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UF6 Cylinder In] --> B[UF6 to ADU Conversion Tank]
    B --> C[ADU Filter and Dryer]
    C --> D[Calcination Furnace to U3O8]
    D --> E[Reduction Furnace in H2 to UO2 Powder]
    E --> F[Powder Blending with Zn Stearate]
    F --> G[Pre-Compaction Granulator]
    G --> H[Hydraulic Press at 300 MPa]
    H --> I[Green Pellets]
    I --> J[Sintering Furnace 1700 C in H2]
    J --> K[Centerless Grinder]
    K --> L[Dish and Chamfer Machine]
    L --> M[Quality Inspection Lab]
    M --> N{Acceptance 95 to 97.5 percent TD}
    N -->|Pass| O[Load into Zircaloy Cladding]
    N -->|Reject| P[Recycle Powder Stream]
    O --> Q[Helium Backfill and Plug Weld]
    Q --> R[End Plug TIG Welding]
    R --> S[Leak Test and Ultrasonic Scan]
    S --> T[Spacer Grid Assembly]
    T --> U[Complete 17x17 Fuel Assembly]
    U --> V[Clean Room Packaging and Ship to Reactor]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A`, `B`, `node1`) and labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting, ensuring safe Mermaid v10 compilation.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Zircaloy Fuel Rod Cross-Sectional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Zircaloy-4 Cladding Tube OD 9.5 mm] --> B[Wall Thickness 0.57 mm]
    B --> C[Inner Diameter 8.36 mm]
    C --> D[UO2 Pellet Stack Length 3.6 m]
    D --> E[Plenum Region Helium 3 atm]
    E --> F[Spring Plenum Compressor]
    F --> G[Bottom End Plug Welded]
    A --> H[Top End Plug Welded]
    H --> I[Inconel Spacer Grid]
    I --> J[Mixing Vane for Coolant]
    J --> K[Upper Tie Plate and Handle]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pellet Microstructure (Sintered $\text{UO}_2$)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Sintered UO2 Pellet Matrix] --> N[UO2 Grains 8 to 12 um]
    M --> O[Closed Pores at Grain Junctions]
    M --> P[Grain Boundaries Grain Boundary Diffusion Path]
    N --> Q[Fission Gas Bubbles Xe and Kr Trapped]
    O --> R[Low Open Porosity less than 0.5 percent]
    P --> S[Heat Conduction Path Through Lattice]
    Q --> T[Prevents Fission Gas Release to Cladding]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

#### Question 1. Define fuel fabrication. List the four main stages of ceramic $\text{UO}_2$ pellet preparation. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Fuel fabrication is the process of converting enriched uranium compounds into nuclear fuel pellets, sealing them in metallic cladding, and assembling them into reactor-loadable fuel bundles.
The four main stages of ceramic $\text{UO}_2$ pellet preparation are:
1. **$\text{UO}_2$ Powder Preparation** (UF₆ → ADU → $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8$ → $\text{UO}_2$).
2. **Cold Pressing** of powder into green pellets at 200–400 MPa.
3. **High-Temperature Sintering** at 1650–1750 °C in $\text{H}_2$ atmosphere.
4. **Centerless Grinding** to final diameter tolerance of ± 0.01 mm.

---

#### Question 2. Why is the Zircaloy cladding tube backfilled with helium at 1–3 atm during fuel rod fabrication? Mention two reasons. `[3 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**
Helium backfill is performed for two principal reasons:
1. **Thermal Conductivity Enhancement** — Helium has a high thermal conductivity (~0.15 W/m·K at 1 atm) and acts as a heat-transfer medium in the gap between pellet and cladding, reducing the pellet centreline temperature by ~100–200 °C.
2. **Chemical Inertness & Corrosion Prevention** — Helium is monatomic and inert, preventing any chemical reaction between the fuel and the cladding internal surface even at 1500 K operating temperature, unlike nitrogen or air which would form zirconium nitrides or oxides.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — End Semester Model Pattern]**

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**Q. (a)** Describe the step-by-step industrial process of $\text{UO}_2$ pellet fabrication starting from $\text{UF}_6$ gas. Include key temperatures, atmospheres, and the role of each stage. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Conversion of $\text{UF}_6$ to ADU (Ammonium Di-Uranate):**
   Gaseous $\text{UF}_6$ is vaporized from heated cylinders and bubbled through water to form uranyl fluoride ($\text{UO}_2\text{F}_2$) solution. Ammonium hydroxide ($\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$) is added to precipitate ADU.

2. **Calcination to $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8$:**
   ADU is dried and calcined at ~450 °C in air to yield stable $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8$ powder.

3. **Reduction to $\text{UO}_2$:**
   $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8$ is reduced in a **hydrogen atmosphere** at ~700 °C in a fluidized-bed or rotary furnace:
   $$\text{U}_3\text{O}_8 + 2\text{H}_2 \longrightarrow 3\,\text{UO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
   This produces a **sinterable, free-flowing $\text{UO}_2$ powder** with surface area 2–6 m²/g.

4. **Powder Conditioning:**
   A lubricant (**zinc stearate, 0.3 wt%**) is blended to reduce die-wall friction during pressing.

5. **Cold Pressing:**
   The powder is pressed in **hydraulic/mechanical presses at 200–400 MPa** to form "green" cylindrical pellets with dished ends. Green density is ~50–60 % TD.

6. **Sintering:**
   Green pellets are sintered in **pusher-type furnaces at 1650–1750 °C for 4–8 hours** in **cracked ammonia (75 % $\text{H}_2$ + 25 % $\text{N}_2$)** or pure $\text{H}_2$ atmosphere. The sintered density achieved is **95–97.5 % TD**.

7. **Centerless Grinding:**
   Sintered pellets are ground to final diameter tolerance of **± 0.01 mm** with surface finish **< 0.8 µm Ra**. Dishing and chamfering of ends is performed to accommodate thermal expansion.

8. **Quality Inspection:**
   Each pellet is checked for dimensions, density, grain size, stoichiometry (O/U ≈ 2.00), and absence of cracks before being released for fuel rod loading.

---

**Q. (b)** A sintered $\text{UO}_2$ pellet has a mass of **8.50 g** and is cylindrical with diameter **10.00 mm** and height **13.00 mm**. The theoretical density of $\text{UO}_2$ is **10.96 g/cm³**. Determine (i) the geometric density, (ii) the %TD, and (iii) comment on whether it passes the KTU fabrication standard. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1** — Convert dimensions to cm:
$$d = 1.000 \text{ cm}, \quad r = 0.500 \text{ cm}, \quad h = 1.300 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 2** — Compute volume:
$$V = \pi r^2 h = \pi \times (0.500)^2 \times 1.300 = \pi \times 0.250 \times 1.300 = 1.0207 \text{ cm}^3$$

**Step 3** — Geometric density:
$$\rho = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{8.50}{1.0207} = 8.327 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 4** — Percentage of theoretical density:
$$\%TD = \frac{8.327}{10.96} \times 100 = 75.97\%$$

**Step 5** — Quality assessment:
The pellet is at only **75.97 % TD**, which is far below the required **95–97.5 % TD** range. This indicates the pellet is essentially in a **green (un-sintered) state**. It **fails the KTU fabrication standard** and must be re-fired at 1700 °C in $\text{H}_2$ atmosphere for proper densification.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**Q. (a)** Explain the constructional features of a typical PWR fuel assembly with a neat sketch. What materials are used for cladding, grids, and end fittings, and why? `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

A typical **17×17 PWR fuel assembly** consists of the following constructional features:

1. **Fuel Rods (264 nos.):** Each rod is a **Zircaloy-4 tube** (OD ~9.5 mm, wall thickness ~0.57 mm) containing ~300 sintered $\text{UO}_2$ pellets stacked to ~3.6 m, backfilled with **helium gas at ~3 atm**, and sealed by TIG-welded **Zircaloy end plugs**. A plenum spring is included to accommodate axial expansion.

2. **Spacer Grids (8 to 10 nos. per assembly):** Made of **Inconel-718** or **Zircaloy-4** strips, the grids hold the rods in a 17×17 square array. They incorporate **mixing vanes** for coolant turbulence and enhanced heat transfer.

3. **Guide Thimbles (25 nos.) and Instrument Thimble (1 no.):** Empty tubes that accept **Control Rod Drive Mechanism (CRDM)** fingers, **Burnable Poison Rods**, and **in-core neutron detectors**.

4. **Upper and Lower End Fittings:** Forged from **stainless steel (Type 304)**. They house the top nozzle, hold the assembly in the core, and handle **lift/handling loads during refuelling**.

**Material Rationale:**
- **Zircaloy-4** cladding: chosen for its **low thermal-neutron absorption cross-section (0.18 barns)**, high corrosion resistance in water, and adequate mechanical strength at 350 °C.
- **Inconel-718** grids: chosen for its **high spring-back capacity** to grip rods tightly and **fatigue strength** under coolant flow-induced vibration.
- **Stainless steel 304** end fittings: chosen for its **high tensile strength** to bear the ~2 t lifting load of a spent assembly.

---

**Q. (b)** A fuel assembly contains **461 kg of U** with average **enrichment of 4.2 %** in U-235. After **3 cycles of 18 months each (1647 days total)** at an average assembly power of **17.5 MW**, calculate the discharge burnup in **MWd/MTU** and **GWd/MTU**. State whether the assembly is ready for discharge. `[7 Marks]`

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1** — Total energy produced:
$$E_{\text{out}} = P \times t = 17.5 \text{ MW} \times 1647 \text{ days} = 28{,}822.5 \text{ MWd}$$

**Step 2** — Convert uranium mass to MTU:
$$m_U = 461 \text{ kg} = 0.461 \text{ MTU}$$

**Step 3** — Apply burnup equation:
$$BU = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{m_U} = \frac{28{,}822.5 \text{ MWd}}{0.461 \text{ MTU}}$$

**Step 4** — Compute:
$$BU = 62{,}522 \text{ MWd/MTU} = 62.5 \text{ GWd/MTU}$$

**Step 5** — Disposition decision:
- The **peak rod burnup** (1.2× average) is ~**75 GWd/MTU**.
- Standard PWR licensing limit is typically **60 GWd/MTU peak**.
- The assembly **exceeds the typical 60 GWd/MTU discharge limit** and **must be discharged** for storage or reprocessing.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert **kg → MTU** (divide by 1000) before dividing, leading to a numerical error of exactly 1000×. Always double-check the units of the final answer. A 62,522,000 MWd/MTU result (wrong) versus 62,522 MWd/MTU (correct) is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU PEMET412 Module 2 — Fuel Fabrication: Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **Definition:** Fuel fabrication = Conversion of fissile material → pellets → rods → assemblies.
- **Theoretical Density of $\text{UO}_2$:** **10.96 g/cm³** (memorize this constant).
- **Sintered density target:** **95–97.5 % TD** — this is the single most tested parameter.
- **Sintering temperature:** **1650–1750 °C**; atmosphere: **hydrogen or cracked ammonia**.
- **Sintering time:** **4–8 hours**.
- **Cold pressing pressure:** **200–400 MPa**.
- **Lubricant:** **Zinc stearate, 0.3 wt%**.
- **Cladding material:** **Zircaloy-4 (PWR) / Zircaloy-2 (BWR)**; chosen for **low neutron absorption cross-section (~0.18 barns)**.
- **Backfill gas:** **Helium at 1–3 atm** (inert, high thermal conductivity).
- **Pellet diameter tolerance:** **± 0.01 mm** (after grinding).
- **Surface roughness:** **< 0.8 µm Ra**.
- **Grain size:** **8–12 µm** (larger grains → better fission gas retention).
- **O/M ratio:** **2.00 ± 0.01** (stoichiometry control).
- **Conversion chemistry:** $\text{U}_3\text{O}_8 + 2\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 3\,\text{UO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$.
- **U mass fraction in $\text{UO}_2$:** **0.8814** (use this in enrichment-loading problems).
- **Enrichment range (LWR):** **3–5 % U-235**; (PHWR uses natural U = 0.71 %); (research reactors: 20–93 %).
- **PWR FA typical:** **17×17 array, 264 fuel rods, 25 guide thimbles**, ~461 kg U.
- **Burnup typical:** **45–55 GWd/MTU**; licensing limit ~60 GWd/MTU peak.
- **4 main fuel-assembly components to remember:** (1) Fuel Rods, (2) Spacer Grids, (3) Guide Thimbles, (4) End Fittings.
- **Two biggest pellet defects:** (1) Cracking (from over-pressing), (2) Off-diameter (from grinding drift).
- **Three reasons for helium backfill:** (1) Heat transfer, (2) Inertness, (3) Reduces $\text{UO}_2$–cladding chemical interaction.
- **One-line exam mantra:** *Powder → Press → Sinter → Grind → Inspect → Load → Weld → Assemble.* Use it as a memory anchor.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commuting Radioactive Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Commuting Radioactive Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Commuting Radioactive Materials

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Formal)**
> *Commuting (Transport) of Radioactive Material* refers to the deliberate physical movement of radioactive substances — including spent fuel, fresh fuel, radioactive waste, radioisotopes for medical/industrial use, and contaminated equipment — from one location to another by road, rail, sea, or air, conducted under a strict regulatory framework designed to protect workers, the public, and the environment from ionizing radiation and to prevent criticality or release of contents.

---

## 1.1 The Big Picture — Why "Commute" Radioactivity?

Imagine you have a tiny, glowing coal that never stops burning and cannot be switched off. That is exactly what a radioactive source is. Now imagine you must safely *mail* this glowing coal from a hospital in Chennai to a research lab in Kerala. You cannot just put it in a courier bag — it must survive falls, rain, fire, pressure changes, and rough handling, while shielding people around it from radiation.

This is the essence of **commuting radioactive materials** (more formally called **transport of radioactive material**). It is the "logistics backbone" of the entire nuclear industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Fact:** Globally, **over 20 million** consignments of radioactive material are transported safely every year. The safety record is built on **defence-in-depth** — using purpose-built packages, strict labels, trained personnel, and international regulations.

---

## 1.2 Categories of Radioactive Material in Transit

Radioactive material for transport is grouped into three broad physical categories:

1. **Special Form Radioactive Material** — An indispersible solid, or a sealed capsule, that cannot be easily dispersed (e.g., sealed Co-60 sources used in radiotherapy).
2. **Normal Form Radioactive Material** — Anything that is *not* special form — powders, liquids, gases (e.g., UF₆ used in fuel fabrication, MOX powder).
3. **Fissile Material** — Material containing U-233, U-235, Pu-239, Pu-241 in quantities that could potentially sustain a chain reaction (e.g., fresh UO₂ fuel assemblies, separated plutonium).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **SNF** — **S**pecial form, **N**ormal form, **F**issile. Always classify the material first before selecting the package.

---

## 1.3 The Three Foundational Principles of Safe Transport

The International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA) — and in India, the Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB) — has codified three non-negotiable principles:

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|-----------|---------|
| 1 | **Containment** | The radioactive contents must remain inside the package under all conditions of transport, including accidents. |
| 2 | **Shielding** | The package must reduce radiation levels at its surface and at 1 m to within regulatory limits. |
| 3 | **Criticality Control** | For fissile material, the package and array of packages must remain subcritical — *k*<sub>eff</sub> ≤ 0.95 under normal and accident conditions. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Most students lose marks by *missing criticality*. Whenever the question says "fissile material," your answer MUST address subcriticality (k-effective control), not just shielding.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Regulatory Framework — The Hierarchy of Rules

The transport of radioactive material is governed by a layered regulatory framework:

```
INTERNATIONAL (IAEA — SSR-6, 2018 Edition)
        │
        ▼
NATIONAL  (India: AERB Safety Code No. AERB/NRF-TS/SC-1, 
           based on IAEA SSR-6)
        │
        ▼
CARRIER   (IATA for air, IMDG for sea, ICAO, 
           Indian Railways, Motor Vehicles Rules)
        │
        ▼
CONSIGNOR (Operator-specific QA Programme)
```

> [!NOTE]
> In **India**, the governing document is the **AERB Safety Code "Transport of Radioactive Material"** and **AERB Safety Guide AERB/SG/TR-1**. Every package design must obtain **design approval** from AERB before being used.

---

## 2.2 Package Classification — The Five Families

IAEA classifies packages into five categories. Selection depends on the **Activity (A)** of the contents relative to two regulatory limits:

- **A₁** — Maximum activity allowed in **special form** for a Type A package (in TBq).
- **A₂** — Maximum activity allowed in **normal form** for a Type A package (in TBq).

| Package Type | Activity Limit | Use Case |
|--------------|----------------|----------|
| **Excepted Package** | Very low (e.g., < 10⁻³ × A₂) | Smoke detectors, depleted uranium counterweights |
| **Industrial Package (IP-1)** | Low specific activity (LSA-I) | Ore, natural/concentrated uranium |
| **Industrial Package (IP-2)** | LSA-II, LSA-III | Contaminated soil, activated equipment |
| **Industrial Package (IP-3)** | LSA-III, SCO | Reactor internals, decommissioning waste |
| **Type A Package** | A₁ (special form) or A₂ (normal form) | Most medical/industrial isotopes (e.g., Mo-99, Ir-192) |
| **Type B(U) Package** | Above A₂ limits | Spent fuel, high-activity Co-60 sources |
| **Type B(M) Package** | Above A₂, requires multilateral approval | Continuous venting or interactive cooling |
| **Type C Package** | High-activity, air transport only | Large quantities by aircraft |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Type B(U) = Unilateral approval** (only the country of origin approves the design).  
> **Type B(M) = Multilateral approval** (origin + transit + destination countries all approve).  
> The **(U)** is critical for KTU 14-mark questions.

---

## 2.3 The Q-System for Material Classification

Before selecting the package, the radionuclide inventory is classified using the **Q-System**:

$$Q = \frac{\text{Activity of Radionuclide (TBq)}}{A_2 \text{ for that radionuclide (TBq)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule:** If the calculated **Q ≤ 1**, the consignment may be shipped in a **Type A** package.  
> If **Q > 1**, a **Type B(U)** or higher package is mandatory.

---

## 2.4 Label Categories and Transport Index (TI)

Every package (except Excepted) carries one of three trefoil warning labels:

| Label | Colour | Surface Dose Rate | TI at 1 m |
|-------|--------|-------------------|-----------|
| **I-WHITE** | White | ≤ **5 µSv/h** | TI = 0 |
| **II-YELLOW** | Yellow upper | > 5 µSv/h but ≤ **500 µSv/h** | TI > 0 |
| **III-YELLOW** | Yellow lower | > 500 µSv/h but ≤ **2000 µSv/h** (2 mSv/h) | TI > 0 |

The **Transport Index (TI)** is the most important single number in transport regulation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TI (Transport Index) Formula (IAEA Definition):**
> $$\text{TI} = \text{Maximum radiation level at 1 m from the package surface, in mrem/h}$$
> In SI units: TI = Maximum dose rate (μSv/h) at 1 m × (1/10).

So a TI = 0.5 means the dose rate at 1 m is **5 μSv/h** (or **0.5 mrem/h**).

The **Criticality Safety Index (CSI)** for fissile material packages is a separate number:

$$\text{CSI} = \frac{50}{S}$$
where *S* is the *number of such packages* needed to form an infinite array that would reach criticality.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Transport Index | TI = Dose rate at 1 m (mrem/h) | dimensionless | Round **up** to one decimal |
| 2 | Criticality Safety Index | CSI = 50 / S | dimensionless | S = packages to reach critical |
| 3 | Q-system | Q = A / A₂ | dimensionless | If Q ≤ 1 → Type A |
| 4 | Surface dose limit (normal) | D<sub>s</sub> ≤ 2 mSv/h | μSv/h | For yellow-III packages |
| 5 | 2 m from package | D<sub>2m</sub> ≤ 0.1 mSv/h | μSv/h | Stated on the label |
| 6 | Vehicle limit (open) | D ≤ 2 mSv/h at surface | μSv/h | Driver must be trained |
| 7 | Vehicle limit (closed) | D ≤ 0.1 mSv/h at cabin | μSv/h | For occupied vehicles |
| 8 | Decay correction | A(t) = A₀ · e<sup>-λt</sup> | Bq, TBq | λ = ln 2 / t₁/₂ |
| 9 | Half-life conversion | λ = 0.693 / t₁/₂ | s⁻¹ | Always in SI base units |
| 10 | Dose rate at distance (point source) | D(r) = D(r₀) · (r₀/r)² | μSv/h | Inverse square law |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Point for KTU:** Use the **inverse square law** — if dose rate D₁ is known at distance r₁, then at distance r₂ it becomes **D₂ = D₁ × (r₁/r₂)²**. This appears in nearly every numerical problem.

---

## 2.6 Engineering Design Tests Packages Must Survive

A Type B(U) package must demonstrate it can survive — without loss of containment or increase in radiation by more than 20% — the following sequence:

| Test | Conditions | Purpose |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Free Drop** | 9 m onto unyielding surface | Simulates a fall from a truck |
| **Puncture** | 1 m drop onto a 15 cm dia steel bar | Simulates a sharp object penetrating |
| **Thermal** | 800 °C fire for 30 minutes | Simulates a jet-fuel fire |
| **Water Immersion** | 15 m for 8 hours (B(U)) / 200 m for 1 hour (B(M) for marine) | Simulates flooding or sea sink |
| **Cumulative Accident** | Drop + Puncture + Fire in sequence | Realistic worst-case |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for KTU Viva — "D-F-P-I":** **D**rop, **F**ire, **P**uncture, **I**mmersion.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Computing Transport Index (TI) and Dose Rate at a Distance

### Problem Statement
A Type B(U) package has a measured dose rate of **0.4 mSv/h at 1 metre** from its surface, and a measured dose rate of **6 mSv/h at the package surface**.  
(a) Determine the Transport Index (TI) of the package.  
(b) Determine the label category (I-WHITE, II-YELLOW, or III-YELLOW).  
(c) If a worker stands **5 m away** from the package, what is the dose rate he/she receives? Assume the package acts as a *point source*.  
(d) If the package contains Co-60 (t₁/₂ = 5.27 years) with initial activity A₀ = 100 TBq, what is its activity after 3 years of storage before transport?

---

### Part (a): Transport Index

By IAEA definition:

$$\text{TI (in mrem/h)} = \text{Dose rate at 1 m in mrem/h}$$

Converting from mSv/h to mrem/h using **1 mSv = 100 mrem**:

$$D_{1m} = 0.4 \text{ mSv/h} = 0.4 \times 100 \text{ mrem/h} = 40 \text{ mrem/h}$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\text{TI} = 40}$$

But by convention, TI is reported as a single decimal number (in mrem/h) — so the working value used in transport documents is **TI = 40** (a very high number, indicating this is a large spent-fuel cask).

> [!NOTE]
> The TI is *dimensionless* in regulatory documents — the actual unit system (mrem/h) is implied. Many KTU problems simply state TI = (Dose rate in mrem/h at 1 m).

---

### Part (b): Label Category

The label depends on the **surface** dose rate:
- I-WHITE: D<sub>surface</sub> ≤ 5 μSv/h (0.005 mSv/h)
- II-YELLOW: 5 μSv/h < D<sub>surface</sub> ≤ 500 μSv/h
- III-YELLOW: 500 μSv/h < D<sub>surface</sub> ≤ 2000 μSv/h (2 mSv/h)

Given **D<sub>surface</sub> = 6 mSv/h = 6000 μSv/h**:

$$6000 \, \mu\text{Sv/h} > 2000 \, \mu\text{Sv/h} = 2 \, \text{mSv/h}$$

This **EXCEEDS** the maximum allowable surface dose rate for any package!

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The package **DOES NOT COMPLY** with IAEA SSR-6. A regulatory limit has been breached. The package design must be modified (more shielding) before it can be shipped. *This is a frequent KTU trick question — the answer is not "III-YELLOW" but "non-compliant."*

For a teaching example, let us *hypothetically* assume the surface dose rate was **1.2 mSv/h = 1200 μSv/h**. Then:
- 5 < 1200 ≤ 2000 → **Label = III-YELLOW** ✓

---

### Part (c): Inverse Square Calculation

For a point source, dose rate falls off with the inverse square of distance:

$$D(r_2) = D(r_1) \times \left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)^2$$

Here: D(r₁) = 0.4 mSv/h at r₁ = 1 m, r₂ = 5 m.

$$D(5\,\text{m}) = 0.4 \times \left(\frac{1}{5}\right)^2 = 0.4 \times \frac{1}{25} = 0.016 \text{ mSv/h}$$

Converting to μSv/h:

$$D(5\,\text{m}) = 0.016 \times 1000 = 16 \, \mu\text{Sv/h}$$

$$\boxed{D(5\,\text{m}) = 16 \, \mu\text{Sv/h}}$$

---

### Part (d): Activity after Decay

The radioactive decay equation:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

The decay constant for Co-60:

$$\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{0.693}{5.27 \text{ years}} = 0.1315 \, \text{year}^{-1}$$

Plugging in A₀ = 100 TBq, t = 3 years:

$$A(3) = 100 \cdot e^{-0.1315 \times 3} = 100 \cdot e^{-0.3945}$$

$$e^{-0.3945} \approx 0.6742$$

$$\boxed{A(3\,\text{years}) = 67.42 \, \text{TBq}}$$

Alternatively, using the half-life formula directly:

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{t/t_{1/2}} = 100 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{3/5.27} = 100 \cdot (0.5)^{0.5693} \approx 67.42 \, \text{TBq}$$

---

## 3.2 Problem 2 — Q-System and Type Selection

### Problem Statement
A medical shipment contains **2.5 TBq of I-131** (non-special form). For I-131, the regulatory value **A₂ = 0.5 TBq**. Determine the Q-value and classify the package.

**Solution:**

By definition:

$$Q = \frac{A}{A_2} = \frac{2.5 \, \text{TBq}}{0.5 \, \text{TBq}} = 5.0$$

Since **Q = 5 > 1**, the consignment **CANNOT** be shipped in a Type A package.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A **Type B(U)** package is required. The design must satisfy the 9-m drop, 800 °C fire, and 15-m immersion test sequence.

---

## 3.3 Problem 3 — Criticality Safety Index (CSI) for a Fissile Array

### Problem Statement
Calculations show that an infinite array of **n = 20** identical packages containing enriched UO₂ pellets would just reach criticality. What is the CSI?

**Solution:**

By the IAEA definition:

$$\text{CSI} = \frac{50}{S}$$

where *S* is the number of packages that, in an array, would reach criticality. Here S = 20:

$$\text{CSI} = \frac{50}{20} = 2.5$$

$$\boxed{\text{CSI} = 2.5}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a transport vehicle, the **sum of all CSIs** in the cargo must not exceed **50**, and individual CSIs are reported next to the fissile label.

---

## 3.4 Problem 4 — Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Type A | Type B(U) | Type B(M) |
|-----------|--------|-----------|-----------|
| Activity limit | ≤ A₁ or A₂ | > A₂ | > A₂ |
| Approval needed | Not normally | Unilateral (origin country) | Multilateral |
| Drop test | 1.2 m free drop | 9 m onto unyielding surface | 9 m |
| Fire test | None | 800 °C, 30 min | 800 °C, 30 min |
| Immersion | None | 15 m, 8 h | 200 m, 1 h (if marine) |
| Ventilation | None | Allowed | May require continuous venting |
| Used for | Medical isotopes, low-activity industrial sources | Spent fuel, high-activity sources | PuO₂, MOX, certain spent-fuel casks |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Package Classification Flowchart

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision logic** a consignor follows to classify a radioactive shipment under IAEA SSR-6.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Radionuclide and Activity] --> B{Is Activity less than Excepted Limit?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Excepted Package]
    B -- No --> D{Fissile Material?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Type B U with CSI Label]
    D -- No --> F{Activity less than A2?}
    F -- Yes --> G{Special Form?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Type A Package]
    G -- No --> I[Type A Package]
    F -- No --> J{Air Transport with High Activity?}
    J -- Yes --> K[Type C Package]
    J -- No --> L[Type B U or Type B M Package]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style L fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Defence-in-Depth Architecture of Transport Safety

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1: Robust Package Design] --> L2[Layer 2: Quality Assurance Programme]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3: Regulatory Approval and Certification]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4: Operational Controls during Transport]
    L4 --> L5[Layer 5: Emergency Response Preparedness]

    L1 --> S1[Survives 9 m drop, 800 C fire, 15 m immersion]
    L2 --> S2[NQA-1 or ISO 9001 QA per IAEA TS-G-1.2]
    L3 --> S3[Competent Authority Certificate of Approval]
    L4 --> S4[TI limits, CSI limits, dose rate limits, route restrictions]
    L5 --> S5[Emergency plans, trained drivers, communication]

    style L1 fill:#FF6347,color:#fff
    style L2 fill:#FF8C00,color:#fff
    style L3 fill:#FFD700,color:#000
    style L4 fill:#90EE90,color:#000
    style L5 fill:#87CEEB,color:#000
    style S1 fill:#FFE4E1,color:#000
    style S2 fill:#FFE4E1,color:#000
    style S3 fill:#FFE4E1,color:#000
    style S4 fill:#FFE4E1,color:#000
    style S5 fill:#FFE4E1,color:#000
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Trefoil Label Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R0[Measure Surface Dose Rate D_s] --> R1{D_s less than or equal 5 microSv per h?}
    R1 -- Yes --> W[Label I WHITE TI equals 0]
    R1 -- No --> R2{D_s less than or equal 500 microSv per h?}
    R2 -- Yes --> Y2[Label II YELLOW]
    R2 -- No --> R3{D_s less than or equal 2000 microSv per h?}
    R3 -- Yes --> Y3[Label III YELLOW]
    R3 -- No --> NV[NON COMPLIANT Package design must be modified]

    style W fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Y2 fill:#FFFF99,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style Y3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style NV fill:#FF0000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define Transport Index (TI) and Criticality Safety Index (CSI) for radioactive material transport. State the IAEA dose-rate limits for the three label categories.**

**Model Answer:**

**Transport Index (TI):** The TI of a package is the maximum radiation dose rate, expressed in **mrem/h**, measured at **1 metre** from the outer surface of the package. In SI units, TI is the dose rate at 1 m in mrem/h, rounded up to one decimal place.

**Criticality Safety Index (CSI):** The CSI is a dimensionless number assigned to a package containing fissile material. It is defined as **CSI = 50 / S**, where **S** is the number of identical packages which, in an array, would be required to maintain a critical configuration. CSI is used in conjunction with the fissile trefoil label.

**Dose-rate limits (surface):**
- I-WHITE: ≤ 5 μSv/h
- II-YELLOW: 5–500 μSv/h
- III-YELLOW: 500–2000 μSv/h (max 2 mSv/h)

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Distinguish between Type A and Type B(U) packages. Mention any three tests a Type B(U) package must survive.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Type A | Type B(U) |
|---------|--------|-----------|
| Activity limit | ≤ A₁ (special) or ≤ A₂ (normal) | > A₂ |
| Approval | Not required (in some countries, notification only) | Required — *Unilateral* Competent Authority approval |
| Drop test | 1.2 m free drop | 9 m onto unyielding surface |
| Fire test | None | 800 °C for 30 minutes |
| Use case | Medical and industrial isotopes | Spent fuel, large Co-60 sources |

**Three tests a Type B(U) package must survive:**
1. **Free drop test** from 9 m onto an unyielding target.
2. **Thermal (fire) test** at 800 °C for 30 minutes.
3. **Water immersion test** at 15 m depth for 8 hours.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks — Choose Any ONE)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **Defence-in-Depth philosophy** of radioactive material transport as per IAEA SSR-6. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Type B(U) package transporting 50 TBq of Ir-192 has a measured dose rate of **0.15 mSv/h at 1 m** and **1.5 mSv/h at the surface**. Determine:
   - (i) The Transport Index (TI) of the package.
   - (ii) The label category to be displayed.
   - (iii) The dose rate received by a worker standing **8 m** from the package (assume point source).
   - (iv) Will the package comply if the regulatory surface limit is 2 mSv/h? **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Defence-in-Depth

Defence-in-depth (DiD) ensures that no single failure leads to radiological consequences. For transport, IAEA defines **five independent layers**:

| Layer | Function | Example |
|-------|----------|---------|
| 1. **Robust Package Design** | Provides passive containment and shielding under all conditions | Double-walled steel cask with lead shielding |
| 2. **Quality Assurance Programme** | Ensures package and operations meet design specs throughout life | NQA-1 / ISO 9001 QA per IAEA TS-G-1.2 |
| 3. **Regulatory Approvals** | Independent competent authority certifies the package | AERB design approval certificate |
| 4. **Operational Controls** | Apply dose limits, TI/CSI limits, route restrictions, segregation | Driver training, vehicle placards, mode-specific rules |
| 5. **Emergency Response** | Plans and resources to handle accidents | Emergency procedures, communication, recovery |

The student should draw a block diagram similar to **Section 4.2** of these notes.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**(i) Transport Index (TI):**
Converting 0.15 mSv/h to mrem/h:
$$D_{1m} = 0.15 \times 100 = 15 \, \text{mrem/h}$$

$$\boxed{\text{TI} = 15}$$

**(ii) Label Category:**
Surface dose rate = 1.5 mSv/h = 1500 μSv/h.
Since 5 < 1500 ≤ 2000 μSv/h:

$$\boxed{\text{Label = III-YELLOW}}$$

**(iii) Dose rate at 8 m:**
$$D(8\,\text{m}) = 0.15 \times \left(\frac{1}{8}\right)^2 = \frac{0.15}{64} = 2.34 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{mSv/h}$$

$$\boxed{D(8\,\text{m}) = 2.34 \, \mu\text{Sv/h}}$$

**(iv) Compliance:**
Surface dose rate = 1.5 mSv/h ≤ 2 mSv/h regulatory limit.

$$\boxed{\text{Yes — the package COMPLIES.}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper]`

**(a)** Explain the **Q-system** for classifying radioactive consignments. A medical consignment carries **3 TBq of Mo-99** in normal form. For Mo-99, **A₂ = 0.29 TBq**. Determine the Q-value and recommend the package type. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **IAEA package classification** for radioactive material with reference to Excepted, Industrial (IP-1/IP-2/IP-3), Type A, Type B(U), Type B(M), and Type C packages. State any three engineered design tests required for a Type B(U) package. **(7 Marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Q-System

The **Q-system** quantifies the radioactive hazard of a consignment relative to the **A₂** limit (maximum activity permitted in a Type A package, in normal form):

$$Q = \frac{\text{Activity of Radionuclide (TBq)}}{A_2 \text{ for that Radionuclide (TBq)}}$$

For Mo-99:

$$Q = \frac{3.0 \, \text{TBq}}{0.29 \, \text{TBq}} = 10.34$$

Since **Q = 10.34 >> 1**, the consignment **CANNOT** be shipped as a Type A package.

**Recommendation:** A **Type B(U)** package with AERB (or equivalent Competent Authority) design approval is mandatory. The package must satisfy the 9-m free drop, 800 °C fire, and 15-m immersion test sequence.

---

#### Part (b) — Package Classification

**Excepted Package:** Used for very low activity materials such as smoke detectors, depleted uranium counterweights, and small tritium markers. Activity limit is typically 10⁻³ × A₂ or 10⁻⁶ × A₁. Minimal labelling.

**Industrial Packages (IP-1, IP-2, IP-3):** Used for Low Specific Activity (LSA-I, II, III) materials and Surface Contaminated Objects (SCO). IP-1 for ores; IP-2 for LSA-II/III; IP-3 for hardened LSA and SCO. Tests are minor (drop, stacking).

**Type A Package:** Used when A ≤ A₁ (special form) or A ≤ A₂ (normal form). Must survive 1.2 m free drop, 1 m corner drop, compression (5× mass × g), and penetration tests. Common for medical isotopes like Tc-99m generators.

**Type B(U) Package:** Used for consignments above A₂. Requires *Unilateral* approval by the competent authority of the country of origin. Must survive the cumulative accident test sequence: 9 m drop → 1 m puncture → 800 °C, 30-min fire → 15 m, 8-h immersion. Examples: spent fuel casks, large Co-60 teletherapy sources.

**Type B(M) Package:** Used when *intermittent venting* or *temperature control* is needed during transport. Requires *Multilateral* approval (origin + transit + destination). The "(M)" stands for "Multilateral."

**Type C Package:** Specifically designed for high-activity consignments by **air transport**. Must survive a more severe accident test sequence including a 40 m free drop onto an unyielding target (simulating an aircraft crash) and a 200 m, 1-h water immersion.

**Three Engineered Design Tests for Type B(U):**
1. **9-m free drop** onto an unyielding surface (impact).
2. **800 °C thermal (fire) test** for 30 minutes (jet-fuel fire).
3. **1-m puncture drop** onto a 15 cm diameter steel bar.
4. **15-m water immersion** for 8 hours.

*(Any three of the above are correct.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Wrong TI Units:** A common mistake is to give TI in mSv/h instead of converting to mrem/h. Always state and use **mrem/h**.
> 2. **Forgetting Criticality:** Fissile material questions must explicitly mention **k-effective ≤ 0.95** or **subcriticality under normal AND accident conditions**.
> 3. **Inverse Square Law Application:** Many students use (r₂/r₁)² instead of (r₁/r₂)². Always remember: dose rate **DECREASES** as you go further, so the multiplier is < 1.
> 4. **Mixing A₁ and A₂:** A₁ is for **special form**, A₂ is for **normal form**. Mo-99 powder is normal form — use A₂.
> 5. **Missing Test Sequence:** Type B(U) tests are CUMULATIVE — drop, then puncture, then fire, in that order.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Transport Index (TI):** Maximum dose rate at **1 m** in **mrem/h** — the single most quoted number for any radioactive shipment.
- [x] **Three Principles:** **Containment, Shielding, Criticality Control** — memorise this triplet for every transport question.
- [x] **Package Family Tree:** **Excepted → IP-1 → IP-2 → IP-3 → Type A → Type B(U) → Type B(M) → Type C** — increasing activity → increasing test severity.
- [x] **Q-System Threshold:** **Q ≤ 1** ⇒ Type A package; **Q > 1** ⇒ Type B or higher.
- [x] **Label Categories (Trefoil):** I-WHITE (≤ 5 μSv/h), II-YELLOW (≤ 500 μSv/h), III-YELLOW (≤ 2000 μSv/h = 2 mSv/h).
- [x] **Type B(U) Tests (Cumulative Sequence):** **9-m Drop → 1-m Puncture → 800 °C / 30-min Fire → 15-m / 8-h Immersion.**
- [x] **CSI Definition:** CSI = **50 / S**, where *S* = number of packages that would form a critical array.
- [x] **Decay Formula:** **A(t) = A₀ · e<sup>-λt</sup>** with **λ = ln 2 / t₁/₂** = **0.693 / t₁/₂**.
- [x] **Inverse Square Law:** **D(r₂) = D(r₁) · (r₁/r₂)²** — the workhorse formula for transport problems.
- [x] **Indian Regulator:** In India, **AERB** enforces the regulations, derived from **IAEA SSR-6**.
- [x] **Defence-in-Depth Layers:** Robust design → QA programme → Regulatory approval → Operational controls → Emergency response.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Storage of Spent Fuel
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Storage of Spent Fuel

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Storage of Spent Fuel

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

**Spent Fuel Storage** refers to the safe, controlled, and temporary holding of irradiated nuclear fuel assemblies after their removal from an operating nuclear reactor, prior to their final disposition through either reprocessing or permanent geological disposal. Spent fuel is classified as **High-Level Waste (HLW)** because it contains highly radioactive fission products, transuranic actinides (plutonium, americium, neptunium, curium), and activated structural materials, all of which emit intense penetrating gamma and neutron radiation while simultaneously generating significant **decay heat**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEMET412 - Module 2):**
> Spent fuel management is the critical bridge between the reactor core and the back-end of the nuclear fuel cycle. The KTU 2024 scheme expects students to understand the **two-stage storage strategy** — immediate **wet storage** in reactor pools, followed by transition to **dry storage** as decay heat decreases.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have just finished using a charcoal briquette in a barbeque grill. The briquette is too hot to touch, still glowing, and you cannot simply throw it in the trash. You must place it in a metal ash bucket, leave the lid slightly open for ventilation, and wait many hours until it cools. Even after cooling, the ashes still contain residual chemical energy and trace contaminants that require careful disposal.

**Spent nuclear fuel is the nuclear equivalent of this hot briquette**, but on a vastly more dangerous scale:

| Property | Charcoal Briquette | Spent Nuclear Fuel |
|---|---|---|
| Time to cool | Hours | Years to Decades |
| Hazard type | Chemical / Thermal | Radioactive / Thermal |
| Storage container | Metal ash bucket | Deep pool / Shielded cask |
| Final disposal | Trash bag | Geological repository |

The fuel must be **cooled**, **shielded**, and **contained** until its radiotoxicity drops to safe levels comparable with the original uranium ore from which it was mined. This cooling period typically ranges from **5 to 10 years** for wet storage, with dry storage extending the timeline to **40–100+ years**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants Used in Spent Fuel Storage:**
> - Decay heat fraction after 1 year: ≈ **0.5–1.0%** of rated thermal power
> - Cooling pool water temperature: **40 °C (max 50 °C)**
> - Minimum pool depth for shielding: **≈ 6 meters** of borated water
> - Criticality limit for soluble boron: **≥ 2000 ppm** in storage pools

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2. The "Why" and "How" of Spent Fuel Storage

### 2.1 Why Must Spent Fuel Be Stored?

When a fuel assembly is discharged from the reactor, it carries three principal hazards:

1. **Decay Heat Generation** — Even after fission stops, radioactive decay of fission products continues. The fuel must be actively or passively cooled.
2. **Intense Gamma and Neutron Radiation** — Requires massive biological shielding (water, concrete, lead, steel).
3. **Criticality Risk** — Residual fissile material (U-235, Pu-239) could potentially achieve criticality if geometrically reconfigured under optimal moderation conditions.
4. **Radiotoxic Inventory** — Long-lived isotopes (Cs-137, Sr-90, Pu-239) remain hazardous for hundreds to thousands of years.

### 2.2 The Two-Stage Storage Strategy

**Stage 1 — At-Reactor (AR) Wet Storage:**

- Spent fuel is moved from the reactor vessel into the **Spent Fuel Pool (SFP)**.
- The pool is a large, deep, stainless-steel-lined concrete basin filled with **borated demineralized water**.
- Water serves three simultaneous functions:
  - **Coolant** — Removes decay heat through natural or forced convection.
  - **Shield** — Attenuates gamma and neutron radiation (water density ≈ 1000 kg/m³).
  - **Neutron Absorber** — Boron (as boric acid, H₃BO₃) prevents criticality.

**Stage 2 — Away-From-Reactor (AFR) Dry Storage:**

- After 5–10 years of wet storage, decay heat drops sufficiently (typically below **~10–20 kW per assembly**) to allow transfer to **dry storage**.
- Dry storage uses air, inert gas (helium), or nitrogen as the cooling medium inside sealed containers.
- Eliminates risk of water leakage, pool boiling, or loss-of-coolant events.

### 2.3 Types of Dry Storage Systems

| System Type | Description | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Concrete Vault** | Fuel in shielded tube within massive concrete structure | Centralized AFR facilities |
| **Horizontal Storage Module** | Sealed steel canister inside concrete overpack | Independent spent fuel storage installations (ISFSI) |
| **Dry Storage Cask (DSC)** | Double-walled steel cylinder with welded closure, surrounded by steel/concrete shielding | On-site pad storage |
| **Silo** | Small sealed container, often below-grade in concrete | BWR fuel storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **Cooling Time vs. Decay Heat (Typical PWR Fuel, 4.5% enrichment, 45 GWd/tHM burnup):**
> - 1 year after discharge: ≈ **10 kW per assembly**
> - 5 years after discharge: ≈ **2.5 kW per assembly**
> - 10 years after discharge: ≈ **1.5 kW per assembly**
> - 40 years after discharge: ≈ **0.4 kW per assembly**

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Spent Fuel Storage

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Decay heat (Way-Wigner approximation) | $P(t) = P_0 \cdot t^{-n}$ | Watts | $n \approx 0.2$ for short cooling, $0.6$–$1.2$ long term |
| Pool water shielding depth | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$ | — | $\mu$ = linear attenuation coefficient of water |
| Boron concentration (criticality) | $k_{eff} \le 0.95$ (with bias) | — | Requires $\ge 2000$ ppm boron |
| Heat removal (natural convection in pool) | $q = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | W | $h \approx 500$–$1000$ W/m²K for water |
| Concrete shield thickness (gamma) | $x = \frac{\ln(I_0 / I)}{\mu_{concrete}}$ | m | $\mu_{concrete} \approx 0.05$–$0.10$ /cm for Co-60 |
| Casks per pad (thermal limit) | $\sum_{i} Q_i \le Q_{pad,max}$ | W | $Q_{pad,max}$ set by passive air cooling |
| Storage pool capacity (assemblies) | $N = \frac{V_{pool}}{V_{assembly} \cdot P_{rack}}$ | — | $P_{rack}$ = packing fraction, typically 2:1 or 3:1 |
| Radiotoxicity time reduction | $TR = \frac{t_{HLW}}{t_{ore}}$ | years | Reaches unity only after **≈ 100,000 years** for Pu |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility in Real-World KTU Context:**
> Spent fuel storage design is governed by three cardinal principles: **(1) Subcriticality** (geometry + neutron absorbers), **(2) Heat removal** (passive > active for safety), and **(3) Containment** (multi-barrier philosophy). Every KTU problem on this topic reduces to optimizing one of these three.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3. Worked Derivations and Numerical Examples

### 3.1 Derivation: Way-Wigner Decay Heat Formula

The decay power of a freshly discharged fuel assembly can be approximated by the empirical **Way-Wigner relation**, derived from the beta-decay spectrum of fission products. The core derivation proceeds as follows:

After nuclear fission, a large number of radioactive fission products are created. Most of these decay via **beta emission** with a wide distribution of half-lives. The aggregate decay power at time $t$ after shutdown can be derived by integrating the decay of all fission products:

$$P(t) = \sum_{i} \lambda_i \cdot N_i(t) \cdot E_{avg,i}$$

where $\lambda_i$ is the decay constant, $N_i(t)$ is the number of atoms of isotope $i$ at time $t$, and $E_{avg,i}$ is the average beta energy. After applying a continuous distribution approximation, the summation transforms into an integral, yielding the **Way-Wigner formula**:

$$\boxed{P(t) = P_0 \cdot t^{-n}}$$

**Constants and bounds:**
- $P_0$ = decay power at reference time (typically 1 second or 1 hour after shutdown)
- For short cooling times ($t$ between 10 s and 100 days): $n \approx 0.2$
- For long cooling times ($t$ between 100 days and 30 years): $n \approx 0.6$ to $1.2$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Decay Heat Calculation

> **Problem:** A PWR fuel assembly operates at a thermal power of $P_{th} = 18$ MW for 3 years continuously. Calculate the decay heat produced **1 year** after shutdown using the Way-Wigner approximation.

**Step 1 — Identify the operating time:**

The operating time $t_{op}$ must be expressed in consistent units. Operating continuously for 3 years:

$$t_{op} = 3 \text{ years} \times 365.25 \text{ days/year} \times 24 \text{ h/day} \times 3600 \text{ s/h} \approx 9.46 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 — Determine the reference power $P_0$ at shutdown ($t = 0$):**

At the moment of shutdown, the entire operational thermal power is essentially converted to "instantaneous" decay power. Thus:

$$P_0 = P_{th} = 18 \text{ MW} = 1.8 \times 10^7 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3 — Convert cooling time $t = 1$ year into seconds:**

$$t = 1 \text{ year} = 3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Way-Wigner formula with $n = 0.2$ (short-term decay regime):**

$$P(t) = P_0 \cdot t^{-n}$$

$$P(1 \text{ yr}) = 1.8 \times 10^7 \cdot (3.156 \times 10^7)^{-0.2}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the exponent:**

$$(3.156 \times 10^7)^{-0.2} = e^{-0.2 \cdot \ln(3.156 \times 10^7)} = e^{-0.2 \cdot 17.265} = e^{-3.453}$$

$$e^{-3.453} \approx 0.0317$$

**Step 6 — Compute the final decay heat:**

$$P(1 \text{ yr}) = 1.8 \times 10^7 \times 0.0317 \approx 5.7 \times 10^5 \text{ W} = 570 \text{ kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** One year after shutdown, a single PWR assembly still produces roughly **570 kW** of decay heat — equivalent to the heat output of **570 household space heaters**. This is why active pool cooling is mandatory.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Shielding Depth Calculation

> **Problem:** A storage pool is filled with water. What is the minimum water depth required to reduce the gamma dose rate from Co-60 by a factor of **1000**, given the linear attenuation coefficient of water for Co-60 gamma rays is $\mu = 0.0642$ /cm?

**Step 1 — Apply the Beer-Lambert exponential attenuation law:**

$$I(x) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$$

We require $I_0 / I = 1000$, so:

$$\frac{I_0}{I(x)} = e^{\mu x} = 1000$$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm of both sides:**

$$\mu x = \ln(1000) = 3 \ln(10) \approx 3 \times 2.3026 = 6.9078$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $x$:**

$$x = \frac{6.9078}{0.0642} \approx 107.6 \text{ cm} \approx 1.08 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the KTU design safety factor of 2.5:**

In practice, spent fuel pools are designed with a safety factor to account for fuel reshuffling, fuel with higher burnup, and neutron shielding (water is also good for neutron moderation, but additional depth is needed). The KTU design minimum is:

$$x_{design} = 2.5 \times 1.08 = 2.7 \text{ m}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** Real spent fuel pools are **6 to 12 meters deep** to provide not only gamma shielding but also **neutron shielding** (from spontaneous fission and alpha-n reactions in actinides) and to allow fuel handling operations with cranes and tools from above.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Criticality Safety — Boron Concentration

> **Problem:** A spent fuel storage rack is designed with a $4 \times 4$ array of fuel assemblies, each with $k_\infty = 1.30$ in isolation. Calculate the minimum boron concentration (in ppm) required to maintain $k_{eff} \le 0.95$, given the neutron absorption cross-section of boron at thermal energies is $\Sigma_a = 1.0$ /cm at 1000 ppm.

**Step 1 — Apply the four-factor formula with poisoning correction:**

The infinite multiplication factor with soluble poison is:

$$k_\infty^{poisoned} = \frac{k_\infty}{1 + \Sigma_a^{B} / \Sigma_a^{fuel}}$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to find the required boron absorption cross-section:**

We need $k_{eff} \le 0.95$. With typical rack geometry factors (≈ 0.85), we require $k_\infty^{poisoned} \le 0.95 / 0.85 = 1.118$.

$$1.118 = \frac{1.30}{1 + \Sigma_a^{B} / \Sigma_a^{fuel}}$$

$$1 + \frac{\Sigma_a^{B}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = \frac{1.30}{1.118} = 1.163$$

$$\frac{\Sigma_a^{B}}{\Sigma_a^{fuel}} = 0.163$$

**Step 3 — Determine the boron concentration:**

Given that $\Sigma_a^{B} = 1.0$ /cm at 1000 ppm, and we need $\Sigma_a^{B} / \Sigma_a^{fuel} = 0.163$:

$$\Sigma_a^{B} = 0.163 \times \Sigma_a^{fuel}$$

Assuming the design point is $\Sigma_a^{fuel} \approx 0.5$ /cm:

$$\Sigma_a^{B} = 0.163 \times 0.5 = 0.0815 \text{ /cm}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to ppm:**

$$C_B = 1000 \text{ ppm} \times \frac{0.0815}{1.0} = 815 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the KTU design safety margin of 2.5x:**

$$C_B^{design} = 2.5 \times 815 = 2038 \text{ ppm} \approx 2000 \text{ ppm}$$

This confirms the standard KTU/IPPAS guideline of **≥ 2000 ppm** boron in storage pools.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Spent Fuel Storage Selection

| Cooling Time | Decay Heat | Recommended Storage | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0–5 years | > 5 kW/asm | **Wet storage (pool)** | Water cooling is most efficient for high heat load |
| 5–10 years | 1–5 kW/asm | **Wet → Dry transition** | Heat dropped to dry-storage compatible levels |
| 10–40 years | 0.5–1 kW/asm | **Dry cask storage** | Passive air cooling, no active systems required |
| > 40 years | < 0.5 kW/asm | **Vault / Geological prep** | Heat load low enough for deep geological disposal |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4. Mermaid Diagrams for Spent Fuel Storage Architecture

### 4.1 Spent Fuel Storage Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reactor Core Discharge] --> B[Spent Fuel Pool SFP]
    B --> C{Cooling Time Exceeded 5 Years?}
    C -->|No| D[Continue Wet Storage in Pool]
    D --> C
    C -->|Yes| E{Decay Heat Below 20 kW per Assembly?}
    E -->|No| D
    E -->|Yes| F[Transfer to Dry Storage Cask]
    F --> G{Storage Site Type?}
    G -->|On-Site| H[ISFSI Independent Spent Fuel Storage Installation]
    G -->|Centralized| I[AFR Away From Reactor Facility]
    H --> J{Eventually Reprocessed or Disposed?}
    I --> J
    J -->|Reprocess| K[Chemical Separation Plant]
    J -->|Direct Disposal| L[Deep Geological Repository]
    K --> M[Recycled U and Pu for MOX Fuel]
    L --> N[Permanent Isolation for 100000 Years]
```

### 4.2 Wet Storage Pool Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PoolBasin[Spent Fuel Pool Concrete Basin]
        WaterBody[Borated Demineralized Water]
        FuelRacks[High Density Fuel Storage Racks]
        FuelAssembly[Spent Fuel Assemblies in Racks]
    end
    subgraph CoolingLoop[Active Cooling System]
        HX[Heat Exchanger]
        Pumps[Coolant Pumps]
        Chiller[Chiller Unit]
    end
    subgraph SafetySystems[Safety and Monitoring]
        BoronMon[Boron Concentration Monitor]
        LevelMon[Water Level Monitor]
        TempMon[Temperature Monitor]
        RadMon[Radiation Monitor]
    end
    FuelAssembly -->|Decay Heat| WaterBody
    WaterBody -->|Hot Leg| HX
    HX -->|Cold Leg| WaterBody
    Pumps --> HX
    Chiller --> HX
    BoronMon -.-> WaterBody
    LevelMon -.-> WaterBody
    TempMon -.-> WaterBody
    RadMon -.-> PoolBasin
```

### 4.3 Dry Storage Cask Cross Section Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CaskLayer[Vertical Dry Storage Cask Cross Section]
        A1[Outer Steel Overpack]
        A2[Concrete Biological Shield]
        A3[Inner Steel Liner]
        A4[Helium Backfill Inert Gas]
        A5[Spent Fuel Basket with Neutron Absorber]
        A6[Spent Fuel Assemblies]
        A7[Ventilation Channels for Passive Air Cooling]
        A8[Sealed Welded Closure Lid]
        A9[Seismic Anchoring Basepad]
    end
    A6 --> A5
    A5 --> A4
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> A1
    A1 --> A7
    A8 --> A1
    A9 --> A1
```

### 4.4 Spent Fuel Storage Strategy Time Line

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0[Reactor Discharge] -->|0 to 5 years| T1[Stage 1 Wet Storage Pool]
    T1 -->|5 to 10 years| T2[Stage 2 Dry Storage Cask]
    T2 -->|40 to 100 years| T3[Stage 3 Extended Dry Storage]
    T3 -->|100 to 1000 years| T4[Stage 4 Reprocessing or Geological Disposal]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:**
> - **Wet storage** (T0 → T1) handles the high-heat phase when gamma and neutron flux are at their peak.
> - **Dry cask storage** (T1 → T2 → T3) provides passive cooling for the bulk of the storage time, with **no moving parts, no water, and no active cooling**.
> - **Final disposition** (T3 → T4) is the political and engineering frontier: only Finland's Onkalo repository (commissioned ~2025) is currently licensed for permanent disposal.

---
