# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5. KTU-Style Practice Questions

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

> **Define "Spent Fuel" and explain why it cannot be disposed of immediately after discharge from the reactor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Spent fuel is nuclear fuel that has been irradiated in a reactor and removed because it can no longer sustain an efficient chain reaction. It contains unburned U-235, newly bred Pu-239, and a complex mixture of radioactive fission products and activation products.

**Reasons it cannot be immediately disposed of (2 marks):**

1. **Decay heat** — Even after fission stops, the fuel continues to generate significant thermal power (~0.5–1% of rated power per assembly immediately after discharge, decreasing with time). This heat must be actively removed to prevent fuel melting.

2. **Radiotoxicity and penetrating radiation** — The gamma and neutron emission from short-lived fission products (e.g., I-131, Cs-137) is extremely intense. Massive shielding (typically 6+ meters of water) is required for personnel protection.

3. **Criticality risk** — Residual fissile content must remain subcritical under all credible storage configurations.

4. **Long-term isolation** — Long-lived actinides (Pu-239 half-life: 24,100 years) require engineered barriers and geological time scales for safe isolation.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

> **Differentiate between "Wet Storage" and "Dry Storage" of spent nuclear fuel. Give one example of each system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Wet Storage | Dry Storage |
|---|---|---|
| Cooling medium | Borated demineralized water | Air, helium, or nitrogen |
| Typical duration | 5–10 years | 40–100+ years |
| Heat removal | Active (pumps + heat exchangers) | Passive (natural convection) |
| Shielding | Water (≥ 6 m depth) | Concrete + steel overpack |
| Risk of leakage | Pool leak / loss of coolant | Minimal (sealed cask) |
| Example | Spent Fuel Pool (SFP) | Dry Storage Cask (DSC) / Vertical Concrete Vault |
| Used for | High decay heat fuel | Cooled, low-heat-load fuel |

> Examiner allocates **1.5 marks** for the differentiation table and **1.5 marks** for the examples.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe the layout and major components of a typical **At-Reactor Spent Fuel Pool (SFP)**. Explain how the three safety functions — cooling, shielding, and criticality control — are simultaneously achieved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A PWR fuel assembly operates at 3000 MW thermal for one year. Using the Way-Wigner approximation, calculate the decay heat after **5 years** of cooling. Comment on the suitability of dry storage at this point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The spent fuel pool is a deep, rectangular, **stainless-steel-lined reinforced concrete basin** with a typical depth of **12 m** and a water inventory of several hundred to a few thousand cubic meters.

**Major Components:**

1. **Pool structure** — Reinforced concrete walls and floor, lined with welded stainless steel sheets to prevent leakage and corrosion.
2. **Storage racks** — High-density racks made of stainless steel with **boron-containing neutron absorber plates** (e.g., Boral, Boraflex) inserted between cells.
3. **Cooling system** — Two or more redundant cooling trains, each consisting of a pump, heat exchanger, and chiller. Hot water is drawn from the pool, cooled, and returned.
4. **Water purification system** — Demineralizers, filters, and deaerators to maintain water clarity, low conductivity, and removal of radioactive ions.
5. **Monitoring systems** — Continuous level, temperature, boron concentration, and radiation monitors.
6. **Fuel handling equipment** — Overhead crane, fuel transfer mechanisms, and underwater cameras.

**Three Safety Functions (1 mark each):**

- **Cooling:** The pool water absorbs the decay heat through natural convection (and forced flow from pumps). The water-to-air heat exchanger rejects this heat to the environment. The high heat capacity of water (4.18 kJ/kg·K) makes it an ideal coolant.
- **Shielding:** A water depth of **≥ 6 m** attenuates gamma radiation to background levels and thermalizes fast neutrons, which are then absorbed by boron.
- **Criticality Control:** Two independent mechanisms: (i) **Fixed neutron absorbers** (Boral plates) in the racks maintain $k_{eff} \le 0.95$ even if racks are fully flooded with unborated water, and (ii) **Soluble boron** (≥ 2000 ppm boric acid) in the pool water provides an additional margin.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Identify parameters. $P_0 = 3000$ MW = $3.0 \times 10^9$ W.

**Step 2:** Convert cooling time to seconds. $t = 5$ years = $1.578 \times 10^8$ s.

**Step 3:** Apply the Way-Wigner formula. For $t$ in the range 100 days to 30 years, the appropriate exponent is $n = 0.6$ (intermediate cooling regime):

$$P(5 \text{ yr}) = P_0 \cdot t^{-0.6}$$

$$P(5 \text{ yr}) = 3.0 \times 10^9 \times (1.578 \times 10^8)^{-0.6}$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate the exponent:

$$(1.578 \times 10^8)^{-0.6} = e^{-0.6 \times \ln(1.578 \times 10^8)} = e^{-0.6 \times 18.879} = e^{-11.327}$$

$$e^{-11.327} \approx 1.21 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Step 5:** Compute the decay heat:

$$P(5 \text{ yr}) = 3.0 \times 10^9 \times 1.21 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.63 \times 10^4 \text{ W} \approx 36.3 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6:** Per-assembly heat (assuming, e.g., 193 assemblies in a PWR core):
$$P_{asm} = 36.3 / 193 \approx 0.19 \text{ kW per assembly}$$

**Comment on dry storage suitability (1 mark):** With only **0.19 kW per assembly**, this is well below the typical dry-storage cask limit of **20–40 kW per cask** (holding ~10–20 assemblies). Therefore, **dry storage is highly suitable** at this cooling time, and the fuel can be transferred to dry casks.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper — Alternative]**

**(a)** Explain the **Away-From-Reactor (AFR) dry storage concept**. Compare **vertical concrete vaults**, **horizontal storage modules**, and **dry storage casks**, highlighting their relative advantages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dry storage cask is to be designed to hold 10 PWR spent fuel assemblies, each producing 2 kW of decay heat. The cask is cooled by natural convection of ambient air at 30 °C. The heat exchanger surface area is 20 m² and the average heat transfer coefficient is $h = 25$ W/m²K. Calculate the **maximum allowable surface temperature** of the cask outer wall to ensure adequate heat removal. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**AFR Dry Storage Concept (3 marks):**

The Away-From-Reactor (AFR) concept centralizes spent fuel storage at a few licensed, secure, and well-monitored sites — separate from individual reactor sites. This decouples fuel storage capacity from reactor operations and enables consolidated long-term management while reactor pools are emptied and refueled.

Once spent fuel has cooled for **5–10 years** in AR pools, it is loaded into sealed dry-storage containers, transported (in heavy-haul shielded transport casks) to an AFR facility, and stored passively for **40–100+ years**.

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Feature | Vertical Vault | Horizontal Module | Dry Storage Cask |
|---|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Vertical shielded tube | Horizontal concrete overpack | Vertical / horizontal steel cylinder |
| Shielding | Massive concrete enclosure | Concrete + steel | Steel + concrete overpack |
| Capacity | 10–50 assemblies per vault | 10–30 assemblies per module | 10–24 assemblies per cask |
| Loading method | Top-loading, complex | Side-loading, simpler | Top-loading, modular |
| **Advantage** | Very high capacity, good for centralization | Easier fuel retrieval | Portable, low cost, modular |
| **Disadvantage** | High civil engineering cost | Limited to specific sites | Limited per-cask capacity |

> The **dry storage cask** is the most widely deployed option worldwide due to its modularity and portability.

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1:** Total heat generation in the cask:

$$Q_{total} = 10 \text{ assemblies} \times 2 \text{ kW/asm} = 20 \text{ kW} = 20{,}000 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Newton's law of cooling:

$$Q = h \cdot A \cdot (T_{wall} - T_{air})$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the temperature difference:

$$\Delta T = T_{wall} - T_{air} = \frac{Q}{h \cdot A} = \frac{20{,}000}{25 \times 20} = \frac{20{,}000}{500} = 40 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the wall surface temperature:

$$T_{wall} = T_{air} + \Delta T = 30 + 40 = 70 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5:** Comment (1 mark):

The wall temperature of **70 °C** is well below the design limit for cask surface temperatures (typically 150–200 °C). This indicates the cask has **substantial thermal margin** and can safely reject 20 kW under natural convection. The design is **safe and conservative**, with no need to upgrade cooling capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing dry storage types (2 marks lost):** Students often mix up casks, vaults, and silos. Memorize the table from Section 4(a) above.
> 2. **Wrong Way-Wigner exponent (3 marks lost):** Use $n = 0.2$ for cooling times under 100 days, and $n = 0.6$ for 100 days to 30 years. Mixing these gives a factor-of-10 error in decay heat.
> 3. **Forgetting unit conversion of time (1 mark lost):** Way-Wigner requires time in **seconds**, not years, unless the $P_0$ is calibrated differently.
> 4. **Skipping the engineering comment (1 mark lost):** KTU examiners explicitly reward the **interpretation** of the numerical result. A bare number with no comment is penalized.
> 5. **Not labeling Mermaid diagrams (2 marks lost):** When asked to draw a schematic, use clear block labels (e.g., "Spent Fuel Basket", "Concrete Shield"), not just boxes.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Spent fuel = HLW.** Treat it with the same rigor as fresh fuel handling plus decay heat and radiotoxicity.
- **Three safety pillars of SFP design:** Cooling (water as coolant), Shielding (≥ 6 m water depth), Criticality control (fixed absorbers + ≥ 2000 ppm soluble boron).
- **Wet storage (5–10 years) → Dry storage (40–100+ years) → Disposal (100,000+ years).** This is the canonical timeline; KTU expects you to know all three stages.
- **Way-Wigner formula:** $P(t) = P_0 \cdot t^{-n}$, with $n = 0.2$ (short term) and $n = 0.6$ (long term). Always convert $t$ to **seconds**.
- **One-year-old PWR fuel** still produces ~1% of rated power as decay heat — design must handle this.
- **Beer-Lambert shielding law:** $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-\mu x}$. For Co-60 in water, $\mu \approx 0.0642$ /cm.
- **Dry cask design** is **passive** — no pumps, no water, no operator action required. This is its central safety advantage.
- **Natural convection cooling** of a cask uses Newton's law: $Q = hA\Delta T$. Always check wall temperature remains below design limit.
- **AFR facilities** consolidate storage at a few national sites; **ISFSI** is the on-site equivalent.
- **Criticality safety** uses the rule $k_{eff} \le 0.95$ including all biases and uncertainties (ANSI/ANS-8.17 standard).
- **The Onkalo repository (Finland)** is the only currently operational deep geological repository licensed for spent fuel disposal.
- **Spent fuel remains more radiotoxic than the original uranium ore for ≈ 100,000 years** if not reprocessed. This fact motivates the entire back-end fuel cycle.
- **Boron in storage pools** must be ≥ 2000 ppm; rack geometry must be **subcritical under optimal moderation** (fully flooded, unborated water — a hypothetical accident condition).
- **KTU exam tip:** For 7-mark derivation questions, always show the **exponent evaluation step** explicitly — students lose 2–3 marks by skipping $e^{-0.2 \cdot \ln(...)}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reprocessing - Solvent Extraction: PUREX, UREX, TRUEX, Pyro Processing: Electrolysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Reprocessing - Solvent Extraction: PUREX, UREX, TRUEX, Pyro Processing: Electrolysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Spent Fuel Reprocessing?

**Spent Fuel Reprocessing** is the chemical operation performed on irradiated nuclear fuel discharged from a reactor, aimed at separating the **valuable fissile/fertile isotopes** (such as $^{235}$U, $^{238}$U, and $^{239}$Pu) from the **fission products** and **minor actinides** (Np, Am, Cm). The separation enables the **recycling of nuclear fuel**, recovery of strategic isotopes, and the conditioning of high-level radioactive waste (HLW) into stable, durable forms for geological disposal.

In formal KTU 2024 terminology, reprocessing is defined as the **post-irradiation chemical treatment of nuclear fuel** to recover usable actinides and partition radioactive waste streams, thereby closing the nuclear fuel cycle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** This topic bridges the **back-end of the nuclear fuel cycle**. You must master both **aqueous (PUREX, UREX, TRUEX)** and **non-aqueous (Pyroprocessing/Electrolysis)** routes. KTU examiners frequently frame 14-mark questions asking for a **comparative discussion of aqueous vs. non-aqueous reprocessing**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Coffee Filter" View

Imagine you have a large tumbler containing a mixture of dissolved salt, sugar, sand, and coffee grounds (analogous to spent fuel containing U, Pu, fission products, and cladding remnants). If you pour this mixture through a coffee filter:

- The **filter paper** acts like the **organic solvent** in solvent extraction.
- The **filtrate (clear liquid)** corresponds to the **purified uranium stream**.
- The **residue (grounds)** corresponds to the **fission products / minor actinides**.

In the **PUREX process**, we exploit the fact that **uranyl nitrate (UO$_2^{2+}$) and Pu$^{4+}$** have a strong affinity for an **organic phase (TBP in kerosene)**, while most fission products prefer to stay in the **aqueous nitric acid phase**. By carefully toggling the **oxidation state of plutonium** (Pu$^{4+}$ ↔ Pu$^{3+}$), we can selectively "pump" elements back and forth between the two immiscible liquids — a chemical "switch" that no filter paper can do.

For **pyroprocessing**, think of it like **electro-plating jewelry from a mixed-metal bath**. The spent fuel is dissolved in a **molten salt** (the conductive electrolyte), and by applying precise voltages, you selectively deposit pure uranium (and later plutonium) onto solid cathodes, leaving the bulk of fission products behind.

## 1.3 Why Reprocess? — The Engineering Drivers

1. **Resource Utilization:** Natural uranium contains only ~0.7% $^{235}$U. Spent fuel still contains ~95% $^{238}$U, ~1% $^{235}$U, and ~1% **Pu** (with ~65% fissile isotopes). Reprocessing recovers this energy.
2. **Waste Volume Reduction:** High-level waste volume is reduced by **~75–80%**, lowering long-term radiotoxicity burden.
3. **Decay Heat Management:** Separating short-lived fission products (Cs-137, Sr-90) allows compact storage.
4. **Proliferation Resistance:** Co-extraction and co-denitration steps can be designed to denature Pu (e.g., MOX with reactor-grade Pu).
5. **Actinide Recycling:** Fast breeder reactors (FBRs) require Pu-rich (or $^{233}$U-rich) fuel, which can only be obtained through reprocessing.

## 1.4 Classification of Reprocessing Technologies

| Class | Method | Examples | State of Fuel | Phase Used |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aqueous** | Solvent Extraction | **PUREX, UREX, TRUEX, DIAMEX, SANEX** | Dissolved in HNO$_3$ | Organic + Aqueous |
| **Non-Aqueous** | Molten Salt Electrolysis | **Pyroprocessing, Electrorefining** | Solid or dissolved in molten salt | Molten Salt (e.g., LiCl-KCl, ZnCl$_2$) |
| **Hybrid** | Fluoride Volatility | UF$_6$ distillation | Volatile fluorides | Gas phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Purex, UREX, and TRUEX** are the three aqueous solvent extraction processes prescribed in your KTU Module 2 syllabus. They differ primarily in **which elements are co-extracted** and in the **extractant chemistry**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamentals of Solvent Extraction

Solvent extraction (liquid–liquid extraction) exploits the **differential solubility** of metal complexes between two **immiscible liquid phases**:

- **Aqueous Phase:** Usually nitric acid (HNO$_3$) of controlled molarity, containing dissolved fuel nitrates.
- **Organic Phase:** A non-polar diluent (kerosene / dodecane) carrying the extractant (TBP, CMPO, etc.).

The key thermodynamic parameter is the **Distribution Ratio (D)**:

$$D_M = \frac{[M]_{org}}{[M]_{aq}}$$

where $D_M$ is the distribution ratio of metal $M$. A high $D$ means the element prefers the organic phase.

The **Separation Factor (SF)** between two metals A and B is given by:

$$SF_{A/B} = \frac{D_A}{D_B}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For a single-stage extraction to be efficient, $D \geq 10$ is desirable. For stripping (back-extraction), $D \leq 0.1$ is required. PUREX is designed around $D_{U,Pu} \sim 30$ in extraction, and $D_{U,Pu} \sim 0.05$ in stripping.

## 2.2 The PUREX Process (Plutonium Uranium Redox EXtraction)

PUREX is the **industrial workhorse** of nuclear reprocessing, used worldwide (La Hague-France, Sellafield-UK, Rokkasho-Japan, Mayak-Russia, Tarapur-India). It separates **U and Pu together** from fission products.

### 2.2.1 Chemical Principles

The extractant is **Tributyl Phosphate (TBP)** — an organophosphorus compound, typically **30% TBP in odorless kerosene (OK)**.

The extraction reaction for hexavalent uranium is:

$$UO_2^{2+}_{(aq)} + 2\,NO_3^{-}_{(aq)} + 2\,TBP_{(org)} \rightarrow UO_2(NO_3)_2 \cdot 2TBP_{(org)}$$

Similarly for tetravalent plutonium (Pu$^{4+}$):

$$Pu^{4+}_{(aq)} + 4\,NO_3^{-}_{(aq)} + 2\,TBP_{(org)} \rightarrow Pu(NO_3)_4 \cdot 2TBP_{(org)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why TBP?** TBP is **selective** for UO$_2^{2+}$ and Pu$^{4+}$ (and to a lesser extent Th$^{4+}$, Np$^{4+}$), but **rejects** most trivalent and monovalent fission products (Cs$^+$, Sr$^{2+}$, REE$^{3+}$). It is also **chemically and radiolytically stable**.

### 2.2.2 Process Flow (Pulse Columns / Mixer-Settlers)

The standard PUREX flow has **three principal cycles** plus auxiliary operations:

1. **Head End:** Chopping, dissolution in 6–8 M HNO$_3$, clarification, and feed adjustment.
2. **Co-decontamination / Extraction:** Fuel solution contacted with 30% TBP. U + Pu transfer to organic; FP remain in aqueous (raffinate → HLW).
3. **Partitioning:** Selective stripping of Pu from U. Achieved by **reducing Pu$^{4+}$ to Pu$^{3+}$** using a reducing agent (Fe$^{2+}$, U$^{4+}$, or hydroxylamine). Pu$^{3+}$ has very low $D$ → transfers to aqueous. U stays in organic.
4. **U Stripping:** U stripped from organic using **dilute HNO$_3$ (0.01–0.05 M)**.
5. **Second & Third Cycles:** Further purification of U and Pu product streams.

### 2.2.3 Redox Chemistry of Plutonium — The "Heart" of PUREX

| Species | Oxidation State | Extractability by TBP |
|---|---|---|
| PuO$_2^{2+}$ (Pu$^{6+}$) | +6 | High (similar to UO$_2^{2+}$) |
| Pu$^{4+}$ | +4 | **High** (extracted) |
| Pu$^{3+}$ | +3 | **Very Low** (not extracted) |
| PuO$_2^{+}$ (Pu$^{5+}$) | +5 | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Valence Adjustment is the KEY trick.** PUREX uses **NaNO$_2$** to oxidize all Pu to Pu$^{4+}$ (or PuO$_2^{2+}$) before extraction. During partitioning, **Fe(II) sulfamate** or **U(IV)** selectively reduces **Pu to Pu$^{3+}$** so it leaves the organic phase, while **U stays as UO$_2^{2+}$** (not reducible by these mild agents).

## 2.3 The UREX Process (Uranium Extraction)

**UREX** is a modified PUREX-type process developed under the U.S. **Advanced Fuel Cycle Initiative (AFCI)** and the **Global Nuclear Energy Partnership (GNEP)**. Its primary goal is to **separate only uranium** from spent fuel — leaving Pu **with the transuranics (TRU)** — to enhance **proliferation resistance**.

### 2.3.1 Chemistry

- TBP in dodecane is used (similar to PUREX).
- A complexing agent — **acetohydroxamic acid (AHA)** — is added to the aqueous phase.
- **AHA selectively complexes Pu(IV) and Np(IV/V)**, depressing their distribution ratios ($D_{Pu}$, $D_{Np}$ → essentially zero).
- **U(VI) is unaffected by AHA** and is preferentially extracted.

The effective reaction:

$$UO_2^{2+} + 2\,NO_3^{-} + 2\,TBP \rightarrow UO_2(NO_3)_2 \cdot 2TBP \quad (extracted)$$

Pu stays as **Pu–AHA complex** in the aqueous phase and is later sent to the **TRUEX step** for co-extraction with the minor actinides.

### 2.3.2 Process Advantages

| Feature | PUREX | UREX |
|---|---|---|
| Primary Output | U + Pu separately | U only (Pu stays with TRU) |
| Proliferation Risk | Moderate (pure Pu) | **Reduced** (Pu mixed with Am, Np, Cm) |
| Waste Stream Partitioning | 2 (U/Pu, FP) | 4 (U, TRU+Ln, FP, Cs/Sr) |
| Reducing Agent Required | Yes (for Pu partition) | No |
| Compatible with P&T | Limited | **High** (enables TRUEX) |

## 2.4 The TRUEX Process (TRansUranic EXtraction)

**TRUEX** was developed at **Argonne National Laboratory (ANL)** to extract **all transuranic elements (An³⁺)** — namely **Am³⁺, Cm³⁺, NpO_2^+, and Pu⁴⁺** — from the acidic raffinate left after UREX, and to **separate them from lanthanides (Ln³⁺)**.

### 2.4.1 The Extractant — CMPO

The active extractant is a bifunctional organophosphorus compound:

- **CMPO** = **Octyl(phenyl)-N,N-diisobutylcarbamoylmethylphosphine oxide**

CMPO is typically used at **0.2 M** in TBP/diluent (the TBP acts as a phase modifier).

The extraction reaction for trivalent actinides (An = Am, Cm):

$$An^{3+}_{(aq)} + 3\,NO_3^{-}_{(aq)} + 3\,CMPO_{(org)} \rightarrow An(NO_3)_3 \cdot 3CMPO_{(org)}$$

### 2.4.2 The Lanthanide Challenge

Lanthanides (Ln³⁺) are chemically very similar to trivalent actinides. The **An–Ln separation factor** in CMPO is unfortunately **only ~5–10**, not enough for engineering separation. Therefore, TRUEX is **always followed by a Ln–An separation step**, such as:

- **TALSPEAK** (Trivalent Actinide–Lanthanide Separation by Phosphorus reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes)
- **SANEX** (Selective ActiNide EXtraction)
- **DIAMEX** (DIAMide EXtraction, French process)

> [!NOTE]
> **TRUEX + TALSPEAK** is the canonical KTU textbook flow for the "**Partitioning and Transmutation (P&T)**" strategy — a high-yield point for 14-mark questions.

## 2.5 Pyroprocessing — Electrolysis Route

**Pyroprocessing** (also called **non-aqueous reprocessing** or **electrorefining**) avoids liquids like HNO$_3$ and organic solvents. Instead, the spent fuel is processed in **molten salt electrolytes** at high temperatures (typically **400–700 °C**).

### 2.5.1 Why Pyroprocessing?

- **Radiation stability:** Molten salts are not degraded by intense radiation as organics are.
- **Compact footprint:** Criticality-safe geometry is easier to achieve (small tanks, no moderators).
- **Proliferation resistance:** U and Pu are **never separated as pure streams**; they are co-deposited.
- **Accommodates metallic fuels** (e.g., EBR-II, PRISM designs) without dissolution.

### 2.5.2 The Electrorefining Cell (Mark-IV / Mk-IV Design)

Key components:

- **Anode:** Spent metallic fuel (U + TRU + FP), often chopped or cast.
- **Cathode:** Solid steel mandrel (for U collection) or liquid Cd pool (for Pu collection).
- **Electrolyte:** Molten **LiCl–KCl eutectic** (m.p. ≈ 354 °C) with dissolved UCl₃ and sometimes actinide chlorides.
- **Operating Temp:** **500 °C (773 K)**.
- **Atmosphere:** Argon (inert).

### 2.5.3 Electrode Reactions

**At the Anode (oxidation of metal):**

$$U_{(s)} \rightarrow U^{3+} + 3\,e^{-}$$

$$Pu_{(s)} \rightarrow Pu^{3+} + 3\,e^{-}$$

**At the Solid Steel Cathode (reduction — uranium is selectively deposited):**

$$U^{3+} + 3\,e^{-} \rightarrow U_{(s)} \quad (E \approx -0.5\,V \text{ vs. Cl}_2/\text{Cl}^-)$$

**At the Liquid Cd Cathode (LCC) — Pu + minor actinides deposit together:**

$$Pu^{3+} + 3\,e^{-} \rightarrow Pu_{(Cd)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Engineering Magic:** The **deposition potential of U is less negative** than that of Pu and TRU. By carefully controlling cell voltage, **U deposits first** on the solid cathode. **Pu and TRU** remain dissolved and are later collected on the **liquid Cd cathode** at a more negative potential. **Fission products** like rare earths, Cs, Sr stay dissolved in the salt; noble metals (Zr, Mo, Ru, Rh, Pd, Tc) remain as **anode sludge**.

### 2.5.4 Quantitative Electrolysis — Faraday's Law of Electrolysis

The mass of metal deposited at the cathode is:

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where:

- $m$ = mass deposited (grams)
- $M$ = molar mass (g/mol)
- $I$ = current (A)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $n$ = valency (electrons transferred per ion)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol** (or ~26.8 Ah/mol)

The volume of metal (for cathode sizing) is:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$$

The current efficiency is:

$$\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{m_{actual} \cdot n \cdot F}{M \cdot I \cdot t}$$

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol / Meaning | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \frac{[M]_{org}}{[M]_{aq}}$ | Distribution ratio | dimensionless | Quantifies extractability |
| 2 | $SF = D_A / D_B$ | Separation factor | dimensionless | Predicts separation efficiency |
| 3 | $m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$ | Faraday's law | grams | Cathode deposit mass in pyroprocessing |
| 4 | $V = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F \cdot \rho}$ | Volume of deposit | m³ | Cathode design |
| 5 | $\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}}$ | Current efficiency | % | Electrolyzer efficiency |
| 6 | $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF}\ln\frac{[Ox]}{[Red]}$ | Nernst equation | V | Predicts deposition potentials |
| 7 | $SF_{An/Ln} \sim 5$–$10$ | An/Ln SF in TRUEX | dimensionless | Why TALSPEAK follows TRUEX |
| 8 | $\rho_{LiCl-KCl} \approx 1635$ kg/m³ | Electrolyte density | kg/m³ | Cell design |
| 9 | $T_{op} \approx 773$ K | Pyro operating temp | K | Electrolyte choice |
| 10 | $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol | Faraday's constant | C/mol | Electrolysis calculations |

> [!NOTE]
> All values in the table are **standard engineering figures** used in textbook calculations and KTU numerical problems. Memorize the **Faraday's law triplet** ($m$, $V$, $\eta$) — these are tested directly.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Quantitative Material Balance in a Single-Stage Extraction

Let us consider a **single-stage mixer-settler** in PUREX where volume $V_{org}$ of organic (TBP) is contacted with volume $V_{aq}$ of aqueous (dissolved fuel). If the initial mass of a metal $M$ is $m_0$, the fraction extracted is $\alpha$ (extraction yield).

The mass balance on metal M is:

$$m_0 = m_{aq} + m_{org}$$

The distribution ratio definition gives:

$$D = \frac{C_{org}}{C_{aq}} = \frac{m_{org}/V_{org}}{m_{aq}/V_{aq}}$$

Rearranging for $m_{org}$:

$$m_{org} = m_{aq} \cdot D \cdot \frac{V_{org}}{V_{aq}}$$

Define the **volume ratio** $\theta = \frac{V_{org}}{V_{aq}}$. Substituting:

$$m_0 = m_{aq} + m_{aq} \cdot D \cdot \theta = m_{aq} \cdot (1 + D\theta)$$

Therefore:

$$m_{aq} = \frac{m_0}{1 + D\theta}$$

The fraction remaining in aqueous:

$$\beta = \frac{m_{aq}}{m_0} = \frac{1}{1 + D\theta}$$

The fraction extracted into organic:

$$\alpha = 1 - \beta = \frac{D\theta}{1 + D\theta}$$

For PUREX with $D_U \sim 30$ and $\theta = 0.3$ (organic:aqueous = 3:10), we get:

$$\alpha_U = \frac{30 \times 0.3}{1 + 30 \times 0.3} = \frac{9}{10} = 0.9 \; (90\%)$$

This shows that a **single PUREX stage recovers ~90% of U** — excellent efficiency, which is why PUREX scales well.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass of Uranium Deposited in Electrorefining

**Problem:** In an electrorefiner operating at $T = 500^{\circ}$C with $I = 200$ A for $t = 4$ hours, calculate the mass of uranium deposited at the solid steel cathode. Molar mass of U = 238 g/mol, $n = 3$ (as U$^{3+}$), $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Step 1:** Write Faraday's first law.

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

**Step 2:** Compute total charge passed.

$$Q = I \cdot t = 200 \times (4 \times 3600) = 200 \times 14400 = 2.88 \times 10^6 \text{ C}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Faraday's equation.

$$m = \frac{238 \times 2.88 \times 10^6}{3 \times 96485}$$

**Step 4:** Numerator evaluation.

$$238 \times 2.88 \times 10^6 = 685.44 \times 10^6 = 6.8544 \times 10^8 \text{ g·C/mol}$$

**Step 5:** Denominator evaluation.

$$3 \times 96485 = 289455 \text{ C/mol}$$

**Step 6:** Final division.

$$m = \frac{6.8544 \times 10^8}{2.89455 \times 10^5} \approx 2368.5 \text{ g}$$

Therefore, **~2.37 kg of uranium** is deposited at the solid cathode in 4 hours at 200 A.

**Step 7 (extension):** If the actual measured mass is 2050 g, the **current efficiency** is:

$$\eta = \frac{m_{actual}}{m_{theoretical}} = \frac{2050}{2368.5} \approx 0.866 = 86.6\%$$

This is typical for pyroprocessing (industry reports 80–95%).

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Aqueous vs Non-Aqueous Reprocessing

| Parameter | PUREX (Aqueous) | UREX (Aqueous) | TRUEX (Aqueous) | Pyroprocessing (Non-Aqueous) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **State of Feed** | Dissolved in HNO₃ | Dissolved in HNO₃ | Acidic raffinate | Solid metal or oxide |
| **Reagent** | 30% TBP in kerosene | TBP + AHA | 0.2 M CMPO in TBP | Molten LiCl–KCl |
| **Temperature** | ~25–50 °C | ~25–50 °C | ~25–50 °C | **500 °C** |
| **Key Separation** | U & Pu from FP | U from Pu+TRU+FP | TRU from Ln+FP | U on solid cathode; TRU on liquid Cd cathode |
| **Pure Pu Stream** | Yes (after partition) | **No** (Pu co-mingled) | No | **No** (Pu-U co-deposit possible) |
| **Proliferation Resistance** | Lower | Higher | Higher | **Highest** |
| **Radiation Stability** | Moderate (TBP degrades) | Moderate | Moderate | **Excellent** |
| **Waste Form** | Borosilicate glass | Glass + ceramic | Glass + ceramic | **Glass-ceramic + metal alloy** |
| **Plant Capacity (typical)** | 1000 tHM/yr | 100 tHM/yr (pilot) | 100 tHM/yr (pilot) | 10–100 tHM/yr (pilot) |
| **Commercial Status** | Mature (industrial) | Pilot | Pilot | Pilot (EBR-II, PRISM) |
| **Throughput** | High | Medium | Medium | **Lower per cell** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU 14-mark answers:** Always use a **comparison table** like the one above as the structural backbone of your answer. Examiners give high marks when the student demonstrates **comparative insight** rather than just listing features.

## 3.4 Derivation: Number of Theoretical Stages (Kremser Equation)

For multi-stage counter-current extraction (the industrial standard in PUREX), the **Kremser equation** gives the fraction of solute remaining in the raffinate:

$$\beta_n = \frac{\theta - 1}{\theta^{n+1} - 1} \quad (\theta \neq 1)$$

where $\theta = D \cdot \frac{V_{org}}{V_{aq}}$ and $n$ = number of stages.

The fraction extracted is $\alpha_n = 1 - \beta_n$.

**Design check:** To achieve $\alpha_{Pu} > 99.99\%$ in PUREX co-decontamination with $D_{Pu} = 10$ and $V_{org}/V_{aq} = 0.3$:

$$\theta = 10 \times 0.3 = 3$$

For $n = 5$ stages:

$$\beta_5 = \frac{3 - 1}{3^6 - 1} = \frac{2}{728} \approx 2.75 \times 10^{-3}$$

This is only **99.7%** recovery — not enough. Trying $n = 8$:

$$\beta_8 = \frac{2}{3^9 - 1} = \frac{2}{19682} \approx 1.02 \times 10^{-4} \rightarrow 99.99\%$$

So **PUREX typically uses 8–12 mixer-settler stages** in the co-decontamination cycle.
