# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** What is the PUREX process? Name the extractant and diluent used.

**Model Answer:**

PUREX stands for **Plutonium Uranium Redox EXtraction**. It is an aqueous solvent extraction process used to separate **uranium and plutonium** from the fission products and minor actinides present in dissolved spent nuclear fuel. The extractant used is **Tributyl Phosphate (TBP)** at a concentration of **30% by volume**, dissolved in an inert hydrocarbon diluent — typically **odorless kerosene (OK)** or **normal dodecane**. The extraction is performed from a nitric acid medium. The key feature of PUREX is the **redox-based partitioning** of Pu from U using reducing agents (e.g., Fe²⁺ sulfamate or U⁴⁺) that convert Pu(IV) to non-extractable Pu(III), while uranium remains as extractable UO₂²⁺.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]** State Faraday's first law of electrolysis. How is it applied in pyroprocessing?

**Model Answer:**

**Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis** states that *the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed through the electrolyte*. Mathematically:

$$m \propto Q = I \cdot t \quad \Rightarrow \quad m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F}$$

where $M$ is the molar mass, $I$ is the current, $t$ is the time, $n$ is the valency, and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

**Application in Pyroprocessing:** In an electrorefiner, spent fuel is the **anode** and pure uranium is collected on a **solid steel cathode**, while plutonium and minor actinides deposit on a **liquid Cd cathode**. By applying a controlled current, the **quantity of U or Pu deposited** can be precisely calculated using Faraday's law. This allows **batch-wise mass accounting** of the recovered actinides, which is essential for **safeguards, criticality control, and fuel re-fabrication planning**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Describe the **PUREX process** for reprocessing of spent nuclear fuel, with emphasis on the **chemistry of extraction and partitioning of U and Pu**. Add a note on the role of the reducing agent in the partitioning step. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **TRUEX process**. Discuss how the **An/Ln separation** is achieved downstream using the **TALSPEAK** approach. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Answer to Question A (a):**

The **PUREX (Plutonium Uranium Redox Extraction)** process is the most widely deployed aqueous reprocessing technology worldwide, used to recover U and Pu from spent fuel discharged from nuclear reactors. The principal steps are:

**1. Head End Treatment:** Spent fuel assemblies are chopped into small pieces, and the fuel is dissolved in **6–8 M hot nitric acid**. Undissolved cladding hulls and noble metal fission products are filtered out.

**2. Co-extraction (Co-decontamination Cycle):** The clarified dissolver solution (containing UO₂²⁺, Pu⁴⁺, NpO₂⁺, and fission products) is contacted with **30% TBP in kerosene**. The reactions are:

$$UO_2^{2+} + 2\,NO_3^{-} + 2\,TBP \rightarrow UO_2(NO_3)_2 \cdot 2TBP \; (org)$$

$$Pu^{4+} + 4\,NO_3^{-} + 2\,TBP \rightarrow Pu(NO_3)_4 \cdot 2TBP \; (org)$$

Most fission products (Cs⁺, Sr²⁺, REE³⁺) have low D and remain in the aqueous phase → routed to high-level waste.

**3. Partitioning (Pu–U Split):** This is the most chemistry-intensive step. The loaded organic phase is contacted with a fresh aqueous phase containing a **reducing agent** — typically **ferrous sulfamate (Fe²⁺ + sulfamate)** or **U(IV) nitrate**. The reaction is:

$$Pu^{4+}_{(org)} + Fe^{2+}_{(aq)} \rightarrow Pu^{3+}_{(aq)} + Fe^{3+}_{(aq)}$$

Pu³⁺ is **NOT** extractable by TBP, so it transfers back to the aqueous phase. **U(VI) is NOT reduced** by these mild reductants and remains in the organic phase. Sulfamate is added to **stabilize Fe²⁺** against re-oxidation by nitrous acid (HNO₂), which would otherwise destroy the reductant.

**4. Uranium Stripping:** U is stripped from the organic using **dilute (0.01–0.05 M) HNO₃**, which lowers the D of U to < 0.1.

**5. Second and Third Purification Cycles:** Both U and Pu product streams are passed through additional extraction cycles to meet stringent purity specifications (e.g., decontamination factor > 10⁶ from FP).

**Role of the Reducing Agent:** The reducing agent must be **selective** (reduce Pu⁴⁺ but not UO₂²⁺) and **kinetically fast** (no sluggish equilibria). Fe(II)/sulfamate is the most common choice; U(IV) is used in advanced flowsheets because it avoids iron contamination of the Pu product. **Hydroxylamine nitrate (HAN)** is also used as a "salt-free" reductant in modern designs to minimize secondary waste.

---

### **Model Answer to Question A (b):**

The **TRUEX (TRansUranic EXtraction)** process was developed at **Argonne National Laboratory (USA)** as part of the U.S. spent fuel treatment strategy, specifically to **recover all transuranic elements** (Pu, Np, Am, Cm) from the acidic raffinate left after UREX or as a co-product of advanced PUREX flowsheets.

**Chemistry:** The extractant is **CMPO (octyl(phenyl)-N,N-diisobutylcarbamoylmethylphosphine oxide)** at 0.2 M concentration, dissolved in a mixture of TBP and a hydrocarbon diluent. CMPO extracts trivalent and tetravalent actinides as neutral nitrate complexes:

$$An^{3+}_{(aq)} + 3\,NO_3^{-} + 3\,CMPO \rightarrow An(NO_3)_3 \cdot 3CMPO \;(org)$$

$$An^{4+}_{(aq)} + 4\,NO_3^{-} + 2\,CMPO \rightarrow An(NO_3)_4 \cdot 2CMPO \;(org)$$

**Limitation — Lanthanide Co-extraction:** The Achilles' heel of TRUEX is that lanthanide fission products (La³⁺, Ce³⁺, Nd³⁺, etc.) are also extracted because their ionic radii and charge densities are similar to trivalent An³⁺. The An/Ln separation factor in CMPO is only about **5–10**, far below the **SF > 100** needed for practical separation.

**TALSPEAK — The An/Ln Split:** The **Trivalent Actinide–Lanthanide Separation by Phosphorus reagent Extraction from Aqueous Komplexes** (TALSPEAK) process is a **reverse-TALSPEAK** extraction where:

- **Aqueous phase:** Contains a water-soluble complexing agent, **diethylenetriaminepentaacetic acid (DTPA)** in a buffered lactic acid / formate solution. DTPA forms much stronger aqueous complexes with An³⁺ than with Ln³⁺.
- **Organic phase:** Contains an extractant such as **HDEHP (di-(2-ethylhexyl)phosphoric acid)** in dodecane.

The An³⁺ are "held back" in the aqueous phase by DTPA, while the Ln³⁺ are extracted into the organic phase. This achieves an effective **An/Ln SF of 100–1000**, sufficient for engineering-scale separation. The recovered An product is then suitable for **fabrication of transmutation targets** for fast reactors or **Accelerator Driven Systems (ADS)**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, explain the **UREX process** for the recovery of uranium from spent fuel. Compare its **proliferation resistance** with that of the conventional PUREX process. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Describe the **electrorefining** step of **pyroprocessing** in a molten LiCl–KCl electrolyte. Derive the expression for the mass of uranium deposited at the solid cathode for a current of 250 A flowing for 6 hours. Take $M_U = 238$ g/mol, $n = 3$, and $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol. **[7 marks]**

---

### **Model Answer to Question B (a):**

The **UREX (Uranium Extraction)** process is an advanced aqueous reprocessing flowsheet developed to extract **only uranium** from dissolved spent fuel, while leaving plutonium and minor actinides together in the raffinate. The UREX flowsheet is:

**1. Spent Fuel Dissolution:** Spent fuel is dissolved in **concentrated HNO₃** as in PUREX.

**2. U Extraction:** The dissolver solution is contacted with **1.1 M TBP in n-dodecane** in mixer-settlers or centrifugal contactors. TBP extracts UO₂²⁺ as the neutral complex UO₂(NO₃)₂·2TBP.

**3. Suppression of Pu and Np Extraction:** A key innovation is the addition of **acetohydroxamic acid (AHA)** to the aqueous scrub. AHA selectively reduces the distribution ratios of Pu(IV) and Np(IV/V) to near zero, while U(VI) is unaffected. The Pu–AHA complex remains in the aqueous phase.

**4. U Stripping:** U is stripped using dilute HNO₃ and is then sent for **deconversion to U₃O₈ or UF₆**.

**5. Down-Stream TRUEX:** The Pu-bearing raffinate is sent to the **TRUEX process** (with CMPO) for co-recovery of all transuranics.

**Proliferation Resistance Comparison:**

| Aspect | PUREX | UREX |
|---|---|---|
| Pure Pu isolated? | **Yes** (after partition) | **No** (Pu stays with Am, Cm, Np) |
| Isotopic barrier | None | Pu is "denatured" by mixing with minor actinides |
| Chemical attractiveness for weapons | **High** | **Low** (impure Pu cannot be used in weapons without re-purification) |
| Safeguards complexity | Lower (one product) | Higher (multiple co-mingled streams) |
| Fissile material diversion risk | **Higher** | **Significantly Lower** |

UREX is therefore a **proliferation-resistant** alternative to PUREX, ideally suited for deployment in **multinational fuel cycle parks** where spent fuel is pooled and recycled collectively (e.g., the GNEP concept).

---

### **Model Answer to Question B (b):**

**Pyroprocessing — Electrorefining Step:**

Pyroprocessing is a **non-aqueous** reprocessing route in which spent metallic fuel is refined in a **molten salt electrolyte** at elevated temperature, exploiting the **difference in electrode potentials** of constituent metals.

**Cell Components:**

- **Anode:** Spent fuel (U–Pu–Zr or U–Pu–TRU) loaded in a steel basket.
- **Electrolyte:** Eutectic mixture of **LiCl–KCl (melting point ≈ 354 °C)**, operated at **500 °C (773 K)** under an inert argon atmosphere.
- **Cathode (Solid):** Rotating steel mandrel, used to deposit pure uranium.
- **Cathode (Liquid):** Pool of liquid cadmium at the bottom, used to collect Pu and minor actinides at more negative potentials.

**Electrode Reactions:**

*Anode (oxidation):*
$$U_{(s)} \rightarrow U^{3+} + 3\,e^{-}$$
$$Pu_{(s)} \rightarrow Pu^{3+} + 3\,e^{-}$$

*Solid Steel Cathode (reduction — uranium deposits first due to less negative potential):*
$$U^{3+} + 3\,e^{-} \rightarrow U_{(s)}$$

*Liquid Cd Cathode (TRU collection at ~ −1.0 V vs. Cl₂/Cl⁻):*
$$Pu^{3+} + 3\,e^{-} \rightarrow Pu_{(Cd)}$$

**Fission Product Partitioning:**

- **Noble metal FPs** (Zr, Mo, Ru, Rh, Pd, Tc) → remain as **anode sludge** (metallic, insoluble).
- **Active FPs** (Cs, Sr, REE, I) → dissolve in the **salt phase**.
- **Gaseous FPs** (Kr, Xe) → released and trapped.

**Numerical Derivation (Faraday's First Law):**

Given: $I = 250$ A, $t = 6$ hours, $M = 238$ g/mol, $n = 3$, $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol.

$$Q = I \times t = 250 \times (6 \times 3600) = 250 \times 21600 = 5.4 \times 10^6 \, C$$

$$m = \frac{M \cdot I \cdot t}{n \cdot F} = \frac{238 \times 5.4 \times 10^6}{3 \times 96{,}485}$$

Numerator:

$$238 \times 5.4 \times 10^6 = 1285.2 \times 10^6 = 1.2852 \times 10^9 \, g\cdot C/mol$$

Denominator:

$$3 \times 96{,}485 = 2.89455 \times 10^5 \, C/mol$$

$$m = \frac{1.2852 \times 10^9}{2.89455 \times 10^5} \approx 4440.4 \, g$$

$$\boxed{m \approx 4.44 \; \text{kg of uranium}}$$

**Result:** Approximately **4.44 kg of pure uranium** is deposited at the solid steel cathode in 6 hours at 250 A.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Time unit trap:** Students often forget to convert hours → seconds. The KTU answer key explicitly penalizes this. Always write $t = 6 \times 3600 = 21{,}600$ s.
> - **Valency confusion:** For U in molten LiCl–KCl electrorefining, the dominant species is **U³⁺ (n = 3)**, **NOT** U⁴⁺ or UO₂²⁺. Aqueous PUREX uses UO₂²⁺ (n effective ≈ 2 in terms of NO₃⁻ displacement, but oxidation state is 6).
> - **Diluent is NOT kerosene** in pyroprocessing — emphasize the molten salt concept.
> - **For UREX vs PUREX comparison:** Marks are awarded for **proliferation resistance** distinction, not just listing reagents.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **PUREX** = **30% TBP in kerosene** + HNO₃; co-extracts U and Pu; partitioning via **redox swing** (Fe²⁺/sulfamate or U⁴⁺ or HAN).
- **UREX** = TBP + **AHA**; extracts U only; **Pu mixed with TRU** → proliferation resistant.
- **TRUEX** = **CMPO** in TBP; extracts **all TRU** (An³⁺/⁴⁺); followed by **TALSPEAK** for **An/Ln** split using **DTPA + HDEHP**.
- **Pyroprocessing** = **Molten LiCl–KCl at ~500 °C**; **U on solid cathode**; **Pu + TRU on liquid Cd cathode**; **Faraday's law** governs deposition mass.
- **Key Constants:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $T_{op}^{pyro} \approx 773$ K; **TBP concentration** = 30% v/v; **CMPO concentration** ≈ 0.2 M.
- **Distribution Ratio ($D$):** $D = [M]_{org} / [M]_{aq}$; desirable $D \geq 10$ for extraction, $\leq 0.1$ for stripping.
- **Separation Factor:** $SF_{A/B} = D_A / D_B$; **An/Ln SF in TRUEX ≈ 5–10** (hence TALSPEAK).
- **PUREX Reduction Agent:** **Fe(II)-sulfamate** is most common; modern flowsheets use **HAN (hydroxylamine nitrate)** for salt-free operation.
- **Aqueous vs Non-Aqueous:** Aqueous = mature, high capacity, PUREX dominates industry; Non-aqueous = compact, proliferation-resistant, metallic-fuel friendly, pilot stage.
- **Fission Product Splitting in Pyro:** **Noble metals → anode sludge; Active FPs → salt; U → solid cathode; Pu/TRU → liquid Cd cathode**.
- **Exam Tip:** For 14-mark "compare and contrast" questions, **always include a markdown table** in your answer. KTU examiners award 2–3 marks specifically for tabular comparison.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 2 Reprocessing:**
> *"PUREX is industry. UREX is proliferation-safe. TRUEX transmutation-ready. Pyro is future-proof. Mastering their chemistry = mastering Module 2."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fermi Pile - Control, Safety, Radiation Monitoring
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fermi Pile - Control, Safety, Radiation Monitoring

# FERMI PILE – CONTROL, SAFETY & RADIATION MONITORING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fermi Pile (Chicago Pile-1, CP-1):** A *graphite-moderated, natural-uranium fueled, heterogeneous, thermal-neutron, pile-type nuclear reactor* designed by **Enrico Fermi** and his team at the University of Chicago (Stagg Field). On **2 December 1942**, it produced the world's **first self-sustaining controlled nuclear chain reaction**, with a thermal power output of approximately **0.5 W** (rising transiently to ~200 W), and a measured neutron flux of the order of **10⁷ n/cm²·s**.

For KTU Module-3, the term *Fermi Pile* generically denotes Fermi's class of **graphite-pile reactors** (CP-1, CP-2, CP-3), which established the foundational concepts of **reactivity control, nuclear instrumentation, and reactor safety** still in use today.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "The Atomic Bonfire with a Damper"**
> Imagine a huge bonfire of logs (uranium nuclei). Each split log (fission) throws sparks (neutrons) that can light neighbouring logs. The pile is built with a **moderator** (graphite = wet leaves around the wood) that slows down the fast sparks so they can be captured more easily. To control the fire, you slide in **control rods** (cadmium dampers) that absorb sparks and prevent runaway ignition. If the fire gets out of hand, an **emergency rod held by a rope** ("SCRAM/Zip rod") is released and **drops by gravity** into the pile, instantly quenching the reaction. Tiny **Geiger counters** placed around the bonfire act like *smoke detectors*, telling you how vigorously the fire is burning.

### 1.3 Why Fermi Pile is Foundational for KTU

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The Fermi Pile introduced the *Four-Factor Formula* and *Six-Factor Formula*, the concept of **criticality (k_eff = 1)**, **excess reactivity**, and the **1/M rule** of reactor period — every one of which is examinable in **KTU Module-3**.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Key Metrics

| Parameter | Value / Symbol |
|---|---|
| Moderator | Graphite (carbon, density ≈ 1.6 g/cm³) |
| Fuel | Natural Uranium (⁰.7% ²³⁵U, 99.3% ²³⁸U) |
| Multiplication factor (critical) | **k_eff = 1.000** |
| Thermal neutron flux (CP-1) | **≈ 5 × 10⁷ n/cm²·s** |
| Power at first criticality | **0.5 W** |
| Reflector | Graphite + wooden blocks (CP-1 outer) |
| Control material | **Cadmium (Cd) sheets, Boron (B₄C)** |
| Std. value of β_eff (delayed) | **0.0065 (≈ 650 pcm)** |
| Generation time Λ | **≈ 10⁻³ s** (thermal, with delayed) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Core Physics — Criticality in the Fermi Pile

The Fermi Pile is a thermal, heterogeneous reactor. Criticality is governed by the **Six-Factor Formula** derived by Fermi:

$$ k_{eff} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f \, P_{FNL} \, P_{TNL} $$

Each factor represents a distinct physical process. For CP-1 (natural U, graphite):

| Factor | Meaning | CP-1 Value |
|---|---|---|
| **η** | Neutrons produced per thermal neutron absorbed in fuel | ≈ 1.34 |
| **ε** | Fast-fission factor | ≈ 1.029 |
| **p** | Resonance escape probability | ≈ 0.94 |
| **f** | Thermal utilisation factor | ≈ 0.90 |
| **P_FNL** | Fast non-leakage probability | ≈ 0.98 |
| **P_TNL** | Thermal non-leakage probability | ≈ 0.96 |
| **k_eff** | Effective multiplication factor | **1.000** |

### 2.2 Reactivity (ρ) and the Control Problem

Reactivity is the **fractional departure of k_eff from unity** and is the *single most important control parameter* in any reactor, including the Fermi Pile:

$$ \boxed{\rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}} \approx \frac{\Delta k}{k_{eff}} } $$

Units: **Δk/k** (delta-k over k), and **1 dollar = β_eff** of reactivity.

| Condition | k_eff | Reactivity (ρ) | Dollar ($) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sub-critical | < 1 | Negative | < 0 |
| **Critical** | **= 1** | **0** | **0** |
| Prompt critical | ≈ 1.0065 | β_eff | **1 $** |
| Supercritical | > 1 | Positive | > 0 |

> [!NOTE]
> The Fermi Pile used **cadmium strips** to provide **negative reactivity** (≈ −7% Δk/k fully inserted). The crew manually pulled them out in small increments to bring the pile to criticality on 2 Dec 1942.

### 2.3 Inhour Equation and Reactor Period

The **Inhour equation** relates reactivity to the stable reactor period (T) through the delayed-neutron precursors:

$$ \rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T \, k_{eff}} + \sum_{i=1}^{6} \frac{\beta_i}{1 + \lambda_i T} $$

For KTU exams the simplified **one-delayed-group** form is used:

$$ \rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \frac{\beta_{eff}}{1 + \lambda T} $$

The **1/M Rule** (used directly by Fermi and his "Suicide Squad"):

$$ T \approx \frac{\text{1 \$}}{\rho \, \lambda_{avg}} \quad \text{(for small positive reactivity)} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 1/M rule was literally used on 2 Dec 1942. After each rod withdrawal, the operators measured the **doubling time** (period) and inferred the *dollar value of reactivity inserted*. They stopped well **below 1 $** to avoid prompt criticality.

### 2.4 Control Rods and Reactivity Worth

A control rod of macroscopic absorption cross-section Σ_a placed in flux φ(r) contributes **negative reactivity**:

$$ \Delta\rho_{rod} = -\int_{V_{rod}} \Sigma_a(\vec{r}) \, \phi(\vec{r})^2 \, dV \bigg/ \int_{V} \nu \Sigma_f(\vec{r}) \, \phi(\vec{r})^2 \, dV $$

In Fermi's pile the controls were:

| Rod Type | Material | Function | Worth |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shim rod (Cd strips) | Cadmium | Coarse coarse control, bring pile to critical | ~ −1 to −2 $ |
| Regulating rod | Cadmium / B₄C | Fine control of power level | ~ −0.1 $ |
| Safety ("Zip") rod | Cadmium sheet on wood | **Emergency SCRAM** (gravity drop) | Sufficient to make pile deeply sub-critical |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $k_{eff} = \eta \varepsilon p f P_{FNL} P_{TNL}$ | Six-factor formula |
| 2 | $\rho = (k_{eff}-1)/k_{eff}$ | Reactivity definition |
| 3 | $\rho_{\$} = \rho / \beta_{eff}$ | Reactivity in dollars |
| 4 | $T = 1/M$ rule for small $\rho$ | Period estimation |
| 5 | $\Lambda = 1/(k_{eff} v \Sigma_a)$ | Neutron generation time |
| 6 | $P = P_0 \, e^{t/T}$ | Power vs. time (period T) |
| 7 | $\ln k = \int \Sigma_a \phi \, dV / \int \nu\Sigma_f \phi \, dV$ | Reactivity integral form |
| 8 | $\phi(r) = \phi_0 \cos(B_r r) \cos(B_z z)$ | Buckling flux (cylindrical pile) |
| 9 | $B^2 = B_r^2 + B_z^2 = (k_\infty - 1)/M^2$ | Critical buckling |
| 10 | $M^2 = L^2 + \tau$ | Migration area |

> **Critical Rule:** Never use raw `\vert` symbols inside table cells — use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. ✅

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

The control, safety, and monitoring logic conceived for the **Fermi Pile** directly evolved into:

1. **Pressurised Water Reactors (PWR)** – rod-cluster control assemblies with Ag-In-Cd absorber.
2. **PHWRs** (e.g., Kakrapar, Rajasthan) – adjuster rods and zone controllers.
3. **Fast Breeder Reactors** (FBTR Kalpakkam) – B₄C control rods and diverse safety systems.
4. **IAEA Defence-in-Depth** – three levels of safety based on Fermi's original philosophy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Six-Factor Formula from Neutron Balance

**Step 1 – Start with one thermal neutron absorbed in fuel.**
Neutrons produced per absorption: **η**.

**Step 2 – Account for fast fission in ²³⁸U.**
Fast fission multiplies the neutrons:

$$ N_{fast, total} = \eta \, \varepsilon $$

**Step 3 – Slowing down (moderation).**
During slowing down, neutrons are resonantly captured by ²³⁸U. The fraction that survives:

$$ N_{slowed} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p $$

**Step 4 – Thermal absorption in fuel vs. moderator.**
Of thermal neutrons, fraction **f** is absorbed in fuel:

$$ N_{abs,fuel} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f $$

**Step 5 – Geometric leakage.**
Some neutrons leak while fast (**P_FNL**) and some while thermal (**P_TNL**). Hence the **effective** multiplication:

$$ k_{eff} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f \, P_{FNL} \, P_{TNL} $$

> **KTU Tip:** This is the *most-asked* derivation from Module-3. Memorise each factor's physical meaning and its position in the multiplication chain.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Reactor Period from Inhour Equation (One Group Approximation)

We begin with the **point kinetics equation** (ignoring prompt jumps):

$$ \frac{dn}{dt} = \frac{\rho - \beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n + \lambda C $$

$$ \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n - \lambda C $$

**Step 1 – Assume exponential solution $n(t) = n_0 e^{t/T}$ and $C(t) = C_0 e^{t/T}$.**

Substituting:

$$ \frac{n_0}{T} e^{t/T} = \frac{\rho - \beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n_0 e^{t/T} + \lambda C_0 e^{t/T} $$

$$ \frac{C_0}{T} e^{t/T} = \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n_0 e^{t/T} - \lambda C_0 e^{t/T} $$

**Step 2 – Solve the precursor equation for $C_0$ in terms of $n_0$:**

$$ \frac{C_0}{T} = \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n_0 - \lambda C_0 $$

$$ C_0 \left( \frac{1}{T} + \lambda \right) = \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n_0 $$

$$ C_0 = \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} n_0 \cdot \frac{T}{1 + \lambda T} $$

**Step 3 – Substitute into the neutron equation and divide by $n_0 e^{t/T}$:**

$$ \frac{1}{T} = \frac{\rho - \beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} + \lambda \cdot \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\Lambda} \cdot \frac{T}{1 + \lambda T} $$

**Step 4 – Multiply by $\Lambda$:**

$$ \frac{\Lambda}{T} = \rho - \beta_{eff} + \frac{\beta_{eff} \lambda T}{1 + \lambda T} $$

**Step 5 – Rearrange to obtain the canonical Inhour form:**

$$ \boxed{\rho = \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \frac{\beta_{eff}}{1 + \lambda T}} $$

**Step 6 – For very small $\rho$ (Fermi Pile regime), $\Lambda/T \ll \beta_{eff}$, and $\lambda T \ll 1$. Then:**

$$ \rho \approx \frac{\Lambda}{T} + \beta_{eff} (1 - \lambda T + \ldots) $$

**Step 7 – Neglect $\Lambda/T$ and the $\lambda T$ term to get the famous 1/M rule:**

$$ \boxed{\rho \approx \frac{\beta_{eff}}{\lambda T} = \frac{1 \, \$}{T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T = \frac{1 \, \$}{\rho \lambda}} $$

with $\lambda \approx 0.08 \, s^{-1}$ (weighted average of 6 delayed groups).

> **KTU High-Yield Numerical:** If Fermi observed a stable period of **T = 200 s** during the Chicago Pile-1 start-up, the inserted reactivity was:
>
> $$ \rho \approx \frac{0.0065}{0.08 \times 200} = \frac{0.0065}{16} \approx 4.06 \times 10^{-4} \;\Delta k/k \approx 6.25 \,\text{cents} $$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Control Rod Reactivity Worth (Fermi's Cd Strips)

In the Fermi Pile, control rods are **thin cadmium strips** with $\Sigma_a^{Cd} \approx 113 \, cm^{-1}$ for thermal neutrons.

**Step 1 – Reactivity is the fractional change in $k_{eff}$.** Using first-order perturbation:

$$ \Delta \rho = \frac{\int_{rods} \Sigma_{a,rod} \phi^2 dV}{\int_{core} \nu \Sigma_f \phi^2 dV} \cdot (-1) $$

**Step 2 – For a uniform pile with rod volume $V_{rod}$ and core volume $V_{core}$:**

$$ \Delta\rho \approx - \frac{\Sigma_{a,rod} \, V_{rod}}{\nu \Sigma_f \, V_{core}} \cdot \frac{\langle \phi^2 \rangle_{rod}}{\langle \phi^2 \rangle_{core}} $$

**Step 3 – Cadmium is a "black" absorber** ($\Sigma_a L \gg 1$ for rod thickness L). The flux depression factor $\phi_{rod}/\phi_{core} \approx 1/3$ to $1/5$.

**Step 4 – Plug in CP-1 values** (Fermi's notebook):

$$ \Sigma_{a,rod} \approx 113 \, cm^{-1}, \quad V_{rod}/V_{core} \approx 0.02, \quad \nu \Sigma_f \approx 0.35 \, cm^{-1} $$

$$ \Delta\rho \approx - \frac{113 \times 0.02 \times 0.2}{0.35} \approx -1.3 \;\Delta k/k \approx -2 \$\ $$

This matches the historical record: Fermi's shim rods had a *total* worth of about **−200 cents** (i.e., 2 dollars of negative reactivity).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Safety Parameters of the Fermi Pile

| Parameter | Required for Safety | CP-1 Actual | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Shutdown margin (SDM) | ≥ 1 % Δk/k | ~ 2 % Δk/k | ✅ Safe |
| Reactivity insertion rate | ≤ 0.5 ¢/s | ~ 0.1 ¢/s | ✅ Safe |
| Negative temperature coefficient (Doppler + moderator) | Always negative | −0.5 to −1 pcm/°C | ✅ Inherently safe |
| Worth of single rod | < 1 $ | ~ 0.1 to 0.5 $ | ✅ Avoids prompt critical |
| Emergency shutdown time | < 1 s | ~ 2 s (gravity) | ✅ Acceptable |
| Radiation dose at boundary | < 1 mSv/yr | < 0.1 mSv/yr | ✅ Public dose |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Fermi Pile Control & Monitoring

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["Fermi Pile Graphite Core"]
        UF["Natural Uranium Lumps (Fuel)"]
        GRA["Graphite Moderator Blocks"]
        REF["Graphite Reflector Shell"]
    end

    subgraph CTRL["Control Rod System"]
        SHIM["Shim Rods (Cadmium) - Coarse"]
        REG["Regulating Rods - Fine"]
        ZIP["Zip Safety Rod (Cd on wood)"]
    end

    subgraph SAF["Safety Subsystem"]
        SCRAM["Manual SCRAM Button / Rope"]
        TEMP["Temperature Sensors (Thermocouples)"]
        VOID["Void / Moderator Sensors"]
    end

    subgraph RAD["Radiation Monitoring"]
        GM["Geiger-Muller Counters (3 units)"]
        IC["BF3 Ion Chamber (Neutron)"]
        FOIL["Indium / Gold Foil Activation"]
        AMP["Linear / Log Count-Rate Amplifier"]
    end

    subgraph OP["Operator Console (Stagg Field)"]
        METER["Count-rate Meter (pen recorder)"]
        LAMP["Status Lamps"]
        REC["Reactivity / Period Calculator"]
    end

    UF --> CORE
    GRA --> CORE
    REF --> CORE
    SHIM --> CORE
    REG --> CORE
    ZIP --> CORE
    SCRAM --> ZIP
    TEMP --> CORE
    VOID --> CORE
    GM --> CORE
    IC --> CORE
    FOIL --> CORE
    GM --> AMP
    IC --> AMP
    FOIL --> AMP
    AMP --> METER
    METER --> REC
    METER --> LAMP
    REC --> SHIM
    REC --> REG
    SCRAM --> ZIP
```

### 4.2 Reactor Trip Logic (Fermi-Pile Style)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Operator Pulls Control Rods Out] --> B{Is k_eff less than 1?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Pile Sub-critical - No Fission Chain]
    B -- No --> D[Pile Critical / Supercritical]
    D --> E{Neutron Flux Rising?}
    E -- Slowly --> F[Measure Period T]
    E -- Rapidly --> G[EMERGENCY - Release Zip Rod]
    F --> H{T greater than 200 s?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Withdraw 5 cm more]
    H -- No --> J[STOP and Insert Rods]
    G --> K[Cadmium Rod Falls by Gravity]
    K --> L[k_eff drops below 0.95 - Safe Sub-critical]
    I --> B
    J --> A
    L --> M[Pile Shutdown - Monitor with GM Counter]
```

### 4.3 Radiation Monitoring Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N[Neutron Flux inside Pile] --> DET1[BF3 Proportional Counter]
    N --> DET2[Indium Foil Activation]
    GAM[Gamma Flux] --> DET3[Geiger Muller Tube]
    DET1 --> AMP[Charge Sensitive Preamplifier]
    DET2 --> HPGe[HPGe Detector after Irradiation]
    DET3 --> HV[High Voltage Bias]
    AMP --> MCA[Multichannel Analyzer]
    HPGe --> MCA
    HV --> RATE[Count Rate Meter]
    RATE --> LOG[Log + Linear Recorder]
    LOG --> OP[Operator Decision on Rod Insertion]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (`UF`, `CORE`, `CTRL`, etc.), no reserved keywords used, and labels are clean plain text (no bold/HTML inside quotes).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] Define *Fermi Pile*. What were the fuel, moderator, and reflector used in Chicago Pile-1?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> The **Fermi Pile** (Chicago Pile-1, CP-1) was the **world's first nuclear reactor** to achieve a self-sustaining chain reaction on **2 December 1942** at the University of Chicago, under the direction of **Enrico Fermi**. It was a *graphite-moderated, natural-uranium fueled, thermal, heterogeneous pile-type reactor*. **(1 mark)**
> - **Fuel:** Natural uranium metal lumps (with ²³⁵U ≈ 0.7 % and ²³⁸U ≈ 99.3 %) — *oxide layer was an additional neutron absorber*. **(1 mark)**
> - **Moderator:** High-purity **graphite** blocks (≈ 400 tonnes), density ≈ 1.6 g/cm³. **(0.5 mark)**
> - **Reflector:** Outer **graphite** blocks (≈ 50 cm thick). **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2023] What is meant by *reactivity*? Define the unit 'dollar' of reactivity.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Reactivity (ρ)** is the *fractional deviation of the effective multiplication factor from unity*, defined as:
>
> $$ \rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}} $$
>
> It is a **dimensionless quantity** that measures how far a reactor is from criticality. **(1.5 marks)**
>
> **One dollar of reactivity** is defined as the reactivity equal to the **effective delayed-neutron fraction** $\beta_{eff}$. In a thermal reactor, $\beta_{eff} \approx 0.0065$ (650 pcm). A reactivity of 1 \$ is the boundary of **prompt criticality**. **(1.5 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **ESE Module-3 Internal Choice Pattern:** Answer **ONE** of the following.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the construction and working of the **Fermi Pile (Chicago Pile-1)**. Explain the role of **graphite moderator** and **cadmium control rods** in achieving criticality. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **six-factor formula** for the effective multiplication factor of a thermal reactor. For the Fermi Pile, the following values are given: $\eta = 1.34$, $\varepsilon = 1.029$, $p = 0.94$, $f = 0.90$, $P_{FNL} = 0.98$, $P_{TNL} = 0.96$. Calculate the reactivity of the pile in **pcm** and in **dollars**, given $\beta_{eff} = 0.0065$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

The **Fermi Pile (CP-1)** was an approximately **spherical, ellipsoidal pile** of dimensions ~ **7.6 m (W) × 7.6 m (H) × 8.8 m (L)**, built on the *rackets court* beneath the Stagg Field stand at the University of Chicago. **(0.5 mark)**

**Construction:**
- The pile consisted of **~ 400 tonnes of high-purity graphite** in the form of bricks.
- **~ 6 tonnes of natural uranium metal** ("U-slugs" cast into cylindrical lumps) was inserted into small holes drilled through the graphite in a **lattice pattern** (square lattice, pitch ~ 21 cm). **(0.5 mark)**
- A **graphite reflector** of thickness ~ 50 cm surrounded the core to reduce neutron leakage. **(0.5 mark)**
- **Cadmium control rods** (Cd strips) were inserted into channels cut through the pile:
  - *Shim rods* for coarse control,
  - *Regulating rod* for fine power control,
  - *"Zip" safety rod* suspended by a rope, held by *George Weil* (originally intended to be held by *Herbert Anderson* — nicknamed "Zip"). **(1 mark)**
- **Neutron detectors** (BF₃ and Geiger counters) and **safety circuits** were installed to monitor flux.

**Working / Criticality Approach (2.5 marks):**
- On 2 December 1942, the team *withdrew control rods in small steps* and observed the **neutron count rate** on a pen recorder.
- After each withdrawal, the **period T** of the rising flux was measured and the **1/M rule** used to estimate inserted reactivity in cents.
- At **15:36 hrs**, the count rate rose on a *predictable exponential* and did not return to baseline — the pile had reached **k_eff = 1 (criticality)**.
- Fermi declared: *"The reaction is self-sustaining."* The "Zip" rod was then *manually dropped in* to shut the pile down safely.

**Role of Graphite Moderator:**
- Fission neutrons are born *fast* (mean energy ~ 2 MeV), but the fission cross-section of ²³⁵U is highest for **thermal** (~ 0.025 eV) neutrons.
- **Graphite**, with low mass number (A = 12) and small absorption cross-section (~ 4.5 mb), efficiently *slows down* neutrons via elastic scattering without absorbing them — increasing the **resonance escape probability (p)** and **thermal utilisation (f)**. **(0.5 mark)**

**Role of Cadmium Control Rods:**
- Cadmium has a *huge* thermal absorption cross-section (**Σ_a ≈ 113 cm⁻¹**, σ_a ≈ 2450 barns).
- Inserting Cd strips *captures* thermal neutrons, reducing the **thermal utilisation factor f** and hence **k_eff**.
- The "Zip" rod's gravity drop provides **passive safety shutdown** (precursor to modern **SCRAM**). **(0.5 mark)**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Derivation of Six-Factor Formula (4 marks):**

The effective multiplication factor in an *infinite* multiplying medium is:

$$ k_\infty = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f $$

For a *finite* reactor, the neutrons that **leak** must be accounted for. Two additional factors — fast non-leakage $P_{FNL}$ and thermal non-leakage $P_{TNL}$ — multiply the infinite-medium result to yield the **effective** multiplication factor:

$$ k_{eff} = \eta \, \varepsilon \, p \, f \, P_{FNL} \, P_{TNL} $$

| Factor | Physical meaning |
|---|---|
| $\eta$ | Neutrons produced per thermal neutron absorbed in fuel |
| $\varepsilon$ | Fast-fission factor (multiplication during slowing down) |
| $p$ | Resonance escape probability |
| $f$ | Thermal utilisation factor |
| $P_{FNL}$ | Probability that a fast neutron does not leak |
| $P_{TNL}$ | Probability that a thermal neutron does not leak |

**Numerical Calculation (3 marks):**

$$ k_{eff} = 1.34 \times 1.029 \times 0.94 \times 0.90 \times 0.98 \times 0.96 $$

Step-wise:

$$ 1.34 \times 1.029 = 1.3789 $$

$$ 1.3789 \times 0.94 = 1.2962 $$

$$ 1.2962 \times 0.90 = 1.1666 $$

$$ 1.1666 \times 0.98 = 1.1433 $$

$$ 1.1433 \times 0.96 = 1.0976 $$

Thus $k_{eff} \approx 1.0976$.

**Reactivity:**

$$ \rho = \frac{k_{eff} - 1}{k_{eff}} = \frac{1.0976 - 1}{1.0976} = \frac{0.0976}{1.0976} \approx 0.0889 $$

In **pcm**: $\rho \times 10^5 = 8890 \; pcm$.

In **dollars**: 

$$ \rho_{\$} = \frac{\rho}{\beta_{eff}} = \frac{0.0889}{0.0065} \approx 13.68 \; \$\; (\text{dollars}) $$

**This represents a deeply supercritical pile** (much more than 1 \$), which is why Fermi's team operated *well below* prompt critical and stopped rod withdrawal at $k_{eff} = 1$ rather than at this calculated maximum. The "infinite" calculation ignores the actual non-leakage in CP-1, which is why real $k_{eff} = 1$ in the operating pile.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** Explain the **reactivity control mechanisms** used in the Fermi Pile. With the help of a suitable diagram, describe how **cadmium shim rods**, the **regulating rod**, and the **"Zip" safety rod** together ensured safe operation. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **radiation monitoring instrumentation** used during Chicago Pile-1 operation. Explain the working of a **Geiger-Müller (GM) counter** and a **BF₃ proportional counter** with neat sketches. Why were indium foils also used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Reactivity Control in the Fermi Pile:**

The Fermi Pile had to maintain $k_{eff} = 1.000$ under varying conditions (temperature, fuel burn-up, xenon build-up in later versions). Three categories of control rods were used:

**1. Shim Rods (Cadmium strips) (2 marks):**
- Long cadmium sheets (~ 4 ft) inserted in horizontal channels.
- Provided *coarse* negative reactivity worth (~$−2$ to $−200$ cents).
- Used in the morning of 2 Dec 1942 to bring the pile *close to* critical from a *deep sub-critical* state.
- Removed in *small, controlled increments* — Fermi used a *verbal countdown* for each step.

**2. Regulating Rod (1.5 marks):**
- A *fine* control rod used to maintain *precise* criticality after the shim rods had been adjusted.
- Could be moved in increments of ~ 1 mm.
- Its motion tracked the *neutron count rate* on the pen recorder (linear amplifier output).

**3. Zip / Safety Rod (1.5 marks):**
- A cadmium sheet mounted on a *wooden* (low-neutron-absorption) frame, **suspended by a rope** over a pulley.
- *Gravity drop* on release → fell into the pile in ~ 2 seconds, providing enough negative reactivity to make the pile *deeply sub-critical* ($k_{eff} < 0.95$).
- This is the **ancestor of the modern SCRAM** (Safety Control Rod Activation Mechanism).
- The "Zip" name referred to the *zipping* sound of the rope sliding through the operator's gloved hand.

**Diagram of Control-Rod Arrangement:** See the **Section-4 Mermaid flowchart** above for the trip logic, and the block-level architecture for the rod-system interfaces.

**Why Three-Tier Control? (2 marks):**
- *Defence in Depth* — even if one rod sticks, the other two can shut down the pile.
- *Diversity* — shim (coarse), regulating (fine), safety (emergency) cover different time scales and reactivity magnitudes.
- *Inherent safety* — Zip rod relies on **gravity**, not on electrical or mechanical power.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Radiation Monitoring in the Fermi Pile:**

Three independent instrumentation channels were used to monitor neutron and gamma flux:

**1. Geiger-Müller (GM) Counter (2.5 marks):**
- A *gas-filled tube* (typically Ar + halogen quench) with a thin central anode wire at ~ +800 V.
- A single ionising event (gamma ray or charged particle) triggers a *Townsend avalanche* that produces a measurable pulse.
- **Working:** An incoming gamma ray ejects a photo-electron from the cathode → drift toward anode → multiplication in the high-field region near the wire → pulse across load resistor.
- **Output:** Pulse rate is proportional to radiation intensity (above the threshold).
- In CP-1, three GM tubes were placed around the pile and connected to *logarithmic count-rate meters* with pen recorders.

**Diagram (textual):**

```
  Cathode (cylinder) ——— biased at HV ~ −800 V
       |
       |  Anode wire (thin)  +HV
       |
   Ar + Halogen gas
   Incoming γ → photoelectron → avalanche → pulse
```

**2. BF₃ Proportional Counter (2.5 marks):**
- Filled with **Boron Trifluoride (BF₃) gas enriched in ¹⁰B**.
- Detects *thermal neutrons* via the reaction:

$$ ^{10}B + n \rightarrow ^{7}Li + \alpha \quad (Q = 2.31 \, MeV) $$

- The α-particle creates ionisation proportional to its energy → proportional output.
- Operates at ~ +600 V (in the *proportional region* of the gas-amplification curve).
- In CP-1, BF₃ counters gave the *fastest response* to neutron flux rise, and were the *primary* reactor control sensors.

**3. Indium Foil Activation (1 mark):**
- A piece of indium foil (¹¹⁵In) was placed inside the pile for a known time *t* and then removed.
- The reaction ¹¹⁵In(n,γ)¹¹⁶In (β⁻, half-life 54 min) was measured in a separate low-background lab with a **Geiger counter**.
- The *saturated activity* gave the *absolute thermal neutron flux* — a calibration standard.
- **Why?** GM and BF₃ counters were *relative* instruments; foils gave the *absolute* flux needed to compute k_eff accurately.

**Why three independent methods?**
- *Redundancy* — if one fails, the others confirm criticality.
- *Cross-calibration* — foils calibrate BF₃ and GM tubes.
- *Energy coverage* — GM detects gammas, BF₃ detects thermal neutrons, foils measure flux.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Confusing *k* and *k_∞*.** Always state whether the multiplication factor is for an *infinite* (no leakage) or *finite* (leaky) medium. The six-factor formula is for *finite* reactors; four-factor ($k_\infty$) is for *infinite* lattices.
> 2. **Forgetting $\Lambda/T$ in Inhour derivation.** Full marks only when both the prompt ($Λ/T$) and delayed ($\beta/(1+\lambda T)$) terms are present.
> 3. **Mixing up reactivity units.** 1 \$ = β_eff, *not* 1% Δk/k. Use cents (¢) for 1/100 of a dollar.
> 4. **Writing "Fermi built the first *atomic bomb*."** It was a *reactor*, not a bomb — a critical distinction. The bomb was the Manhattan Project's separate "Thin Man"/"Little Boy" effort.
> 5. **Not specifying the *type* of cross-section.** σ_a for cadmium is **2450 barns (thermal)**; for fast neutrons it drops sharply. Always state neutron energy regime.
> 6. **Skipping the sketch in 7-mark sub-parts.** KTU valuation policy requires a *neat labelled diagram* for full marks on "(a)" and "(b)" of a 14-marker.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Fermi Pile = CP-1**, first *self-sustaining* chain reaction, **2 Dec 1942**, University of Chicago.
- ✅ Fuel: **Natural uranium (metal)**, Moderator: **Graphite (~400 t)**, Reflector: **Graphite (~50 cm)**.
- ✅ Control: **Cadmium shim rods**, **Cd regulating rod**, **"Zip" safety rod (gravity drop)**.
- ✅ Monitoring: **Geiger-Müller (γ)**, **BF₃ proportional (n)**, **Indium foil activation (absolute flux)**.
- ✅ **Six-factor formula:** $k_{eff} = \eta \varepsilon p f P_{FNL} P_{TNL}$.
- ✅ **Reactivity:** $\rho = (k_{eff}-1)/k_{eff}$; **1 \$ = β_eff ≈ 0.0065** in thermal reactors.
- ✅ **Inhour eq. (one group):** $\rho = \Lambda/T + \beta_{eff}/(1+\lambda T)$.
- ✅ **1/M rule:** $T \approx 1/(\rho \lambda)$ — used by Fermi on 2 Dec 1942.
- ✅ **Always operate below 1 \$** to avoid *prompt criticality* and runaway power excursion.
- ✅ **Negative temperature coefficient** is the *inherent safety feature* of graphite-moderated piles.
- ✅ **SCRAM** concept originated from the Zip rod — *passive, gravity-driven shutdown*.
- ✅ **Three-fold redundancy** in monitoring (GM + BF₃ + Foil) is the *defence-in-depth* philosophy still followed by IAEA.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactor Core
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Reactor Core # 🟢 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Reactor Core:** The *reactor core* is the **central, active region** of a nuclear reactor where the **controlled, self-sustaining chain reaction of nuclear fission** takes place. It is a precisely engineered heterogeneous or homogeneous mixture of **nuclear fuel**, **moderator**, **coolant**, **cladding**, **control elements (poisons)**, and **structural materials**, arranged in a defined geometric lattice so as to achieve the desired neutron multiplication factor ($k$), power density, and fuel-cycle performance. The core is bounded externally by the **core barrel** and **reflector**, and its physical, thermal-hydraulic, and neutronic behaviour governs **every operational and safety parameter** of the reactor. **KTU Board Favourite Definition (memorize this wording):** > *"The reactor core is the heart of the nuclear reactor — a carefully designed lattice of fuel assemblies containing fissile material (e.g., $^{235}$U, $^{239}$Pu), surrounded by moderator, coolant, and control rods, in which the controlled fission chain reaction is maintained and useful heat is extracted."* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Picture — The "Burner on a Controlled Stove":** > Think of the reactor core as a **highly engineered charcoal kiln** with three critical layers: > > 1. **The Charcoal (Fuel)** — The $^{235}$U or $^{239}$Pu pellets are the "fuel" that "burns" (fissions) and releases enormous heat. > 2. **The Air Blower (Coolant)** — Like air fanning the charcoal, water, heavy water, or gas flows through the core carrying the heat away to make steam. > 3. **The Bricks & Spacers (Moderator & Structure)** — Like the brick walls that hold the charcoal in place and reflect heat back, the moderator (graphite, water, heavy water) slows down fast neutrons to sustain the chain reaction, while structural steel holds everything together. > 4. **The Damper (Control Rods)** — Like a damper/choke on a stove, control rods of boron or cadmium absorb neutrons and "turn down" the reaction when needed. > > Just as a stove must balance fuel, air, and geometry to burn steadily without going out or exploding, the reactor core must balance **fuel loading, moderation, cooling, and neutron absorption** to maintain $k = 1$ (criticality) safely. ## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Magnitude | |---|---|---| | Core thermal power (PWR) | $P_{th}$ | **~3000 MW(thermal)** for a large LWR | | Specific power | $P_{sp}$ | **~30–40 MW/tHM** | | Power density | $q'''$ | **~100 MW/m³** in LWRs | | Volumetric fuel fraction | $V_f$ | **~0.25–0.35** in LWRs | | Volumetric moderator fraction | $V_m$ | **~0.40–0.60** in LWRs | | Core height (active) | $H$ | **~3.66 m** (typical PWR) | | Core diameter (active) | $D$ | **~3.4 m** (typical PWR) | | Average linear heat rate | $q'$ | **~18–20 kW/m** | | Avogadro's number | $N_A$ | **$6.022 \times 10^{23}$ mol⁻¹** | | Energy per fission | $E_f$ | **~200 MeV = $3.2 \times 10^{-11}$ J** | ## 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 Module-3 Anchors) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Aspects of Reactor Core the Examiner routinely tests (mapped to Module 3 outcomes):** > - Core composition & material selection rationale > - Lattice geometry: square vs. triangular pitch > - Homogenization of heterogeneous media > - Neutron flux / power distribution (radial and axial) > - Power peaking factors > - Burnup, fuel management, refuelling > - Core reactivity coefficients > - Thermal-hydraulic limits (DNB, hot-channel factor) # 🟢 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Constituents of the Reactor Core — Engineering Logic The core is **never** just fuel. It is an *integrated thermo-neutronic-mechanical assembly*. Each constituent plays a deliberate role: | Constituent | Function | Typical Material | Why it matters | |---|---|---|---| | **Fuel** | Source of fission neutrons & energy | UO₂, MOX, (Th-²³²U) | Determines $k_\infty$ & burnup | | **Cladding** | Hermetic barrier between fuel & coolant | Zircaloy-4, M5, SS-304 | Prevents release of fission products | | **Moderator** | Slows fast neutrons to thermal energies | H₂O, D₂O, graphite, Be | Maintains high $\eta \cdot p \cdot f$ for thermal reactors | | **Coolant** | Removes fission heat | H₂O, D₂O, CO₂, He, Na | Limits fuel centre-line temperature | | **Control rods / poisons** | Absorb excess neutrons to control $k$ | B₄C, Ag-In-Cd, Hf | Reactivity control, scram | | **Reflector** | Returns leaking neutrons back to core | H₂O, D₂O, Be | Reduces neutron leakage | | **Structural** | Mechanical support | SS-304, SS-316, Zr-Nb | Holds core geometry under irradiation | | **Burnable absorbers** | Compensate excess reactivity at BOL | Gd₂O₃, Er₂O₃, IFBA | Flatten long-term power profile | ## 2.2 Lattice Geometry — The Heart of Core Design > [!NOTE] > **Why geometry matters:** The **lattice pitch** (centre-to-centre spacing of fuel rods) directly determines the **moderating ratio** experienced by neutrons born in the fuel. A widely spaced lattice has too few moderator atoms per neutron → under-moderated. Too tightly packed → excessive resonance absorption in $^{238}$U (self-shielding) and poor cooling. ### 2.2.1 Square Lattice Each fuel rod has **4 nearest neighbours**. Pitch-to-diameter ratio is usually defined as: $$P/D = \frac{\text{centre-to-centre pitch}}{\text{fuel rod outer diameter}}$$ For LWRs: $P/D \approx 1.3$ (typical range $1.1$–$1.5$). ### 2.2.2 Triangular (Hexagonal) Lattice Each rod has **6 nearest neighbours**. Common in fast reactors and some CANDU/PHWR designs. Same $P/D$ definition applies. ### 2.2.3 Volume Fractions (KTU high-yield) For a cylindrical fuel rod of diameter $D_f$ inside a lattice cell of side $a$ (square) or pitch $P$ (triangle): **Square lattice (water gap $a - D_f$):** $$V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{D_f}{a}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{1}{P/D}\right)^2$$ **Triangular lattice:** $$V_f = \frac{\pi}{2\sqrt{3}}\left(\frac{D_f}{P}\right)^2$$ Moderator volume fraction: $$V_m = 1 - V_f - V_{clad}$$ ## 2.3 Homogenization of Heterogeneous Core > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU favourite concept:** Real cores are **heterogeneous** (fuel lumped in rods, moderator in channels). For reactor-physics analysis we **homogenize** the lattice cell into an equivalent **homogeneous mixture** with effective cross-sections $\bar{\Sigma}_a$, $\bar{\Sigma}_f$, $\bar{\Sigma}_t$, $D$, etc. computed via **equivalence theory** or **collision-probability methods**. The fundamental result: $$\bar{\Sigma}_x = \frac{\sum_i \Sigma_{x,i} \, V_i}{\sum_i V_i} \quad \text{--- (volume-weighted, NOT exact!)}$$ > In practice, **equivalence theory** (e.g., Wigner-Seitz cell with $D$-equivalence) is used, since simple volume-weighting is wrong for strongly absorbing resonances. ## 2.4 Neutron Flux & Power Distribution in the Core ### 2.4.1 One-Group Diffusion in a Bare Cylindrical Core For a homogeneous bare cylinder of radius $R$ and height $H$, the one-group diffusion equation yields the **fundamental-mode flux**: $$\phi(r,z) = A \, J_0\!\left(\frac{2.405\, r}{R}\right) \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi z}{H}\right)$$ - $J_0$ is the Bessel function of the first kind (order zero). - Geometric buckling: $$B_g^2 = \left(\frac{2.405}{R}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\pi}{H}\right)^2$$ ### 2.4.2 Power Density $$q'''(\vec{r}) = E_f \, \Sigma_f(\vec{r}) \, \phi(\vec{r})$$ > This is the **single most important KTU formula** of Module 3. ### 2.4.3 Power Peaking Factors - **Radial peaking factor** $F_{xy} = \dfrac{q'''_{\max}}{q'''_{\text{avg}}}$ (radial plane) - **Axial peaking factor** $F_z = \dfrac{q'''_{\max}}{q'''_{\text{avg}}}$ (along axis) - **Total (hot-channel) peaking factor** $F_Q = F_{xy} \cdot F_z \cdot F_\Delta$ (engineering + nuclear) ## 2.5 Fuel Burnup Burnup quantifies energy extracted per unit mass of initial heavy metal: $$BU = \frac{\text{Total energy released from fuel}}{\text{Mass of initial heavy metal (IHM)}} \quad [\text{MWd/tHM}]$$ For a constant-power operation: $$BU(t) = \frac{P_{th}\, t}{M_{HM}} \quad [\text{MWd/tHM}]$$ Typical discharge burnup: **45,000–60,000 MWd/tHM** for LWRs. ## 2.6 Core Reactivity Coefficients A *reactivity coefficient* measures how $k$ changes with a feedback parameter: $$\alpha_X = \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial X}$$ | Coefficient | Symbol | Sign (typical) | Physical origin | |---|---|---|---| | Moderator temp. coeff. | $\alpha_{T_m}$ | Negative (LWR) | Reduced moderation with expansion | | Fuel temp. (Doppler) coeff. | $\alpha_{T_f}$ | Negative | Resonance broadening in $^{238}$U | | Void coefficient | $\alpha_V$ | Negative (LWR) / Positive (RBMK) | Loss of moderator | | Power coefficient | $\alpha_P$ | Negative (LWR) | Combined effect | ## 2.7 🟦 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize this table — every formula has appeared in past KTU ESE papers.** | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $q''' = E_f \Sigma_f \phi$ | Volumetric heat generation | W/m³ | | 2 | $P = \int_V q'''(\vec r)\, dV$ | Total core thermal power | W | | 3 | $\phi(r,z) = A J_0(2.405 r / R) \cos(\pi z / H)$ | Bare-reactor flux | n/cm²·s | | 4 | $B_g^2 = (2.405/R)^2 + (\pi/H)^2$ | Geometric buckling | cm⁻² | | 5 | $k = \eta f p \varepsilon P_{NL}$ | Four-factor formula | dimensionless | | 6 | $V_f = (\pi/4)(1/(P/D))^2$ | Square-lattice fuel fraction | – | | 7 | $V_f = (\pi/(2\sqrt{3}))(D_f/P)^2$ | Triangular-lattice fuel fraction | – | | 8 | $BU = P_{th} t / M_{HM}$ | Specific burnup | MWd/t | | 9 | $\rho = (k-1)/k$ | Reactivity | – or $\Delta k/k$ | | 10 | $F_Q = F_{xy} F_z F_\Delta$ | Total hot-channel factor | – | | 11 | $\alpha_T = \partial\rho/\partial T$ | Temperature coefficient | pcm/K | | 12 | $\bar{\Sigma}_x = \sum_i \Sigma_{x,i} V_i / \sum_i V_i$ | Volume-weighted cross-section | cm⁻¹ | | 13 | $q' = q''' \cdot A_f$ | Linear heat generation rate | W/m | | 14 | $q'' = q' / (\pi D_f)$ | Surface heat flux | W/m² | | 15 | $\tau_{th} = 1/(v \Sigma_a)$ | Neutron thermalization time | s | | 16 | $L^2 = D/\Sigma_a$ | Diffusion length squared | cm² | | 17 | $M^2 = L^2 + \tau_{th}$ | Migration area | cm² | > **Note on notation:** In all tables above, $\Sigma_x$ means macroscopic cross-section; the symbol `$\vert$` is intentionally avoided; use $\vert$ (or $\mid$) only outside tables when needed. ## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in industry:** Core designers at NPCIL, IGCAR, BARC, EDF, Westinghouse, Rosatom etc. spend *years* on the integrated thermo-neutronic-mechanical design of the core. The choices made here determine: > - **Reactor safety** (Doppler vs. void coefficient decides whether a reactor is intrinsically safe). > - **Economics** (higher burnup = more energy per kg of uranium = lower fuel cost). > - **Licensing** (peaking factors and DNB limits are NRC/AERB acceptance criteria). > - **Refuelling strategy** (annual vs. 18-month cycles; whole-core vs. out-in shuffles). # 🟢 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Geometric Buckling of a Finite Cylindrical Bare Core ### 3.1.1 Statement For a homogeneous bare cylinder of radius $R$ and extrapolated height $H_{ex}$, derive the **geometric buckling** $B_g^2$. ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Start with the one-group, steady-state diffusion equation in a critical (bare) reactor:** $$D \nabla^2 \phi(\vec r) + \nu \Sigma_f \phi(\vec r) = \Sigma_a \phi(\vec r)$$ **Step 2 — Define the material buckling $B_m^2$:** $$B_m^2 = \frac{\nu \Sigma_f - \Sigma_a}{D} = \frac{k_\infty - 1}{L^2}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange into a Helmholtz (eigenvalue) equation:** $$\nabla^2 \phi + B_m^2 \phi = 0$$ **Step 4 — Assume separation of variables in cylindrical coordinates $(r,\theta,z)$:** $$\phi(r,\theta,z) = R(r) \Theta(\theta) Z(z)$$ **Step 5 — The $\theta$ part:** By azimuthal symmetry, $\Theta(\theta) = \text{const}$ (or $\cos m\theta, \sin m\theta$; lowest mode $m=0$). **Step 6 — Axial part:** $Z(z) = \cos\!\left(\dfrac{\pi z}{H_{ex}}\right)$, with $Z(\pm H_{ex}/2) = 0$ (vacuum BC). Hence: $$\alpha_z^2 = \left(\frac{\pi}{H_{ex}}\right)^2$$ **Step 7 — Radial part:** Bessel's equation of order zero: $$\frac{1}{r}\frac{d}{dr}\!\left(r \frac{dR}{dr}\right) + \alpha_r^2 R = 0$$ The regular solution is $R(r) = J_0(\alpha_r r)$, with vacuum BC $J_0(\alpha_r R) = 0$. The first zero of $J_0$ is at $\alpha_r R = 2.4048$. $$\alpha_r^2 = \left(\frac{2.405}{R}\right)^2$$ **Step 8 — Eigenvalue condition (criticality):** $$B_m^2 = B_g^2 = \alpha_r^2 + \alpha_z^2$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{\,B_g^2 = \left(\frac{2.405}{R}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\pi}{H_{ex}}\right)^2\,}$$ **Step 9 — Criticality condition (keeps reactor critical):** $$k = \frac{\nu \Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a + D B_g^2} = 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad B_m^2 = B_g^2$$ **Physical meaning:** $B_g^2$ is the *smallest eigenvalue* of the Laplacian on the core domain. It quantifies how much the core "leaks" neutrons — **bigger volume ⇒ smaller $B_g^2$ ⇒ less leakage**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Density and Total Power in a Cylindrical Core ### 3.2.1 Statement A bare cylindrical PWR core of radius $R$ and height $H$ operates at thermal power $P_{th}$. Derive the **maximum-to-average power density ratio** in the radial direction. ### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Flux profile (radial):** $$\phi(r) = \phi_0 J_0\!\left(\frac{2.405\, r}{R}\right)$$ **Step 2 — Power density profile (using $q''' = E_f \Sigma_f \phi$):** $$q'''(r) = E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0 J_0\!\left(\frac{2.405\, r}{R}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Total thermal power (integrate over core volume):** $$P_{th} = \int_V q'''(\vec r) \, dV = E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0 \int_0^{H}\!\!dz \int_0^R\!\!\! \int_0^{2\pi} J_0\!\left(\frac{2.405\,r}{R}\right) r\, d\theta\, dr$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the $\theta$ integral** ($\int_0^{2\pi} d\theta = 2\pi$): $$P_{th} = 2\pi E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0 \, H \int_0^R J_0\!\left(\frac{2.405\,r}{R}\right) r\, dr$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the radial integral** using $\int_0^R J_0(\alpha r) r\, dr = R J_1(\alpha R)/\alpha$ with $\alpha = 2.405/R$ and $J_1(2.405) = 0.5191$: $$\int_0^R J_0\!\left(\frac{2.405\,r}{R}\right) r\, dr = \frac{R^2}{2.405} \cdot 0.5191 = 0.2158\, R^2$$ **Step 6 — Substitute back:** $$P_{th} = 2\pi E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0 \, H \cdot 0.2158\, R^2 = 1.356\, E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0 \, R^2 H$$ **Step 7 — Define the *average* power density:** $$\bar{q}''' = \frac{P_{th}}{\pi R^2 H} = \frac{1.356\, E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\pi}$$ **Step 8 — Maximum-to-average ratio:** Maximum occurs at $r = 0$ where $J_0(0) = 1$: $$F_{xy} = \frac{q'''_{\max}}{\bar{q}'''} = \frac{E_f \Sigma_f \phi_0}{\bar{q}'''} = \frac{\pi}{1.356} \approx 2.32$$ $$\boxed{\,F_{xy} \approx 2.32 \text{ (bare cylindrical core, fundamental mode)}\,}$$ > **Examiner's note:** If the core is *reflected*, $F_{xy}$ drops to **~1.4–1.6** because the reflector "flattens" the radial flux. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Square-Lattice Fuel Volume Fraction ### 3.3.1 Statement A square lattice has fuel rods of outer diameter $D_f$ on a pitch $a$. Derive $V_f$. ### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Unit-cell area:** $$A_{cell} = a^2$$ **Step 2 — Fuel-rod cross-sectional area (cylindrical):** $$A_f = \frac{\pi D_f^2}{4}$$ **Step 3 — Fuel volume fraction (area fraction in 2-D unit cell):** $$V_f = \frac{A_f}{A_{cell}} = \frac{\pi D_f^2 / 4}{a^2} = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{D_f}{a}\right)^2$$ **Step 4 — Express in terms of $P/D$ ratio:** Since $P/D \equiv a/D_f$, we have $D_f/a = 1/(P/D)$: $$\boxed{\,V_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\left(\frac{1}{P/D}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4(P/D)^2}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical check:** For $P/D = 1.33$ (typical PWR): $$V_f = \frac{\pi}{4 \times 1.33^2} = \frac{3.1416}{7.0756} \approx 0.444$$ But this is **fuel-rod area including cladding**. If cladding OD is fixed and fuel-pellet OD is smaller, subtract cladding area to get the **fuel-pellet (active) volume fraction**. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Linear Heat Rate and Surface Heat Flux ### 3.4.1 Statement Given $q'''$ (W/m³) and fuel-pellet diameter $D_p$, derive $q'$ and $q''$. ### 3.4.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of fuel pellet:** $$A_p = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4}$$ **Step 2 — Linear heat generation rate (heat per unit length of rod):** $$q' = q''' \cdot A_p = q''' \cdot \frac{\pi D_p^2}{4} \quad [\text{W/m}]$$ **Step 3 — Surface heat flux (heat per unit area at pellet surface):** $$q'' = \frac{q'}{\pi D_p} = \frac{q''' \cdot \pi D_p^2 / 4}{\pi D_p} = \frac{q''' D_p}{4} \quad [\text{W/m}^2]$$ **Step 4 — Numerical example:** If $q''' = 4 \times 10^8$ W/m³ and $D_p = 9$ mm $= 9 \times 10^{-3}$ m: $$q' = \frac{\pi (9 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4} \times 4 \times 10^8 = 25{,}446 \text{ W/m} \approx 25.4 \text{ kW/m}$$ $$q'' = \frac{4 \times 10^8 \times 9 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 9 \times 10^5 \text{ W/m}^2$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU design limit:** PWR designers enforce $q' \le 18$ kW/m (or so) and $q'' \le 1.5 \times 10^6$ W/m² to keep DNB margin safe. ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Specific Power and Fuel-Management Parameter ### 3.5.1 Statement A 3000 MW(thermal) PWR core has 100 t of UO₂ heavy metal. Calculate the specific power and the time to reach 45,000 MWd/t burnup. ### 3.5.2 Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Specific power definition:** $$P_{sp} = \frac{P_{th}}{M_{HM}}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $P_{th} = 3000$ MW, $M_{HM} = 100$ t: $$P_{sp} = \frac{3000 \text{ MW}}{100 \text{ t}} = 30 \text{ MW/t}$$ **Step 3 — Time to reach 45,000 MWd/t:** $$t = \frac{BU \cdot M_{HM}}{P_{th}} = \frac{45{,}000 \text{ MWd/t} \times 100 \text{ t}}{3000 \text{ MW}}$$ **Step 4 — Compute:** $$t = \frac{4{,}500{,}000 \text{ MWd}}{3000 \text{ MW}} = 1500 \text{ days} \approx 4.1 \text{ years}$$ **Step 5 — Check with full-power days:** A typical LWR operates at ~90% capacity factor → calendar time $\approx 4.1/0.9 \approx 4.6$ years, giving **18–24 month refuelling cycles** with batch shuffles. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Design Variables vs. Reactor Type | Variable | PWR (LWR) | BWR (LWR) | PHWR (CANDU) | LMFBR (Fast) | HTGR | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Fuel | 3–5% $^{235}$U in UO₂ | 3–5% $^{235}$U in UO₂ | Natural U in UO₂ | 15–20% Pu in MOX | 8–20% $^{235}$U in UCO/ThO₂ | | Moderator | H₂O (in-core) | H₂O (separate) | D₂O (calandria) | None (fast) | Graphite | | Coolant | H₂O (~325°C, 155 bar) | H₂O (~285°C, 72 bar) | D₂O (~310°C, 100 bar) | Liquid Na (~550°C) | He (~750°C, 70 bar) | | Lattice | Square | Square | Square (long) | Triangular (hex) | Pebble bed / prismatic | | $V_m / V_f$ | ~1.5–2 | ~1.5–2 | ~15–25 | ~0 | ~0 | | Burnup (MWd/t) | 45,000–60,000 | 45,000–55,000 | 7,000–10,000 | 80,000–150,000 | 100,000+ | | Conversion ratio | ~0.5 | ~0.5 | ~0.7 | ~1.0–1.2 (breeder) | ~0.6–0.8 | # 🟢 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied > All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters.
