topics.title: Fuel Handling
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fuel Handling

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fuel Handling** in a nuclear power plant refers to the entire integrated set of mechanical, radiological, and thermal operations involved in the **receipt, storage, transfer, loading, unloading, inspection, cooling, transport, and accounting** of nuclear fuel assemblies — beginning from the arrival of fresh fuel at the plant and terminating at the disposal/reprocessing of spent fuel.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMET412)** curriculum, fuel handling is treated as a **core operational discipline** bridging reactor physics, thermal-hydraulics, mechanical design, and radiation protection engineering. It is functionally classified into:

| Phase | Operation | Location |
|---|---|---|
| Phase I | Fresh fuel receipt & inspection | Receipt bay (clean area) |
| Phase II | Fresh fuel storage | New Fuel Vault (NFV) |
| Phase III | Fuel transfer to reactor | Fuel transfer canal |
| Phase IV | Loading/Unloading reactor | Reactor refuelling cavity |
| Phase V | Spent fuel cooling | Spent Fuel Storage Pool (SFSP) |
| Phase VI | Spent fuel shipping/disposal | Dry cask / reprocessing plant |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Pharmacy" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy:**
> Imagine a hospital pharmacy handling radioactive medicines (e.g., radiopharmaceuticals). The workflow is:
> 1. **Receiving** sealed bottles (fresh fuel arrives in shielded containers)
> 2. **Storing** in locked cabinets (new fuel vault with criticality safety)
> 3. **Dispensing** via shielded trolleys into patient rooms (transfer to reactor)
> 4. **Retrieving** used bottles back to a lead-shielded waste room (spent fuel to pool)
> 5. **Long-term decay storage** in a deep cellar (spent fuel pool) before disposal

In a nuclear plant, the **"radiation intensity"** of fuel decays naturally with time (governed by **radioactive half-life**), but unlike ordinary medicines, **spent fuel remains dangerously radioactive for thousands of years**. Hence the need for engineered, multi-barrier, passively safe handling systems.

## 1.3 Why Fuel Handling is a Critical Engineering Discipline

- **High specific activity:** Fresh UO₂ fuel (4% enriched) has **α-activity ≈ 10⁵ Bq/g**; spent fuel (after 40 GWd/t burnup) has **β-γ activity ≈ 10¹⁶ Bq per assembly**.
- **Criticality risk:** Improper arrangement can cause an **uncontrolled nuclear chain reaction** → criticality accident (e.g., Tokaimura, 1999).
- **Decay heat:** A 1000 MWe reactor's spent fuel generates ≈ **2.5 MW of decay heat** immediately after shutdown → potential for fuel melt if cooling fails.
- **Radiological hazard:** Without shielding, dose rates at contact with a fresh spent assembly exceed **10⁵ Sv/hr** (fatal in seconds).

> [!WARNING]
> **Safety Imperative:** Fuel handling operations are governed by the **ALARA principle** (As Low As Reasonably Achievable) and must comply with **AERB Safety Code (AERB/NPP-01)** and **IAEA SSR-6** regulations.

---

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Bolded Constants)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Mass of one PHWR fuel bundle | m | **≈ 23 kg (natural U)** |
| Mass of one PWR fuel assembly | m | **≈ 660 kg** |
| Mass of one BWR fuel assembly | m | **≈ 320 kg** |
| Spent Fuel Pool capacity (typical) | N | **~10 reactor cores** |
| Pool water depth | h | **≥ 6 m (criticality safe)** |
| Pool water temperature | T | **≤ 50 °C** |
| Specific heat of water | cₚ | **4.186 kJ/kg·K** |
| Pool boron concentration (PWR) | C_B | **≥ 2000 ppm** |
| Dose rate target at cask surface | — | **< 2 mSv/hr** |
| Decay heat fraction (immediately after shutdown) | f | **≈ 6–7% of operating power** |

---

## 1.5 Refuelling Strategies (Three Principal Schemes)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme — Syllabus Highlight:**
> The three standard refuelling strategies must be studied comparatively:
>
> **(a) Batch Refuelling:** Entire core replaced at once (e.g., marine reactors, Magnox).
>
> **(b) Scatter (On-Power) Refuelling:** Fuel continuously shuffled/replaced during full-power operation (CANDU/PHWR).
>
> **(c) Partial (Zone) Refuelling:** A fraction of assemblies replaced every cycle (PWR, BWR — outage-based).

| Scheme | Reactor Type | Outage Required | Flux Flatness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Batch | Magnox, HTGR, Naval | Full shutdown | Best |
| Scatter/On-Power | PHWR (CANDU) | None | Excellent |
| Partial Zone | PWR, BWR | ~25 days every 18 months | Moderate |

---

## 1.6 Topic Roadmap for Module 3

```
Fresh Fuel → Receipt Bay → New Fuel Vault → Transfer Canal → Reactor
                                    ↓
                            Spent Fuel Pool → Cooling (5–10 yr)
                                    ↓
                          Dry Storage Cask / Reprocessing Plant
```

This roadmap will be expanded progressively in Sections 2–4.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Logic

### Stage I — Fresh Fuel Receipt and Inspection

**Objective:** Receive fuel from manufacturer (NFC, Hyderabad in India; Westinghouse, Framatome globally) with full chain-of-custody verification.

**Procedure:**
1. **Arrival in Type-B(U) shipping cask** (e.g., capacity 2 PWR assemblies).
2. **Radiological survey** of cask surface — verify dose rate < **2 mSv/hr**.
3. **Cask opened inside a controlled ventilation cell** (negative pressure, HEPA-filtered exhaust).
4. **Visual inspection** of each assembly — check for clad damage, pellet cracks, bowing.
5. **Identity verification** using engraved serial numbers + RFID tags.
6. **Documentation** in the **Fuel Management System (FMS)** database.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Concept — Criticality Safety:**
> Fresh fuel (even at 4% enrichment) must NEVER be accumulated in arrays exceeding **critical mass** in air-equivalent moderation conditions. The standard control: **5×5 array rule** ("5-by-5 rule") — never place more than 5×5 assemblies in dry storage without neutron absorber panels.

---

### Stage II — Fresh Fuel Storage (New Fuel Vault / NFV)

- **Design basis:** Storing fresh fuel in a configuration that **cannot achieve criticality under any credible condition**, including flooding with water (postulated accident).
- **Construction:** Reinforced concrete vault with **borated aluminum plates** or **borosilicate glass (Pyrex) racks** providing neutron absorption.
- **Criticality control equation:**

$$
k_{eff} \le 0.95 \quad (\text{under all credible conditions including double contingency})
$$

- **Effective multiplication factor** depends on geometry, enrichment, moderation, and reflection:

$$
k_{eff} = \frac{\nu \Sigma_f}{\Sigma_a}
$$

where $\nu$ = neutrons per fission, $\Sigma_f$ = macroscopic fission cross-section, $\Sigma_a$ = macroscopic absorption cross-section.

---

### Stage III — Fuel Transfer to Reactor

Two configurations exist:

**(a) PWR/BWR — Offline Refuelling:**
- Reactor vessel head removed → refuelling cavity flooded with borated water.
- **Fuel Handling Machine (FHM)** — a polar crane-mounted telescoping mast with a grappling head — lifts the spent assembly from the core, transfers it to the spent fuel pool, then places a fresh assembly into the vacated core position.

**(b) PHWR/CANDU — On-Power Refuelling:**
- Two on-power fuelling machines operate simultaneously at opposite ends of a horizontal fuel channel.
- **Fuelling Machine (Forward)** — pushes fresh bundle in.
- **Fuelling Machine (Aft)** — receives the spent bundle.
- Reactor **remains at full power** during refuelling — net reactivity insertion = **0**.

---

### Stage IV — Spent Fuel Pool (SFP) Operations

**Design requirements (10 CFR 50 / AERB equivalent):**
1. Pool depth ≥ **6 m** (water shield to reduce γ-dose).
2. Borated water (typically **≥ 2000 ppm boron**) for criticality control.
3. Passive cooling capability for **72 hours** without active power.
4. Active cooling: pumps + heat exchangers sized to remove peak decay heat.
5. Pool divided into **"clean" and "decay"** sections by a gate.

**Decay heat generated by spent fuel** (most critical parameter for SHP cooling design):

After shutdown, total decay power follows:

$$
P_d(t) = P_d(0) \cdot f(t)
$$

For times **< 10⁴ seconds** after shutdown, the **ANS Standard 5.1 / 1979 ANSI/ANS-5.1** expression is used:

$$
P_d(t) = 0.0622 \cdot P_0 \cdot \left[ t^{-0.2} - (t+T_0)^{-0.2} \right]
$$

where:
- $P_0$ = pre-shutdown thermal power (W)
- $t$ = time after shutdown (seconds)
- $T_0$ = total irradiation time (seconds)

For **long-term cooling** ($t > 10^6$ s, i.e., > 12 days), simpler asymptotic formula:

$$
P_d(t) \approx 0.0622 \cdot P_0 \cdot t^{-0.2}
$$

**At 1 second after shutdown:** $P_d \approx 6.7\%$ of $P_0$
**At 1 hour after shutdown:** $P_d \approx 1.5\%$ of $P_0$
**At 1 day after shutdown:** $P_d \approx 0.4\%$ of $P_0$
**At 1 year after shutdown:** $P_d \approx 0.03\%$ of $P_0$

---

### Stage V — Spent Fuel Transportation

**Two cask categories:**

| Type | Use | Capacity |
|---|---|---|
| Type A | Low-specific-activity | Limited |
| **Type B(U)** | Unilateral approval, robust | Several PWR assemblies |
| Type B(M) | Multilateral approval (special features) | Several PWR assemblies |

**Design criteria (IAEA SSR-6):**
- Must survive **9 m drop test** onto unyielding surface.
- **800 °C fire exposure for 30 minutes.**
- **Immersion under 200 m water head** (accident scenario).

**Shielding requirement:**
Lead (γ) + depleted uranium + neutron-absorbing liners (for criticality).
Typical loaded cask mass: **80–120 tonnes**.

---

### Stage VI — Long-Term Storage / Reprocessing

**Option A — Wet Storage (Pool):** Extend SFP life (need reracking with high-density racks → borated stainless-steel).

**Option B — Dry Cask Storage:** Spent fuel in helium-filled sealed canisters inside concrete/steel overpacks. Used when pools are full.

**Option C — Reprocessing:** PUREX process (Plutonium Uranium Redox EXtraction) recovers U and Pu for MOX fuel fabrication.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Refuelling Outage Optimization:** Nuclear plants earn revenue by being online. Fuel handling is the critical-path activity — efficiency saves ₹2–5 crore/day in lost revenue (typical Indian plant revenue).
2. **Spent Fuel Pool Capacity Planning:** Determines whether a plant needs dry cask construction (₹200–500 crore investment).
3. **Criticality Safety Licensing:** Required before any storage expansion (AERB approval in India).
4. **Decommissioning Planning:** Long-term spent fuel inventory determines waste repository sizing (e.g., Finnish Onkalo facility).
5. **Defence Applications:** Naval reactor refuelling occurs once every 10–25 years — entire submarine reactor compartment is replaced.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation I — Decay Heat Equation from Fission Product Inventory

### Step 1: Physical Foundation

When a **fissile nucleus** undergoes fission, it produces **two fission product nuclei** plus **neutrons and γ-rays**. The fission products are neutron-rich and undergo **β⁻ decay** chains. The energy released by these decays (after reactor shutdown) is the **decay heat**.

Each fission product nuclide $i$ has a decay constant $\lambda_i$ and releases energy $E_i$ per decay. The instantaneous decay power from nuclide $i$ is:

$$
P_i(t) = \lambda_i \, E_i \, N_i(t)
$$

### Step 2: Build-Up of a Fission Product Nuclide During Reactor Operation

During steady reactor operation at neutron flux $\phi$ with macroscopic fission cross-section $\Sigma_f$, the production rate of fission product $i$ is:

$$
\text{Production rate of } i = Y_i \, \Sigma_f \, \phi \quad (\text{nuclei/cm}^3 \cdot s)
$$

where $Y_i$ = cumulative fission yield for nuclide $i$.

The destruction (decay) rate is $\lambda_i N_i$. The differential balance during operation:

$$
\frac{dN_i}{dt} = Y_i \, \Sigma_f \, \phi - \lambda_i \, N_i
$$

### Step 3: Solve the Differential Equation (During Irradiation)

This is a first-order linear ODE. Assuming $N_i(0) = 0$ (initially none present) and constant flux:

$$
N_i(t) = \frac{Y_i \, \Sigma_f \, \phi}{\lambda_i} \left[ 1 - e^{-\lambda_i t} \right]
$$

**Logic:** The term in brackets rises from 0 to 1 as the nuclide approaches **secular equilibrium** with its production rate. Short-lived nuclides saturate quickly; long-lived ones accumulate linearly.

### Step 4: Decay After Shutdown

When the reactor shuts down at $t = T_0$, the source term vanishes:

$$
\frac{dN_i}{dt} = -\lambda_i \, N_i \quad \Rightarrow \quad N_i(t) = N_i(T_0) \, e^{-\lambda_i (t-T_0)}
$$

where $t$ is the post-shutdown time, and $N_i(T_0)$ is from Step 3.

### Step 5: Compute Total Decay Heat Power

$$
P_d(t) = \sum_i \lambda_i \, E_i \, N_i(T_0) \, e^{-\lambda_i (t-T_0)}
$$

Using the saturation value $N_i^{sat} = Y_i \Sigma_f \phi / \lambda_i$, and substituting $P_0 = E_f \Sigma_f \phi$ (operating power per unit volume, where $E_f$ ≈ 200 MeV per fission):

$$
P_d(t) = P_0 \sum_i \frac{Y_i \, E_i}{E_f} \left[ 1 - e^{-\lambda_i T_0} \right] \, e^{-\lambda_i (t-T_0)}
$$

### Step 6: Approximation to ANS Empirical Form

For a distribution of fission products with typical $\lambda_i$ averaging around $\bar{\lambda}$ such that the sum reduces to a power-law:

$$
P_d(t) \approx 0.0622 \, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - (t+T_0)^{-0.2} \right]
$$

**Logic of the power-law $-0.2$ exponent:** Empirical fit to thousands of nuclides; the $-0.2$ slope arises because at short times many short-lived isotopes dominate, and at long times only a few long-lived ones remain — the resulting decay "curve" is shallower than a single exponential.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — SFP Heat Load Calculation

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (KTU-style, 7 marks):**
> A 1000 MWe PWR operates at thermal power 3000 MW. After a planned shutdown for refuelling, fresh fuel assemblies are loaded while spent assemblies are transferred to the Spent Fuel Pool. Calculate:
> (a) Decay heat generated **1 hour** and **1 day** after shutdown.
> (b) Pool cooling water flow required to keep ΔT = 10 K.

### Solution — Part (a): Decay Heat

Given:
- $P_0 = 3000$ MW $= 3 \times 10^9$ W
- $T_0 \gg t$ (assume long irradiation), so use simplified form:

$$
P_d(t) \approx 0.0622 \, P_0 \, t^{-0.2}
$$

**At t = 1 hour = 3600 s:**

$$
P_d = 0.0622 \times 3 \times 10^9 \times (3600)^{-0.2}
$$

Compute $(3600)^{0.2}$:

$$
\log_{10}(3600) = 3.5563, \quad 0.2 \times 3.5563 = 0.7113, \quad 10^{0.7113} = 5.142
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_d = 0.0622 \times 3 \times 10^9 / 5.142 = 3.63 \times 10^7 \, W \approx 36.3 \, MW
$$

**At t = 1 day = 86400 s:**

$$
(86400)^{0.2} : \log_{10}(86400) = 4.9365, \quad 0.2 \times 4.9365 = 0.9873, \quad 10^{0.9873} = 9.713
$$

$$
P_d = 0.0622 \times 3 \times 10^9 / 9.713 = 1.92 \times 10^7 \, W \approx 19.2 \, MW
$$

### Solution — Part (b): Required Cooling Flow

Using:

$$
\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \, c_p \, \Delta T
$$

With $\dot{Q} = 36.3$ MW (worst-case at 1 hr), $\Delta T = 10$ K, $c_p = 4186$ J/kg·K:

$$
\dot{m} = \frac{36.3 \times 10^6}{4186 \times 10} = \frac{36.3 \times 10^6}{41860} = 867 \, kg/s
$$

Converting to volumetric flow (ρ = 1000 kg/m³):

$$
\dot{V} = 867 / 1000 = 0.867 \, m^3/s \approx 3130 \, m^3/hr
$$

**Engineering Conclusion:** A large SFP cooling pump (≈ **0.9 m³/s**) is required even hours after shutdown — this is why **passive cooling redundancy** is mandatory.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — Criticality Safety in New Fuel Vault

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A NFV contains PWR fresh fuel assemblies at 4.5% enrichment. Each assembly has $N_{U235}$ ≈ 0.18 kg of U-235. Determine the minimum number of assemblies that can be safely stored in a dry, moderated configuration.

### Solution

Critical mass of U-235 in water-moderated geometry ≈ **37 kg** (sphere, unreflected).

Assuming water moderation via postulated flood accident:

$$
N_{max} = \frac{M_{critical}}{N_{U235} / \text{asm}} = \frac{37}{0.18} \approx 205 \, \text{assemblies}
$$

Apply **5×5 rule** (administrative control): Never exceed 25 in a single storage block.

**Apply double-contingency principle:** Use **boron-stabilized storage racks** so that even if two independent failures occur (e.g., one rack collapses + flood), $k_{eff} \le 0.95$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Refuelling Schemes

| Parameter | Batch (Magnox) | Scatter (CANDU) | Partial Zone (PWR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cycle length | 5–10 years | Continuous | 12–24 months |
| Refuelling outage | Full plant shutdown | None | ~25 days |
| Downtime fraction | ~10% (per cycle) | ~0% | ~7–10% |
| Shuffling complexity | None | High | Moderate |
| Flux flatness achievable | Best (but transient) | Excellent | Moderate |
| Capacity factor | 70–80% | > 90% | 85–92% |
| Typical example | Wylfa, UK | Kaiga, Kakrapar | Tarapur, Kudankulam |
| Criticality risk during refuel | Low | High (continuous) | Medium |

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Derivation II — Heat Load on SFP Per Assembly

### Step 1: Single Assembly Power After Discharge

For an assembly operating at linear heat rate $q' = 18$ kW/m over a length $L = 4.2$ m:

$$
P_{asm} = q' \times L = 18 \times 4.2 = 75.6 \, kW \text{ (during operation)}
$$

### Step 2: Decay Heat Per Assembly

Immediately after shutdown:

$$
P_{d,asm}(0) \approx 0.067 \times P_{asm} = 5.07 \, kW
$$

After 1 day:

$$
P_{d,asm}(1d) \approx 0.004 \times 75.6 = 0.302 \, kW
$$

### Step 3: Total Pool Load for $N$ Assemblies

$$
P_{pool}(t) = N \cdot P_{d,asm}(t)
$$

For $N = 200$ assemblies after 1 day:

$$
P_{pool} = 200 \times 0.302 = 60.4 \, kW
$$

**Engineering application:** This 60.4 kW load, although small, must be **continuously removed** 24/7 for years — defining the long-term pool cooler duty.

---

## 3.6 Conversion Logic Summary (Step Reference)

| From | To | Logic |
|---|---|---|
| MeV/fission → Joules | J | Multiply by $1.602 \times 10^{-13}$ |
| Reactor thermal → electric | — | $\eta \approx 33\%$; so 1 MWe ≈ 3 MWt |
| Burnup MWd/kg → GWd/t | — | Multiply by 1 |
| Activity Bq → Ci | Ci | 1 Ci = $3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq |
| Dose rate Sv → rem | rem | 1 Sv = 100 rem |
| Power (W) → Heat (J) | J | $Q = P \times t$ |

## 4.1 End-to-End Fuel Handling Block Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MANUFACTURER NFC Hyderabad] --> B[FRESH FUEL SHIPMENT Type B U Cask]
    B --> C[RECEIPT BAY Radiation Survey Dose Rate Check]
    C --> D[VISUAL INSPECTION Clad Damage Pellet Cracks]
    D --> E[NEW FUEL VAULT NFV Borated Racks Criticality Safe]
    E --> F[FUEL TRANSFER CANAL Underwater Borated]
    F --> G[REACTOR VESSEL Refuelling Cavity]
    G --> H[IN-CORE OPERATION 18 to 24 Months]
    H --> I[SPENT FUEL ASSEMBLY Decay Heat 6 to 7 percent]
    I --> J[SPENT FUEL POOL SFP Borated Water Cooling]
    J --> K[COOLING 5 to 10 Years Active Pumps HX]
    K --> L{REPROCESS OR DIRECT DISPOSAL}
    L --> M[DRY CASK STORAGE Helium Atmosphere]
    L --> N[PUREX REPROCESSING U and Pu Recovery]
    N --> O[MOX FUEL FABRICATION]
    M --> P[GEOLOGICAL REPOSITORY Deep Underground]
    O --> Q[REACTOR Recycle of Pu]
```

**Diagram Annotation Logic:**
- Node **A → B → C → D → E**: Fresh fuel lifecycle (Phase I, II).
- Nodes **F → G → H → I**: Reactor service and discharge (Phase III, IV).
- Nodes **J → K → L → M/N/O/P**: Back-end fuel cycle (Phase V, VI).
- Diamond **L**: Decision branch — reprocess (closed fuel cycle) or direct disposal (open cycle).

---

## 4.2 PWR Refuelling Cavity — Component Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SPENT_FUEL_POOL[SPENT FUEL POOL SFP]
        SP1[Decay Section High Heat]
        SP2[Storage Section High Density Racks]
        SP3[Resin Clean Up System]
    end

    subgraph CAVITY[REFUEL CAVITY Above Reactor Vessel]
        RV[Reactor Vessel Open Head]
        FH1[Fuel Handling Machine FHM Polar Crane]
        UP1[Upender Tool Optional]
    end

    subgraph GATE[TRANSFER GATE]
        TG[Gate Valve Interlock]
    end

    SP1 --- TG
    TG --- RV
    FH1 -.-> RV
    FH1 -.-> SP1
```

**Operational Logic:**
- The **Transfer Gate** (TG) is opened only after water levels equalize and radiation monitoring confirms water clarity.
- The **Fuel Handling Machine (FHM)** moves on a **polar rail** above the pool, with a mast that telescopes down to the assembly top nozzle.
- The **Upender Tool (UP1)** rotates the assembly from horizontal (shipping) to vertical (reactor) orientation.

---

## 4.3 On-Power Refuelling Schematic — CANDU/PHWR

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FC[FUEL CHANNEL Horizontal Pressure Tube] --> FM1[FUELLING MACHINE FRONT End New Fuel Push]
    FM1 --> FC
    FC --> FM2[FUELLING MACHINE AFT End Spent Fuel Receive]
    FM2 --> SNF[Spent Fuel Bay]
    FM1 --> NFA[New Fuel Stack]
    NFA --> FM1
```

**Operational Logic:**
- Two **fuelling machines** clamp onto opposite ends of a single horizontal pressure tube.
- A new bundle is **pushed in** from one end; the spent bundle is **pushed out** from the other (think of a pneumatic capsule system).
- Reactivity balance: **fresh fuel reactivity = spent fuel reactivity deficit** → no net reactivity insertion.

---

## 4.4 Safety Interlock Logic Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Operator Issues Move Command]) --> chk1{Fuel Assembly ID Verified}
    chk1 -- NO --> block1[BLOCK MOVE Criticality Prevention]
    chk1 -- YES --> chk2{Water Level above Safe Mark}
    chk2 -- NO --> block2[BLOCK MOVE Loss of Shield]
    chk2 -- YES --> chk3{Dose Rate within Limit}
    chk3 -- NO --> block3[BLOCK MOVE ALARA Exceeded]
    chk3 -- YES --> chk4{Destination Cell Empty}
    chk4 -- NO --> block4[BLOCK MOVE Conflict Avoidance]
    chk4 -- YES --> ALLOW[ALLOW MOVE Execute]
    ALLOW --> log[Log in FMS Database]
```

**Operational Logic:** Each block represents a **defence-in-depth interlock**. Any single failure cannot lead to an unsafe move. This is the standard **reactor protection philosophy** applied to mechanical fuel handling.

---

## 4.5 Cooling Water Circuit — Spent Fuel Pool

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SFP[Spent Fuel Pool] --> SPUMP[SFP Pumps]
    SPUMP --> HX[Decay Heat Exchangers]
    HX --> CC[Component Cooling Water CCW]
    HX --> SFP
    SFP --> QC[Pool Water Clean Up Resin Skid]
    QC --> SFP
    SFP -. Borated Makeup .-> DEMIN[Demineralized Water System]
    DEMIN -. Boric Acid .-> SFP
```

**Engineering Logic:**
- The pool is a **closed loop**; cooling water passes through HX to dump heat to the **Component Cooling Water** system, which in turn transfers heat to the **Essential Service Water (ESW)** reservoir or cooling tower.
- **Loss-of-cooling accident (LOCA)** is mitigated by passive inventory: 6 m depth provides **~30 hours** of safe cooling without makeup.
