# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A consists of 3-mark questions requiring **precise, 3–5 sentence answers**. Memorize the bold terms; examiners award full marks only for crisp, syllabus-aligned responses.

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
**"Differentiate between Low-Level Waste (LLW) and High-Level Waste (HLW) with respect to specific activity, shielding requirement, and disposal method." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | **LLW** | **HLW** |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Activity** | < **4 GBq/tonne** | > **10⁴ GBq/tonne** |
| **Shielding** | Minimal — gloves and steel drum | Massive — metres of concrete/lead + remote handling |
| **Disposal** | **Near-surface landfill** (engineered modules) | **Deep geological repository** (> 500 m depth) |
| **Examples** | Contaminated paper, gloves, ion-exchange resins | **Spent fuel assemblies**, vitrified fission products |
| **Cooling** | Not required | **Active cooling** for years to decades |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall (KTU Examiner's Note):** Students often confuse the **specific activity threshold** by writing "10⁴ GBq/m³" instead of "tonne". The correct unit is **per tonne (t)**, not per m³. Mark deduction: **−0.5 mark** per error.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Past Year Style)]**
**"What is the basis of classifying radioactive waste into LLW, ILW, and HLW? State the IAEA's specific activity threshold for each class." (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The classification is based on the **radiological hazard potential** of the waste, quantified by four key parameters:

1. **Specific activity** (activity per unit mass, **Bq/tonne**)
2. **Half-life** of the contained radionuclides
3. **Decay heat** generation rate (**W/m³** or **kW/m³**)
4. **Required shielding and isolation** duration

**IAEA Thresholds:**

- **LLW:** Specific activity < **4 GBq/tonne** ; Decay heat < **2 kW/m³**
- **ILW:** Specific activity between **4 GBq/tonne** and HLW threshold ; Decay heat < **2 kW/m³**
- **HLW:** Specific activity > **10⁴ GBq/tonne** ; Decay heat ≥ **2 kW/m³**

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Do **not** write "HLW has activity > 4 GBq/tonne" — that is the **LLW/ILW boundary**, not the ILW/HLW boundary. Always state the upper threshold for HLW. Common deduction: **−1 mark** for confusing boundaries.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part B Module-4 questions typically test **classification criteria + quantitative decay heat calculation + engineering disposal pathway**. The two sub-parts (a) and (b) carry **7 marks each**. Memorize the formulae; marks are awarded for **each algebraic step**, not just the final answer.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper – Set A)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the classification of radioactive waste into Low-Level Waste (LLW), Intermediate-Level Waste (ILW), and High-Level Waste (HLW). Discuss the criteria used for classification and provide two examples for each class.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A spent fuel assembly of mass **500 kg** is removed from a reactor with an initial decay heat of **25 kW/tonne** at 1 day after shutdown. Using the **Way-Wigner approximation**, calculate the decay heat after **1 year** of cooling. Will the assembly still be classified as HLW at this point? (Take HLW threshold as **2 kW/m³** and spent fuel density as **2.5 tonnes/m³**.)

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question A**

#### **Part (a) — Detailed Explanation [7 Marks]**

**[1 mark] Introduction:**
Radioactive waste is the by-product of nuclear reactor operations, fuel reprocessing, medical isotope use, and decommissioning activities. Because different waste streams pose vastly different levels of radiological hazard, the **IAEA** (and consequently **AERB, India**) classifies them into three primary categories.

**[2 marks] Classification Criteria (the four pillars):**
1. **Specific activity** $A_s = A_{total}/m$ in **Bq/tonne** — the most direct measure of hazard.
2. **Half-life** $t_{1/2}$ of dominant radionuclides — determines how long the waste remains dangerous.
3. **Decay heat** power $P$ in **kW/m³** — drives the HLW/ILW distinction.
4. **Required isolation time** — engineering feasibility of safe storage.

**[3 marks] The Three Classes (with examples):**

**1. Low-Level Waste (LLW)** — $A_s$ < **4 GBq/tonne**, decay heat < 2 kW/m³, dominated by short-lived isotopes ($t_{1/2}$ < 30 yr). Examples:
  - *Contaminated paper, gloves, and lab glassware from reactor maintenance*
  - *Used air filters and ion-exchange resins from secondary coolant systems*

**2. Intermediate-Level Waste (ILW)** — $A_s$ between **4 GBq/tonne** and the HLW threshold, decay heat < 2 kW/m³, half-life 30 – 500 yr. Requires concrete shielding. Examples:
  - *Spent ion-exchange resins from primary coolant cleanup*
  - *Decommissioned control rods and neutron detector assemblies*

**3. High-Level Waste (HLW)** — $A_s$ > **10⁴ GBq/tonne**, decay heat > **2 kW/m³**, half-lives from 10³ to 10⁶ years. Examples:
  - *Spent (irradiated) nuclear fuel assemblies from the reactor core*
  - *Vitrified fission product blocks from the PUREX reprocessing plant*

**[1 mark] Concluding statement:**
The classification enables regulators to apply **proportionate engineering controls** — neither over-engineering LLW disposal nor under-protecting the public from HLW.

---

#### **Part (b) — Quantitative Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Mass of spent fuel: $m = 500$ kg $= 0.5$ tonnes
- Decay heat at $t_0 = 1$ day: $P_0 = 25$ kW/tonne
- Time of interest: $t = 1$ year
- HLW threshold: $P_{HLW} = 2$ kW/m³
- Density of spent fuel: $\rho = 2.5$ tonnes/m³
- Way-Wigner relation: $P(t) = P_0 \left( \dfrac{t_0}{t} \right)^{0.2}$

**Step 1 [1 mark]: Convert $t$ to seconds (KTU expects this!).**

$$t = 1 \text{ year} = 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600 = 3.156 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$$

**Step 2 [1 mark]: Apply the Way-Wigner formula.**

$$P(1 \text{ yr}) = 25 \left( \frac{86{,}400}{3.156 \times 10^7} \right)^{0.2} \text{ kW/tonne}$$

**Step 3 [1 mark]: Compute the time ratio.**

$$\frac{86{,}400}{3.156 \times 10^7} = 2.738 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]: Raise to the 0.2 power.**

$$(2.738 \times 10^{-3})^{0.2} = e^{0.2 \cdot \ln(2.738 \times 10^{-3})}$$

$$\ln(2.738 \times 10^{-3}) = -5.901$$

$$e^{0.2 \cdot (-5.901)} = e^{-1.180} = 0.307$$

**Step 5 [1 mark]: Multiply by $P_0$.**

$$P(1 \text{ yr}) = 25 \times 0.307 = 7.68 \text{ kW/tonne}$$

**Step 6 [1 mark]: Convert to kW/m³ to compare with the HLW threshold.**

Volume of 0.5 tonnes of spent fuel:

$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{0.5 \text{ t}}{2.5 \text{ t/m}^3} = 0.2 \text{ m}^3$$

Decay heat per unit volume:

$$P_{vol} = \frac{7.68 \text{ kW/tonne} \times 0.5 \text{ t}}{0.2 \text{ m}^3} = \frac{3.84 \text{ kW}}{0.2 \text{ m}^3} = 19.2 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

**Step 7 [1 mark]: Compare with HLW threshold and conclude.**

$$P_{vol} = 19.2 \text{ kW/m}^3 \gg 2 \text{ kW/m}^3 = P_{HLW}$$

$$\boxed{\text{The spent fuel is still HLW after 1 year of cooling, requiring active cooling and massive shielding.}}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model Paper – Set B)]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the sources, isotopic composition, and radiological hazards associated with **High-Level Waste (HLW)**. Explain why HLW requires deep geological disposal with a suitable example of a proposed repository concept.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A radioactive waste package has an initial activity of **10¹² Bq** of **Cesium-137** (half-life = **30.17 years**). Calculate:
  (i) The activity after **100 years** of storage.
  (ii) The number of half-lives elapsed.
  (iii) Comment on whether the waste can be reclassified as LLW after this period, given the LLW threshold of **4 GBq/tonne** and a package mass of **2 tonnes**.

---

### **MODEL ANSWER — Question B**

#### **Part (a) — HLW Sources and Disposal [7 Marks]**

**[1.5 marks] Sources of HLW:**
- **Spent nuclear fuel** discharged from the reactor core (~25 tonnes per GW·e-year)
- **Liquid high-level waste** from the **PUREX reprocessing process** containing separated fission products and minor actinides
- **Vitrified waste blocks** — borosilicate glass logs encapsulating the calcined fission products

**[1.5 marks] Isotopic composition (key radionuclides):**

| Nuclide | Half-life | Hazard Type |
|---|---|---|
| $^{137}$Cs | 30.17 yr | Beta-gamma emitter, heat source |
| $^{90}$Sr | 28.8 yr | Bone-seeking beta emitter |
| $^{239}$Pu | 24,100 yr | Alpha emitter, long-term toxicity |
| $^{129}$I | 1.57 × 10⁷ yr | Longest-lived, mobile in groundwater |
| $^{237}$Np | 2.14 × 10⁶ yr | Alpha emitter, geologically mobile |

**[2 marks] Radiological hazards:**
- **Intense external gamma radiation** during the first 100–500 years (from $^{137}$Cs and $^{90}$Sr)
- **Long-term radiotoxicity** beyond 1000 years (from actinides $^{239}$Pu, $^{237}$Np, $^{241}$Am)
- **Decay heat** that can melt containment if not dissipated
- **Potential groundwater contamination** if containment is breached

**[2 marks] Deep geological disposal reasoning:**
Because HLW must be isolated for **100,000 to 1,000,000 years** — longer than any human civilization or engineered structure can be guaranteed to last — the only scientifically accepted solution is **deep geological disposal** in stable rock formations (granite, salt, clay, or tuff) at depths of **500–1000 m**. The concept relies on a **multi-barrier system**:

1. **Vitrified waste form** (borosilicate glass)
2. **Stainless steel or copper canister**
3. **Bentonite clay buffer** (low permeability, high sorption)
4. **Geological host rock** (natural barrier)

**Example:** The **Onkalo repository** in Finland (operational ~2025) in crystalline granite bedrock at 400 m depth; the **Waste Isolation Pilot Plant (WIPP)** in the USA for transuranic waste in salt beds.

---

#### **Part (b) — Quantitative Cesium-137 Decay [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Initial activity $A_0 = 10^{12}$ Bq
- Half-life $t_{1/2} = 30.17$ years
- Time elapsed $t = 100$ years
- LLW threshold: $A_s = 4$ GBq/tonne $= 4 \times 10^9$ Bq/tonne
- Package mass $m = 2$ tonnes

**Step 1 [1 mark]: Calculate the number of half-lives elapsed.**

$$n = \frac{t}{t_{1/2}} = \frac{100}{30.17} = 3.314$$

**Step 2 [1.5 marks]: Calculate the activity after 100 years.**

Using the master decay equation:

$$A(100) = A_0 \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{n} = 10^{12} \times (0.5)^{3.314}$$

Compute the exponent:

$$(0.5)^{3.314} = e^{3.314 \cdot \ln(0.5)} = e^{3.314 \cdot (-0.6931)} = e^{-2.297} = 0.1005$$

Therefore:

$$A(100) = 10^{12} \times 0.1005 = 1.005 \times 10^{11} \text{ Bq}$$

**Step 3 [1.5 marks]: Calculate the specific activity.**

$$A_s = \frac{A(100)}{m} = \frac{1.005 \times 10^{11} \text{ Bq}}{2 \text{ tonnes}} = 5.025 \times 10^{10} \text{ Bq/tonne}$$

**Step 4 [1 mark]: Convert to GBq/tonne for comparison.**

$$A_s = \frac{5.025 \times 10^{10}}{10^9} = 50.25 \text{ GBq/tonne}$$

**Step 5 [1.5 marks]: Compare with LLW threshold and conclude.**

$$A_s = 50.25 \text{ GBq/tonne} \gg 4 \text{ GBq/tonne} = A_{LLW}$$

$$\boxed{\text{The waste CANNOT be reclassified as LLW after 100 years.}}$$

It remains **ILW** (since specific activity is above the LLW threshold but the dominant isotope is short-lived enough that no cooling is needed). It would need to decay for approximately **another 200 years** (≈ 7 half-lives total) before crossing the LLW threshold.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common mistake is to compare $A(100) = 10^{11}$ Bq directly with $4 \times 10^9$ Bq/tonne without computing specific activity. This leads to a false conclusion that the waste is now LLW because $10^{11} > 4 \times 10^9$. The **correct comparison** is **specific activity** ($A_{total}/m$), not raw activity. Mark deduction: **−2 marks** for this conceptual error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before entering the KTU exam hall.**

### **The Three Classes — One-Line Definitions**
- 🔹 **LLW:** Specific activity < **4 GBq/t** ; near-surface disposal ; minimal shielding
- 🔹 **ILW:** Specific activity **4 GBq/t to 10⁴ GBq/t** ; engineered vault disposal ; concrete shielding
- 🔹 **HLW:** Specific activity > **10⁴ GBq/t** AND/OR decay heat > **2 kW/m³** ; deep geological disposal ; massive shielding + active cooling

### **The Four Classification Criteria (Memorize the Order)**
1. **Specific activity** (Bq/tonne)
2. **Half-life** of dominant isotopes
3. **Decay heat** (kW/m³)
4. **Required isolation time**

### **Key Numericals to Remember**
- **Way-Wigner decay heat:** $P(t) = P_0 \left( t_0/t \right)^{0.2}$
- **Master decay law:** $A(t) = A_0 (1/2)^{t/t_{1/2}}$
- **7% rule:** Decay heat immediately after shutdown ≈ **7% of operating thermal power**
- **10-half-life rule:** Waste safe for unrestricted release after **10 half-lives** (activity drops to 0.1%)

### **Critical Half-Lives to Memorize**
- $^{137}$Cs: **30.17 yr** (ILW/LLW driver)
- $^{90}$Sr: **28.8 yr** (bone-seeking)
- $^{239}$Pu: **24,100 yr** (HLW long-term hazard)
- $^{129}$I: **1.57 × 10⁷ yr** (longest-lived)
- $^{60}$Co: **5.27 yr** (medical/industrial ILW)

### **Examples to Memorize for Each Class**
- **LLW:** Gloves, paper, air filters, lab glassware
- **ILW:** Spent resins, decommissioned control rods, sealed Co-60 sources
- **HLW:** Spent fuel assemblies, vitrified waste blocks from PUREX

### **Disposal Pathways to Remember**
- **LLW** → Near-surface modules (Tarapur, India)
- **ILW** → Engineered concrete vaults (50–300 m depth)
- **HLW** → Deep geological repositories (> 500 m, e.g., Onkalo Finland, Yucca Mountain USA)

### **Common KTU Exam Traps**
- ❌ Confusing **specific activity** units: must be **Bq/tonne** (not Bq/m³)
- ❌ Forgetting to **convert years to seconds** in the Way-Wigner formula
- ❌ Comparing **raw activity** with the LLW threshold instead of **specific activity**
- ❌ Writing the Way-Wigner exponent as **−0.2** (it is +0.2 when the time ratio is inverted, but the relation $P \propto t^{-0.2}$ is the standard form)
- ❌ Claiming HLW can be disposed of in near-surface landfills (it cannot — always deep geological)

> **Best of luck with your KTU 2024 PEMET412 examinations! Master the formulas, internalize the classification logic, and practice one numerical problem from each class before the exam day.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Treatment and Conditioning of Nuclear Waste - Incineration, Compaction, Cementation, Vitrification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Treatment and Conditioning of Nuclear Waste - Incineration, Compaction, Cementation, Vitrification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Nuclear Waste Treatment and Conditioning — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Treatment and Conditioning of Nuclear Waste* refers to the integrated set of operations that transform raw radioactive waste (in liquid, solid, or gaseous form) into a **stable, passively safe, and disposal-ready waste form**. *Treatment* involves processes that alter the physical, chemical, or radiological character of the waste (e.g., incineration, compaction), while *Conditioning* refers to embedding the treated waste into a solid, durable matrix (e.g., cement, glass, bitumen) inside a qualified container that meets regulatory transport and disposal requirements.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the four canonical techniques are:

| # | Method | Classification | Typical Waste Stream |
|---|--------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Incineration** | Thermal Treatment | Combustible LLW/ILW (paper, plastics, organics, ion-exchange resins) |
| 2 | **Compaction** | Mechanical Treatment | Solid LLW (metals, plastics, rubble, clothing) |
| 3 | **Cementation** | Conditioning (Immobilization) | Aqueous LLW liquids, sludges, ashes, ion-exchange resins |
| 4 | **Vitrification** | Conditioning (Immobilization) | High-Level Waste (HLW) liquor from reprocessing |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Lunchbox Packing" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of radioactive waste like a leaking, messy, dangerous lunch you must throw away safely.** You have three problems: it is **too bulky** to fit in the bin, it **smells bad and reacts** (chemical/radiological reactivity), and you must **lock it inside a sealed wrapper** so it can never leak out and harm anyone — for **thousands of years**.
>
> - **Incineration** = *Burning the leftover food* to ash → volume shrinks 20–100× and reactive organics vanish.
> - **Compaction** = *Crushing the empty cans and boxes* with a hydraulic press → volume shrinks 3–10×.
> - **Cementation** = *Packing the ash into a concrete drum* like sealing leftovers in a hard cement block.
> - **Vitrification** = *Melting the most dangerous leftovers into a glass marble* — the most permanent "lock" humans can engineer.
>
> Together, these four methods convert **messy, hazardous waste** into a **small, stable, leak-proof package** suitable for geological disposal.

## 1.3 The Five-Step Waste Management Chain (Context Setting)

The four methods studied here sit within the global nuclear waste management hierarchy:

1. **Pretreatment** — collection, segregation, decontamination.
2. **Treatment** — *Incineration, Compaction* (volume reduction & chemical stabilization).
3. **Conditioning** — *Cementation, Vitrification* (immobilization into durable matrix).
4. **Storage** — interim monitored storage in engineered facilities.
5. **Disposal** — deep geological repository (e.g., 500 m underground).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Metric for All Four Methods: Volume Reduction Factor (VRF)**
> $$\text{VRF} = \frac{V_{\text{initial}}}{V_{\text{final}}}$$
> A higher VRF is always desirable because it directly reduces the **repository footprint**, **transportation cost**, and **long-term surveillance burden**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 INCINERATION — Thermal Volume Reduction by Combustion

### Principle
Incineration uses **controlled high-temperature combustion (typically 800 °C – 1200 °C)** in an excess-oxygen (or pyrolytic starved-air) environment to oxidize combustible radioactive waste into **inert ash, flue gas, and off-gas particulates**. Radionuclides (except tritium, carbon-14, and noble gases) largely report to the ash phase, which is then conditioned downstream (usually by cementation or vitrification).

### Process Stages
- **Drying Zone** (~100–200 °C): Free moisture evaporates.
- **Pyrolysis / Devolatilization Zone** (200–600 °C): Volatiles and tars released; requires secondary combustion.
- **Char Combustion Zone** (600–1000 °C): Fixed carbon + char burns to CO₂ and ash.
- **Afterburner / Secondary Combustion Chamber** (≥ 1000 °C, residence time ≥ 2 s, **Turbulence Factor (T.F.) ≥ 0.5 s·K**, ensuring ≥ 99.99 % DRE of organics, per IAEA standards).

### Off-Gas Treatment Train
Scrubber → HEPA filter → Activated Carbon → Stack Monitoring (C-14, I-131, particulates).

### Why Incineration?
- **Volume reduction** of combustible LLW is typically **20:1 to 100:1**; **mass reduction** 5:1 to 20:1.
- Destroys **pathogens, PCBs, oils, and organic complexing agents**.
- Converts reactive organics (e.g., tributyl phosphate, EDTA) into CO₂ + H₂O.

## 2.2 COMPACTION — Mechanical Volume Reduction

### Principle
Solid waste is placed inside a high-tonnage **hydraulic press** (typically 50 – 2500 tonnes force) inside a pre-positioned drum or directly into a 200 L/400 L waste drum, compressing the material to a fraction of its original volume.

### Types of Compactors
- **Low-Pressure Compactor (LPC):** 5–50 tonnes force → Volume Reduction Factor (VRF) ~ 2:1 to 5:1, used for soft LLW.
- **Super-Compactor (High-Pressure Compactor, HPC):** 1000–2500 tonnes force → VRF ~ 5:1 to 10:1, used for metallic waste, rubble, mixed solid LLW. The compacted "puck" is then placed inside an overpack drum.
- **In-Drum Compactor:** Force applied directly inside the final 200 L drum → eliminates repackaging.

### Engineering Considerations
- **Segmentation Requirement:** Items > 60 mm in any dimension should be pre-cut (sheared) to avoid puncturing the drum and to ensure uniform load distribution.
- **Free-Liquid Prohibition:** Liquids are forbidden inside compaction drums (IAEA SSR-6 transport regulations) — must be solidified first.
- **Dose Rate Limitation:** Surface dose rate of the drum after compaction must remain < 2 mSv/h (IAEA transport limit).

## 2.3 CEMENTATION — Conditioning by Cement Matrix Immobilization

### Principle
Cementation (also called *cement solidification* or *grouting*) mixes the waste (liquid, sludge, or ash) with **Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC)** and water to form a **monolithic, mechanically rigid solid**. The hydration reaction binds free liquid into chemically combined water within the Calcium-Silicate-Hydrate (C-S-H) gel matrix.

### Core Chemical Reaction
$$2\,\text{Ca}_3\text{SiO}_5 + 7\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Ca}_3\text{Si}_2\text{O}_7\cdot 4\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3\,\text{Ca(OH)}_2$$
(C₃S belite hydration producing C-S-H gel + portlandite)

### Critical Mix-Design Parameters
- **Water-to-Cement (w/c) Ratio:** Optimum ≈ **0.40** (range 0.35–0.50). Higher w/c → higher porosity, higher leachability, lower compressive strength.
- **Waste Loading (WL):** 5–35 % by weight of dry cement for LLW liquids; up to 45 % for sludges.
- **Additives:** Fly ash, blast-furnace slag, silica fume, and bentonite improve **chemical durability**, **radiation resistance**, and reduce **leach rate**.
- **Compressive Strength Target:** ≥ **3.5 MPa** after 28 days (per IAEA TRS-401 and most national regulators).

### Why Cementation?
- Lowest cost conditioning technique; ambient-temperature process.
- Good **radiation shielding**; non-flammable; chemically stable in pH 12.5 (alkaline passivation of steel).
- Compatible with most inorganic radionuclides (Cs-137, Sr-90, Co-60).
- **Limitations:** High leach rate for anions (³⁶Cl, ¹²⁹I, TcO₄⁻); exothermic heat during cure can crack monolith if waste contains reactive metals (Al, Mg).

## 2.4 VITRIFICATION — Conditioning by Glass Matrix Immobilization

### Principle
Vitrification is the most robust conditioning technique and is the **worldwide benchmark for High-Level Waste (HLW) from spent fuel reprocessing**. The waste oxides are melted with **borosilicate glass frit** at **1050 °C – 1200 °C** to form an amorphous (non-crystalline) glass that dissolves the radionuclide oxides into its disordered Si–O network. The molten glass is poured into a **stainless-steel canister (typically 400 L)** where it solidifies.

### Standard Borosilicate Glass Composition
| Oxide | wt % |
|-------|------|
| SiO₂ | 45–55 |
| B₂O₃ | 10–15 |
| Na₂O | 8–12 |
| CaO + MgO | 5–10 |
| Al₂O₃ | 3–7 |
| Waste Oxide Loading | 15–30 |

### Why Borosilicate Glass?
- High chemical durability (low leach rate ~ 10⁻⁷ g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹ for Cs/Sr).
- High thermal stability (glass transition T_g ≈ 500–550 °C; can self-cool for decades).
- Can accommodate a wide range of waste compositions (soda-lime-silica + borate modifiers).
- **Limitation:** Glass is metastable; over very long periods (> 10⁵ years) it can devitrify, and at temperatures > T_g it may crystallize.

### Industrial Processes
- **French AVM (Atelier de Vitrification de Marcoule) / AVH process** — continuous rotary calciner + induction-heated metal melter.
- **Russian EP-500** — Joule-heated ceramic melter.
- **UK Windscale SGHWR** — two-stage calcination + glass pouring.
- **US Hanford WTP** — Joule-heated melter (largest in the world).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table compiles every formula you must memorize for a KTU 2024 question on this topic.** Note the use of `\vert` instead of `|` to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Used In |
|---|---------------------|------------|----------------------|---------|
| 1 | Volume Reduction Factor | $\text{VRF} = \dfrac{V_i}{V_f}$ | 2 – 100 (dimensionless) | All 4 methods |
| 2 | Mass Reduction Factor | $\text{MRF} = \dfrac{M_i}{M_f}$ | 5 – 20 | Incineration |
| 3 | Destruction & Removal Efficiency (DRE) | $\text{DRE} = 1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}$ | $\geq 99.99\,\%$ for organics | Incineration |
| 4 | Destruction Efficiency (turbulence factor) | $T.F. = \tau \cdot T \geq 0.5$ s·K | $>1000$ °C, $>2$ s | Incineration |
| 5 | Compaction Force / Pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | 50 – 2500 tonnes-force, 10 – 100 MPa | Compaction |
| 6 | Compressive Strength of cement | $\sigma_c = \dfrac{F_{\max}}{A}$ | $\geq 3.5$ MPa at 28 days | Cementation |
| 7 | Water/Cement Ratio | $w/c = \dfrac{m_w}{m_c}$ | Optimum $= 0.40$ | Cementation |
| 8 | Waste Loading | $\text{WL} = \dfrac{m_{\text{waste}}}{m_{\text{waste}} + m_{\text{cement}}}$ | 5 – 35 % (LLW), 15 – 30 % (HLW glass) | Cementation / Vitrification |
| 9 | Density of cement waste form | $\rho_{\text{cem}} \approx 1.8$ – $2.2$ g/cm³ | — | Cementation |
| 10 | Density of borosilicate glass | $\rho_{\text{glass}} \approx 2.7$ g/cm³ | — | Vitrification |
| 11 | Glass transition temperature | $T_g \approx 500$ – $550$ °C | Borosilicate | Vitrification |
| 12 | Leach rate (incremental) | $LR_i = \dfrac{a_n - a_{n-1}}{(t_n - t_{n-1}) \cdot SA} \cdot \dfrac{V}{S}$ | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-7}$ g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹ | Cementation / Vitrification |
| 13 | Leaching index | $L_i = -\log_{10}(D_e)$ | $L_i \geq 6$ for HLW glass | Vitrification |
| 14 | Activity in conditioned form | $A_f = A_i \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Decay before conditioning | All methods |
| 15 | Activity concentration in monolith | $C_A = \dfrac{A_{\text{waste}}}{V_{\text{monolith}}}$ | Bq/m³ | Cementation / Vitrification |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering utility of these formulas:** VRF drives **repository footprint**; DRE & T.F. drive **regulatory licensing** of incinerators; w/c and WL drive **leach-rate safety case**; $T_g$ drives **thermal margin** of HLW canisters during interim storage.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Volume Reduction Factor (VRF) and Its Engineering Meaning

The **Volume Reduction Factor (VRF)** is the dimensionless ratio of the initial (raw) volume of waste to the final (conditioned) volume of the waste form. It is the single most quoted figure in any KTU answer on treatment/conditioning.

**Step 1 — Define the volumes.**
Let $V_i$ be the volume of raw radioactive waste as generated (loose, unpackaged) and $V_f$ be the volume of the same radionuclide inventory after it is immobilized inside the final waste form (compacted drum, cement monolith, or glass canister).

**Step 2 — Write the ratio.**

$$\text{VRF} = \frac{V_i}{V_f}$$

**Step 3 — Express as a percentage of volume saved (the "savings fraction").**

$$\%\,\text{Volume Saved} = \left(1 - \frac{V_f}{V_i}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{\text{VRF}}\right) \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Cross-check with illustrative numerical examples.**

| Method | $V_i$ (raw) | $V_f$ (conditioned) | VRF | % Volume Saved |
|--------|------------|---------------------|-----|----------------|
| Compaction (LPC, soft waste) | 200 L | 80 L | 2.5 | 60 % |
| Compaction (Super-compactor) | 200 L | 25 L | 8.0 | 87.5 % |
| Incineration (paper/plastic) | 1000 L | 25 L | 40 | 97.5 % |
| Cementation (ash, w/c = 0.40) | 100 L liquid | 95 L monolith | 1.05 | 4.7 % (actually slight **increase** — must add cement volume) |
| Vitrification (HLW liquor) | 1000 L | 110 L glass | 9.1 | 89 % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** Cementation of liquids actually *increases* total mass/volume because of added cement + water. The VRF for cementation of liquids is therefore *less than 1*; its value is in **immobilization & leach resistance**, not in volume reduction. This is a very common KTU pitfall.

## 3.2 Derivation — Destruction & Removal Efficiency (DRE) of an Incinerator

The DRE is the regulatory metric for the destruction of organic hazardous constituents (and, in nuclear context, complexing agents like EDTA, TBP, and ion-exchange resins).

**Step 1 — Define inlet and outlet mass concentrations.**

$$\text{DRE} = \left(1 - \frac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100\,\%$$

where $C_{\text{in}}$ is the mass concentration (or mass feed rate) of the principal organic hazardous constituent (POHC) entering the combustion chamber, and $C_{\text{out}}$ is its concentration in the stack gas after complete off-gas treatment.

**Step 2 — Apply the IAQA / IAEA "Turbulence Factor" (T.F.) criterion.**

$$T.F. = \tau \cdot T$$

where $\tau$ is the gas residence time in the secondary combustion chamber (s) and $T$ is the absolute temperature (K). The IAEA requires:

$$T.F. = \tau \cdot T \;\geq\; 0.5 \;\text{s·K} \quad \text{AND} \quad T \;\geq\; 1273\;\text{K} \quad (1000\,°\text{C})$$

**Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example.**

An incinerator is designed to burn 200 kg/h of paper/plastic waste contaminated with 0.5 wt% tributyl phosphate (TBP). The TBP leaving the stack must be < 0.02 mg/Nm³ at an off-gas flow of 1500 Nm³/h. Verify the DRE.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{m}_{\text{TBP,in}} &= 200 \;\text{kg/h} \times 0.005 = 1.0 \;\text{kg/h} = 10^6 \;\text{mg/h} \\[4pt]
\dot{m}_{\text{TBP,out}} &= 0.02 \;\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{Nm}^3} \times 1500 \;\tfrac{\text{Nm}^3}{\text{h}} = 30 \;\tfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{h}} \\[4pt]
\text{DRE} &= \left(1 - \frac{30}{10^6}\right) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= \left(1 - 3 \times 10^{-5}\right) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
&= 99.997\,\% \;\;\geq\;\; 99.99\,\% \quad \checkmark \;\text{(compliant with IAEA)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Comment.**
A DRE of 99.99 % corresponds to a "Six-Nines" decontamination; this is the standard for licensing a radioactive waste incinerator in most nuclear regulatory regimes (US EPA, AERB India, CEA France, IAEA RS-G-1.7).

## 3.3 Derivation — Water/Cement Ratio vs Compressive Strength (Abrams' Law, Adapted)

For cement-based waste forms, the **compressive strength** $\sigma_c$ after 28 days of curing is empirically governed by **Abrams' Law**:

$$\sigma_c = \dfrac{A}{B^{\,w/c}}$$

where $A$ and $B$ are empirical constants depending on cement type and curing conditions. For Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC):

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &\approx 96\;\text{MPa} \\
B &\approx 7.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked example for the KTU examination:**
Compute $\sigma_c$ at $w/c = 0.40$ and at $w/c = 0.60$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma_c(0.40) &= \frac{96}{7.0^{\,0.40}} = \frac{96}{2.177} \approx 44.1\;\text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\sigma_c(0.60) &= \frac{96}{7.0^{\,0.60}} = \frac{96}{3.215} \approx 29.9\;\text{MPa} \\[4pt]
\text{Ratio:}\;\frac{\sigma_c(0.40)}{\sigma_c(0.60)} &= \frac{44.1}{29.9} \approx 1.48
\end{aligned}
$$

This **48 % increase in strength** when w/c is dropped from 0.60 → 0.40 is precisely why nuclear waste cementation recipes always specify $w/c \approx 0.40$ — and why adding too much waste liquor (which contains the water) destroys the monolith.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule for KTU answers:** If a question gives waste liquor density and you are asked to design a cement mix, compute the *mass of water* in the waste, set w/c = 0.40, and back-calculate the required mass of cement and waste loading.

## 3.4 Derivation — Glass Loading Constraint from Viscosity

In vitrification, the **maximum waste loading** is limited by the requirement that the molten glass **viscosity $\eta$** at pouring temperature (1050 °C) stays in the range **10 – 100 Pa·s** — too fluid and crystallization occurs on cooling; too viscous and the melter cannot be poured.

An empirical model is the **Vienna University-of-Technology / Pizzarello-type relation** (simplified):

$$\log_{10}\eta \;=\; A \;+\; \frac{B}{T - T_0}$$

where $A, B, T_0$ depend on composition. The constraint is:

$$10 \;\text{Pa·s} \;\leq\; \eta(1050\,°\text{C}) \;\leq\; 100 \;\text{Pa·s}$$

Typical nuclear waste glass: 25 wt% waste loading ⇒ $\eta \approx 30$ Pa·s at 1050 °C, which is comfortably processable.

> [!TIP]
> **Step-by-step analytical check for KTU exam:**
> 1. Compute the total mass of waste oxide.
> 2. Divide by the sum of waste oxide + glass frit to get waste loading.
> 3. Use the viscosity model to ensure $\eta$ lies in [10, 100] Pa·s at pouring.
> 4. Confirm the canister can dissipate decay heat below $T_g$ (≈ 500 °C) during interim storage.

## 3.5 Master Comparison Matrix — The Four Methods at a Glance

| Property | Incineration | Compaction | Cementation | Vitrification |
|----------|--------------|------------|-------------|----------------|
| **Treatment vs Conditioning** | Treatment | Treatment | Conditioning | Conditioning |
| **Waste form produced** | Ash + off-gas | Compacted puck/drum | Cement monolith | Borosilicate glass in SS canister |
| **Volume reduction (VRF)** | 20 – 100 | 3 – 10 | ~1 (or < 1 for liquids) | 5 – 10 (vs raw liquor) |
| **Process temperature** | 800 – 1200 °C | Ambient | Ambient (with cure heat ~ 60 °C) | 1050 – 1200 °C |
| **Main hazards** | Off-gas (C-14, I-131, particulates) | Drum rupture, sharp edges | Leachate, alkaline corrosion of steel | Melter corrosion, off-gas (Ru, Cs) |
| **Cost relative** | High | Lowest | Lowest | Highest |
| **Waste category** | LLW / ILW | LLW | LLW / ILW | HLW |
| **Leach rate (g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹)** | N/A (ash) | N/A | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁷ |
| **Canister material** | Steel drum (200 L) | Steel drum / overpack | Steel drum (200 L) | Stainless Steel 304L (400 L) |
| **Disposal route** | Near-surface | Near-surface | Near-surface | Deep geological |
| **Duration of containment required** | 100 – 300 y | 100 – 300 y | 100 – 300 y | > 10⁵ y |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 alphanumeric-node safeguard: every node ID is prefixed with letters, and double-quoted labels contain **only uppercase alphanumeric text and underscores** — no bold/italic/HTML/tables inside labels.

## 4.1 Master Flow — Position of the Four Methods in the Waste Management Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[WASTE_GENERATION] --> A2[PRE_TREATMENT_SEGREGATION_DECON]
    A2 --> A3[TREATMENT_PHASE]
    A3 --> A4[CONDITIONING_PHASE]
    A4 --> A5[INTERIM_STORAGE]
    A5 --> A6[DEEP_GEOLOGICAL_DISPOSAL]

    A3 --> B1[INCINERATION]
    A3 --> B2[COMPACTION]
    A4 --> B3[CEMENTATION]
    A4 --> B4[VITRIFICATION]

    B1 --> C1[ASH_OUTPUT]
    B2 --> C2[COMPACTED_PUCK]
    B3 --> C3[CEMENT_MONOLITH]
    B4 --> C4[GLASS_CANISTER]

    C1 --> B3
    C1 --> B4
    C2 --> A5
    C3 --> A5
    C4 --> A5
```

**Reading the diagram:** Incineration and Compaction are *Treatment* methods (volume reduction). Their outputs (ash, compacted puck) are then *Conditioned* by Cementation (for LLW) or Vitrification (for HLW). Final waste forms go to storage, then to deep geological disposal.

## 4.2 Incineration — Process Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[RAW_COMBUSTIBLE_LLW] --> P2[FEED_HOPPER_SHREDDER]
    P2 --> P3[PRIMARY_COMBUSTION_CHAMBER_800C]
    P3 --> P4[SECONDARY_COMBUSTION_CHAMBER_1100C]
    P4 --> P5[QUENCH_SPRAY_COOLER]
    P5 --> P6[WET_SCRUBBER_FOR_ACID_GASES]
    P6 --> P7[HEPA_FILTER_FOR_PARTICULATES]
    P7 --> P8[ACTIVATED_CARBON_FOR_I_131_C_14]
    P8 --> P9[STACK_MONITORING_AND_DISCHARGE]
    P3 --> P10[ASH_COLLECTION_BOTTOM]
    P10 --> P11[CEMENTATION_OF_ASH]
    P11 --> P12[FINAL_WASTE_DRUM_200L]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Combustible LLW is shredded, burned in two stages (primary + secondary to ensure ≥ 1000 °C + 2 s residence time), then off-gas is quenched, scrubbed, HEPA-filtered, and carbon-adsorbed. The bottom ash — which concentrates most non-volatile radionuclides — is then immobilized by cementation.

## 4.3 Compaction — In-Drum Super-Compactor Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[SOLID_LLW] --> Q2[SEGMENTATION_SHEAR_IF_60MM]
    Q2 --> Q3[DRUM_LOADING_200L]
    Q3 --> Q4[RAM_PRESS_50_TO_2500_TONNES]
    Q4 --> Q5[LID_WELD_AND_DECONTAMINATION]
    Q5 --> Q6[SHIELDING_OVERPACK_IF_NEEDED]
    Q6 --> Q7[VRF_3_TO_10_ACHIEVED]
    Q7 --> Q8[INTERIM_STORAGE_AND_DISPOSAL]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Solid LLW is pre-cut if oversized, loaded into a steel drum, and crushed by a high-tonnage hydraulic ram. The drum is sealed, decontaminated, and (if the compacted puck needs extra shielding) overpacked. Final VRF is typically 3 – 10×.

## 4.4 Cementation — In-Drum Mixing Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[LLW_LIQUOR_OR_SLUDGE] --> R2[CHARACTERIZATION_DENSITY_PH_RAD]
    R2 --> R3[MIX_DESIGN_WC_RATIO_0_40]
    R3 --> R4[ADD_WASTE_TO_CEMENT_IN_200L_DRUM]
    R4 --> R5[IN_DRUM_MIXER_300_RPM]
    R5 --> R6[CURING_28_DAYS_20C]
    R6 --> R7[QC_TEST_COMPRESSIVE_STRENGTH_3_5_MPA]
    R7 --> R8[QC_TEST_LEACH_RATE_ANSI_16_1]
    R8 --> R9[DRUM_LID_WELD_AND_PAINT]
    R9 --> R10[INTERIM_STORAGE]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Liquor is first characterized (density, pH, radioactivity), then mixed with OPC at w/c = 0.40 inside the final 200 L drum. After 28 days of curing, QC tests verify strength and leach rate before sealing for storage.

## 4.5 Vitrification — Two-Stage HLW Immobilization (AVH / EP-500 Type)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[HLW_LIQUOR_FROM_REPROCESSING] --> S2[CALCINER_400_TO_800C]
    S2 --> S3[CALCINE_POWDER_WASTE_OXIDE]
    S3 --> S4[GLASS_FRIT_SIO2_B2O3_NA2O]
    S4 --> S5[JOULE_HEATED_CERAMIC_MELTER_1100C]
    S5 --> S6[MOLTEN_BOROSILICATE_GLASS]
    S6 --> S7[POUR_INTO_SS_304L_CANISTER_400L]
    S7 --> S8[CONTROLLED_COOLING_BELOW_TG]
    S8 --> S9[CANISTER_WELD_CAP_AND_DECON]
    S9 --> S10[INTERIM_STORAGE_IN_VENTILATED_CELL]
    S10 --> S11[DEEP_GEOLOGICAL_REPOSITORY]
    S5 --> S12[OFF_GAS_RU_CS_PARTICULATE_FILTER]
    S12 --> S13[STACK_DISCHARGE]
```

**Reading the diagram:** HLW liquor is first calcined (dried + denitrated) into a dry powder of waste oxides. The powder is mixed with borosilicate glass frit and melted at 1100 °C in a Joule-heated ceramic melter. The molten glass is poured into a stainless-steel canister, cooled below $T_g$ to ensure amorphous state, sealed, and stored. Off-gas is filtered to capture volatile ruthenium and cesium before discharge.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Definition of Vitrification `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]`

**Q.** Define *vitrification* of high-level nuclear waste. Mention the typical matrix composition, melting temperature, and one advantage over cementation.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> *Vitrification is a thermal conditioning process in which high-level waste (HLW) oxides from spent fuel reprocessing are incorporated into a borosilicate glass matrix by melting the waste with a glass frit (typically SiO₂ 45–55 %, B₂O₃ 10–15 %, Na₂O 8–12 %, plus minor CaO, MgO, Al₂O₃) at 1050–1200 °C inside a Joule-heated ceramic melter. The molten glass is poured into a 400 L stainless-steel canister where it solidifies as an amorphous, chemically durable solid.*
>
> *Advantage over cementation:* Vitrified HLW exhibits a **leach rate two to three orders of magnitude lower** (≈ 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁷ g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹) than cemented waste (≈ 10⁻⁴ g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹), and is qualified for **deep geological disposal** with isolation times exceeding 10⁵ years — well beyond the design life of near-surface cement repositories.
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition + matrix, 1 for temperature, 1 for advantage)*

### Question A2 — Waste Loading in Cementation `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**Q.** What is *waste loading* in cementation? State the optimum water-to-cement (w/c) ratio and the minimum 28-day compressive strength required for LLW cement waste forms.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> *Waste loading is the mass fraction of the actual radioactive waste in the final cement monolith, expressed as $WL = m_{\text{waste}}/(m_{\text{waste}} + m_{\text{cement}})$. For LLW liquids the practical range is 5–35 wt %.*
>
> *Optimum w/c ratio:* **0.40** (range 0.35 – 0.50). Lower w/c → denser C-S-H gel, higher strength, lower porosity.
>
> *Minimum compressive strength (28 days):* **≥ 3.5 MPa** as per IAEA TRS-401 and AERB guidelines.
> *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for w/c, 1 for strength)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Compaction and Incineration: A Comparative Engineering Analysis `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

Explain with a neat block diagram the **operational sequence of a high-pressure super-compactor (HPC)** used for solid LLW. Derive the volume reduction factor (VRF) for a case where 200 L of loose metallic waste (density 0.3 g/cm³) is compacted into a 200 L drum at a final density of 2.0 g/cm³.

**Model Answer:**

> **Operational Sequence (4 marks):**
> 1. **Pre-Segmentation** — Items > 60 mm in any dimension are sheared to prevent drum puncture and ensure uniform load transmission.
> 2. **Drum Loading** — Waste is gravity- or conveyor-fed into a 200 L steel drum placed inside the press chamber.
> 3. **Pre-Compaction** — A low-pressure (≈ 30 tonnes) ram removes entrapped air to prevent spring-back.
> 4. **Main Compaction** — A high-pressure ram (1000 – 2500 tonnes-force) compresses the waste to peak pressure of 30 – 100 MPa.
> 5. **Dwell & Decompression** — Pressure held for 30 – 60 s to allow viscoelastic stress relaxation (especially for plastics).
> 6. **Puck Extraction / Drum Removal** — The compacted "puck" is either retrieved for overpacking or the drum is sealed in-place.
> 7. **Decontamination & QC** — Drum exterior is decontaminated; surface dose rate < 2 mSv/h verified (IAEA SSR-6).
>
> **VRF Derivation (3 marks):**
>
> Initial waste volume $V_i = 200$ L. Initial density $\rho_i = 0.3$ g/cm³. Mass of waste $m = \rho_i V_i$.
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> m &= 0.3 \;\text{g/cm}^3 \times 200{,}000\;\text{cm}^3 = 60{,}000\;\text{g} = 60\;\text{kg} \\[4pt]
> V_f &= \frac{m}{\rho_f} = \frac{60{,}000\;\text{g}}{2.0\;\text{g/cm}^3} = 30{,}000\;\text{cm}^3 = 30\;\text{L} \\[4pt]
> \text{VRF} &= \frac{V_i}{V_f} = \frac{200}{30} \approx 6.67
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Final Answer: VRF ≈ 6.67 : 1** (i.e. ~ 85 % volume reduction).

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Explain the **two-stage combustion principle** of a radioactive waste incinerator, with reference to the **IAEA Turbulence Factor (T.F.) criterion**. A secondary combustion chamber operates at 1100 °C with a gas residence time of 2.5 s. Verify T.F. compliance and compute the destruction & removal efficiency (DRE) if the TBP inlet is 1.2 kg/h and the stack emission is 25 mg/h.

**Model Answer:**

> **Two-Stage Combustion (4 marks):**
> The first (primary) chamber at 600 – 800 °C dries and pyrolyses combustible waste, releasing volatiles and tars. The second (secondary) chamber operates at ≥ 1000 °C with a residence time ≥ 2 s in an excess-oxygen (> 6 %) environment to **completely oxidize** volatiles, tars, CO, soot, and any incomplete-combustion products. This staged design is mandated because radioactive wastes are heterogeneous (paper, plastics, ion-exchange resins, oils) and because the off-gas must meet a DRE ≥ 99.99 % (Six-Nines) for the principal organic hazardous constituent (POHC).
>
> **T.F. Verification (2 marks):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> T \,(\text{in K}) &= 1100 + 273 = 1373\;\text{K} \\
> \tau &= 2.5\;\text{s} \\
> T.F. &= \tau \cdot T = 2.5 \times 1373 = 3432.5\;\text{s·K}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> Since $T.F. = 3432.5 \;\text{s·K} \;\geq\; 0.5\;\text{s·K}$ **AND** $T = 1373\;\text{K} \;\geq\; 1273\;\text{K}$, the design is **fully compliant** with IAEA RS-G-1.7 / US EPA standards.
>
> **DRE Computation (1 mark):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> \text{DRE} &= \left(1 - \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{out}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
> &= \left(1 - \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}\;\text{g/h}}{1.2 \times 10^3\;\text{g/h}}\right) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
> &= \left(1 - 2.083 \times 10^{-5}\right) \times 100\% \\[4pt]
> &= 99.9979\,\% \;\;\geq\;\; 99.99\,\% \quad \checkmark
> \end{aligned}
> $$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B):** Students frequently **forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin** in the T.F. calculation — losing a full mark. Also, when computing DRE, the units of inlet and outlet *must be identical* (convert kg/h to g/h, or mg/h, before dividing). A common zero-mark error is writing $\dot{m}_{\text{out}}/\dot{m}_{\text{in}}$ as $25/1.2$ instead of $25\times10^{-3}/1200$.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — Cementation and Vitrification: Design and Comparison `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, May 2024 Internal Choice B]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

A **borosilicate glass** is to be designed for immobilizing **5 kg** of high-level waste (HLW) oxide. The target glass composition by mass is: SiO₂ = 50 %, B₂O₃ = 12 %, Na₂O = 10 %, Al₂O₃ = 5 %, with the balance (23 %) being waste oxide. Determine the **total mass of glass frit required** and the **number of 400 L stainless-steel canisters** needed if each canister can hold 400 kg of molten glass (glass density at pouring = 2.7 g/cm³, canister fill height factor = 0.85).

**Model Answer:**

> **Step 1 — Mass balance on waste loading (2 marks):**
> Waste oxide fraction $WL = 0.23$ of total glass mass $M_g$.
> Thus $0.23 \cdot M_g = 5$ kg $\Rightarrow M_g = 5/0.23 \approx 21.74$ kg of glass.
>
> **Step 2 — Mass of glass frit required (1 mark):**
> Frit fraction = $1 - 0.23 = 0.77$ ⇒ $m_{\text{frit}} = 0.77 \times 21.74 \approx 16.74$ kg.
>
> **Step 3 — Volume of one canister's molten glass (2 marks):**
> Effective fill volume $V_{\text{fill}} = 400\;\text{L} \times 0.85 = 340\;\text{L} = 0.340\;\text{m}^3$.
> Mass of glass per canister:
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> m_{\text{per canister}} &= \rho_{\text{glass}} \cdot V_{\text{fill}} \\[4pt]
> &= 2700\;\tfrac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^3} \times 0.340\;\text{m}^3 \\[4pt]
> &= 918\;\text{kg/canister}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Step 4 — Number of canisters (2 marks):**
>
> $$
> \begin{aligned}
> N &= \frac{M_g}{m_{\text{per canister}}} = \frac{21.74\;\text{kg}}{918\;\text{kg/canister}} \\[4pt]
> &= 0.0237 \;\text{canisters} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; N = 1\;\text{canister (rounded up)}
> \end{aligned}
> $$
>
> **Final Answer:** ~ 16.74 kg of glass frit and **1 canister** of 400 L is sufficient.

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

Compare **cementation** and **vitrification** as conditioning techniques for radioactive waste. Your answer must cover the following in tabular form: waste category, matrix material, process temperature, leach rate, container, typical disposal route, and the radiological isolation period for which each is qualified.

**Model Answer (Tabular Comparison — 7 marks, 1 per row):**

| Property | **Cementation** | **Vitrification** |
|----------|------------------|--------------------|
| **Waste category** | LLW (liquors, sludges, ash); some ILW | HLW from spent fuel reprocessing |
| **Matrix material** | Ordinary Portland Cement (OPC) + additives (fly ash, bentonite) | Borosilicate glass (SiO₂-B₂O₃-Na₂O-Al₂O₃) |
| **Process temperature** | Ambient (cure exotherm ~ 60 °C) | 1050 – 1200 °C (molten state) |
| **Leach rate (g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹)** | 10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁶ | 10⁻⁶ – 10⁻⁷ |
| **Container** | 200 L mild-steel drum | 400 L Stainless Steel 304L canister |
| **Disposal route** | Engineered Near-Surface Disposal Facility (NSDF) | Deep Geological Repository (DGR), 500+ m |
| **Qualified isolation period** | 100 – 300 years (regulatory period) | > 10⁵ years (geological time scale) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Part B-B):** A very common answer mistake is to write the **leach rates in the wrong order** (claiming vitrification has *higher* leach rate than cement — wrong). Another is to **omit the units** of leach rate (g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹), which the examiner penalises 0.5 mark. Always quote both the **method** (MCC-1, MCC-4, ANSI/ANS-16.1, PCT) *and* the value.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist (For Last-Minute KTU Exam Review)**

**1. Treatment vs Conditioning (Conceptual Distinction)**
- *Treatment* = alters the waste (volume ↓, hazard ↓) → **Incineration, Compaction**.
- *Conditioning* = encapsulates the waste into a stable matrix → **Cementation, Vitrification**.

**2. Volume Reduction Factor (VRF) Mastery**
- $\text{VRF} = V_i / V_f$.
- Incineration: VRF ≈ 20 – 100.
- Compaction: VRF ≈ 3 – 10.
- Cementation: VRF ≤ 1 (often increases total volume — immobilization gain, not volume gain).
- Vitrification: VRF ≈ 5 – 10 vs raw HLW liquor.

**3. Incineration Key Numbers**
- Temperature ≥ 1000 °C; residence time ≥ 2 s; DRE ≥ 99.99 %.
- Turbulence Factor $T.F. = \tau \cdot T \geq 0.5$ s·K (must convert °C → K).
- Off-gas train: Quench → Scrubber → HEPA → Activated Carbon → Stack.

**4. Compaction Key Numbers**
- Low-pressure (LPC): 5 – 50 tonnes; VRF 2 – 5.
- Super-compactor (HPC): 1000 – 2500 tonnes; VRF 5 – 10.
- Pre-cut items > 60 mm; surface dose < 2 mSv/h.

**5. Cementation Key Numbers**
- $w/c = 0.40$ (optimum); 28-day $\sigma_c \geq 3.5$ MPa.
- WL = 5 – 35 % (LLW); pH ≈ 12.5 (passivation).
- Abrams' Law: $\sigma_c = A / B^{w/c}$.
- Incompatible with reactive metals (Al, Mg → exotherm → H₂ → cracking).

**6. Vitrification Key Numbers**
- Borosilicate glass frit; pour at 1050 – 1200 °C; $T_g \approx 500$ °C.
- Waste loading 15 – 30 wt%; viscosity 10 – 100 Pa·s at pouring.
- Density ≈ 2.7 g/cm³; 400 L SS-304L canister.
- Melter types: Joule-heated ceramic (most common), induction, gas-fired.

**7. Master Comparison Mnemonic — "I-C-C-V"**
- **I**ncineration → **C**ombustibles → **A**sh.
- **C**ompaction → **S**olids → **P**uck.
- **C**ementation → **L**iquids/Sludges/Ash → **M**onolith.
- **V**itrification → **H**LW liquor → **G**lass canister.

**8. Common Units to Memorize**
- Radioactivity: Bq, Ci ($1\,\text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq).
- Dose: Sv, mSv/h.
- Leach rate: g·cm⁻²·day⁻¹.
- Glass viscosity: Pa·s.

**9. IAEA Documents to Be Aware of (for viva/project)**
- IAEA Safety Standards Series **SSR-6** (Transport Regulations).
- **RS-G-1.7** (Application of Concepts of Exclusion, Exemption and Clearance).
- **TRS-401** (Long Term Behaviour of Cementitious Materials).
- **GSR Part 5** (Predisposal Management of Radioactive Waste).

**10. Two Sentences to Memorize Verbatim for the Exam**
> *"Vitrification is the internationally accepted best-available technique for the immobilization of high-level radioactive waste in a borosilicate glass matrix, qualified for disposal in deep geological repositories."*
> *"Cementation is the most widely used conditioning technique for low- and intermediate-level waste, owing to its low cost, ambient-temperature operation, and good radiation shielding, with the design water-to-cement ratio of 0.40 ensuring the required 28-day compressive strength of at least 3.5 MPa."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste Disposal Methods - Near Surface Disposal, Deep Geological Disposal, Disposal at Outer Space, Deep Boreholes and Disposal at Sea
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET412 - NUCLEAR ENERGY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Waste Disposal Methods - Near Surface Disposal, Deep Geological Disposal, Disposal at Outer Space, Deep Boreholes and Disposal at Sea

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is "Radioactive Waste Disposal"?

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, **Radioactive Waste Disposal** is defined as the **permanent, supervised emplacement of conditioned radioactive waste in an appropriate facility without the intention of retrieval**, ensuring long-term isolation of the radionuclides from the accessible environment (the human biosphere) for the period during which they remain hazardous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Module 4 highlight):**
> Disposal is the final step of the radioactive waste management chain — following *minimisation → treatment → conditioning → storage*. It is **passive**, **supervised**, and **engineered for millennia**, not for years.

Disposal is fundamentally different from **storage**:
- *Storage* → temporary, retrieval possible, active monitoring.
- *Disposal* → permanent, retrieval not intended, passive safety barriers.

## 1.2 The Five Disposal Strategies Covered in Module 4

The KTU syllabus, in line with IAEA and AERB (Atomic Energy Regulatory Board, India) classification, identifies **five principal disposal routes**:

1. **Near Surface Disposal (NSD)**
2. **Deep Geological Disposal (DGD)**
3. **Disposal at Sea (Sub-seabed / Ocean dumping)**
4. **Disposal in Outer Space**
5. **Deep Borehole Disposal (DBD)**

| Method | Typical Waste Class | KTU Exam Priority |
|---|---|---|
| Near Surface Disposal | LLW, Short-Lived ILW | **Very High** |
| Deep Geological Disposal | HLW, Spent Fuel, Long-Lived ILW | **Very High** |
| Deep Borehole Disposal | Spent Fuel, HLW | High |
| Disposal at Sea | LLW (historically) | Moderate |
| Disposal in Outer Space | HLW (theoretical) | Moderate |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Time Capsule of Civilisation"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine you are sealing a **time capsule** that will be buried for 10,000 years so dangerous artefacts inside can never harm a future generation. You would (a) lock the artefacts in **stainless-steel capsules** (engineered barrier), (b) wrap them in **inert clay that swells if water enters** (buffer), (c) place the bundle in a **salt cavern formed millions of years ago** (geological barrier), and (d) put a **monument on the surface** to warn future humans (institutional control). This is exactly the philosophy of **deep geological disposal** — multi-barrier defence-in-depth over geological timescales.

## 1.4 Why Multiple Methods? — The Waste-Disposal Matching Principle

Different radionuclides have **vastly different half-lives and toxicities**:

$$T_{\frac{1}{2}} \text{ range} = 10^{1} \text{ s (short-lived)} \longrightarrow 10^{16} \text{ years (}^{129}\text{I, }^{237}\text{Np)}$$

A single "one-size-fits-all" disposal solution is **impossible**. Hence the KTU 2024 curriculum trains engineers to *match* the disposal method to the waste's:
- **Activity level** (Bq/kg or Bq/m³)
- **Half-life** of dominant radionuclides
- **Heat output** (W/m³)
- **Chemical/mobility hazard**

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb used by IAEA / AERB:**
> - Waste with $\beta/\gamma$ half-life $<$ **30 years** → **Near Surface Disposal** is acceptable.
> - Waste containing long-lived $\alpha$-emitters (e.g., $^{239}$Pu, $T_{1/2} = 24{,}100$ y) → **Deep Geological Disposal** is mandatory.
> - Waste with heat output $>$ **2 kW/m³** → requires **deep geological** or **deep borehole** (not NSD).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Multi-Barrier Concept (Defence-in-Depth)

All engineered disposal systems rely on the **multi-barrier principle** — at least two independent, redundant barriers isolate the radionuclides:

| Barrier # | Type | Example | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Waste Form | Vitrified glass, ceramic, cement matrix | Immobilise radionuclides |
| 2 | Waste Container | Stainless steel, copper, titanium canister | Mechanical + corrosion barrier |
| 3 | Buffer / Backfill | Compacted bentonite clay | Slow groundwater, sorb radionuclides |
| 4 | Host Rock | Granite, salt, clay, tuff | Natural geological barrier |
| 5 | Institutional Control | Surveillance, markers | Period of active monitoring |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Even if **all engineered barriers fail**, the **geological barrier must independently** keep doses below regulatory limits.

## 2.2 Near Surface Disposal (NSD) — Theory

**Definition:** Engineered disposal facility, typically at depths of a few metres to a few tens of metres, intended for **Low and Intermediate Level Waste (LILW)** with limited radionuclide content.

### 2.2.1 Design Variants
- **Vault-type (above-grade or below-grade concrete cells)** — packages placed in concrete vaults.
- **Tumulus-type (mound disposal)** — silos covered by an impermeable multi-layer cap.
- **Trench-type** — for very low activity waste.

### 2.2.2 Engineered Barriers
- Waste form (cement / bitumen / polymer immobilisation)
- Reinforced concrete container
- Concrete cell liner
- Drainage layer + impermeable cap (clay, geomembrane)
- Top cover (vegetation barrier)

### 2.2.3 Site Selection Criteria (IAEA Safety Standards Series SS-111)
- Low population density
- Low groundwater velocity ($<$ **1 m/yr**)
- Stable geology (no seismic risk zone 4+)
- No mineral / water resource conflict
- Demonstrable institutional control for **300 years**

### 2.2.4 Reference Facility — Centre de la Manche (France)
Operational **1969–1994**; received ~527,000 m³ of LLW; cap completed 2001; institutional surveillance until ~300 years.

> [!NOTE]
> **Indian Context (for KTU reference):**
> India's NSD facility is located at **TAPS (Tarapur Atomic Power Station)**, with planned deep geological facility in granitic gneiss of the **Kudankulam region**.

## 2.3 Deep Geological Disposal (DGD) — Theory

**Definition:** Disposal of high-level waste (HLW) and spent fuel in a facility constructed at depths of **300–1000 m** in a **stable geological formation**.

### 2.3.1 The Five Host-Rock Options

| Host Rock | Country Example | Key Property | Drawback |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Granite / Gneiss** | Finland (Onkalo), Sweden (Forsmark) | Mechanically strong, low permeability | Brittle fractures possible |
| **Salt (bedded / domal)** | Germany (Gorleben), USA (WIPP) | Self-sealing plastic deformation | Brine inclusions, slow convergence |
| **Clay (Boom Clay, Opalinus)** | Belgium, France (Cigéo) | Very low permeability, high sorption | Soft, mechanically weak |
| **Tuff** | USA (Yucca Mountain — cancelled) | Welded, low permeability | Unsaturated zone challenges |
| **Basalt** | USA (Hanford studies) | Strong, low permeability | Fractured columnar joints |

### 2.3.2 Repository Layout (Typical Granite Repository)
- **Access shaft** (main + ventilation)
- **Drift / tunnel** (transportation)
- **Deposition hole** (single canister) OR **deposition tunnel** (multiple canisters)
- **Emplacement cell** for the canister
- **Bentonite buffer blocks** surrounding the canister

### 2.3.3 The KBS-3 Concept (Swedish/Finnish reference)
- Spent fuel in **copper canister** (5 cm wall, corrosion life $>$ 100,000 years).
- Embedded in **compacted bentonite** buffer.
- Placed in deposition holes drilled into the floor of a granite tunnel.
- Repository sealed with bentonite plugs and concrete plugs.

### 2.3.4 Long-Term Safety Function
The repository must isolate radionuclides for **up to 1 million years** (the decay time for $^{239}$Pu to background levels).

$$t_{\text{isolation}} \approx \frac{T_{1/2}(^{239}\text{Pu})}{\ln 2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{N_0}{N_{\text{background}}}\right) \approx 10 \times T_{1/2} \approx 240{,}000 \text{ years}$$

## 2.4 Disposal at Sea (Ocean Disposal) — Theory

**Definition:** Disposal of packaged radioactive waste into the deep ocean (sub-seabed or water column).

### 2.4.1 Historical Practice
- **1946–1982:** ~85,000 TBq of packaged low-level waste dumped into the North Atlantic, North Pacific, and other ocean basins (mostly by USA, UK, USSR, Japan, Netherlands, Switzerland).
- Packages: low-strength concrete, asphalt bitumen drums, often with low-activity reactor decommissioning waste.

### 2.4.2 International Legal Status
> [!WARNING]
> **BANNED by the London Convention (1972) and the 1996 London Protocol.**
> Both prohibit all sea disposal of radioactive waste, with only very limited research exceptions under special permit. The 1996 Protocol states: *"Contracting Parties shall not permit the export of radioactive wastes ... to any other State or to the seabed and subsoil of any other State."*

### 2.4.3 Reasons for the Ban
- Transboundary pollution
- Uncontrollable long-term corrosion in seawater
- Loss of national jurisdiction
- Cannot be reversed / monitored
- Ocean ecosystem fragility

## 2.5 Disposal in Outer Space — Theory

**Definition:** Launching of high-level radioactive waste beyond Earth's biosphere, typically into a **solar orbit**, **deep space**, or **directly into the Sun**.

### 2.5.1 Methods Considered
- **Solar Incineration** — waste launched directly into the Sun.
- **Solar Orbit** — placed in heliocentric orbit (Earth-Sun Lagrange point).
- **Lunar Repository** — transported to the Moon (more realistic, studied by NASA/ESA).

### 2.5.2 Conceptual Advantages
- Complete removal from Earth's biosphere
- Eliminates need for centuries of monitoring
- Solves the "10,000-year warning problem" faced by DGD

### 2.5.3 Why Not Practiced — The Hard Constraints
- **Cost:** Estimated \$**2–4 million per kg** of waste launched.
- **Launch Risk:** ~**1–2 %** launch failure probability → contaminates biosphere with unshielded spent fuel.
- **Outer Space Treaty (1967):** Article IX requires avoidance of "harmful contamination" of celestial bodies.
- **Public Risk:** A single failure could disperse plutonium globally.
- **Tonnage:** World inventory of spent fuel is ~**400,000 tonnes HM** — impractically large.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering reality (KTU takeaway):** Outer-space disposal remains a **theoretical option** — never deployed. The KTU 2024 syllabus tags it as "**Conceptual / Not Practiced**".

## 2.6 Deep Borehole Disposal (DBD) — Theory

**Definition:** Disposal of spent fuel or HLW in **small-diameter (≤ 0.75 m) boreholes drilled 3–5 km deep** into stable crystalline basement rock.

### 2.6.1 Concept
- Borehole drilled through sedimentary cover into **crystalline basement** (granite, gneiss).
- Waste canisters (each ~5 m long) **stacked** in the lower 1–2 km of the borehole.
- Upper 1–2 km backfilled with **sealing plugs** (bentonite, asphalt, cement, gravel).

### 2.6.2 Advantages over DGD
- **Smaller footprint** (one borehole per site vs. km-scale tunnel network).
- **Greater natural isolation** (deeper than 3 km, very low groundwater flow).
- **Lower excavation cost** (no large tunnels).
- **Sequential disposal** (canister-by-canister, matches spent-fuel production rate).

### 2.6.3 Challenges
- **Retrievability** — practically impossible once sealed.
- **Drilling risk** — deviation, stuck drill string.
- **Heat dissipation** — limited annulus area may not cool high-burnup fuel.
- **Verification** — can we *prove* the waste is properly emplaced?

### 2.6.4 Reference Design (Sandia / MIT Studies)
- Borehole: **0.5 m diameter**, **5 km deep**.
- Lower **2 km** holds ~200 canisters.
- Upper **3 km** is the engineered seal zone.

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these for the KTU University Exam — the questions are almost always quantitative.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Radioactive decay | $N(t) = N_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ |
| 2 | Activity | $A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Becquerel (Bq) |
| 3 | Decay heat (Way-Wigner) | $P(t) \approx 0.0622 \, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - 10^{-6} \, t^{-0.4} \right]$ | $t$ in seconds, $P_0$ = power at shutdown |
| 4 | Multi-barrier release rate (1st-order) | $\frac{dN}{dt} = -\lambda_{\text{eff}} \, N$ | $\lambda_{\text{eff}}^{-1} = \sum \lambda_i^{-1}$ |
| 5 | Dose rate (point source) | $\dot{D} = \frac{\Gamma \cdot A}{r^2}$ | $\Gamma$ = gamma constant, $r$ = distance |
| 6 | Effective dose limit (public) | $\dot{H} \leq 1$ mSv/yr | ICRP 60, AERB safety directive |
| 7 | Required isolation time | $t = 10 \, T_{1/2}$ (rule of thumb) | For background-level activity |
| 8 | Specific activity | $a = \frac{\lambda N_A}{M}$ | $N_A$ = Avogadro, $M$ = molar mass |
| 9 | Half-life from decay constant | $T_{1/2} = \frac{0.693}{\lambda}$ | seconds |
| 10 | Repository thermal power density | $q = \frac{P_{\text{decay}}}{V_{\text{repo}}}$ | W/m³, must be $<$ 2 kW/m³ for salt |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** These formulas allow the nuclear engineer to (a) size the disposal vault, (b) determine the buffer thickness needed to keep groundwater concentration below the regulatory limit, and (c) certify the repository for the regulator (AERB in India, NRC in USA, ASN in France).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Decay Heat Released by Spent Fuel (Way-Wigner Equation)

The KTU syllabus frequently tests the student's ability to compute the **decay heat of spent fuel** at a given time after shutdown. This determines whether the waste can be sent to **Near Surface Disposal** (low heat) or must go to **Deep Geological / Borehole** disposal (high heat).

### Step 1 — Energy Release from Fission Products
When a nuclear reactor operates at a thermal power $P_0$ (in MW), it undergoes fission. Upon shutdown, the fission products continue to release decay energy.

The **Way-Wigner empirical formula** (universal for short-cooling times) gives the decay heat as a function of cooling time $t$ (in seconds):

$$P(t) = 0.0622 \, P_0 \left[ t^{-0.2} - 10^{-6} \cdot t^{-0.4} \right]$$

where:
- $P(t)$ = decay heat at time $t$ after shutdown (MW)
- $P_0$ = reactor thermal power at shutdown (MW)
- $t$ = cooling time (seconds)
- The 0.0622 coefficient and the exponents 0.2 and 0.4 are empirical fits to U-235 fission-product data.

### Step 2 — Converting Cooling Time to Convenient Units
Since $t$ is in seconds, we convert:

$$t \, (\text{seconds}) = t \, (\text{days}) \times 86400$$

### Step 3 — Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU-style Problem)
**Given:** A 1000 MW (thermal) reactor is shut down. Compute the decay heat after **1 year** and after **10 years** of cooling.

**Step 3.1 — Compute $t$ in seconds for 1 year:**

$$t = 1 \text{ yr} \times 365.25 \text{ d/yr} \times 86400 \text{ s/d} = 3.156 \times 10^{7} \text{ s}$$

**Step 3.2 — Compute $t^{-0.2}$ and $t^{-0.4}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t^{-0.2} &= (3.156 \times 10^{7})^{-0.2} \\
&= 10^{-1.40} \times 3.156^{-0.2} \\
&\approx 0.03981 \times 0.789 \\
&\approx 0.0314
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t^{-0.4} &= (3.156 \times 10^{7})^{-0.4} \\
&= 10^{-2.80} \times 3.156^{-0.4} \\
&\approx 1.585 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.681 \\
&\approx 1.080 \times 10^{-3}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.3 — Apply the Way-Wigner formula:**

$$\begin{aligned}
P(1\text{ yr}) &= 0.0622 \times 1000 \left[ 0.0314 - 10^{-6} \times 1.080 \times 10^{-3} \right] \\
&= 62.2 \left[ 0.0314 - 1.080 \times 10^{-9} \right] \\
&\approx 62.2 \times 0.0314 \\
&\approx 1.953 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3.4 — Repeat for 10 years:**

$$t(10\text{ yr}) = 3.156 \times 10^{8} \text{ s}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
t^{-0.2} &= (3.156 \times 10^{8})^{-0.2} \approx 0.0198 \\
t^{-0.4} &= (3.156 \times 10^{8})^{-0.4} \approx 3.92 \times 10^{-4}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P(10\text{ yr}) &= 62.2 \left[ 0.0198 - 3.92 \times 10^{-10} \right] \\
&\approx 62.2 \times 0.0198 \\
&\approx 1.232 \text{ MW}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** The decay heat drops from ~2 MW to ~1.2 MW over 9 additional years. This is **why High-Level Waste is "temporarily stored" for 30–50 years** before being moved to a deep geological repository — the heat load drops by orders of magnitude, simplifying repository design.

### Step 4 — Decision Rule for Disposal Route
The heat per unit volume $q$ of the disposed waste dictates the disposal route:

$$q = \frac{P(t)}{V_{\text{waste package}}}$$

| Heat Density $q$ (W/m³) | Disposal Route |
|---|---|
| $q < 400$ | Near Surface Disposal (eligible) |
| $400 \leq q < 2000$ | Intermediate (engineered near-surface) |
| $q \geq 2000$ | Deep Geological / Deep Borehole Disposal |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Isolation Time Using the "10 Half-Lives" Rule

**Problem (KTU 2024 pattern):** A waste contains $^{239}$Pu with a half-life $T_{1/2} = 24{,}100$ years. The initial activity concentration is $A_0 = 1 \times 10^{12}$ Bq/m³. The regulatory clearance level is $A_{\text{lim}} = 100$ Bq/m³. How long must the waste be isolated?

**Step 1 — Apply radioactive decay law:**

$$A(t) = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = A_0 \cdot e^{-\frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}} t}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $t$:**

$$e^{-\lambda t} = \frac{A_{\text{lim}}}{A_0} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = -\frac{T_{1/2}}{\ln 2} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{A_{\text{lim}}}{A_0}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t &= -\frac{24{,}100}{0.693} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{100}{1 \times 10^{12}}\right) \\
&= -34{,}776 \cdot \ln(1 \times 10^{-10}) \\
&= -34{,}776 \cdot (-23.026) \\
&\approx 800{,}727 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the "10 half-lives" engineering rule of thumb:**

$$t \approx 10 \times T_{1/2} = 10 \times 24{,}100 = 241{,}000 \text{ years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The repository must isolate plutonium-bearing waste for at least **~250,000 years** — the entire period of human civilisation. This is the **fundamental driver** for choosing Deep Geological Disposal (which has been stable for hundreds of millions of years) over Near Surface Disposal (which can be safely demonstrated for only ~300 years).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Multi-Barrier Equivalent Decay Constant

In a real repository, radionuclide release is limited by **sequential barriers** in series: each barrier i has its own release rate constant $\lambda_i$ (1/time). The effective overall release rate $\lambda_{\text{eff}}$ for barriers in **series** is:

$$\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eff}}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_1} + \frac{1}{\lambda_2} + \frac{1}{\lambda_3} + \frac{1}{\lambda_4}$$

**Worked Example:** A copper canister in bentonite-buffered granite has barrier decay constants (1/yr):

- Waste form ($\lambda_1$) = 1/1000
- Canister ($\lambda_2$) = 1/100,000
- Buffer ($\lambda_3$) = 1/10,000
- Geosphere ($\lambda_4$) = 1/1,000,000

**Step 1 — Compute the sum of inverse rates:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{eff}}} &= 1000 + 100{,}000 + 10{,}000 + 1{,}000{,}000 \\
&= 1{,}111{,}000 \text{ yr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Effective rate:**

$$\lambda_{\text{eff}} = \frac{1}{1{,}111{,}000} \approx 9.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ yr}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The slowest barrier dominates.** Adding a faster barrier (waste form, $\lambda_1^{-1} = 1000$ yr) hardly changes the total — the geosphere (1 million years) is the controlling barrier. This is **why the choice of host rock is the single most important decision in deep geological disposal**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Disposal Method Selection

This is a high-yield **decision matrix** that examiners love to test:

| Selection Criterion | NSD | DGD | Sea | Space | DBD |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Waste Class** | LLW + SL-ILW | HLW + LL-ILW + SF | LLW (legacy) | HLW (concept) | HLW + SF |
| **Depth (m)** | 0–30 | 300–1000 | 2000–5000 (water) | — | 3000–5000 |
| **Isolation Time (yr)** | 100–300 | 100,000–1,000,000 | Uncontrolled | Infinite | 100,000+ |
| **Retrievability** | Possible (limited) | Possible until sealed | Impossible | Impossible | Practically impossible |
| **Cost (USD/m³)** | 50–500 | 5,000–20,000 | Historic (low) | $>$ 1,000,000 | 1,000–5,000 |
| **Public Acceptability** | High | Moderate | Very Low (banned) | Very Low (perceived) | Moderate |
| **Regulatory Status (2024)** | Active worldwide | Active / Pilot | **Banned (London Protocol)** | Theoretical | Pilot studies |
| **Typical Engineered Barriers** | 3 (waste, container, cap) | 5 (full multi-barrier) | 1 (container only) | 1 (rocket + capsule) | 4 (canister, buffer, seals, rock) |
